### Decoded Body

```text
What is Help Workshop?

Help Workshop is a program that you use to create Help (.hlp) files, edit project and contents files, and test and report on help files. Help Workshop takes the information in the project (.hpj) file to combine the topic (.rtf) files, bitmaps, and other sources into one Help file that can be viewed using the Microsoft Windows Help program.

Help Workshop’s executable name is hcw.exe.

{button ,AL(`BAS_COMPILE_MSG;HCW;HCRTF;TMPDIR')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_COMPILE_MSG;HCW;HCRTF;TMPDIR')
```

\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Help Workshop is a program that you use to create Help (.hlp) files, edit project and contents files, and test and report on help files. Help Workshop takes the information in the project (.hpj) file to combine the topic (.rtf) files, bitmaps, and other sources into one Help file that can be viewed using the Microsoft Windows Help program.
\par Help Workshop\plain\f0\fs16 \'92\f1 s executable name is hcw.exe.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295

### Decoded Body

```text
Help Workshop components

Help Workshop includes the following files and documents:

·	Help Workshop (Hcw.exe and Hcrtf.exe).  Enables you to edit project and contents files, and to compile, test, and create reports for Help files.

·	Help Author's Guide (Hcw.hlp).  Describes how to author and compile Help files by using Help Workshop.

·	Hotspot Editor version 2.0 (Shed.exe).  Enables you to create a graphic that has multiple hotspots.7BM6.K

·	Multi-Resolution Bitmap Compiler version 1.1 (Mrbc.exe).  Enables you to create bitmaps that have different resolutions. You can combine these bitmaps into a single graphic to compensate for differences between the aspect ratios of the bitmaps and the aspect ratio supported by a user's display.

{button ,AL(`BAS_MULT_RESOLUTION;BAS_THE_HELP_COMPILER;MRBC;SHED')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_MULT_RESOLUTION;BAS_THE_HELP_COMPILER;MRBC;SHED')
```

\b Help Workshop components

\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Help Workshop (Hcw.exe and Hcrtf.exe).  Enables you to edit project and contents files, and to compile, test, and create reports for Help files.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Help Author's Guide (Hcw.hlp).  Describes how to author and compile Help files by using Help Workshop.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Hotspot Editor version 2.0 (Shed.exe).  Enables you to create a graphic that has multiple \ul hotspots.\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7BM6.K}
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Multi-Resolution Bitmap Compiler version 1.1 (Mrbc.exe).  Enables you to create bitmaps that have different resolutions. You can combine these bitmaps into a single graphic to compensate for differences between the aspect ratios of the bitmaps and the aspect ratio supported by a user's display.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295

### Decoded Body

```text
Notational Conventions

This Help file uses the following conventions.

Convention	Use

Bold	Indicates options and commands specific to Help authoring (for example, the CloseWindow macro) and C language-specific terms and options (for example, the WinHelp function).

[brackets]	Encloses optional items in syntax statements. For example, [window-name] indicates a window name might be needed in the syntax statement. Type only the information within the brackets, not the brackets themselves.

{-braces}	Encloses RTF statements in topic files. Type the bold braces and commands, and substitute your values for parameters that are not displayed in bold.

Semicolon (;)	Separates keywords in topic footnotes and macro strings. Also introduces comments in the project (.hpj) file.

Horizontal ellipsis (...)	Indicates that the item shown might appear more than once.

Vertical ellipsis(. . .)	Indicates that the example omits a portion of the file or program.

User	Indicates the person using the finished Help system.

Author	Indicates the person (or persons) preparing the Help system.

Developer	Indicates the person (or persons) developing the program from which the Help file is accessed, if there is one.
```

\par \b Bold\plain\f1\fs16 \tab Indicates options and commands specific to Help authoring (for example, the \b CloseWindow\plain\f1\fs16  macro) and C language-specific terms and options (for example, the \b WinHelp\plain\f1\fs16  function).
\par [brackets]\tab Encloses optional items in syntax statements. For example, [window-name] indicates a window name might be needed in the syntax statement. Type only the information within the brackets, not the brackets themselves.
\par \b \{\-\plain\f1\fs16 braces\b \'7d\plain\f1\fs16 \tab Encloses RTF statements in topic files. Type the bold braces and commands, and substitute your values for parameters that are not displayed in bold.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1975\ri125\fi-1865\tx1975 Semicolon (;)\tab Separates keywords in topic footnotes and macro strings. Also introduces comments in the project (.hpj) file.
\par Horizontal ellipsis (...)\tab Indicates that the item shown might appear more than once.

    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Software requirements

To author Windows Help version 4.0 files, you need the following software on your computer:

·	Help Workshop (Hcw.exe and Hcrtf.exe)

·	WinHelp version 4.0 (Winhlp32.exe)

·	A word processor that saves files in rich-text format (RTF), inserts custom footnotes, supports single and double underlining, and allows hidden text.

·	Windows 95 or Windows NT.

Notes

·	Although you can author Help files on any version of Windows NT, WinHelp version 4.0 runs only on Windows NT version 3.51 or later. Help Workshop runs on Windows 95 or later, and on any version of Windows NT.

·	If you are familiar with RTF coding, you can prepare and save your topic files using any word processor or text editor that can retain the files in that format.

·	Do not use Microsoft WordPad to author topic files.

{button ,AL("COMPRESS;FTS;RTFHelpRTFStatements")} Related Topics%!AL("COMPRESS;FTS;RTFHelpRTFStatements")
```

### Decoded Body

```text
New Help Topics dialog box

The Help Topics dialog box contains a Contents tab, an Index tab, and a Find tab. This dialog box appears when users:

·	Click Help Topics on the Help menu in a program

·	Double-click a Help file in Windows Explorer or My Computer or a Help shortcut icon on the desktop. The Help file must be associated with a contents (.cnt) file that specifies a Contents tab.

The Contents tab contains information that is specified by the new contents (.cnt) file. The Index tab contains the keywords for one or more Help files. The Find tab provides full-text search capability for all Help files.

{button ,AL(`PRO_CNT_CREATE')} Related Topics%!AL(`PRO_CNT_CREATE')
```

\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Double-click a Help file in Windows Explorer or My Computer or a Help shortcut icon on the desktop. The Help file must be associated with a contents (.cnt) file that specifies a Contents tab.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 The Contents tab contains information that is specified by the new contents (.cnt) file. The Index tab contains the keywords for one or more Help files. The Find tab provides full-text search capability for all Help files.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295

### Decoded Body

```text
New context menu

When users click a Help window using their right mouse button, a menu that contains the following commands appears:

·	Annotate

·	Copy

·	Print Topic

·	Font

·	Keep Help On Top

·	Use System Colors

These commands also appear if users click the Options button in a Help window. An Options button appears on a Help window's button bar if you specify it in your project (hpj) file.

Notes

·	When you are viewing a Help file in Help Author mode,PZJ46U two additional commands appear on the menu:

·	Topic Information

·	Ask On Hotspots

{button ,AL(`askonhot')} Related Topics%!AL(`askonhot')
```

·	Keep Help On Top

·	Display History Window

·	Font

·	Use System Colors

Most of these commands also appear when users click a Help window using their right mouse button, and when users click the Options button in a Help window. An Options button appears on a Help window's button bar if you specify it in your project (hpj) file.
```

\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Keep Help On Top
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Display History Window

### Decoded Body

```text
New annotation capabilities

WinHelp version 4.0 supports read-only annotation files. System administrators can insert annotations in a Help file, and then add a read-only attribute to the annotation (.ann) file. This prevents users from adding or editing annotations, but enables them to view annotations by clicking the paper-clip icon in annotated topics.

System administrators can place annotation files in network locations.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b New annotation capabilities
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 WinHelp version 4.0 supports read-only annotation files. System administrators can insert annotations in a Help file, and then add a read-only attribute to the annotation (.ann) file. This prevents users from adding or editing annotations, but enables them to view annotations by clicking the paper-clip icon in annotated topics.
\par System administrators can place annotation files in network locations.
\par
```

  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Help Workshop;Help Workshop, new features;new features;new features, graphical interface in Help Workshop;WinHelp 3.x files
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Graphical interfaces

Help Workshop has a new graphical interface that enables you to compile your Help files and to create and edit project (.hpj) and contents (.cnt) files. Using the graphical interface, you can easily specify or change settings. If you don't understand what a setting is for, you can use context-sensitive Help to learn more about it.

Note

·	Help Workshop can read (.hpj) files that were created for version 3.x of WinHelp. However, if you use Help Workshop to edit these files, they are saved in a slightly different format, and the MS-DOS–based compiler will be unable to process them.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b Graphical interfaces
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Help Workshop has a new graphical interface that enables you to compile your Help files and to create and edit project (.hpj) and contents (.cnt) files. Using the graphical interface, you can easily specify or change settings. If you don't understand what a setting is for, you can use context-sensitive Help to learn more about it. 
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Note
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab Help Workshop can read (.hpj) files that were created for version 3.x of WinHelp. However, if you use Help Workshop to edit these files, they are saved in a slightly different format, and the MS-DOS\plain\f0\fs16 \'96\f1 based compiler will be unable to process them.
\par
```

## 18. New topic file features

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:0075`
  - Raw:

    BROWSE0027:0075
    ```
- Marker `$`

    New topic file features

    ```
- Marker `A`

  - Raw:

    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `authorable buttons;avi files in Help topics;buttons,;buttons, authorable;creating;creating, authorable buttons;Help Workshop;Help Workshop, new features;multimedia;multimedia, adding to topics;new features;new features, new topic file features;topic files;topic files, new features`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    authorable buttons;avi files in Help topics;buttons,;buttons, authorable;creating;creating, authorable buttons;Help Workshop;Help Workshop, new features;multimedia;multimedia, adding to topics;new features;new features, new topic file features;topic files;topic files, new features
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
New topic file features

Help Workshop provides two new topic statements and allows longer hotspot7BM6.K definitions.

Authorable buttons

You can create an authorable button by using the {-button} command in the topic (.rtf) file. Any WinHelp macro(s) can be assigned to the button. The width of the button is determined by the text defined for it. If no text is specified, WinHelp will create a 12-by-12–pixel button.

Multimedia support

You can include multimedia files (.avi) in Help topics by using the {-mci} command in the topic file.

Increased hotspot capacity

The size limitation for a hotspot7BM6.K has been raised to 4095 characters.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b New topic file features
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Help Workshop provides two new topic statements and allows longer \ul hotspot\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7BM6.K} definitions.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Authorable buttons
\par \plain\f1\fs16 You can create an authorable button by using the \{\-button\'7d command in the topic (.rtf) file. Any WinHelp macro(s) can be assigned to the button. The width of the button is determined by the text defined for it. If no text is specified, WinHelp will create a 12-by-12\plain\f0\fs16 \'96\f1 pixel button.
\par \b Multimedia support
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 You can include multimedia files (.avi) in Help topics by using the \{\-mci\'7d command in the topic file.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Increased hotspot capacity
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The size limitation for a \ul hotspot\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7BM6.K} has been raised to 4095 characters.
\par
```

    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
New window-type footnote

Topics are usually displayed in the main or current window type unless another window type is specified in jump instructions for a hotspot7BM6.K or for an item on the Contents tab. However, you can override the window type when the topic is accessed from the Index or Find tabs, or by an ALink7V8V.U or KLink1R9H33X macro.

You can use the greater-than sign (>) footnote in your topic (.rtf) files to specify a window type for any given topic. The window type you specify must be defined in the project (.hpj) file.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 
\b New window-type footnote
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Topics are usually displayed in the main or current window type unless another window type is specified in jump instructions for a \ul hotspot\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7BM6.K} or for an item on the Contents tab. However, you can override the window type when the topic is accessed from the Index or Find tabs, or by an \ul ALink\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7V8V.U} or \ul KLink\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 1R9H33X} macro.
\par You can use the greater-than sign (>) footnote in your topic (.rtf) files to specify a window type for any given topic. The window type you specify must be defined in the project (.hpj) file.
\par
```

## 20. Secondary-window features

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Secondary-window features`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Secondary-window features
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `1BQUZJT`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    1BQUZJT
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `absolute position;Back button;button bar;button bar, in secondary windows;new features;new features, secondary-window features;secondary windows;secondary windows, new features;window types;windows,;windows, secondary;windows, specifying type;WinHelp,;WinHelp, new features`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    absolute position;Back button;button bar;button bar, in secondary windows;new features;new features, secondary-window features;secondary windows;secondary windows, new features;window types;windows,;windows, secondary;windows, specifying type;WinHelp,;WinHelp, new features
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Secondary-window features

Secondary windows2ZK4PT can now contain a button bar, which is completely configurable in the project (.hpj) file. You can use standard buttons and add your own.

You can now specify as many as 255 secondary-window types in your project file. Up to nine of these secondary windows can be displayed at the same time. You can also specify an absolute position9UIBLJ for a Help window.

You can add a Back button to a secondary window. Each window type has its own history list and will display only the history of topics that were displayed in that window type. This functionality is designed for Help files that use a secondary window exclusively. You can use either the Back() macro or add the Back button to the secondary window's button bar.

You can set secondary windows to automatically adjust to the length of a topic. In addition, users can more easily specify that secondary windows should stay on top of other windows.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 
\b Secondary-window features
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\ul\fs16 Secondary windows\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 2ZK4PT} can now contain a button bar, which is completely configurable in the project (.hpj) file. You can use standard buttons and add your own.
\par You can now specify as many as 255 secondary-window types in your project file. Up to nine of these secondary windows can be displayed at the same time. You can also specify an \ul absolute position\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 9UIBLJ} for a Help window.
\par You can add a Back button to a secondary window. Each window type has its own history list and will display only the history of topics that were displayed in that window type. This functionality is designed for Help files that use a secondary window exclusively. You can use either the Back() macro or add the Back button to the secondary window's button bar.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 You can set secondary windows to automatically adjust to the length of a topic. In addition, users can more easily specify that secondary windows should stay on top of other windows.
\par
```

## 21. New macros and enhanced macro processing

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `New macros and enhanced macro processing`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    New macros and enhanced macro processing
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `New_macros`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    New_macros
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `Help Workshop;Help Workshop, new features;long names for macros;macros,;macros, long names for;macros, new features;new features;new features, enhanced macro processing;new features, new macros;quotation marks;quotation marks, unnecessary in macros;WinHelp,;WinHelp, new features`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Help Workshop;Help Workshop, new features;long names for macros;macros,;macros, long names for;macros, new features;new features;new features, enhanced macro processing;new features, new macros;quotation marks;quotation marks, unnecessary in macros;WinHelp,;WinHelp, new features
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
New macros and enhanced macro processing

More than 20 new macros are available for WinHelp version 4.0 Help files, and Help Workshop has fewer limitations on macro processing. In addition, there is no practical limit to the number of macros that can be nested.

Help Workshop now automatically changes macro names to their short form. (Using long names for macros in .rtf files greatly enhances the readability of those files.) For macros requiring numeric arguments, you can use more descriptive string values instead. In most cases, quotation marks are no longer necessary. If the final parameter for a macro is 0 or " ", that parameter can be omitted.

Hotspot macros can now use as many as 4096 characters.

For a list of macros, see the Macro Quick Reference.MACRO_REF_OVR
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 
\b New macros and enhanced macro processing
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 More than 20 new macros are available for WinHelp version 4.0 Help files, and Help Workshop has fewer limitations on macro processing. In addition, there is no practical limit to the number of macros that can be nested.
\par Help Workshop now automatically changes macro names to their short form. (Using long names for macros in .rtf files greatly enhances the readability of those files.) For macros requiring numeric arguments, you can use more descriptive string values instead. In most cases, quotation marks are no longer necessary. If the final parameter for a macro is 0 or " ", that parameter can be omitted.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 Hotspot macros can now use as many as 4096 characters.
\par For a list of macros, see the \uldb Macro Quick Reference.\plain\f1\fs16 {\v MACRO_REF_OVR}
\par
```

Bitmaps supplied by Help Workshop

Help Workshop comes with new bitmaps that you can use. You can specify the filename for any of these bitmaps in your topic (.rtf) files, even though the bitmap is not in your bitmaps folder. If your bitmaps folder contains a bitmap that has the same filename as one specified below, that bitmap is used instead of the one supplied by Help Workshop.

Bitmap	Filename

{bmc bm1.bmp}	bullet.bmp

{bmc bm2.bmp}	emdash.bmp

{bmct bm3.bmp}	shortcut.bmp

{bmc bm4.bmp}	onestep.bmp

{bmc bm5.bmp}	open.bmp

{bmc bm0.bmp}	cshelp.bmp
```

## 23. Greater color support

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Greater color support`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Greater color support
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `HH396.`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    HH396.
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `bitmaps,;bitmaps, color depth;color depth;colors;colors, bitmaps;Help Workshop;Help Workshop, new features;new features;new features, greater color support;WinHelp,;WinHelp, new features`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    bitmaps,;bitmaps, color depth;color depth;colors;colors, bitmaps;Help Workshop;Help Workshop, new features;new features;new features, greater color support;WinHelp,;WinHelp, new features
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Greater color support

WinHelp version 4.0 supports any color depth that the operating system supports (256 colors, 24-bit color, and so on). If you use 16-color bitmaps, you are no longer limited to the standard 16 colors.

Help Workshop version enables you to specify more than one color-depth version of a graphic in a single {-bmx} bitmap statement in your topic (.rtf) file. WinHelp determines the color-depth capability of a user's monitor, and then uses the most appropriate bitmap file.

Note

·	You can now make the white background color in bitmaps transparent, so the colored background of a Help topic shows through.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 
\b Greater color support
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 WinHelp version 4.0 supports any color depth that the operating system supports (256 colors, 24-bit color, and so on). If you use 16-color bitmaps, you are no longer limited to the standard 16 colors. 
\par Help Workshop version enables you to specify more than one color-depth version of a graphic in a single \{\-bmx\'7d bitmap statement in your topic (.rtf) file. WinHelp determines the color-depth capability of a user's monitor, and then uses the most appropriate bitmap file.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Note
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab You can now make the white background color in bitmaps transparent, so the colored background of a Help topic shows through.
\par
```

## 25. Duplicate accelerators

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Duplicate accelerators`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Duplicate accelerators
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `E6G33D`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    E6G33D
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `accelerator keys;accelerator keys, duplicate;duplicate accelerators;new features;new features, duplicate accelerators;WinHelp,;WinHelp, new features`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    accelerator keys;accelerator keys, duplicate;duplicate accelerators;new features;new features, duplicate accelerators;WinHelp,;WinHelp, new features
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Duplicate accelerators

If two or more buttons use the same accelerator, WinHelp now attempts to move the accelerator of the second button so that the letter chosen does not conflict with the accelerator of any other button. If WinHelp cannot create a unique accelerator and the user presses that accelerator key, WinHelp displays a dialog box containing the text of every button that contains the duplicated accelerator and asks the user to choose.

If no accelerator is specified for the text of a button, WinHelp automatically chooses the first letter that does not conflict with any other button. If WinHelp cannot find a unique accelerator, WinHelp uses the first letter of the text string. WinHelp ignores conflicts with menu accelerators.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 
\b Duplicate accelerators
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 If two or more buttons use the same accelerator, WinHelp now attempts to move the accelerator of the second button so that the letter chosen does not conflict with the accelerator of any other button. If WinHelp cannot create a unique accelerator and the user presses that accelerator key, WinHelp displays a dialog box containing the text of every button that contains the duplicated accelerator and asks the user to choose.
\par If no accelerator is specified for the text of a button, WinHelp automatically chooses the first letter that does not conflict with any other button. If WinHelp cannot find a unique accelerator, WinHelp uses the first letter of the text string. WinHelp ignores conflicts with menu accelerators.
\par
```

## 26. New topic-linking features

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `New topic-linking features`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    New topic-linking features
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `35SG_9Q`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    35SG_9Q
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `ALinks;ALinks, new topic-linking features;interfile jumps;jumps;jumps, new topic-linking features;jumps, to another Help file;KLinks;KLinks, new topic-linking features;linking multiple Help files;linking topics;new features;new features, new topic-linking features;WinHelp,;WinHelp, new features`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    ALinks;ALinks, new topic-linking features;interfile jumps;jumps;jumps, new topic-linking features;jumps, to another Help file;KLinks;KLinks, new topic-linking features;linking multiple Help files;linking topics;new features;new features, new topic-linking features;WinHelp,;WinHelp, new features
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
New topic-linking features

WinHelp version 4.0 provides two new linking macros, KLink1R9H33X and ALink,7V8V.U that enable you to provide jumps to multiple topics based on keywords9ML77Q rather than specific context strings.

These jumps are resolved when the user clicks them instead of when the file is compiled. This enables you to create interfile jumps for Help files that might change after the original jump was created or for Help files that might or might not have been installed at the time the program was released.

KLink macros use K-keywords — the same keywords that you use to create the index. If a user clicks a hotspot7BM6.K that runs the KLink macro, WinHelp displays the Topics Found dialog box, which contains a list of all the topics that have the associated keywords throughout the family of Help files. The macro can be configured so that if only one topic is found, the jump occurs immediately without displaying the Topics Found dialog box. KLink (and ALink) macros can access multiple Help files if they are properly specified in the contents file.

ALink macros function similarly to KLink macros except A-keywords never appear in the index. Jumps using ALink macros are unaffected by any changes to the index.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 
\b New topic-linking features
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 WinHelp version 4.0 provides two new linking macros, \ul KLink\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 1R9H33X} and \ul ALink,\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7V8V.U} that enable you to provide jumps to multiple topics based on \ul keywords\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 9ML77Q} rather than specific context strings.
\par These jumps are resolved when the user clicks them instead of when the file is compiled. This enables you to create interfile jumps for Help files that might change after the original jump was created or for Help files that might or might not have been installed at the time the program was released.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 KLink macros use K-keywords \plain\f0\fs16 \'97\f1  the same keywords that you use to create the index. If a user clicks a \ul hotspot\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7BM6.K} that runs the KLink macro, WinHelp displays the Topics Found dialog box, which contains a list of all the topics that have the associated keywords throughout the family of Help files. The macro can be configured so that if only one topic is found, the jump occurs immediately without displaying the Topics Found dialog box. KLink (and ALink) macros can access multiple Help files if they are properly specified in the contents file.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 ALink macros function similarly to KLink macros except A-keywords never appear in the index. Jumps using ALink macros are unaffected by any changes to the index.
\par
```

## 27. New WinHelp API commands

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `New WinHelp API commands`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    New WinHelp API commands
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `New_API`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    New_API
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `tables`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    tables
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `new features;new features, new WinHelp API commands;WinHelp API commands, new;WinHelp function;WinHelp,;WinHelp, new features`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    new features;new features, new WinHelp API commands;WinHelp API commands, new;WinHelp function;WinHelp,;WinHelp, new features
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
New WinHelp API commands

The following new commands are available for the WinHelp function.

Command	Purpose

HELP_CONTEXTMENU	Displays context-sensitive Help when the right mouse button is clicked.

HELP_FINDER	Displays the Help Topics dialog box in its last state.

HELP_SETPOPUP_POS	Sets the position for the next pop-up window. Overrides the actual mouse position.

HELP_TCARD	Indicates that a command is for a training-card instance of the WinHelp program. A program combines the HELP_TCARD flag with other commands by using the bitwise OR operator.

HELP_WM_HELP	Displays context-sensitive Help when the F1 key is pressed.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 
\b New WinHelp API commands
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The following new commands are available for the WinHelp function.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li2155\ri125\fi-2025\brdrb\brdrs\tx2155 \b Command\tab Purpose
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li2155\ri125\fi-2025\tx2155 \plain\f1\fs16 HELP_CONTEXTMENU\tab Displays context-sensitive Help when the right mouse button is clicked.
\par HELP_FINDER\tab Displays the Help Topics dialog box in its last state.
\par HELP_SETPOPUP_POS\tab Sets the position for the next pop-up window. Overrides the actual mouse position.
\par HELP_TCARD\tab Indicates that a command is for a training-card instance of the WinHelp program. A program combines the HELP_TCARD flag with other commands by using the bitwise OR operator.
\par HELP_WM_HELP\tab Displays context-sensitive Help when the F1 key is pressed.
\par
```

## 29. Character set as part of font description

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `$`

    Character set as part of font description
    ```
- Marker `#`

  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    74Z0SU
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `character sets;character sets, including as part of font description;font handling features;Help Workshop;Help Workshop, new features;new features;new features, character set as part of font description;WinHelp,;WinHelp, new features`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    character sets;character sets, including as part of font description;font handling features;Help Workshop;Help Workshop, new features;new features;new features, character set as part of font description;WinHelp,;WinHelp, new features
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Character set as part of font description

WinHelp version 4.0 expects Help files to include the character set as part of the font description. If you are using a word processor that includes this information as part of the font table description (for example, Microsoft Word for Windows version 6.0), Help Workshop includes this information automatically. If the information is not supplied in the topic (.rtf) file, Help Workshop uses default values for the character set.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf

\b Character set as part of font description

If you are using a word processor that includes this information as part of the font table description (for example, Microsoft Word for Windows version 6.0), Help Workshop includes this information automatically. If the information is not supplied in the topic (.rtf) file, Help Workshop uses default values for the character set.
\par
```

## 32. Automatic localization of quotation marks

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Automatic localization of quotation marks`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Automatic localization of quotation marks
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `2APQ_RY`
  - Raw:

    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `characters;characters, automatic localization of quotation marks;font handling features;Help Workshop;Help Workshop, new features;localizing;localizing, quotation marks;new features;new features, automatic localization of quotation marks;quotation marks;quotation marks, automatic localization of`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    characters;characters, automatic localization of quotation marks;font handling features;Help Workshop;Help Workshop, new features;localizing;localizing, quotation marks;new features;new features, automatic localization of quotation marks;quotation marks;quotation marks, automatic localization of
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Automatic localization of quotation marks

The \ldblquoteXC5Y79>tables and \rdblquote12UI60T>tables RTF statements are used for generating quotation marks. The actual characters that are displayed in the Help file are determined by the language specified in the project (.hpj) file.

WinHelp displays French-style quotation marks for the following character sets:

·	Algerian

·	Belgian

·	French Canadian

·	French (Luxembourg)

·	French (Standard)

·	French (Swiss)

·	Moroccan

·	Tunisian

WinHelp displays German-style quotation marks for the following character sets:

·	German (Austrian)

·	German (Liechtenstein)

·	German (Luxembourg)

·	German (Standard)

·	German (Swiss)

All other non – double-byte character sets display the standard curly quotation marks. Curly quotation marks are displayed in Times Roman, using the current font's point size.

Examples

{bmc bm11.bmp}
```

The actual characters that are displayed in the Help file are determined by the language specified in the project (.hpj) file.
\par WinHelp displays French-style quotation marks for the following character sets:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Algerian
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Belgian
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab French Canadian
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab French (Luxembourg)
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab French (Standard)
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab French (Swiss)
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Moroccan
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Tunisian
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 WinHelp displays German-style quotation marks for the following character sets:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab German (Austrian)
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab German (Liechtenstein)
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab German (Luxembourg)
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab German (Standard)
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab German (Swiss)
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 All other non \plain\f0\fs16 \'96\f1  double-byte character sets display the standard curly quotation marks. Curly quotation marks are displayed in Times Roman, using the current font's point size.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Examples
\par \pard\sb115\sa115\li115\ri125\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 \{bmc bm11.bmp\}
\par

### Decoded Body

```text
New context-sensitive help features

In WinHelp 4.0, the procedure for applying context-sensitive pop-up8XSV6X Help topics to interface elements and controls has been greatly simplified. At the same time, it is now possible to apply context-sensitive Help directly to a greater range of individual interface elements. This increase in ease and range encourages Help authors and developers to make fuller use of context-sensitive Help. Where conceptual overviews are needed for dialog boxes, an overview topic can be linked to a Help button in those dialog boxes.

{button ,AL(`cs_help')} Related Topics%!AL(`cs_help',0,`main')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b New context-sensitive help features
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 In WinHelp 4.0, the procedure for applying context-sensitive \ul pop-up\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 8XSV6X} Help topics to interface elements and controls has been greatly simplified. At the same time, it is now possible to apply context-sensitive Help directly to a greater range of individual interface elements. This increase in ease and range encourages Help authors and developers to make fuller use of context-sensitive Help. Where conceptual overviews are needed for dialog boxes, an overview topic can be linked to a Help button in those dialog boxes.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`cs_help')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`cs_help',0,`main')}
\par
```

## 34. Help Author command

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Help Author command`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Help Author command
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `IV.JR3`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    IV.JR3
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `Help Author command;Help Workshop;Help Workshop, new features;new features;new features, Help Author command;numbers;numbers, in title bar;title bar;title bar, numbers in;WinHelp,;WinHelp, new features`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Help Author command;Help Workshop;Help Workshop, new features;new features;new features, Help Author command;numbers;numbers, in title bar;title bar;title bar, numbers in;WinHelp,;WinHelp, new features
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Help Author command

A new Help Author command provides additional authoring information and debugging capabilities. When you check the Help Author command on the File menu in Help Workshop:

·	The text on the title bar of the Help topic is replaced with the topic number. This number is determined by the order in which topics appear in your topic (.rtf) files, as listed in the project (.hpj) file.

·	You can press ctrl+shift+right arrow, and ctrl+shift+left arrow to step through the topics in your Help file and ctrl+shift+home and ctrl+shift+end to move to the beginning or end of your Help file.

·	WinHelp displays specific information about problems in the Help (.hlp) or contents (.cnt) file.

·	Topic Information and Ask On Hotspots commands appear when you click a topic using your right mouse button. The Topic Information command displays information about the current topic, including the text of the title ($) footnote, the macros specified in the entry macro (!) footnote, the name of the current window type, and the Help filename and path. The Ask On Hotspots command changes the effect of clicking a hotspot.7BM6.K If this command is checked, WinHelp displays the topic ID or macro syntax of any hotspot you click and prompts you to specify whether the macro or hotspot jump should be carried out.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 
\b Help Author command
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 A new Help Author command provides additional authoring information and debugging capabilities. When you check the Help Author command on the File menu in Help Workshop:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab The text on the title bar of the Help topic is replaced with the topic number. This number is determined by the order in which topics appear in your topic (.rtf) files, as listed in the project (.hpj) file.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab You can press \scaps ctrl+shift+right\'a0arrow\plain\f1\fs16 , and \scaps ctrl+shift+left\'a0arrow \plain\f1\fs16 to step through the topics in your Help file and \scaps ctrl+shift+home \plain\f1\fs16 and\scaps  ctrl+shift+end \plain\f1\fs16 to move to the beginning or end of your Help file.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab WinHelp displays specific information about problems in the Help (.hlp) or contents (.cnt) file.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Topic Information and Ask On Hotspots commands appear when you click a topic using your right mouse button. The Topic Information command displays information about the current topic, including the text of the title ($) footnote, the macros specified in the entry macro (!) footnote, the name of the current window type, and the Help filename and path. The Ask On Hotspots command changes the effect of clicking a \ul hotspot.\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7BM6.K} If this command is checked, WinHelp displays the topic ID or macro syntax of any hotspot you click and prompts you to specify whether the macro or hotspot jump should be carried out.
\par
```

Report command

You can generate a report that contains information about a Help file by clicking the File menu in Help Workshop, and then clicking Report. ```

Reports can contain information about a Help file's topic titles, hash numbers, and \ul keywords.\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 9ML77Q} You can also request a report containing all the ASCII text in a Help file.
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
New test commands

The following commands are available on the Help Workshop Test menu:

·	Contents File.  Checks the contents file for parameter limits, level coherence, nesting depth, duplicate elements, missing parameters, file availability, and other components.

·	Send A Macro.  Enables you to see how a specific macro will perform in a Help file.

·	WinHelp API.  Enables you to call a WinHelp API as if it were invoked from another program.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf

\b New test commands
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The following commands are available on the Help Workshop Test menu:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Contents File.  Checks the contents file for parameter limits, level coherence, nesting depth, duplicate elements, missing parameters, file availability, and other components. 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Send A Macro.  Enables you to see how a specific macro will perform in a Help file.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab WinHelp API.  Enables you to call a WinHelp API as if it were invoked from another program.
\par
```

    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Easy jumping to topics

A new Jump dialog box enables you to jump directly to a topic by specifying its topic ID.7.61TY You can open the Jump dialog box by pressing ctrl+shift+j in any topic window except pop-up windows.8XSV6X

You can run any macro from the Jump dialog box by specifying a macro string, preceded by an exclamation point (!), instead of a topic ID. You can specify several macros by separating them with colons (:) or semicolons (;).
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf

\b Easy jumping to topics
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 A new Jump dialog box enables you to jump directly to a topic by specifying its \ul topic ID.\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7.61TY} You can open the Jump dialog box by pressing \scaps ctrl+shift+j\plain\f1\fs16  in any topic window except \ul pop-up windows.\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 8XSV6X}
\par You can run any macro from the Jump dialog box by specifying a macro string, preceded by an exclamation point (!), instead of a topic ID. You can specify several macros by separating them with colons (:) or semicolons (;).
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Missing-bitmaps error

When Help Workshop cannot find a bitmap, it inserts its own bitmap in its place and includes the name of the missing bitmap. In this way, you can readily tell the difference between a missing bitmap and a bitmap that WinHelp cannot display.
```

## 39. Improved error handling

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Improved error handling`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Improved error handling
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `A4US7A`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    A4US7A
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `error handling;Help Workshop;Help Workshop, new features;messages;messages, improved error handling;new features;new features, improved error handling`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    error handling;Help Workshop;Help Workshop, new features;messages;messages, improved error handling;new features;new features, improved error handling
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Improved error handling

Errors are no longer reported by levels. Accordingly, Help Workshop ignores the WARNINGS= option in the project (.hpj) file. Problems are now divided into Notes, Warnings, and Errors. All message identifiers begin with the letters "HC" and end with a 4-digit number, for example, "HC1000." You can look up a message identifier in this Help file for more information. Help Workshop displays:

·	Notes for problems that probably do not affect the Help file adversely.

·	Warnings if a Help file contains problems but can still be compiled.

·	Errors if the Help file cannot be compiled.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 
\b Improved error handling
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Errors are no longer reported by levels. Accordingly, Help Workshop ignores the WARNINGS= option in the project (.hpj) file. Problems are now divided into Notes, Warnings, and Errors. All message identifiers begin with the letters "HC" and end with a 4-digit number, for example, "HC1000." You can look up a message identifier in this Help file for more information. Help Workshop displays:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Notes for problems that probably do not affect the Help file adversely.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Warnings if a Help file contains problems but can still be compiled.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Errors if the Help file cannot be compiled.
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
New monitoring of WinHelp messages

You can now monitor the WinHelp messages that are processed while a Help file is running. Help Workshop displays information about WinHelp API calls, context-sensitive calls, hotspot7BM6.K jumps, embedded window commands, which macros are being executed, which topics and pop-up windows8XSV6X are current, and which configuration (.gid) file is being read. To access this feature, click the View menu in Help Workshop, and then click WinHelp Messages.
```

To access this feature, click the View menu in Help Workshop, and then click WinHelp Messages.
\par
```

## 41. Help file search path

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Help file search path`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Help file search path
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `12CTJPQ`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    12CTJPQ
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `new features;new features, Help file search path;WinHelp,;WinHelp, new features`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    new features;new features, Help file search path;WinHelp,;WinHelp, new features
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Help file search path

WinHelp now searches for Help files in the following locations, in the order listed.

·	If a path is specified, WinHelp first searches for the file in the specified path. If the file is not found, the path is removed and the following locations are searched.

·	The folder of the current Help file.

·	The current folder.

·	The System subfolder of the Windows folder.

·	The Windows folder.

·	The folders listed in the PATH environment.

·	The location specified in the Winhelp.ini file.

·	The registry.

If WinHelp cannot find a Help file, it displays a dialog box that enables the user to specify the location of the Help file. If the users finds the file, WinHelp will add the location information in the registry.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 
\b Help file search path
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 WinHelp now searches for Help files in the following locations, in the order listed.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab If a path is specified, WinHelp first searches for the file in the specified path. If the file is not found, the path is removed and the following locations are searched.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab The folder of the current Help file.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab The current folder.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab The System subfolder of the Windows folder.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab The Windows folder.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab The folders listed in the PATH environment.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab The location specified in the Winhelp.ini file.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab The registry.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 If WinHelp cannot find a Help file, it displays a dialog box that enables the user to specify the location of the Help file. If the users finds the file, WinHelp will add the location information in the registry.
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Bitmap search path

Help Workshop now searches in three locations for a bitmap. If a path for a bitmap is specified, it first searches in that location. If it cannot find the bitmap, the path is removed and the following locations are searched:

·	The folder(s) specified in the project (.hpj) file for bitmaps

·	The folder(s) specified in the project file for topic (.rtf) files

·	The folder that contains the topic file in which the bitmap is specified

·	The folder that contains the project file
```

\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab The folder(s) specified in the project (.hpj) file for bitmaps
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab The folder(s) specified in the project file for topic (.rtf) files
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab The folder that contains the topic file in which the bitmap is specified

## 43. Training cards

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:0077`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:0077
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Training cards`
  - Raw:

    Training cards
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `NEW_WINHELP_TCARDS`
  - Raw:

    NEW_WINHELP_TCARDS
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `NEW_USER;NEW_WINHELP_TCARDS;whats_new`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    NEW_USER;NEW_WINHELP_TCARDS;whats_new
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `new features;new features, training cards;WinHelp,;WinHelp, new features`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    new features;new features, training cards;WinHelp,;WinHelp, new features
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Training cards

Training cards are a new feature that enables WinHelp to determine which Help topic to display by communicating with another program. You can use this feature when the topic that should be displayed next is dependent on the actions a user takes in a program. For example, you could create a topic that contains only the first step of a procedure. When the user carries out that step, the program tells WinHelp to display the topic that contains the next step.

When you use the training cards feature, you must work with a program developer to set it up in the program's code.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 


\b Training cards
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Training cards are a new feature that enables WinHelp to determine which Help topic to display by communicating with another program. You can use this feature when the topic that should be displayed next is dependent on the actions a user takes in a program. For example, you could create a topic that contains only the first step of a procedure. When the user carries out that step, the program tells WinHelp to display the topic that contains the next step. 
\par When you use the training cards feature, you must work with a program developer to set it up in the program's code.
\par
```

## 44. Help macros

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:0065`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:0065
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Help macros`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Help macros
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `MACRO_OVERVIEW`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    MACRO_OVERVIEW
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `MACRO_OVERVIEW;new_macro`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    MACRO_OVERVIEW;new_macro
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `macros,;macros, about Help macros;running macros`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    macros,;macros, about Help macros;running macros
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Help macros

WinHelp provides a set of macros that enable you to control and customize Help functionality. Macro reference topics describe the macros included with WinHelp and explain how to use them in your Help file.

When creating your Help files, you can make your Help macros run by:

·	Placing macros in the project (.hpj) file, so WinHelp runs the macros whenever a user opens the Help file or a particular window type.

·	Placing macros in the project file, so WinHelp runs the macros whenever a user chooses a keyword from the Index tab in the Help Topics dialog box.

·	Placing macros in a topic footnote, so the macros run when a user displays that topic.

·	Placing macros in the contents (.cnt) file, so WinHelp runs the macros whenever a user double-clicks the associated page icon.

·	Configuring the menu bar and button bar, so WinHelp runs a macro when a user clicks the menu item or button.

·	Adding hotspots to your topic, so the macro runs when a user clicks the hotspot.

·	Using an external program to send a function call that prompts WinHelp to run a Help macro.

Help macros are designed to imitate standard C-language format; however, standard Help macros do not support variables or expression evaluation.

Note

·	If you find that the standard Help macro set falls short of your ultimate goal, you can also create your own Help macros by using DLL functions.

{button ,AL("macro_syntax;macro_dlls;macro_error_chk;macro_return_vals;macro_from_app")} Related Topics%!AL("macro_syntax;macro_dlls;macro_error_chk;macro_return_vals;macro_from_app")
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Help macros
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 WinHelp provides a set of macros that enable you to control and customize Help functionality. Macro reference topics describe the macros included with WinHelp and explain how to use them in your Help file.
\par When creating your Help files, you can make your Help macros run by:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 Placing macros in the project (.hpj) file, so WinHelp runs the macros whenever a user opens the Help file or a particular window type.
\par \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 Placing macros in the project file, so WinHelp runs the macros whenever a user chooses a keyword from the Index tab in the Help Topics dialog box.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 Placing macros in a topic footnote, so the macros run when a user displays that topic.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Placing macros in the contents (.cnt) file, so WinHelp runs the macros whenever a user double-clicks the associated page icon.
\par \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 Configuring the menu bar and button bar, so WinHelp runs a macro when a user clicks the menu item or button.
\par \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 Adding hotspots to your topic, so the macro runs when a user clicks the hotspot.
\par \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 Using an external program to send a function call that prompts WinHelp to run a Help macro.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 Help macros are designed to imitate standard C-language format; however, standard Help macros do not support variables or expression evaluation.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Note
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab If you find that the standard Help macro set falls short of your ultimate goal, you can also create your own Help macros by using DLL functions.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL("macro_syntax;macro_dlls;macro_error_chk;macro_return_vals;macro_from_app")\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL("macro_syntax;macro_dlls;macro_error_chk;macro_return_vals;macro_from_app")}
\par
```

## 45. Macro syntax

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:0110`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:0110
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Macro syntax`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Macro syntax
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `JLUVN5`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    JLUVN5
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `macro_all;macro_syntax;new_macro`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    macro_all;macro_syntax;new_macro
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `characters;characters, in macro statements;macros,;macros, syntax;syntax of macro statements`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    characters;characters, in macro statements;macros,;macros, syntax;syntax of macro statements
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Macro syntax

Help macro statements have two main components: the macro name and the macro parameters enclosed in parentheses. The macro name must be spelled exactly as it is shown in the syntax and the parameters must be used in the order they are given in the syntax. Parameters provide information for the macro; for example, the JumpId macro, which jumps to a topic with a specific topic ID, has parameters for the name of the Help file and the topic's ID.

All Help macros use the following syntax:

	MacroName(parameter1, parameter2, ...)

The opening parenthesis, closing parenthesis, and commas are required characters when included in the syntax statement for a macro. Macro names are not case sensitive. Help Workshop automatically changes macro names to their short form and removes all unnecessary white space. This enables you to use long names for macros in your topic (.rtf) file to enhance readability.

Parameters can be text strings or numbers, and are separated by commas. If the final parameter for a macro is 0 or "", that parameter can be omitted.

Some macros have no parameters, but the parentheses are still required.

If you create custom macros, the macro name should begin with an alphabetic character, followed by any combination of alphabetic characters, numbers, or the underscore character, as in this example:

	PlayAudio()

You can include more than one macro in a macro string by placing a colon or semicolon between each macro. WinHelp runs the macros sequentially.

Backslashes are used as an escape character, allowing you to include quotes as part of a macro parameter. If you need a literal backslash (for example, in a path specification) you must double the backslash for every level of macro nesting. For path specifications, use a forward slash instead of backslashes.

{button ,AL("syntax_more;conventions")} Related Topics%!AL("syntax_more;conventions")
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Macro syntax
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Help macro statements have two main components: the macro name and the macro parameters enclosed in parentheses. The macro name must be spelled exactly as it is shown in the syntax and the parameters must be used in the order they are given in the syntax. Parameters provide information for the macro; for example, the JumpId macro, which jumps to a topic with a specific topic ID, has parameters for the name of the Help file and the topic's ID.
\par All Help macros use the following syntax:
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \tab MacroName(parameter1, parameter2, ...)
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 The opening parenthesis, closing parenthesis, and commas are required characters when included in the syntax statement for a macro. Macro names are not case sensitive. Help Workshop automatically changes macro names to their short form and removes all unnecessary white space. This enables you to use long names for macros in your topic (.rtf) file to enhance readability.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 Parameters can be text strings or numbers, and are separated by commas. If the final parameter for a macro is 0 or "", that parameter can be omitted.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 Some macros have no parameters, but the parentheses are still required.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 If you create custom macros, the macro name should begin with an alphabetic character, followed by any combination of alphabetic characters, numbers, or the underscore character, as in this example:
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \tab PlayAudio()
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 You can include more than one macro in a macro string by placing a colon or semicolon between each macro. WinHelp runs the macros sequentially.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 Backslashes are used as an escape character, allowing you to include quotes as part of a macro parameter. If you need a literal backslash (for example, in a path specification) you must double the backslash for every level of macro nesting. For path specifications, use a forward slash instead of backslashes.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL("syntax_more;conventions")\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL("syntax_more;conventions")}
\par
```

    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Macro error checking

Help Workshop checks the validity of each macro included in a Help file as it compiles. If it finds an error, it displays an error message and stops processing that macro. Help Workshop checks whether you:

·	Spelled the macro's name correctly

·	Specified the correct macro syntax (for example, the number of opening and closing parentheses and quotation marks should match)

·	Specified the required number of macro parameters

·	Specified types that match the parameter types (for example, you specified numeric parameters with numerals)
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Macro error checking
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Help Workshop checks the validity of each macro included in a Help file as it compiles. If it finds an error, it displays an error message and stops processing that macro. Help Workshop checks whether you:\fs24 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2\fs18 \'b7\tab \f1\fs16 Spelled the macro's name correctly
\par \f2\fs18 \'b7\tab \f1\fs16 Specified the correct macro syntax (for example, the number of opening and closing parentheses and quotation marks should match)
\par \f2\fs18 \'b7\tab \f1\fs16 Specified the required number of macro parameters
\par \f2\fs18 \'b7\tab \f1\fs16 Specified types that match the parameter types (for example, you specified numeric parameters with numerals)
\par
```

  - Decoded: `macros,;macros, using string parameters;quotation marks;quotation marks, using in string parameters;string parameters;string parameters, using string parameters`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    macros,;macros, using string parameters;quotation marks;quotation marks, using in string parameters;string parameters;string parameters, using string parameters
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Using string parameters

Some macros require that string parameters be enclosed within quotation marks. In most cases, quotation marks are not necessary if the macro is in a topic (.rtf) or project (.hpj) file. Quotation marks can be either double quotation marks (") or matching single quotation marks(`').

Using single quotation marks eliminates ambiguities in situations where strings are nested within other strings.

Note

·	When calling a macro from an external program or from a contents (.cnt) file, string parameters must be enclosed within quotation marks, and numeric parameters must use a number.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Using string parameters
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Some macros require that string parameters be enclosed within quotation marks. In most cases, quotation marks are not necessary if the macro is in a topic (.rtf) or project (.hpj) file. Quotation marks can be either double quotation marks (") or matching single quotation marks(`').
\par Using single quotation marks eliminates ambiguities in situations where strings are nested within other strings.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Note
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab When calling a macro from an external program or from a contents (.cnt) file, string parameters must be enclosed within quotation marks, and numeric parameters must use a number.
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Using numeric parameters

WinHelp and Help Workshop recognize decimal and hexadecimal numbers for numeric parameters. Use a prefix of 0x to indicate a hexadecimal number. For example, the following numbers both represent decimal 64:

	64

0x40

To specify a negative number, add a minus sign before the number.
```

0x40
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 To specify a negative number, add a minus sign before the number.
\par
```

  - Raw:

    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Using special characters in macro strings

To use the following characters within a macro as part of a parameter value, you must preface the character with a backslash.

·	Double quotation mark (")

·	Single opening quotation mark (`)

·	Single closing quotation mark (')

·	Backslash (\)

Commas or parentheses do not need to be preceded by a backslash within a macro string.

Note

·	For every level of nested macros that you use, you must double the backslashes. To specify a filename path, use forward slashes rather then backslashes, as in c:/myapp/myapp.exe.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Using special characters in macro strings
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 To use the following characters within a macro as part of a parameter value, you must preface the character with a backslash. 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f3\fs10 \tab \f1\fs16 Double quotation mark (")
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Single opening quotation mark (`)
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Single closing quotation mark (')
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Backslash (\'5c)
\par Commas or parentheses do not need to be preceded by a backslash within a macro string.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Note
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab For every level of nested macros that you use, you must double the backslashes. To specify a filename path, use forward slashes rather then backslashes, as in c:/myapp/myapp.exe.
\par
```

- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `DLLs;DLLs, calls in macros;macros,;macros, making DLL calls in`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DLLs;DLLs, calls in macros;macros,;macros, making DLL calls in
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Making DLL calls in Help macros

You can call functions in a DLL if you inform WinHelp about the call by using the RegisterRoutine macro. For example, you could register the MessageBox function that is part of Windows with the following:

	[CONFIG]

RegisterRoutine("user", "MessageBox", "USSU")

Having registered this function, you could display a message box with a call similar to the following:

	MessageBox(hwndContext, "This is my message", "Help Message", 0)

{button ,AL("DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW;RegisterRoutine")} Related Topics%!AL("DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW;RegisterRoutine")
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Making DLL calls in Help macros
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 You can call functions in a DLL if you inform WinHelp about the call by using the RegisterRoutine macro. For example, you could register the MessageBox function that is part of Windows with the following:
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \tab [CONFIG]\line
RegisterRoutine("user", "MessageBox", "USSU")
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 Having registered this function, you could display a message box with a call similar to the following:
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \tab MessageBox(hwndContext, "This is my message", "Help Message", 0)
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295

## 51. Return values in Help macros

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:0116`

    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Return values in Help macros`

    Return values in Help macros
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `2LS_6W1`
  - Raw:

    2LS_6W1
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:

    main
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `macro_return_vals`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    macro_return_vals
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `macros,;macros, return values in;return values in macros`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    macros,;macros, return values in;return values in macros
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Return values in Help macros

Generally, Help Workshop ignores return values in macros. However, the IsMark and IfThenElse macros can be used together to test a condition and run a macro if the condition evaluates to a non-zero, or true, value. The following example shows a typical use of IsMark and IfThenElse:

	IfThenElse(FileExist(myapp.exe), ExecFile(myapp.exe),  JumpId(how to install myapp))

The first parameter of the IfThenElse macro is a boolean value (zero or non-zero number). Since FileExist returns a boolean value, it can be used as the first parameter. Help runs this macro as follows:

1	WinHelp runs the FileExist macro and obtains a boolean result from it (non-zero if the file exists, zero if it doesn’t).

2	The IfThenElse macro runs. WinHelp passes the value returned from FileExist to the first parameter and passes the ExecFile and JumpId macro strings to the second and third parameters.

3	If the number passed to the first parameter is not zero, the IfThenElse macro runs the ExecFile macro; otherwise, it runs the JumpId macro.

{button ,AL("FileExist;ExecFile;IfThenElse;JumpId")} Related Topics%!AL("FileExist;ExecFile;IfThenElse;JumpId")
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Return values in Help macros
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Generally, Help Workshop ignores return values in macros. However, the IsMark and IfThenElse macros can be used together to test a condition and run a macro if the condition evaluates to a non-zero, or true, value. The following example shows a typical use of IsMark and IfThenElse:
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \tab IfThenElse(FileExist(myapp.exe), ExecFile(myapp.exe),  JumpId(how to install myapp))
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 The first parameter of the IfThenElse macro is a boolean value (zero or non-zero number). Since FileExist returns a boolean value, it can be used as the first parameter. Help runs this macro as follows:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 1\tab WinHelp runs the FileExist macro and obtains a boolean result from it (non-zero if the file exists, zero if it doesn\'92t).
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 2\tab The IfThenElse macro runs. WinHelp passes the value returned from FileExist to the first parameter and passes the ExecFile and JumpId macro strings to the second and third parameters.
\par 3\tab If the number passed to the first parameter is not zero, the IfThenElse macro runs the ExecFile macro; otherwise, it runs the JumpId macro.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL("FileExist;ExecFile;IfThenElse;JumpId")\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL("FileExist;ExecFile;IfThenElse;JumpId")}
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Using DLL functions as Help macros

If you want to add a feature to your Help file that WinHelp does not support, and you have programming experience for Windows, you can develop custom extensions to support the functions you need. You can use calls to functions in the DLLs as Help macros by registering the functions in the project (.hpj) file using the RegisterRoutine macro.

{button ,AL("DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW;registerroutine")} Related Topics%!AL("DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW;registerroutine")
```

  - Raw:

    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Calling macros from a contents file

When you call a macro from a contents (.cnt) file, you cannot use Help Workshop to turn macros into a format that WinHelp can understand. This means you must use the short name for the macro, use quotation marks around all string parameters, use numeric values for numeric parameters, and supply all the parameters for a macro . You cannot omit most parameters as you can in your topic (.rtf) file. The only parameters you can omit are trailing arguments that would have a value of 0 or "".

{button ,AL("macro_from_app ")} Related Topics%!AL("macro_from_app ")
```

### Raw RTF Body

\b\fs24 Calling macros from a contents file
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 When you call a macro from a contents (.cnt) file, you cannot use Help Workshop to turn macros into a format that WinHelp can understand. This means you must use the short name for the macro, use quotation marks around all string parameters, use numeric values for numeric parameters, and supply all the parameters for a macro . You cannot omit most parameters as you can in your topic (.rtf) file. The only parameters you can omit are trailing arguments that would have a value of 0 or "".
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295

## 54. Calling macros from a program

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:0118`
  - Raw:

    BROWSE0027:0118
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Calling macros from a program`
  - Raw:

    Calling macros from a program
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `1_IUL3Y`
  - Raw:

    1_IUL3Y
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
