    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `macro_from_app`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    macro_from_app
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `calling macros;macros,;macros, calling from program;programs, calling macros from`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    calling macros;macros,;macros, calling from program;programs, calling macros from
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Calling macros from a program

A program can send a HELP_COMMAND parameter in the WinHelp API call that specifies a macro to run. This WinHelp call would look like the following:

	WinHelp(hwndProgram, "HelpFile[>WindowName]", HELP_COMMAND, "macro");

The following example uses the ALink macro in the Myhelp.hlp file:

	case IDM_HELP_KEYBOARD:

	WinHelp (hwnd, "myhelp.hlp", HELP_COMMAND, (DWORD)  "AL(`HELP_KEYBOARD\’)");

	return 0;

When you call a macro from a program, you cannot use Help Workshop to turn macros into a format that WinHelp can understand. This means you must use the short name for the macro, use quotation marks around all string parameters, use numeric values for numeric parameters, and supply all the parameters for a macro . You cannot omit most parameters as you can in your topic (.rtf) file. The only parameters you can omit are trailing arguments that would have a value of 0 or "".

{button ,KL("winhelp")} Related Topics%!AL("winhelp")
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Calling macros from a program
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 A program can send a HELP_COMMAND parameter in the WinHelp API call that specifies a macro to run. This WinHelp call would look like the following: 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \b \tab \plain\f1\fs16 WinHelp(hwndProgram, "HelpFile[>WindowName]", HELP_COMMAND, "macro");
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 The following example uses the ALink macro in the Myhelp.hlp file:
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295\tx445 \tab case IDM_HELP_KEYBOARD:\line
\tab WinHelp (hwnd, "myhelp.hlp", HELP_COMMAND, (DWORD)  "AL(`HELP_KEYBOARD\'5c\'92)");\line
\tab return 0;
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295\tx445 When you call a macro from a program, you cannot use Help Workshop to turn macros into a format that WinHelp can understand. This means you must use the short name for the macro, use quotation marks around all string parameters, use numeric values for numeric parameters, and supply all the parameters for a macro . You cannot omit most parameters as you can in your topic (.rtf) file. The only parameters you can omit are trailing arguments that would have a value of 0 or "".
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,KL("winhelp")\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL("winhelp")}\uldb 
\par
```

## 55. Nested macros and nested string parameters

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:0119`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:0119
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Nested macros and nested string parameters`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Nested macros and nested string parameters
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `E8ZVQL`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    E8ZVQL
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `macro_nested`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    macro_nested
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `macros,;macros, using nested macros;macros, using nested string parameters;string parameters,;string parameters, nested`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    macros,;macros, using nested macros;macros, using nested string parameters;string parameters,;string parameters, nested
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Nested Macros and Nested String Parameters

Help Workshop supports nested macros, a macro that is included in another macro as a parameter value. Because nested macros often have their own string parameters, you must frequently specify a string enclosed within another string.

For example, the following macro creates a button called Time that uses the ExecFile macro as a parameter. When the user clicks the button, the Microsoft Windows Clock program starts. Because the ExecFile macro takes a string as its first parameter, the string is enclosed in single quotation marks:

	CreateButton("btn_time", "&Time", "ExecFile(`clock', 0)")

If the nested macro has any string parameters, they must have quotation marks that are different from the enclosing macro quotation marks. In other words, if double quotation marks enclose a macro, you must enclose any nested strings in single quotation marks.

You can also use single quotation marks for the outermost parameters. You can avoid confusion with nested string parameters by using single quotation marks for all string parameters. Just be sure to match the opening and closing quotation marks correctly.

If a nested macro contains a path, be sure to double each backslash for every level that the macro is nested, starting with double the typical number of backslashes in a macro nested one level. Double the backslashes again for a macro nested two levels, and so on. Alternatively, you might be able to use forward slashes in place of backslashes. You do not need to double forward slashes in paths in nested macros. Forward slashes, however, are not compatible with all networks. Make sure forward slashes are compatible with your network before using them.

{button ,AL("ExecFile")} Related Topics%!AL("ExecFile")
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\plain\f1\b\fs24 Nested Macros and Nested String Parameters
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Help Workshop supports nested macros, a macro that is included in another macro as a parameter value. Because nested macros often have their own string parameters, you must frequently specify a string enclosed within another string.
\par For example, the following macro creates a button called Time that uses the ExecFile macro as a parameter. When the user clicks the button, the Microsoft Windows Clock program starts. Because the ExecFile macro takes a string as its first parameter, the string is enclosed in single quotation marks:
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \tab CreateButton("btn_time", "&Time", "ExecFile(`clock', 0)")
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 If the nested macro has any string parameters, they must have quotation marks that are different from the enclosing macro quotation marks. In other words, if double quotation marks enclose a macro, you must enclose any nested strings in single quotation marks.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 You can also use single quotation marks for the outermost parameters. You can avoid confusion with nested string parameters by using single quotation marks for all string parameters. Just be sure to match the opening and closing quotation marks correctly.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 If a nested macro contains a path, be sure to double each backslash for every level that the macro is nested, starting with double the typical number of backslashes in a macro nested one level. Double the backslashes again for a macro nested two levels, and so on. Alternatively, you might be able to use forward slashes in place of backslashes. You do not need to double forward slashes in paths in nested macros. Forward slashes, however, are not compatible with all networks. Make sure forward slashes are compatible with your network before using them.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL("ExecFile")\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL("ExecFile")}\uldb 
\par \plain\f1\fs16 
\par
```

- Marker `K`

  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    creating;creating, topic files;Help topics,;Help topics, creating;topic files;topic files, creating;topics,;topics, creating
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
To create a topic file

1	Open a new file in a word processor or text editor that can handle rich text format.

2	Write your individual topics, separating each topic with a hard page break.

3	Add appropriate footnotes at the beginning of the file.

	See Related Topics below.

4	Save the file in rich text format with the .rtf filename extension.

Tip

·	Before creating a complete topic file, test the compatibility of your word processor or text editor by compiling a short test topic file.

{button ,AL(`POP_OVERVIEW_TOPIC;BAS_TOPIC_FOOTNOTE;PRO_ADD_TOPIC_ID;PRO_ADD_TITLE;PRO_ADD_INDEX_ENTRIES')} Related Topics%!AL(`POP_OVERVIEW_TOPIC;BAS_TOPIC_FOOTNOTE;;PRO_ADD_TOPIC_ID;PRO_ADD_TITLE;PRO_ADD_INDEX_ENTRIES')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b To create a topic file
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 1\tab Open a new file in a word processor or text editor that can handle rich text format.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 2\tab Write your individual topics, separating each topic with a hard page break.
\par 3\tab Add appropriate footnotes at the beginning of the file.
\par \tab See Related Topics below.
\par 4\tab Save the file in rich text format with the .rtf filename extension.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Tip
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab Before creating a complete topic file, test the compatibility of your word processor or text editor by compiling a short test topic file.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL(`POP_OVERVIEW_TOPIC;BAS_TOPIC_FOOTNOTE;PRO_ADD_TOPIC_ID;PRO_ADD_TITLE;PRO_ADD_INDEX_ENTRIES')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`POP_OVERVIEW_TOPIC;BAS_TOPIC_FOOTNOTE;;PRO_ADD_TOPIC_ID;PRO_ADD_TITLE;PRO_ADD_INDEX_ENTRIES')}
\par
```

- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `PRO_ADD_TOPIC_ID;PRO_TOPIC`
  - Raw:

    ```rtf
    PRO_ADD_TOPIC_ID;PRO_TOPIC
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `# (number sign) footnotes;adding;adding, footnotes to topics;footnotes;footnotes, adding to topics;footnotes, number sign (#);footnotes, topic ID footnote;number sign (#) footnote;topic IDs;topic IDs, adding to topics;topics,;topics, adding footnotes to`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    # (number sign) footnotes;adding;adding, footnotes to topics;footnotes;footnotes, adding to topics;footnotes, number sign (#);footnotes, topic ID footnote;number sign (#) footnote;topic IDs;topic IDs, adding to topics;topics,;topics, adding footnotes to
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
To add a topic ID to a topic

1	Place the insertion point at the beginning of the topic you want to add a topic ID7.61TY to.

2	Insert a number sign (#) as a footnote mark.

3	Type the topic ID name as the footnote text, observing these restrictions:

·	Topic IDs can contain spaces, but avoid leading and trailing spaces.

·	Do not use reserved characters (# = + @ * % !).

·	Use no more than 255 characters.

·	Do not begin a topic ID with a number if the ID will also be used in the [MAP] section.

Notes

·	To enable Help Workshop to track errors, start all topics IDs that will be called from a program with IDH_ . Help Workshop will list any undefined topics that begin with IDH_ in the [MAP] section of the project file, and will warn of any topic of any IDH_ topic IDs that do not appear in the [MAP] section.

·	If you want users to jump to a specific location in a topic, you can insert a topic ID at that location.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b To add a topic ID to a topic
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 1\tab Place the insertion point at the beginning of the topic you want to add a \ul topic ID\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7.61TY} to.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 2\tab Insert a number sign (#) as a footnote mark.
\par 3\tab Type the topic ID name as the footnote text, observing these restrictions:
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li455\ri125\fi-155\tx455 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Topic IDs can contain spaces, but avoid leading and trailing spaces.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Do not use reserved characters (# = + @ * % !).
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Use no more than 255 characters.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Do not begin a topic ID with a number if the ID will also be used in the [MAP] section.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx455 \b Notes
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab To enable Help Workshop to track errors, start all topics IDs that will be called from a program with IDH_ . Help Workshop will list any undefined topics that begin with IDH_ in the [MAP] section of the project file, and will warn of any topic of any IDH_ topic IDs that do not appear in the [MAP] section.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab If you want users to jump to a specific location in a topic, you can insert a topic ID at that location. 
\par
```

  - Decoded: `adding;adding, footnotes to topics;A-footnotes;A-keywords;ALinks;ALinks, marking a topic as an ALink target;footnotes;footnotes, adding to topics;footnotes, A-footnote;keywords;keywords, adding A-keywords to topics;topic keywords;topics,;topics, adding footnotes to`
  - Raw:


### Decoded Body

```text
To mark a topic as an ALink target

1	Place the insertion point at the beginning of the topic.

2	Insert an A as a footnote mark.

3	Type one or more keywords as the footnote text, observing the following restrictions:

·	Separate keywords with a semicolon.

·	Spaces before and after keywords are removed.

·	Do not use carriage returns.

·	Use no more than 255 characters per keyword.

Notes

·	The ALink7V8V.U macro searches for topics containing an A-keyword.

·	Keywords are case-sensitive, but Help Workshop assumes that two keywords that differ only in case are meant to be the same keyword. Help Workshop changes one of the keywords to agree with the other and displays a warning message.
```

```rtf
\par 3\tab Type one or more keywords as the footnote text, observing the following restrictions:
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li455\ri125\fi-155\tx455 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Separate keywords with a semicolon.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Spaces before and after keywords are removed.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Do not use carriage returns.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Use no more than 255 characters per keyword.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx455 \b Notes
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab The \ul ALink\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7V8V.U} macro searches for topics containing an A-keyword.\f2 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \'b7\f1 \tab Keywords are case-sensitive, but Help Workshop assumes that two keywords that differ only in case are meant to be the same keyword. Help Workshop changes one of the keywords to agree with the other and displays a warning message.
\par
```

```rtf
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab The \ul KLink\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7V8V.U} macro searches for topics containing a K-keyword.\f2 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \'b7\f1 \tab Keywords are case-sensitive, but Help Workshop assumes that two keywords that differ only in case are meant to be the same keyword. Help Workshop changes one of the keywords to agree with the other and displays a warning message.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL(`BAS_ADDING_KEYWORDS;PRO_ADD_LEVEL2_KEYWORDS;BAS_WRITE_INDEX')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BAS_ADDING_KEYWORDS;PRO_ADD_LEVEL2_KEYWORDS;BAS_WRITE_INDEX')}
\par
```

## 61. To create second-level index entries for a topic

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:0098`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:0098
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `To create second-level index entries for a topic`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    To create second-level index entries for a topic
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `PRO_ADD_LEVEL2_KEYWORDS`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    PRO_ADD_LEVEL2_KEYWORDS
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `PRO_ADD_LEVEL2_KEYWORDS`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    PRO_ADD_LEVEL2_KEYWORDS
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `adding;adding, index entries;creating;creating, index entries;index entries, creating;index,;index, creating entries for;keywords;keywords, adding K-keywords to topics;K-keywords;second-level index entries;topic keywords`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    adding;adding, index entries;creating;creating, index entries;index entries, creating;index,;index, creating entries for;keywords;keywords, adding K-keywords to topics;K-keywords;second-level index entries;topic keywords
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
To create second-level index entries for a topic

1	In the K-footnote of a topic, type the first-level keyword entry followed by a semicolon (;).

2	Immediately after the semicolon, type the first-level entry again, followed by a comma (,) or a colon (:), a space, the second-level entry, and a semicolon.

Example

The following K-footnote text results in the index entries shown below it:

macro; macro, library; macro, automatic; macro, complex; macro, simple

	macro

		automatic

		complex

		library

		simple

Notes

·	Entries appear alphabetically in the index, regardless of their order in footnote text.

·	Spaces before and after keywords are removed.

·	You can change the characters you use to identify second-level index entries. For more information, click Related Topics.

{button ,AL(`PRO_SPECIFY_SEP_CHAR;PRO_ADD_INDEX_ENTRIES;BAS_WRITE_INDEX;BAS_ADDING_KEYWORDS')} Related Topics%!AL(`PRO_SPECIFY_SEP_CHAR;PRO_ADD_INDEX_ENTRIES;BAS_WRITE_INDEX;BAS_ADDING_KEYWORDS')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b To create second-level index entries for a topic
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 1\tab In the K-footnote of a topic, type the first-level keyword entry followed by a semicolon (;).
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 2\tab Immediately after the semicolon, type the first-level entry again, followed by a comma (,) or a colon (:), a space, the second-level entry, and a semicolon.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Example
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 The following K-footnote text results in the index entries shown below it:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 macro; macro, library; macro, automatic; macro, complex; macro, simple
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295\tx445 \tab macro\line
\tab \tab automatic\line
\tab \tab complex\line
\tab \tab library\line
\tab \tab simple
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295\tx445 \b Notes
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab Entries appear alphabetically in the index, regardless of their order in footnote text.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Spaces before and after keywords are removed.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab You can change the characters you use to identify second-level index entries. For more information, click Related Topics.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL(`PRO_SPECIFY_SEP_CHAR;PRO_ADD_INDEX_ENTRIES;BAS_WRITE_INDEX;BAS_ADDING_KEYWORDS')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`PRO_SPECIFY_SEP_CHAR;PRO_ADD_INDEX_ENTRIES;BAS_WRITE_INDEX;BAS_ADDING_KEYWORDS')}
\par
```

### WinHelp Footnotes

  - Raw:

- Marker `$`

- Marker `#`

  - Raw:

- Marker `A`

  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    PRO_ADD_BROWSE_CODE;PRO_TOPIC
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `+ (plus sign) footnotes;adding;adding, browse sequences;adding, footnotes to topics;browse sequences;footnotes;footnotes, adding to topics;footnotes, browse sequence footnote;footnotes, plus sign (+);plus sign ($) footnote;topics,;topics, adding footnotes to;topics, adding to a browse sequence`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    + (plus sign) footnotes;adding;adding, browse sequences;adding, footnotes to topics;browse sequences;footnotes;footnotes, adding to topics;footnotes, browse sequence footnote;footnotes, plus sign (+);plus sign ($) footnote;topics,;topics, adding footnotes to;topics, adding to a browse sequence
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
To add a topic to a browse sequence

1	At the beginning of the topic that you want to include in a browse sequence,1SRKR1R insert a plus sign (+) as a footnote mark.

2	Type a browse code6KLNWH as the footnote text. Observe the following restrictions:

·	Do not use reserved characters (# = + @ * % !) or spaces.

·	Use no more than 50 characters.

Notes

·	To use browse sequences, you must also activate the browse buttons in the project file.

·	WinHelp creates an auto-browse sequence for topics to which you have added a browse footnote (+) but for which you have specified no browse code.

Tips

·	 When using numbers in browse codes, include placeholding zeros (001, 002, 003, and so on).

·	To make it easier to add topics later, use the same name, but no numbers for each browse footnote in a sequence. Help Workshop will arrange your browse sequence in which the order that the topics appear. To change the browse sequence, simply change the order in which topics appear in your .rtf file.

·	If your Help file has only one browse sequence, use "auto" as the browse code. Help Workshop automatically creates a browse sequence based on the order in which topics appear in your topic files.

{button ,AL(`PRO_ADD_BROWSE_BUTTONS;BrowseButtons;BAS_ADDING_BROWSE_CODE;browse_seq')} Related Topics%!AL(`PRO_ADD_BROWSE_BUTTONS;BrowseButtons;BAS_ADDING_BROWSE_CODE;browse_seq')
```

```rtf
\b To add a topic to a browse sequence
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 1\tab At the beginning of the topic that you want to include in a \ul browse sequence,\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 1SRKR1R} insert a plus sign (+) as a footnote mark.
\par 2\tab Type a \ul browse code\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 6KLNWH} as the footnote text. Observe the following restrictions:
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li455\ri125\fi-155\tx455 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Do not use reserved characters (# = + @ * % !) or spaces.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Use no more than 50 characters.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx455 \b Notes
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab To use browse sequences, you must also activate the browse buttons in the project file.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab WinHelp creates an auto-browse sequence for topics to which you have added a browse footnote (+) but for which you have specified no browse code.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Tips
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab  When using numbers in browse codes, include placeholding zeros (001, 002, 003, and so on).
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab To make it easier to add topics later, use the same name, but no numbers for each browse footnote in a sequence. Help Workshop will arrange your browse sequence in which the order that the topics appear. To change the browse sequence, simply change the order in which topics appear in your .rtf file.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab If your Help file has only one browse sequence, use "auto" as the browse code. Help Workshop automatically creates a browse sequence based on the order in which topics appear in your topic files.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295
```

- Marker `A`

- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `* (asterisk) footnotes;adding;adding, footnotes to topics;asterisk (*) footnote;build tags;build tags, adding to topics;footnotes;footnotes, adding to topics;footnotes, asterisk (*);footnotes, build tag footnote;topics,;topics, adding footnotes to;topics, marking to exclude or include`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    * (asterisk) footnotes;adding;adding, footnotes to topics;asterisk (*) footnote;build tags;build tags, adding to topics;footnotes;footnotes, adding to topics;footnotes, asterisk (*);footnotes, build tag footnote;topics,;topics, adding footnotes to;topics, marking to exclude or include
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
To mark a topic so you can exclude or include it in a Help file

1	Place the insertion point at the beginning of the topic, ahead of any other footnote marks.

2	Insert an asterisk (*) as a footnote mark.

3	Type one or more build tags12MF62E as the corresponding footnote text. Separate build tags with a semicolon.

4	In your project (.hpj) file, make sure you have specified which topics to include in a build. For more information, click Related Topics.

Notes

·	Build tags are not case sensitive.

·	Topics without build tags are always included in a build, as are all topics that are not expressly excluded from a build in your project file.

{button ,AL(`PRO_HPJ_BUILD_TAGS;BAS_SPECIFY_BUILD;BUILD;BUILDTAGS')} Related Topics%!AL(`PRO_HPJ_BUILD_TAGS;BAS_SPECIFY_BUILD;BUILD;BUILDTAGS')
```

```rtf
\par 2\tab Insert an asterisk (*) as a footnote mark.
\par 3\tab Type one or more \ul build tags\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 12MF62E} as the corresponding footnote text. Separate build tags with a semicolon.
```

## 66. To add a nonscrolling region to a topic

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:009d`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:009d
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `To add a nonscrolling region to a topic`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    To add a nonscrolling region to a topic
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `PRO_NONSCROLL`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    PRO_NONSCROLL
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `PRO_NONSCROLL;PRO_TOPIC`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    PRO_NONSCROLL;PRO_TOPIC
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `adding;adding, nonscrolling regions to topics;Help topics,;Help topics, nonscrolling regions in;nonscrolling regions;pop-up windows;pop-up windows, avoiding nonscrolling regions in;topics,;topics, adding nonscrolling regions to`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    adding;adding, nonscrolling regions to topics;Help topics,;Help topics, nonscrolling regions in;nonscrolling regions;pop-up windows;pop-up windows, avoiding nonscrolling regions in;topics,;topics, adding nonscrolling regions to
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
To add a nonscrolling region to a topic

1	Make sure that the paragraph or paragraphs you want to be in the nonscrolling region_B12QW are at the top of the topic.

2	Apply the Keep With Next paragraph style (or equivalent) to the paragraphs.

Notes

·	Users can select (for copying or printing purposes) either the scrolling region or the nonscrolling region of a topic, but not both. However, both scrolling and nonscrolling regions are copied when users click a topic using their right mouse button, and then click Copy.

·	Do not add nonscrolling regions to topics that will be displayed in pop-up windows.

·	You can specify different background colors for nonscrolling and scrolling regions by opening your project (.hpj) file in Help Workshop, clicking the Windows button, and then clicking the Color tab.

·	If you use a transparent bitmap in both the scrolling region and the non-scrolling region of the same topic, the transparent area of both bitmaps is displayed using the background color of the scrolling region.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf

\b To add a nonscrolling region to a topic
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 1\tab Make sure that the paragraph or paragraphs you want to be in the \ul nonscrolling region\plain\f1\fs16 {\v _B12QW} are at the top of the topic. 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 2\tab Apply the Keep With Next paragraph style (or equivalent) to the paragraphs.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Notes
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab Users can select (for copying or printing purposes) either the scrolling region or the nonscrolling region of a topic, but not both. However, both scrolling and nonscrolling regions are copied when users click a topic using their right mouse button, and then click Copy.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Do not add nonscrolling regions to topics that will be displayed in pop-up windows.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab You can specify different background colors for nonscrolling and scrolling regions by opening your project (.hpj) file in Help Workshop, clicking the Windows button, and then clicking the Color tab.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab If you use a transparent bitmap in both the scrolling region and the non-scrolling region of the same topic, the transparent area of both bitmaps is displayed using the background color of the scrolling region.
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
To prevent a line of text from wrapping

1	Place the insertion point anywhere in the line that you do not want to wrap.

2	Apply the Keep Lines Together paragraph style (or equivalent).

Note

·	If the text of the paragraph is too wide for the window, WinHelp adds a horizontal scroll bar.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b To prevent a line of text from wrapping
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 1\tab Place the insertion point anywhere in the line that you do not want to wrap.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 2\tab Apply the Keep Lines Together paragraph style (or equivalent). 
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Note
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab If the text of the paragraph is too wide for the window, WinHelp adds a horizontal scroll bar.
\par
```

  - Raw:

    ```rtf
    ! (exclamation point);! (exclamation point), footnote;# (number sign) footnotes;$ (dollar sign) footnotes;* (asterisk) footnotes;+ (plus sign) footnotes;> (greater-than sign);> (greater-than sign), footnote;A-footnotes;asterisk (*) footnote;dollar sign ($) footnote;exclamation point (!);exclamation point (!), footnote;footnotes;footnotes, about topic footnotes;footnotes, A-footnote;footnotes, asterisk (*);footnotes, browse sequence footnote;footnotes, build tag footnote;footnotes, dollar sign ($);footnotes, entry macro footnote;footnotes, exclamation point (!);footnotes, greater-than sign (>);footnotes, K-footnote;footnotes, multi-index keyword footnote;footnotes, number sign (#);footnotes, plus sign (+);footnotes, title footnote;footnotes, topic ID footnote;footnotes, window type footnote;greater-than sign (>);greater-than sign (>), footnote;K-footnotes;number sign (#) footnote;plus sign ($) footnote;title footnotes;topic footnotes;topic IDs;topic title footnotes
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Topic footnotes

Instructions and commands specific to a topic are inserted as footnote text for that topic. Each footnote is identified by a custom footnote mark, as shown in the following table:

ParameterFootnote mark

Purpose
Topic IDPRO_ADD_TOPIC_IDpound sign (#)Defines a unique identifier for a topic. This footnote is required for every topic.
TitlePRO_ADD_TITLEdollar sign ($)Specifies the title of the topic as it appears in the Topics Found dialog box, the Bookmark dialog box, and the History window.
KeywordPRO_ADD_KEYWORDKSpecifies words used in the index and in KLink macros.1R9H33X
A-Keyword25J8VDHASpecifies words that identify the topic to ALink macros.7V8V.U
Browse CodePRO_ADD_BROWSE_CODEplus sign (+)Defines topic's place in a browse sequence.1SRKR1R
Entry MacroPRO_ADD_MACRO_COMMANDexclamation point (!)Specifies a macro that runs when the user opens the topic.
Build tagPRO_INSERT_BUILD_TAGasterisk (*)Identifies topics for conditional builds.12MF62E
Window typePRO_ADD_WINDOW_TYPEgreater than sign (>)Specifies a default window type for the topic.
Note

·	Only the topic ID7.61TY footnote (#) is mandatory. Include the others only if you want the functionality they offer.
```

```rtf
\trowd\trgaph105\trleft-106 \cellx1435\cellx2695\cellx6355\pard\intbl\sb55\sl-235\li115\ri125 \uldb Keyword\plain\f1\fs16 {\v PRO_ADD_KEYWORD}\cell\pard \pard\intbl\sb55\sl-235\li115\ri125 \pard\intbl\qc\sb55\sl-235\li115\ri125 K\cell\pard \pard\intbl\qc\sb55\sl-235\li115\ri125 \pard\intbl\sb55\sl-235\li115\ri125 Specifies words used in the index and in \ul KLink macros.\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 1R9H33X}\cell\intbl\row
```

## 70. Topic keywords

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`

    ```rtf
    Topic keywords
    ```

- Marker `A`

- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `A-keywords;index,;index, multi-index keywords;keywords;keywords, about topic keywords;keywords, adding multi-index keywords to a topic;K-keywords;multi-index keywords;topic keywords`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    A-keywords;index,;index, multi-index keywords;keywords;keywords, about topic keywords;keywords, adding multi-index keywords to a topic;K-keywords;multi-index keywords;topic keywords
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Topic keywords

Topic keywords are words or phrases associated with a topic, usually for indexing purposes. The same topic keyword may be associated with more than one topic. You assign keywords to a topic in topic footnotes..NSQ_T

There are three kinds of keywords:

·	Keywords specified by a K-footnote appear on the Index tab. K-keywords are also used by the KLink macro.1R9H33X

·	Keywords specified by an A-footnote do not appear in the Index tab, but are used by the ALink macro.7V8V.U

·	In addition to K- and A-keywords, you can assign multi-index keywords,2MZ3DCP using any of the remaining uppercase and lowercase letters of the alphabet. Each of these is used to create a separate index. This index can be searched only when Help is called from a program.

{button ,AL(`BAS_TOPIC_FOOTNOTE;BAS_WRITE_INDEX;BAS_ADDING_ALINK_KEYWORD;BAS_ADD_MULT_KEYWORD')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_TOPIC_FOOTNOTE;BAS_WRITE_INDEX;BAS_ADDING_ALINK_KEYWORD;BAS_ADD_MULT_KEYWORD')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b Topic keywords
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Topic keywords are words or phrases associated with a topic, usually for indexing purposes. The same topic keyword may be associated with more than one topic. You assign keywords to a topic in topic \ul footnotes.\plain\f1\fs16 {\v .NSQ_T}
\par There are three kinds of keywords:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Keywords specified by a K-footnote appear on the Index tab. K-keywords are also used by the \ul KLink macro.\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 1R9H33X}
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Keywords specified by an A-footnote do not appear in the Index tab, but are used by the \ul ALink macro.\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7V8V.U}
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab In addition to K- and A-keywords, you can assign \ul multi-index keywords,\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 2MZ3DCP} using any of the remaining uppercase and lowercase letters of the alphabet. Each of these is used to create a separate index. This index can be searched only when Help is called from a program.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`BAS_TOPIC_FOOTNOTE;BAS_WRITE_INDEX;BAS_ADDING_ALINK_KEYWORD;BAS_ADD_MULT_KEYWORD')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BAS_TOPIC_FOOTNOTE;BAS_WRITE_INDEX;BAS_ADDING_ALINK_KEYWORD;BAS_ADD_MULT_KEYWORD')}
\par
```

  - Raw:
    ```rtf

    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Browse sequences

A browse sequence is the order in which topics appear when users click the browse buttonsMFKK8E in a Help window.

A Help file can have a single browse sequence1SRKR1R or several smaller browse sequences. Each browse sequence is self-contained, and users cannot move from one browse sequence to another by using the browse buttons.

To specify a browse sequence, you must first add browse buttons to the project (.hpj) file, and then assign plus sign (+) footnotes.NSQ_T containing browse sequence codes to the topics that you want to include in the sequence.

Note

·	If your Help file has only one browse sequence, use "auto" as the browse footnote. Help Workshop will automatically create a browse sequence based on the order in which topics appear in your topic (.rtf) files.

{button ,AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_TOPIC_FOOTNOTE')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_TOPIC_FOOTNOTE')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b Browse sequences
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 A browse sequence is the order in which topics appear when users click the \ul browse buttons\plain\f1\fs16 {\v MFKK8E} in a Help window.
\par A Help file can have a single \ul browse sequence\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 1SRKR1R} or several smaller browse sequences. Each browse sequence is self-contained, and users cannot move from one browse sequence to another by using the browse buttons.
\par To specify a browse sequence, you must first add browse buttons to the project (.hpj) file, and then assign plus sign (+) \ul footnotes\plain\f1\fs16 {\v .NSQ_T} containing browse sequence codes to the topics that you want to include in the sequence.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Note
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab If your Help file has only one browse sequence, use "auto" as the browse footnote. Help Workshop will automatically create a browse sequence based on the order in which topics appear in your topic (.rtf) files.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_TOPIC_FOOTNOTE')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_TOPIC_FOOTNOTE')}
\par
```

## 72. Creating Help topics

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Creating Help topics`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Creating Help topics
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `1JQ9RMK`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    1JQ9RMK
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `creating;creating, topics;files,;files, .rtf;files, about topic files;Help topics,;Help topics, about topics;Help topics, creating;rich-text format;RTF files;topic files;topic files, about topic files and topics;topics,;topics, about Help topics;topics, creating`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    creating;creating, topics;files,;files, .rtf;files, about topic files;Help topics,;Help topics, about topics;Help topics, creating;rich-text format;RTF files;topic files;topic files, about topic files and topics;topics,;topics, about Help topics;topics, creating
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Creating Help topics

The Help topic is the basic unit of information in a Help file. Topics can range in size from short examples containing a single picture or word to lengthy explanations involving several graphics and hundreds of words.

A topic contains one or more of the following components:

·	Informational material. This is usually text, but can be graphics or multimedia.

·	Hotspots. These are textual or graphical elements that users can click to jump to other topics or to run macros.

·	Footnotes. These provide instructions to Help Workshop regarding the topic's unique topic ID,7.61TY the title that will appear in the Topics Found dialog box, keywords9ML77Q that will appear in the index, the position of a topic in a browse sequence,1SRKR1R the topic's inclusion in or exclusion from a Help file, and the type of window in which you want the topic to appear.

Topics are stored in topic files and saved in rich-text format (.rtf), one topic to a page. You can have any number of topics per topic file, and any number of topic files per Help file.

{button ,AL(`POPUP_TOPICS_DESIGN;3X_CONTENTS_DESIGN;TOPICS_DESIGN;BAS_OVERVIEW;BAS_CREATE_TOPIC_FILE;BAS_TOPIC_FOOTNOTE')} Related Topics%!AL(`POPUP_TOPICS_DESIGN;3X_CONTENTS_DESIGN;TOPICS_DESIGN;BAS_OVERVIEW;BAS_CREATE_TOPIC_FILE;BAS_TOPIC_FOOTNOTE')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 
\b Creating Help topics
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The Help topic is the basic unit of information in a Help file. Topics can range in size from short examples containing a single picture or word to lengthy explanations involving several graphics and hundreds of words.
\par A topic contains one or more of the following components:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Informational material. This is usually text, but can be graphics or multimedia.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Hotspots. These are textual or graphical elements that users can click to jump to other topics or to run macros.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Footnotes. These provide instructions to Help Workshop regarding the topic's unique \ul topic ID,\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7.61TY} the title that will appear in the Topics Found dialog box, \ul keywords\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 9ML77Q} that will appear in the index, the position of a topic in a \ul browse sequence,\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 1SRKR1R} the topic's inclusion in or exclusion from a Help file, and the type of window in which you want the topic to appear.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 Topics are stored in topic files and saved in rich-text format (.rtf), one topic to a page. You can have any number of topics per topic file, and any number of topic files per Help file.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`POPUP_TOPICS_DESIGN;3X_CONTENTS_DESIGN;TOPICS_DESIGN;BAS_OVERVIEW;BAS_CREATE_TOPIC_FILE;BAS_TOPIC_FOOTNOTE')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`POPUP_TOPICS_DESIGN;3X_CONTENTS_DESIGN;TOPICS_DESIGN;BAS_OVERVIEW;BAS_CREATE_TOPIC_FILE;BAS_TOPIC_FOOTNOTE')}
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Observe these restrictions when adding index keywords:

·	Separate keywords with a semicolon.

·	Spaces before and after keywords are removed.

·	Do not use carriage returns.

·	Use no more than 255 characters per keyword.
```

### Decoded Body

```text
To create a new project file

1	In Help Workshop, click the File menu, and then click New.

2	Double-click Help Project.

3	Type the name of the project (.hpj) file, and then click OK.

Note

·	When you create a project file, Help Workshop configures the minimum settings you need to create a Help file. Before you can compile, however, you must click the Files button and specify at least one .rtf file.

{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;NEW_AUTHOR_HPJ')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;NEW_AUTHOR_HPJ')
```

### Raw RTF Body


### Decoded Body

```text
To add copyright information

1	In Help Workshop, open the project (.hpj) file.

2	Click Options.

3	In the Copyright Information area, type the copyright information you want to display in the Version dialog box or when users paste or print Help text.

Notes

·	You can specify up to 255 characters of copyright information for the Version dialog box. There is no limit to the number of characters you can specify for the copyright information that appears when users paste or print Help text.

·	If you add %date at the end of the text you want to display in the Version dialog box, Help Workshop will substitute the current date whenever you compile the Help file.

{button ,AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR')
```

```rtf
\par 3\tab In the Copyright Information area, type the copyright information you want to display in the Version dialog box or when users paste or print Help text.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Notes
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab You can specify up to 255 characters of copyright information for the Version dialog box.     ```
```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `compression;Help files;Help files, compression options;Help Workshop;Help Workshop, editing project files;project files;project files, specifying compression options`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    compression;Help files;Help files, compression options;Help Workshop;Help Workshop, editing project files;project files;project files, specifying compression options
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
To specify compression options

1	In Help Workshop, open the project (.hpj) file.

2	Click Options.

3	Click the Compression tab, and then specify the compression settings you want.

Tips

·	For help on an item, click {bmc bm0.bmp} at the top of the dialog box, and then click the item.

·	Help Workshop typically takes three times longer to compile a help file using maximum compression than it does to compile a file with no compression.

·	You can reduce compile time by temporarily turning off compression without affecting the compression setting in the project file. To do this, click the Compile button,22T9EAQ and then make sure the Turn Off Compression box is checked.

{button ,AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_COMPRESSION')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_COMPRESSION')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 1\tab In Help Workshop, open the project (.hpj) file.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 2\tab Click Options.
\par 3\tab Click the Compression tab, and then specify the compression settings you want.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Tips
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab For help on an item, click \{bmc bm0.bmp\} at the top of the dialog box, and then click the item.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Help Workshop typically takes three times longer to compile a help file using maximum compression than it does to compile a file with no compression.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab You can reduce compile time by temporarily turning off compression without affecting the compression setting in the project file. To do this, click the \ul Compile button,\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 22T9EAQ} and then make sure the Turn Off Compression box is checked.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295

\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
To specify the language of a Help file

1	In Help Workshop, open the project (.hpj) file.

2	Click Options.

3	Click the Sorting tab, and then specify the language you want.

Notes

·	By default, Help Workshop uses a language based on the regional setting specified in Control Panel.

·	If the version of Windows you are running does not support the language you specify, Help Workshop cannot compile the project file.

{button ,AL(`BAS_WRITE_INDEX;BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_TRANSLATING')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_WRITE_INDEX;BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_TRANSLATING')
```

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`

    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:00a6
    ```

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `PRO_SPECIFY_SEP_CHAR`
  - Raw:

- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `PRO_SPECIFY_SEP_CHAR`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    PRO_SPECIFY_SEP_CHAR
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `Help Workshop;Help Workshop, editing project files;index,;index, specifying the keyword separator;keywords;keywords, specifying a separator character;project files;project files, specifying index keyword separator;second-level index entries;separator for index keywords;topic keywords`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Help Workshop;Help Workshop, editing project files;index,;index, specifying the keyword separator;keywords;keywords, specifying a separator character;project files;project files, specifying index keyword separator;second-level index entries;separator for index keywords;topic keywords
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
To change characters that identify second-level index entries

1	In Help Workshop, open the project (.hpj) file.

2	Click Options.

3	Click the Sorting tab.

4	In the box for specifying characters for separating index entries, type one or more characters.

Example

If you specify "@&%", the line "display; display@configuring; keyboard; keyboard&configuring; mouse;mouse%configuring; network, configuring" would appear this way in the index:

	display

		configuring

	keyboard

		configuring

	mouse

		configuring

	network, configuring

Note

·	The default characters are a comma (,) and colon (:). Any characters you specify here will override these default characters.

{button ,AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_TRANSLATING')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_TRANSLATING')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b To change characters that identify second-level index entries
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 1\tab In Help Workshop, open the project (.hpj) file.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 2\tab Click Options.
\par 3\tab Click the Sorting tab.
\par 4\tab In the box for specifying characters for separating index entries, type one or more characters.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Example
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li85\ri125\tx265 \plain\f1\fs16 If you specify "@&%", the line "display; display@configuring; keyboard; keyboard&configuring; mouse;mouse%configuring; network, configuring" would appear this way in the index:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295\tx445 \tab display\line
\tab \tab configuring\line
\tab keyboard\line
\tab \tab configuring\line
\tab mouse\line
\tab \tab configuring\line
\tab network, configuring
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295\tx445 \b Note
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab The default characters are a comma (,) and colon (:). Any characters you specify here will override these default characters.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_TRANSLATING')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_TRANSLATING')}
\par
```


### Decoded Body

```text
To save compiler messages to a file

1	In Help Workshop, open the project (.hpj) file.

2	Click Options.

3	Click the Files tab.

4	In the Log File box, type the name of the file. If you want the file to be saved in a folder other than the project folder, specify a path.

Notes

·	Help Workshop can display up to 64K of messages in Windows 95 and 1 MB of messages in Windows NT. You can save more messages than Help Workshop can display by specifying a log file, which you can open using any ASCII text editor.

·	You can suppress some of the messages Help Workshop displays. For more information, click Related Topics.

{button ,AL(`PRO_HPJ_CONTROL_ERRORS;BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_THE_HELP_COMPILER;PRO_HPJ_CONTROL_ERRORS')} Related Topics%!AL(`PRO_HPJ_CONTROL_ERRORS;BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_THE_HELP_COMPILER;PRO_HPJ_CONTROL_ERRORS')
```

  - Raw:


### Decoded Body

```text
To store DLL-related data files within the Help file

1	In Help Workshop, open the project (.hpj) file.

2	Click Data Files.

3	To select a data file, click Add.

Notes

·	DLL data filenames are case sensitive. To retrieve a data file, Help uses the filename without the path.

·	WinHelp provides callback functions for DLLs to enable a program or DLL to retrieve the appropriate data file from the Help file.

·	To remove a data file, click it, and then click Remove.

{button ,AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAGGAGE')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAGGAGE')
```

```rtf
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 2\tab Click Data Files.
```

### Decoded Body

```text
To specify where to store temporary files

1	In Help Workshop, open the project (.hpj) file.

2	Click Options.

3	Click the Files tab.

4	In the TMP Folder box, type the name of the folder you want Help Workshop to use for temporary files.

Notes

·	Help Workshop uses the folder you specify for temporary files instead of the folder specified by the TEMP environment variable.

·	Help Workshop rarely uses temporary files unless your Help file exceeds 8 MB in size.

{button ,AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_THE_HELP_COMPILER')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_THE_HELP_COMPILER')
```

  - Raw:

    ```rtf
    PRO_HPJ_REPLACE_PATH
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `PRO_HPJ;PRO_HPJ_REPLACE_PATH`
  - Raw:

    ```rtf
    PRO_HPJ;PRO_HPJ_REPLACE_PATH
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `Help Workshop;Help Workshop, editing project files;path, specifying in project files;project files;project files, changing the path to source files`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Help Workshop;Help Workshop, editing project files;path, specifying in project files;project files;project files, changing the path to source files
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
To change the path to source files if you move your Help project

1	In Help Workshop, open the project (.hpj) file.

2	Click Options.

3	Click the Files tab.

4	To specify a new path, click Edit.

Notes

·	Substituting a path is unnecessary if all files have been specified using paths that are relative to the location of the project file. Help Workshop automatically uses relative paths when it adds filenames.

·	Substituting a path is useful when you move your entire Help project to a different drive or network location. Instead of manually changing all the file paths in your project file, you can quickly change them all here.

{button ,AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR')
```

```rtf
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 2\tab Click Options.
\par 3\tab Click the Files tab.
\par 4\tab To specify a new path, click Edit.
```


### Decoded Body

```text
To generate an index file for full-text search

1	In Help Workshop, open the project (.hpj) file.

2	Click Options.

3	Click the FTS tab.

4	Make sure Generate Full Text Search Index is checked, and then change other settings as needed.

Notes

·	For help on an item, click {bmc bm0.bmp} at the top of the dialog box, and then click the item.

·	To generate a full-text search index file when compiling, Maximum compression or Hall compression must be checked on the Compression tab.

·	If you do not generate a full-text search index file, WinHelp prompts users to generate it when they first open your Help file and click the Find tab.

{button ,AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_FULLTEXT_INDEX')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_FULLTEXT_INDEX')
```

- Marker `K`


### Decoded Body

```text
To specify which topics to include in a build

1	In Help Workshop, open the project (.hpj) file.

2	Click Options.

3	Click the Build Tags tab.

4	Add the tags for the topics you want to be included in your help file.

Notes

·	For help on an item, click {bmc bm0.bmp} at the top of the dialog box, and then click the item.

·	Topics without build tags12MF62E are always included in builds. Topics with build tags are included only if they contain one or more of the build tags you specify on the Build Tags tab.

·	You create build tags by adding footnotes to topic (.rtf) files. For more information, click Related Topics.

·	If you specify a build tag in either the Include or Exclude section of the Build Tags tab, then Help Workshop will ignore any BUILD option that might have been specified.

{button ,AL(`PRO_INSERT_BUILD_TAG;BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_INSERTING_BUILD_TAG')} Related Topics%!AL(`PRO_INSERT_BUILD_TAG;BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_INSERTING_BUILD_TAG')
```

```rtf
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab You create build tags by adding footnotes to topic (.rtf) files. For more information, click Related Topics.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab If you specify a build tag in either the Include or Exclude section of the Build Tags tab, then Help Workshop will ignore any BUILD option that might have been specified.
```

### Decoded Body

```text
To change the font used in WinHelp dialog boxes

1	In Help Workshop, open the project (.hpj) file.

2	Click Options.

3	Click the Fonts tab.

4	To change the font, click Change.

Notes

·	If you choose a font that is not available on a user's computer, Windows will use the font that is its closest match.

·	Changing the font for WinHelp dialog boxes is useful if your Help file will be shipped to international markets unlocalized. For example, WinHelp is localized to use larger fonts in Far East versions. If you do not want large fonts used in WinHelp dialog boxes, you can specify your own.

{button ,AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_TRANSLATING')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_TRANSLATING')
```

## 92. To run two language versions of a Help file side by side

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:00af`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:00af
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `To run two language versions of a Help file side by side`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    To run two language versions of a Help file side by side
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `PRO_HCW_SPLIT_SCREEN`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    PRO_HCW_SPLIT_SCREEN
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `PRO_HCW;PRO_HCW_SPLIT_SCREEN`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    PRO_HCW;PRO_HCW_SPLIT_SCREEN
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `Help files;Help files, testing two language versions;Test macro;Test macro, running different language versions;testing;testing, translated Help files`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Help files;Help files, testing two language versions;Test macro;Test macro, running different language versions;testing;testing, translated Help files
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
To run two language versions of a Help file side by side

1	Start the original-language Help file and display any topic that is in a main window.O8.Q2

2	Press CTRL+SHIFT+J.

3	Type the following, and then click OK:

	!Compare(“filename.ext”)

	For filename.ext, specify the name of the translated version of the Help file. Specify the path, if necessary, typing two backlashes in place of each one you would typically type.

Note

·	For best results, temporarily rename your contents (.cnt) file for both Help files, and start the original-language Help file by typing the following in the command prompt:

	winhlp32 -g filename.ext

	For filename.ext, type the name of the original-language Help file.

·	If the translated version of the Help file was properly prepared, moving through one file (by using CTRL+SHIFT+RIGHT ARROW or CTRL+SHIFT+LEFT ARROW) updates both windows.

{button ,AL(`BAS_TRANSLATING;Compare')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_TRANSLATING;Compare')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b To run two language versions of a Help file side by side
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 1\tab Start the original-language Help file and display any topic that is in a \ul main window.\plain\f1\fs16 {\v O8.Q2}
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 2\tab Press CTRL+SHIFT+J.
\par 3\tab Type the following, and then click OK: 
\par \b \tab !Compare(\'93\plain\f1\fs16 filename.ext\b \'94)
\par \tab \plain\f1\fs16 For filename.ext, specify the name of the translated version of the Help file. Specify the path, if necessary, typing two backlashes in place of each one you would typically type.\b 
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 Note
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab For best results, temporarily rename your contents (.cnt) file for both Help files, and start the original-language Help file by typing the following in the command prompt:
\par \tab \b winhlp32 -g \plain\f1\fs16 filename.ext
\par \tab For filename.ext, type the name of the original-language Help file.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab If the translated version of the Help file was properly prepared, moving through one file (by using CTRL+SHIFT+RIGHT ARROW or CTRL+SHIFT+LEFT ARROW) updates both windows.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`BAS_TRANSLATING;Compare')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BAS_TRANSLATING;Compare')}
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
To change every occurrence of a font used in a Help file

1	In Help Workshop, open the project (.hpj) file.

2	Click Options.

3	Click the Fonts tab.

4	Click Add, and then specify the settings you want.

Note

·	For help on an item, click {bmc bm0.bmp} at the top of the dialog box, and then click the item.

·	This procedure enables you to change the fonts used in a Help file without having to reformat the text in every topic (.rtf) file. There is no limit to the number of fonts you can replace.

{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_FONTS;NEW_FONT_HANDLING;BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_FONTS;NEW_FONT_HANDLING;BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR')
```

```rtf
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 2\tab Click Options.
\par 3\tab Click the Fonts tab.
\par 4\tab Click Add, and then specify the settings you want.
```

```text
1	In Help Workshop, open the project (.hpj) file.

2	Click the Windows button.

3	Click Add.

4	Type the name of the secondary window.2ZK4PT Do not type main as the name of the window.

5	In the list, click the name of the standard window you want to base the secondary window on.

Notes

·	For help on an item, click {bmc bm0.bmp} at the top of the dialog box, and then click the item.

·	You can define up to 255 secondary windows.

·	You can define a default window for an entire Help file or for individual topics. For more information, click Related Topics.
```

### Decoded Body

```text
To specify a title for a window

1	In Help Workshop, open the project (.hpj) file.

2	Click the Windows button.

3	In the Window Type list, click the window type.

4	In the Title Bar Text box, type the title.

Notes

·	The title appears in the window's title bar and on its taskbar button.

·	If a secondary window does not have a title, but the contents (.cnt) file for the Help file specifies a title, the title from the contents file is used. If neither the secondary window nor the contents file specify a title, no text appears in the title bar.

{button ,AL(`BAS_HELP_TITLE;BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_HPJ_CREATE_WINDOW')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_HELP_TITLE;BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_HPJ_CREATE_WINDOW')
```

- Marker `K`

  - Raw:
    ```rtf

    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
To specify the size and position of a window

1	In Help Workshop, open the project (.hpj) file.

2	Click the Windows button.

3	Click the Position tab.

4	In the Window Type list, click the window type.

5	To set the size and position of the window, click Auto-Sizer.

Notes

·	If you want WinHelp to determine the position of a window, click Default Positions.

·	If you want to set the size and position manually, type the settings in the Left, Top, Width, and Height boxes.

·	If you want to automatically size the secondary window to the length of the topic, click the General tab, and then make sure Auto-Size Height is checked. (This feature is not available for the main Help window.)

{button ,AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_HPJ_CREATE_WINDOW')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_HPJ_CREATE_WINDOW')
```

```rtf
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 2\tab Click the Windows button.
\par 3\tab Click the Position tab.
\par 4\tab In the Window Type list, click the window type.

\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Notes
```

```text
To specify background colors for a window

1	In Help Workshop, open the project (.hpj) file.

2	Click the Windows button.

3	Click the Color tab.

4	In the Window Type list, click the window type.

5	To select colors for the nonscrolling and scrolling regions, click the Change buttons.

Note

·	The background color you specify can be overridden if users change their window or window text colors in Display properties or if users click a topic using their right mouse button, and then click Use System Colors.

·	If you use a transparent bitmap in both the scrolling region and the non-scrolling region of the same topic, the transparent area of both bitmaps is displayed using the background color of the scrolling region.

{button ,AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_HPJ_CREATE_WINDOW')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_HPJ_CREATE_WINDOW')
```

```text
2	Click the Windows button.

3	Click the Buttons tab.

5	In the Buttons area, make sure the buttons you want to appear in the window are checked.

Tips

·	If you do not want a button bar, make sure all the button types in the Buttons area are cleared.

·	You can add custom buttons by clicking the Macros tab and adding CreateButton macros. You cannot add custom buttons unless you have used at least one standard WinHelp button.
```

```text
To run a macro when an index entry is clicked

1	In Help Workshop, open the project (.hpj) file.

2	Click Options, and then click the Macros tab.

3	Click Add, and then specify the settings you want.

Notes

·	For help on an item, click {bmc bm0.bmp} at the top of the dialog box, and then click the item.

·	Running a macro when users click an index entry is useful for starting a wizard, a series of training cards, or a DLL.

·	The keywords you assign here will appear in the index regardless of whether they are specified as K-footnotes in topics.

·	The title you specify will appear only if users double-click a keyword that is linked to a macro and to one or more topics. In this case, clicking the title in the Topics Found dialog box starts the macro.
```

## 107. To enable a program to display an individual Help topic

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `K`

    ```rtf
    context-sensitive Help;context-sensitive Help, mapping topic IDs;Help Workshop;Help Workshop, mapping topic IDs;mapping topic IDs;project files;project files, mapping topic IDs;topic IDs;topic IDs, mapping
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
To enable a program to display an individual Help topic

1	In Help Workshop, open the project (.hpj) file.

2	Click Map.

3	If you want to add an individual topic, click Add.

If you want to include a C header file, click Include.

Note

·	When assigning topic IDs to context-sensitive Help topics, make sure the first four characters in the ID are IDH_. Help Workshop recognizes that topic IDs beginning with these characters are ones that a program will call. While compiling, Help Workshop displays a list of any topic IDs that are in your topic (.rtf) files, but have not been mapped to numeric values. Help Workshop also lists IDs that have been mapped to numeric values, but are not in your topic files. Using the IDH_ naming convention will help you find and resolve problems in your context-sensitive Help. If you want to use a prefix other than IDH_, specify it in the Map dialog box.

{button ,AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_HPJ_ADD_MAP')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_HPJ_ADD_MAP')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b To enable a program to display an individual Help topic
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 1\tab In Help Workshop, open the project (.hpj) file.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 2\tab Click Map.
\par 3\tab If you want to add an individual topic, click Add.
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li295\ri125\tx295 If you want to include a C header file, click Include.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Note
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab When assigning topic IDs to context-sensitive Help topics, make sure the first four characters in the ID are IDH_. Help Workshop recognizes that topic IDs beginning with these characters are ones that a program will call. While compiling, Help Workshop displays a list of any topic IDs that are in your topic (.rtf) files, but have not been mapped to numeric values. Help Workshop also lists IDs that have been mapped to numeric values, but are not in your topic files. Using the IDH_ naming convention will help you find and resolve problems in your context-sensitive Help. If you want to use a prefix other than IDH_, specify it in the Map dialog box. 
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_HPJ_ADD_MAP')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_HPJ_ADD_MAP')}
\par
```


### Decoded Body

```text
To alias one topic ID to another

1	In Help Workshop, open the project (.hpj) file.

2	Click Alias.

3	Click Add, and then specify the settings you want.

Tips

·	For help on an item, click {bmc bm0.bmp} at the top of the dialog box, and then click the item.

·	Aliases are an easy way to redirect jumps to topics without changing every instance of a jump throughout the Help file.

·	Aliases are also useful where you've made a change that causes a mapped numeric value to refer to a topic other than the one you intended. An alias enables you to display the intended topic in the Help file without changing the code in the program.
```

```rtf
\par 3\tab Click Add, and then specify the settings you want.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Tips
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab For help on an item, click \{bmc bm0.bmp\} at the top of the dialog box, and then click the item.

\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Aliases are also useful where you've made a change that causes a mapped numeric value to refer to a topic other than the one you intended. An alias enables you to display the intended topic in the Help file without changing the code in the program.
\par
```

## 111. To create a contents file

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:00c0`
  - Raw:

  - Raw:

- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `contents files;contents files, creating;Contents tab;Contents tab, creating entries for;creating;creating, contents files`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    contents files;contents files, creating;Contents tab;Contents tab, creating entries for;creating;creating, contents files
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
To create a contents file

1	In Help Workshop, click the File menu, and then click New.

2	Double-click Help Contents.

3	In the Default Filename box, type the Help filename that most of your topics are in.

4	In the Default Title box, type the text you want to appear in the title bar of the Help Topics dialog box. This text also appears in title bars that do not have a title specified in the [WINDOWS] section of the project file.

5	Click Add Above or Add below to add the headings and topics that you want to display on the Contents tab.

6	If you want to include the keywords of several files on the Index tab, click Index Files and specify the files you want to include.

{button ,AL(`BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;PRO_ADD_MULT_KEYWORDS')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;PRO_ADD_MULT_KEYWORDS')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b To create a contents file
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 1\tab In Help Workshop, click the File menu, and then click New.
\par 2\tab Double-click Help Contents.
\par 3\tab In the Default Filename box, type the Help filename that most of your topics are in. 
\par 4\tab In the Default Title box, type the text you want to appear in the title bar of the Help Topics dialog box. This text also appears in title bars that do not have a title specified in the [WINDOWS] section of the project file.
\par 5\tab Click Add Above or Add below to add the headings and topics that you want to display on the Contents tab.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 6\tab If you want to include the keywords of several files on the Index tab, click Index Files and specify the files you want to include.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;PRO_ADD_MULT_KEYWORDS')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;PRO_ADD_MULT_KEYWORDS')}
\par
```

  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    contents files;contents files, specifying a default window;default window;topic windows;topic windows, default window type, specifying;window types;windows,;windows, default type, specifying
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
To specify a default window in which to display topics

1	In Help Workshop, open your contents (.cnt) file.

2	Click the Edit button in the upper right corner of the Help Workshop window.

3	In the Default Window box, specify the window type.

Notes

·	Topics appear in the default window when users open them from the Index and Find tabs, unless a different window is specified for a topic by a window-type footnote (>). Topics also appear in the default window when users open them from the Contents tab, unless the contents entry includes a file or window type specification.

·	If a default window is not specified, topics appear in the main Help window.

·	The window type you specify must be defined in your project (.hpj) file. For more information, click Related topics.

{button ,AL(`BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;PRO_HPJ_CREATE_WINDOW')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;PRO_HPJ_CREATE_WINDOW')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 1\tab In Help Workshop, open your contents (.cnt) file.
\par 2\tab Click the Edit button in the upper right corner of the Help Workshop window.
\par 3\tab In the Default Window box, specify the window type.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Notes
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\tab \f1 Topics appear in the default window when users open them from the Index and Find tabs, unless a different window is specified for a topic by a window-type footnote (>). Topics also appear in the default window when users open them from the Contents tab, unless the contents entry includes a file or window type specification.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\tab \f1 If a default window is not specified, topics appear in the main Help window.
\par \f2 \'b7\tab \f1 The window type you specify must be defined in your project (.hpj) file. For more information, click Related topics.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-115\li295\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295

\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
To include additional contents files

1	Open the main contents file, and then click the place where you want the information in the second contents file to appear.

2	Click Add Above or Add Below, and then click Include.

3	Type the name of the contents file that you want to include.

Notes

·	If the included contents file is not available when WinHelp initializes the Help file, the included file will be not appear on the Contents tab.

·	A default Help filename specified in the included contents file affects only the included file, not the main contents file.

{button ,AL(`BAS_CNT_COMBINE_HLP_FILES;BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_CNT_COMBINE_HLP_FILES;BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW')
```

### Decoded Body

```text
To add topics to the Contents tab

1	In Help Workshop, open the contents (.cnt) file.

2	Click the item above or below which you want to add a topic, and then click Add Above or Add Below.

3	Click Topic, and then type a title in the Title box.

4	In the Topic ID box, type the topic ID.

5	If the topic is not in the default Help file, type the name of the file that contains the topic in the Help File box.

6	If you want to display the topic in a window type other than the default window, specify the window in the Window Type box. The window you specify must be defined in the project (.hpj) file.

Notes

·	If you do not specify information in the Help File, WinHelp searches the default Help file for the topic. If you do not specify information in the Window Type box, WinHelp displays the topic in the window type specified in the default Help file.

·	You can assign a macro to a topic instead of a topic ID. In the Topic ID box, type an exclamation point (!) followed by the macro. To separate multiple macros, use colons (:).

{button ,AL(`BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;PRO_CNT_ADD_BOOK')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;PRO_CNT_ADD_BOOK')
```

```rtf
\par 5\tab If the topic is not in the default Help file, type the name of the file that contains the topic in the Help File box.

\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\tab \f1 When a heading is indented to the left or right, its topics and subheadings move with it.
```

## 117. To combine keywords from several Help files

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`

- Marker `#`

- Marker `A`

- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `combined index;combining multiple Help files;contents files;contents files, specifying indexes to be combined;index,;index, combining keywords from multiple Help files;keywords;keywords, combining from multiple Help files;linking multiple Help files;merging multiple Help files`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf

    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
To combine keywords from several Help files

1	In Help Workshop, open the contents (.cnt) file.

2	Click the Index Files button.

3	Click Add.

4	In the Help Title box, type the title of the Help file. The title will appear if users choose Custom in the Find Setup Wizard. It also appears in the Topics Found dialog box if WinHelp finds identical topic titles in different Help files.

5	In the Help Filename box, type the name of the Help file whose keywords you want to add to your index.

Note

{bmc bm1.bmp}	All Help files included in the index are automatically searched when an ALink7V8V.U or KLink7V8V.U macro is run.

{button ,AL(`BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;BAS_CNT_COMBINE_HLP_FILES')} Related Topics%!AL("BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;BAS_CNT_COMBINE_HLP_FILES")
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b To combine keywords from several Help files
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 1\tab In Help Workshop, open the contents (.cnt) file.
\par 2\tab Click the Index Files button.
\par 3\tab Click Add.
\par 4\tab In the Help Title box, type the title of the Help file. The title will appear if users choose Custom in the Find Setup Wizard. It also appears in the Topics Found dialog box if WinHelp finds identical topic titles in different Help files.
\par 5\tab In the Help Filename box, type the name of the Help file whose keywords you want to add to your index.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Note
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 \{bmc bm1.bmp\}\tab All Help files included in the index are automatically searched when an \ul ALink\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7V8V.U} or \ul KLink\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7V8V.U} macro is run.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295

\par
```
