    ```rtf
    macros,;macros, ShortCut;ShortCut macro
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
ShortCut macro

ShortCut(window-class, program[, wParam[, lParam[, topic-ID]]])

Runs the specified program if it is not already running. If the specified program is running, WinHelp activates it. If the wParam parameter is specified, a WM_COMMAND message with the specified wParam and lParam values are sent to the program.

Parameter	Description

window-class	Specifies the class name of a top level window of the program. WinHelp uses this class name to determine if the program is already running.

program	Specifies the executable name of the program. This is the name of the program that runs if the window class name cannot be found. The .exe extension does not need to be included.

wParam	Specifies the first argument to the WM_COMMAND message that is sent to the program. If this value is not specified, the program is started, but no message is sent and the program is not activated.

lParam	Specifies the second argument to the WM_COMMAND message.

topic-ID	Specifies the topic ID of the topic to jump to if the program cannot be found. If this parameter is not specified, WinHelp displays a message box indicating that the program could not be found. If it is specified and no filename is included, the filename specified by the :Base command in the contents (.cnt) file is used. If there is no associated contents file, the current Help file is used. To specify a specific filename, the topic ID should end with an '@' character and the name of the Help file.

Comments

If you need to include a topic ID, but do not want to send the program any messages, you can either use -1 for the wParam argument and 0 for the lParam argument, or you can simply include the commas without specifying the values for the arguments as follows:

	ShortCut(class_name, myapp, , , topic-ID)

Some programs, such as the Microsoft Visual C++ compiler (MSVC), cannot receive a message in the same call that runs them. In this case, you must first call the ShortCut macro without specifying wParam or lParam. You can then call this macro again and send the wParam and lParam messages. Note that the second time you call this macro, you can use an empty string ("") for the program name.

Help Workshop automatically converts the ShortCut macro to SH, which is the only form of this macro that WinHelp can use.

The ShortCut macro is new for WinHelp version 4.0.

Example

The following example copies the current topic to the Clipboard, runs or activates Notepad, and pastes the text from the Clipboard into Notepad:

	CopyTopic():ShortCut(notepad, notepad, 0x0302)

{button ,AL("macro_all;MACRO_REF_PROGRAMS;ControlPanel;ExecFile ")} Related Topics%!KL("macros_all;ControlPanel;ExecFile;MACRO_REF_PROGRAMS")
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 ShortCut macro
\par \pard\keepn\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \fs16 ShortCut(\plain\f1\fs16 window-class\b ,\plain\f1\fs16 \'a0program[\b ,\plain\f1\fs16 \'a0wParam[\b ,\plain\f1\fs16 \'a0lParam[\b ,\plain\f1\fs16 \'a0topic-ID]]]\b )\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 Runs the specified program if it is not already running. If the specified program is running, WinHelp activates it. If the wParam parameter is specified, a WM_COMMAND message with the specified wParam and lParam values are sent to the program.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1575\ri125\fi-1455\brdrb\brdrs\tx1575 \b Parameter\tab Description\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1575\ri125\fi-1455\tx1575 window-class\tab Specifies the class name of a top level window of the program. WinHelp uses this class name to determine if the program is already running.
\par program\tab Specifies the executable name of the program. This is the name of the program that runs if the window class name cannot be found. The .exe extension does not need to be included.
\par wParam\tab Specifies the first argument to the WM_COMMAND message that is sent to the program. If this value is not specified, the program is started, but no message is sent and the program is not activated.
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1575\ri125\fi-1455\tx1575 lParam\tab Specifies the second argument to the WM_COMMAND message.
\par topic-ID\tab Specifies the topic ID of the topic to jump to if the program cannot be found. If this parameter is not specified, WinHelp displays a message box indicating that the program could not be found. If it is specified and no filename is included, the filename specified by the :Base command in the contents (.cnt) file is used. If there is no associated contents file, the current Help file is used. To specify a specific filename, the topic ID should end with an '@' character and the name of the Help file.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1575 \b Comments
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1575 \plain\f1\fs16 If you need to include a topic ID, but do not want to send the program any messages, you can either use -1 for the wParam argument and 0 for the lParam argument, or you can simply include the commas without specifying the values for the arguments as follows:
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \tab ShortCut(class_name, myapp, , , topic-ID)
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 Some programs, such as the Microsoft Visual C++ compiler (MSVC), cannot receive a message in the same call that runs them. In this case, you must first call the ShortCut macro without specifying wParam or lParam. You can then call this macro again and send the wParam and lParam messages. Note that the second time you call this macro, you can use an empty string ("") for the program name.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 Help Workshop automatically converts the ShortCut macro to SH, which is the only form of this macro that WinHelp can use.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 The ShortCut macro is new for WinHelp version 4.0.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Example
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 The following example copies the current topic to the Clipboard, runs or activates Notepad, and pastes the text from the Clipboard into Notepad:
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \tab CopyTopic():ShortCut(notepad, notepad, 0x0302)
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb25\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL("macro_all;MACRO_REF_PROGRAMS;ControlPanel;ExecFile ")\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!KL("macros_all;ControlPanel;ExecFile;MACRO_REF_PROGRAMS")}
```

### Decoded Body

```text
TCard macro

TCard(command)

Sends a message to the program that is invoking WinHelp as a training card.8AINQO

Parameter	Description

command	Specifies one of the following commands, a numeric value, or a topic ID. If one of the following values is specified, its numeric equivalent is sent as the wParam value of the WM_TCARD message:

Value	Meaning

IDABORT (3)	The user clicked an authorable Abort button.

IDCANCEL (2)	The user clicked an authorable Cancel button.

IDCLOSE (8)	The user closed the training card.

IDHELP (9)	The user clicked an authorable Help button.

IDIGNORE (5)	The user clicked an authorable Ignore button.

IDOK (1)	The user clicked an authorable OK button.

IDNO (7)	The user clicked an authorable No button.

IDRETRY (4)	The user clicked an authorable Retry button.

IDYES (6)	The user clicked an authorable Yes button.

If a numeric value is used, the training card program is sent HELP_TCARD_DATA (16) for the wParam parameter, and the numeric value is passed as the lParam value of the WM_TCARD message.

If a topic ID is used, the ID is mapped to the numeric value found in the project file and it is passed to the program.

Comment

The TCard macro is new for WinHelp version 4.0.

{button ,AL("macro_all")} Related Topics%!AL("macro_all")
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 TCard macro
\par \pard\keepn\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \fs16 TCard(\plain\f1\fs16 command\b )\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 Sends a message to the program that is invoking WinHelp as a \ul training card.\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 8AINQO}
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1615\ri125\fi-1455\brdrb\brdrs\tx1615 \b Parameter\tab Description\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1615\ri125\fi-1455\tx1615 command\tab Specifies one of the following commands, a numeric value, or a topic ID. If one of the following values is specified, its numeric equivalent is sent as the wParam value of the WM_TCARD message:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li3065\ri125\fi-1435\brdrb\brdrs\tx3055 \b Value\tab Meaning\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li3065\ri125\fi-1435\tx3055 IDABORT (3)\tab The user clicked an authorable Abort button.
\par IDCANCEL (2)\tab The user clicked an authorable Cancel button.
\par IDCLOSE (8)\b \tab \plain\f1\fs16 The user closed the training card.
\par IDHELP (9)\tab The user clicked an authorable Help button.
\par IDIGNORE (5)\tab The user clicked an authorable Ignore button.
\par IDOK (1)\tab The user clicked an authorable OK button.
\par IDNO (7)\tab The user clicked an authorable No button.
\par IDRETRY (4)\tab The user clicked an authorable Retry button.
\par IDYES (6)\tab The user clicked an authorable Yes button.
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1575\ri125\tx3195 If a numeric value is used, the training card program is sent HELP_TCARD_DATA (16) for the wParam parameter, and the numeric value is passed as the lParam value of the WM_TCARD message.
\par If a topic ID is used, the ID is mapped to the numeric value found in the project file and it is passed to the program.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx3195 \b Comment
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx3195 \plain\f1\fs16 The TCard macro is new for WinHelp version 4.0.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Test macro

Test(test-num)

Runs an internal WinHelp test.

Parameter	Description

test-num	Specifies the testing option. This parameter can be one of the following values:

Value	Description

1	Displays all the topics in the Help file, starting with the current topic.

2	Displays all the topics in the Help file, starting with the first topic.

3	Continuously displays all the topics in the Help file, starting with the first topic.

4	Displays all the topics in the Help file, starting with the first topic, and then quits.

5	Jumps to all topics specified in the contents file excluding macros.

6	Jumps to all topics specified in the contents file, excluding macros, and then quits.

7	Turns on Help Mode for the current instance of WinHelp only, and then launches a second instance of WinHelp on the same Help file. Both instances of WinHelp resize their windows so they can appear side-by-side and both display the first topic in the current Help file. Moving through one file (using ctrl+shift commands) updates both windows. The functionality is the same as the Compare macro.

Comment

The Test macro is new for WinHelp version 4.0.

{button ,AL("macro_all;Compare")} Related Topics%!AL("macro_all;Compare")
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Test macro
\par \pard\keepn\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \fs16 Test(\plain\f1\fs16 test-num\b )\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 Runs an internal WinHelp test.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1435\ri125\fi-1305\brdrb\brdrs\tx1435 \b Parameter\tab Description\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1435\ri125\fi-1305\tx1435 test-num\tab Specifies the testing option. This parameter can be one of the following values:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li2515\ri125\fi-1075\brdrb\brdrs\tx2515 \b Value\tab Description\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li2515\ri125\fi-1075\tx2515 1\tab Displays all the topics in the Help file, starting with the current topic.
\par 2\tab Displays all the topics in the Help file, starting with the first topic.
\par 3\tab Continuously displays all the topics in the Help file, starting with the first topic.
\par 4\tab Displays all the topics in the Help file, starting with the first topic, and then quits.
\par 5\tab Jumps to all topics specified in the contents file excluding macros.
\par 6\tab Jumps to all topics specified in the contents file, excluding macros, and then quits.
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li2515\ri125\fi-1075\tx2515 7\tab Turns on Help Mode for the current instance of WinHelp only, and then launches a second instance of WinHelp on the same Help file. Both instances of WinHelp resize their windows so they can appear side-by-side and both display the first topic in the current Help file. Moving through one file (using \scaps ctrl+shift \plain\f1\fs16 commands) updates both windows. The functionality is the same as the Compare macro.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx2515 \b Comment
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx2515 \plain\f1\fs16 The Test macro is new for WinHelp version 4.0.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL("macro_all;Compare")\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL("macro_all;Compare")}
\par
```

- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `KLinks;KLinks, testing;macros,;macros, TestKLink;testing;testing, KLinks;TestKLink macro`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    KLinks;KLinks, testing;macros,;macros, TestKLink;testing;testing, KLinks;TestKLink macro
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
TestKLink macro

TestKLink("keyword[; keyword]")

Tests whether a KLink macro has an effective link to at least one topic.

Parameter	Description

keyword	Specifies one or more K-keywords to search for. Separate multiple keywords using semicolons. If a keyword contains a comma, enclose the entire keyword string in quotation marks.

Comments

The TestKLink macro is typically used as the first parameter in an IfThen or IfThenElse macro.

Help Workshop converts this macro into a KLink macro with a TEST parameter.

Example

The following macro enables or disables a SeeAlso button, depending on whether at least one topic contains a “print” keyword:

	IfThenElse(TestKLink(print), ChangeEnable(btn_seealso, KLink(print)), DisableButton(btn_seealso))

{button ,AL("macro_all;KLink;TestALink")} Related Topics%!AL("macro_all;KLink;TestALink")
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 TestKLink macro
\par \pard\keepn\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \fs16 TestKLink(\plain\f1\fs16 "keyword[\b ;\'a0\plain\f1\fs16 keyword]"\b )\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 Tests whether a KLink macro has an effective link to at least one topic.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1435\ri125\fi-1305\brdrb\brdrs\tx1435 \b Parameter\tab Description\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1435\ri125\fi-1305\tx1575 keyword\tab Specifies one or more K-keywords to search for. Separate multiple keywords using semicolons. If a keyword contains a comma, enclose the entire keyword string in quotation marks.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1575 \b Comments
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1575\ri125\fi-1445\tx1575 \plain\f1\fs16 The TestKLink macro is typically used as the first parameter in an IfThen or IfThenElse macro.
\par Help Workshop converts this macro into a KLink macro with a TEST parameter.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1575 \b Example
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1575 \plain\f1\fs16 The following macro enables or disables a SeeAlso button, depending on whether at least one topic contains a \plain\f0\fs16 \'93\f1 print\plain\f0\fs16 \'94\f1  keyword:
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \tab IfThenElse(TestKLink(print), ChangeEnable(btn_seealso, KLink(print)), DisableButton(btn_seealso))
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL("macro_all;KLink;TestALink")\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL("macro_all;KLink;TestALink")}
\par
```

- Marker `K`

  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    macros,;macros, UpdateWindow;UpdateWindow macro;UW macro
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
UpdateWindow macro

UpdateWindow([filename>]window-name, topic-ID)

Jumps to the topic with the specified topic ID in the specified window, and then returns the focus to the window that called the macro.

Parameter	Description

filename	Specifies the name of the Help file to jump to, if it is not the current Help file. If specified, this parameter must be followed by a greater than sign (>).

window-name	Specifies the window type to display the topic in.

topic-ID	Specifies the ID of the topic to jump to.

Comments

If the secondary window was not already created, the UpdateWindow macro is ignored.

This macro is usually used in an entry macro2BTJW15 to update the secondary window whenever the topic is shown.

Help Workshop automatically converts the UpdateWindow macro to JW, which is the only form of this macro that WinHelp can use.

The UpdateWindow macro is new for WinHelp version 4.0.

{button ,AL("MACRO_REF_LINK;macro_all;CloseSecondarys;CloseWindow;PositionWindow;JumpId;FocusWindow")} Related Topics%!AL("MACRO_REF_LINK;macro_all;CloseSecondarys;CloseWindow;PositionWindow;JumpId;FocusWindow")
```

```rtf
\b\fs24 UpdateWindow macro
\par \pard\keepn\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \fs16 UpdateWindow(\plain\f1\fs16 [filename\b >\plain\f1\fs16 ]window-name\b ,\plain\f1\fs16 \'a0topic-ID\b )\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 Jumps to the topic with the specified topic ID in the specified window, and then returns the focus to the window that called the macro.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1625\ri125\fi-1495\brdrb\brdrs\tx1615 \b Parameter\tab Description\plain\f1\fs16

\par window-name\tab Specifies the window type to display the topic in.
\par topic-ID\tab Specifies the ID of the topic to jump to.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1615 \b Comments
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1615 \plain\f1\fs16 If the secondary window was not already created, the UpdateWindow macro is ignored.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1615 This macro is usually used in an \ul entry macro\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 2BTJW15} to update the secondary window whenever the topic is shown.
```

  - Raw:
    ```rtf

    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
:Base command

:Base HLP-filename[>window-name]

When you specify a default filename in your contents (.cnt) file , Help Workshop adds a :base command to the file.

WinHelp searches the default Help file for all topics specified in the contents file, unless another filename is specified in the syntax for a topic.

If a :base command is specified in an included contents file, the :base command will apply only to the topic jumps in the included file.

{button ,AL(`BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;CNT_REF')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;CNT_REF')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf

\b\fs24 :Base command
\par \pard\keepn\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \fs16 :Base \plain\f1\fs16 HLP-filename[\b >\plain\f1\fs16 window-name]
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 When you specify a default filename in your contents (.cnt) file , Help Workshop adds a :base command to the file.
\par WinHelp searches the default Help file for all topics specified in the contents file, unless another filename is specified in the syntax for a topic.
\par If a :base command is specified in an included contents file, the :base command will apply only to the topic jumps in the included file.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295
```


### Decoded Body

```text
:Include command

:Include cnt-filename

When you click the Include radio button in the Edit Contents Tab Entry dialog box, and then specify a filename, Help Workshop adds an :Include command to your contents (.cnt) file.

If WinHelp cannot find the file specified by the :Include command when the user opens the Help file, no error message appears. If WinHelp finds the specified file, the topics in the included contents file are added to the Contents tab at the position where the :Include command exists in the original contents file.

{button ,AL(`BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;CNT_REF')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;CNT_REF')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf

\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 When you click the Include radio button in the Edit Contents Tab Entry dialog box, and then specify a filename, Help Workshop adds an :Include command to your contents (.cnt) file.
\par If WinHelp cannot find the file specified by the :Include command when the user opens the Help file, no error message appears. If WinHelp finds the specified file, the topics in the included contents file are added to the Contents tab at the position where the :Include command exists in the original contents file.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295
```

### Decoded Body

```text
:Link command

:Link HLP-filename

When you add a file using the Link Files button, Help Workshop adds a :Link command to your contents (.cnt) file. This command specifies which file(s) will be searched if a user clicks a hotspot that launches the ALink7V8V.U or KLink7V8V.U macro. Keywords in Help files specified by the :Link command do not appear on the Index tab and the unique words in the file do not appear on the Find tab.

{button ,AL(`BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;index_cnt')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;index_cnt')
```

This command specifies which file(s) will be searched if a user clicks a hotspot that launches the \ul ALink\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7V8V.U} or \ul KLink\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7V8V.U} macro. Keywords in Help files specified by the :Link command do not appear on the Index tab and the unique words in the file do not appear on the Find tab.

### Decoded Body

```text
Heading statements

x visible-text

When you click the Add Above or Add Below button, click the Heading radio button, and then type text, Help Workshop adds a heading statement to your contents (.cnt) file. The heading statement specifies the text you want to display next to a book icon on the Contents tab of the Help Topics dialog box.KMK38I

Parameter	Description

x	Specifies an integer that denotes the level of the book. This number determines the indent level of the book when it is displayed on the Contents tab. The number 1 is the first level, 2 is the second level, and so on.

visible-text	Specifies the text that the user sees next to the book icon on the Contents tab.

Comments

Heading statements are containers for other headings and for topics. A heading statement cannot jump to a topic or run a macro.

You can use as many as 1023 characters in a book statement.

{button ,AL(`BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;topic_cnt')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;topic_cnt')
```

```rtf
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 When you click the Add Above or Add Below button, click the Heading radio button, and then type text, Help Workshop adds a heading statement to your contents (.cnt) file. The heading statement specifies the text you want to display next to a book icon on the Contents tab of the \ul Help Topics dialog box.\plain\f1\fs16 {\v KMK38I}
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1575\ri125\fi-1455\brdrb\brdrs\tx1575 \b Parameter\tab Description\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1575\ri125\fi-1445\tx1575 x\tab Specifies an integer that denotes the level of the book. This number determines the indent level of the book when it is displayed on the Contents tab. The number 1 is the first level, 2 is the second level, and so on.
\par visible-text\tab Specifies the text that the user sees next to the book icon on the Contents tab.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1575 \b Comments
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1575 \plain\f1\fs16 Heading statements are containers for other headings and for topics. A heading statement cannot jump to a topic or run a macro.

\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295
```

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `>`

- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `CNT_STMT_REF;topic_cnt`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    CNT_STMT_REF;topic_cnt
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `contents files;contents files, topic statements;topic statements in contents files`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    contents files;contents files, topic statements;topic statements in contents files
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Topic statements

x visible-text = topic-ID[@HLP-filename][>window-name]

When you click the Add Above or Add Below button, click the Topic radio button, and then type a topic title and ID, Help Workshop adds a topic statement to your contents (.cnt) file. The topic statement specifies the text you want to display next to a page icon on the Contents tab of the Help Topics dialog box.KMK38I It also specifies which topic will appear when a user double-clicks the topic icon.

Parameter	Description

x	Specifies an integer that denotes the level of the topic. This number determines the indent level of the topic when it is displayed on the Contents tab. The number 1 is the first level, 2 is the second level, and so on. This number is one higher than the book that contains the topic.

visible-text	Specifies the text that the user sees next to the page icon on the Contents tab.

topic-ID	Specifies the topic ID7.61TY of the topic that will appear when the user selects this item in the Contents tab.

HLP-filename	Specifies the name of a Help file. This parameter must be specified only if the topic exists in a Help file other than the default Help file,2KN4_IS which is specified by the :Base command. If you specify this parameter, it must be preceded by an at (@) sign.

window-name	Specifies the window type in which you want the topic to appear. This parameter must be specified only if you want to display the topic in a window other than the one specified by the :Base command. If you specify this parameter, it must be preceded by a greater-than (>) sign .

Comments

You can use as many as 1023 characters in a topic statement.

You can also specify a macro in a topic statement, using the following syntax:

	x visible-text=!macro

For macro, substitute the macro syntax. (Use colons to separate multiple macros.) When the user selects the topic from the Contents tab, the specified macro runs. Note that when specifying a macro from a contents file, you must use the short name and specify all quotation marks and parameters.

{button ,AL(`BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;book_cnt;base_cnt')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;book_cnt;base_cnt')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Topic statements
\par \pard\keepn\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 x\'a0visible-text = topic-ID[\b @\plain\f1\fs16 HLP-filename][\b >\plain\f1\fs16 window-name]
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 When you click the Add Above or Add Below button, click the Topic radio button, and then type a topic title and ID, Help Workshop adds a topic statement to your contents (.cnt) file. The topic statement specifies the text you want to display next to a page icon on the Contents tab of the \ul Help Topics dialog box.\plain\f1\fs16 {\v KMK38I} It also specifies which topic will appear when a user double-clicks the topic icon.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1575\ri125\fi-1455\brdrb\brdrs\tx1575 \b Parameter\tab Description\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1575\ri125\fi-1445\tx1575 x\tab Specifies an integer that denotes the level of the topic. This number determines the indent level of the topic when it is displayed on the Contents tab. The number 1 is the first level, 2 is the second level, and so on. This number is one higher than the book that contains the topic.
\par visible-text\tab Specifies the text that the user sees next to the page icon on the Contents tab.
\par topic-ID\tab Specifies the \ul topic ID\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7.61TY} of the topic that will appear when the user selects this item in the Contents tab.
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1575\ri125\fi-1445\tx1575 HLP-filename\tab Specifies the name of a Help file. This parameter must be specified only if the topic exists in a Help file other than the \ul default Help file,\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 2KN4_IS} which is specified by the :Base command. If you specify this parameter, it must be preceded by an at (@) sign.
\par window-name\tab Specifies the window type in which you want the topic to appear. This parameter must be specified only if you want to display the topic in a window other than the one specified by the :Base command. If you specify this parameter, it must be preceded by a greater-than (>) sign .
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1575 \b Comments
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1575 \plain\f1\fs16 You can use as many as 1023 characters in a topic statement.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1575 You can also specify a macro in a topic statement, using the following syntax:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx355 \tab x\'a0visible-text\b =!\plain\f1\fs16 macro
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx355 For macro, substitute the macro syntax. (Use colons to separate multiple macros.) When the user selects the topic from the Contents tab, the specified macro runs. Note that when specifying a macro from a contents file, you must use the short name and specify all quotation marks and parameters.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;book_cnt;base_cnt')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;book_cnt;base_cnt')}
```

## 239. Project file section reference

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:00ea`

- Marker `#`

  - Raw:

    ```rtf
    19MZWVG
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:

- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `HPJ_Sections`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    HPJ_Sections
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `project files;project files, about sections in;reference to project file sections;sections in project files`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    project files;project files, about sections in;reference to project file sections;sections in project files
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
project file section reference

The following table describes the sections that can be used in a project file.

Section	Function

[ALIAS]	Aliases one or more topic IDs to a different topic ID.

[BAGGAGE]	Lists files that are to be placed within the Help file. (The Help file contains its own file system.)

[BITMAPS]	Specifies bitmap files. This section is maintained for backwards compatibility only.

[BUILDTAGS]	Specifies build tags. This section is maintained for backwards compatibility only.

[CONFIG]	Specifies author-defined menus and buttons used in the Help file and registers DLLs and DLL functions used as macros within the Help file. This section is required if the Help file uses any of these features.

[CONFIG:name]	Same as the [CONFIG] section, but used for individual window configurations (for name, substitute the name of the window).

[EXCLUDE]	Specifies the build tags that identify the topics that should be excluded from the Help file.

[FILES]	Specifies topic (.rtf) files to be included in the build. This section is required.

[FONTS]	Specifies the fonts in topic files that are to be replaced with different fonts, point sizes, or character sets.

[INCLUDE]	Specifies the build tags that identify the topics that should be included in the Help file.

[MACROS]	Specifies macros to run when a user selects from the index one of the keywords listed in this section.

[MAP]	Associates topic IDs with context numbers (numeric values) that a program can use to invoke the Help file.

[OPTIONS]	Specifies options that control the build process. This section is required.

[WINDOWS]	Defines the characteristics of the main Help window and secondary window types used in the Help file.

Semicolons (;) are used to indicate a comment in the project file, except in the [MACROS] section. In all other sections, Help Workshop ignores all text from the semicolon to the end of the line on which it occurs.

{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;HPJ_REF')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;HPJ_REF')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 project file section reference
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The following table describes the sections that can be used in a project file.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1435\ri125\fi-1305\brdrb\brdrs\tx1435 \b Section\tab Function\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1435\ri125\fi-1305\tx1435 [ALIAS]\tab Aliases one or more topic IDs to a different topic ID.
\par [BAGGAGE]\tab Lists files that are to be placed within the Help file. (The Help file contains its own file system.)
\par [BITMAPS]\tab Specifies bitmap files. This section is maintained for backwards compatibility only.
\par [BUILDTAGS]\tab Specifies build tags. This section is maintained for backwards compatibility only.
\par [CONFIG]\tab Specifies author-defined menus and buttons used in the Help file and registers DLLs and DLL functions used as macros within the Help file. This section is required if the Help file uses any of these features.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1435\ri125\fi-1305\tx1435 [CONFIG:name]\tab Same as the\b  \plain\f1\fs16 [CONFIG] section, but used for individual window configurations (for name, substitute the name of the window).
\par [EXCLUDE]\tab Specifies the build tags that identify the topics that should be excluded from the Help file.
\par [FILES]\tab Specifies topic (.rtf) files to be included in the build. This section is required.
\par [FONTS]\tab Specifies the fonts in topic files that are to be replaced with different fonts, point sizes, or character sets.
\par [INCLUDE]\tab Specifies the build tags that identify the topics that should be included in the Help file.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1435\ri125\fi-1305\tx1435 [MACROS]\tab Specifies macros to run when a user selects from the index one of the keywords listed in this section.
\par [MAP]\tab Associates topic IDs with context numbers (numeric values) that a program can use to invoke the Help file.
\par [OPTIONS]\tab Specifies options that control the build process. This section is required.
\par [WINDOWS]\tab Defines the characteristics of the main Help window and secondary window types used in the Help file.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1435 Semicolons (;) are used to indicate a comment in the project file, except in the [MACROS] section. In all other sections, Help Workshop ignores all text from the semicolon to the end of the line on which it occurs.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;HPJ_REF')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;HPJ_REF')}
\par
```

## 240. [ALIAS] section

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:00eb`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:00eb
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `[ALIAS] section`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    [ALIAS] section
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `C0X909`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    C0X909
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `ALIAS;HPJ_REF`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    ALIAS;HPJ_REF
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `ALIAS section of project files;project files;project files, [ALIAS] section`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    ALIAS section of project files;project files;project files, [ALIAS] section
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
[ALIAS] section

[ALIAS]

context_string=alias-topic_id

The [ALIAS] section in your project (.hpj) file associates one set of topic IDs with an alternate set of topic IDs. Alias strings correspond to the IDs assigned by the #-footnotes in your topic (.rtf) files. If this section is included, it must precede the [MAP] section in the project file.

Parameter	Description

context_string	Specifies the program ID or other topic ID that you want to reassign.

alias-topic_id	Specifies the topic ID that appears in the #-footnote of the topic you want Help to recognize. An alias topic ID has the same form and follows the same conventions as a standard topic ID.

Comments

Because topic IDs must be unique for each topic and cannot be used for any other topic in the Help project, the [ALIAS] section provides a way to reassign topic IDs that are no longer used or are invalid. For example, suppose the program defines a context string for each field in a dialog box, but your Help file only provides one topic for all the fields. You can use the [ALIAS] section to map all the program context strings to your one Help topic. In this way, no matter which field the user has selected in the dialog box, Help will display your Help topic when the user requests context-sensitive Help.

You can also use the [ALIAS] section to combine Help topics without having to recode your files. For example, if you create a topic that replaces the information in three other topics, you could manually search through your files for invalid cross-references to the deleted topics. The easier approach, however, would be to use the [ALIAS] section to assign the name of the new topic to the deleted topics.

You can use alias names in the [MAP] section of the project file. If you do, however, the [ALIAS] section must precede the [MAP] section.

Example

The following example creates several aliases within an [ALIAS] section:

	[ALIAS]

sm_key=key_shrtcuts

cc_key=key_shrtcuts

st_key=key_shrtcuts	;combined into keyboard shortcuts topic

clskey=us_dlog_bxs

maakey=us_dlog_bxs	;covered in using dialog boxes topic

chk_key=dlogprts

drp_key=dlogprts

lst_key=dlogprts

opt_key=dlogprts

tbx_key=dlogprts	;combined into parts of dialog box topic

frmtxt=edittxt

wrptxt=edittxt

seltxt=edittxt	;covered in editing text topic

{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;map')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;map')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 [ALIAS] section
\par \pard\keepn\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \fs16 [ALIAS]\line
\plain\f1\fs16 context_string\b =\plain\f1\fs16 alias-topic_id
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 The [ALIAS] section in your project (.hpj) file associates one set of topic IDs with an alternate set of topic IDs. Alias strings correspond to the IDs assigned by the #-footnotes in your topic (.rtf) files. If this section is included, it must precede the [MAP] section in the project file.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1625\ri125\fi-1495\brdrb\brdrs\tx1615 \b Parameter\tab Description\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1625\ri125\fi-1495\tx1615 context_string\tab Specifies the program ID or other topic ID that you want to reassign.
\par alias-topic_id\tab Specifies the topic ID that appears in the #-footnote of the topic you want Help to recognize. An alias topic ID has the same form and follows the same conventions as a standard topic ID.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1615 \b Comments
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1615 \plain\f1\fs16 Because topic IDs must be unique for each topic and cannot be used for any other topic in the Help project, the [ALIAS] section provides a way to reassign topic IDs that are no longer used or are invalid. For example, suppose the program defines a context string for each field in a dialog box, but your Help file only provides one topic for all the fields. You can use the [ALIAS] section to map all the program context strings to your one Help topic. In this way, no matter which field the user has selected in the dialog box, Help will display your Help topic when the user requests context-sensitive Help.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1615 You can also use the [ALIAS] section to combine Help topics without having to recode your files. For example, if you create a topic that replaces the information in three other topics, you could manually search through your files for invalid cross-references to the deleted topics. The easier approach, however, would be to use the [ALIAS] section to assign the name of the new topic to the deleted topics.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1615 You can use alias names in the [MAP] section of the project file. If you do, however, the [ALIAS] section must precede the [MAP] section.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1615 \b Example
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1615 \plain\f1\fs16 The following example creates several aliases within an [ALIAS] section:
\par \pard\keep\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295\tx2155 \tab [ALIAS]\line
sm_key=key_shrtcuts\line
cc_key=key_shrtcuts\line
st_key=key_shrtcuts\tab ;combined into keyboard shortcuts topic\line
clskey=us_dlog_bxs\line
maakey=us_dlog_bxs\tab ;covered in using dialog boxes topic\line
chk_key=dlogprts\line
drp_key=dlogprts\line
lst_key=dlogprts\line
opt_key=dlogprts\line
tbx_key=dlogprts\tab ;combined into parts of dialog box topic\line
frmtxt=edittxt\line
wrptxt=edittxt\line
seltxt=edittxt\tab ;covered in editing text topic
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;map')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;map')}
\par
```

- Marker `K`

### Decoded Body

```text
[BAGGAGE] section

[BAGGAGE]

filename

The [BAGGAGE] section lists files that Help Workshop stores within the Help file's internal file system. The files may then be read by a Help DLL.

Comments

Help Workshop stores all filenames listed in the [BAGGAGE] section exactly as they are typed. Baggage filenames are case-sensitive. To retrieve a baggage file, Help uses the filename without the path. This means that you must specify the filename exactly as it appears in the [BAGGAGE] section.

To access the data from the Help file's internal file system, WinHelp provides callback functions for DLLs so that the DLL can retrieve the appropriate data file from the Help file's [BAGGAGE] section.

The {-mci} command automatically places its files in the baggage section of the Help file.

{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;ROOT')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;ROOT')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 [BAGGAGE] section
\par \pard\keepn\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \fs16 [BAGGAGE]\line
\plain\f1\fs16 filename
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 The [BAGGAGE] section lists files that Help Workshop stores within the Help file's internal file system. The files may then be read by a Help DLL.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Comments\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 Help Workshop stores all filenames listed in the [BAGGAGE] section exactly as they are typed. Baggage filenames are case-sensitive. To retrieve a baggage file, Help uses the filename without the path. This means that you must specify the filename exactly as it appears in the [BAGGAGE] section.
\par To access the data from the Help file's internal file system, WinHelp provides callback functions for DLLs so that the DLL can retrieve the appropriate data file from the Help file's [BAGGAGE] section.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 The \{\-mci\'7d command automatically places its files in the baggage section of the Help file.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;ROOT')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;ROOT')}
\par
```

  - Raw:

### Decoded Body

```text
BMROOT

BMROOT=pathname

You specify the BMROOT option in the [OPTIONS] section of the project (.hpj) file. BMROOT provides Help Workshop with information about where to find the graphics files specified in the source files.

By default, Help Workshop searches for graphics files in the same folder as the project file, then in the same folder as the .rtf file that specified the graphics, and then in all folders specified by the ROOT option. The BMROOT option is necessary only if graphics files are not in one of these locations.

You can specify any number of BMROOT options.

{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;ROOT;BITMAPS;PRO_HPJ_BITMAP_LOCATION')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;ROOT;BITMAPS;PRO_HPJ_BITMAP_LOCATION')
```

```rtf
\b\fs24 BMROOT
\par \pard\keepn\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \fs16 BMROOT=\plain\f1\fs16 pathname
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 You specify the BMROOT option in the [OPTIONS] section of the project (.hpj) file. BMROOT provides Help Workshop with information about where to find the graphics files specified in the source files.
\par By default, Help Workshop searches for graphics files in the same folder as the project file, then in the same folder as the .rtf file that specified the graphics, and then in all folders specified by the ROOT option. The BMROOT option is necessary only if graphics files are not in one of these locations.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 You can specify any number of BMROOT options.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295

\par
```

## 244. BUILD

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:00ef`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:00ef
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `BUILD`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BUILD
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `C.69DU`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    C.69DU
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `BUILD;HPJ_REF`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BUILD;HPJ_REF
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `BUILD option in OPTIONS section;project files;project files, BUILD option`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BUILD option in OPTIONS section;project files;project files, BUILD option
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
BUILD

BUILD=expression

You specify the BUILD option in the [OPTIONS] section of the project (.hpj) file. You can use the BUILD option to include certain topics in and exclude them from the compiled Help file. The topics you include and exclude must contain the specified build tags. Use this option only if your topic (.rtf) files have build tags.

A topic contains a build tag if it includes a build tag footnote (*). Topics without build tags are always compiled, regardless of the expression in the BUILD option.

Parameter	Description

expression	A logical statement that specifies which topics to include in or exclude from the build. This parameter consists of a combination of build tags (which must be specified in the [BUILDTAGS] section) and the following logical operators.

Operator	Description

~	Help Workshop compiles a topic only if the tag is not present. This operator has the highest precedence; Help Workshop applies it before any other operator.

&	Help Workshop compiles a topic only if it contains both build tags used in the expression. Help Workshop applies this operator only after the ~ operator has been applied.

|	Help Workshop compiles a topic if it has at least one of the build tags used in the expression. This operator has the lowest precedence; Help Workshop applies it only after all other operators have been applied.

Parentheses may be used to override operator precedence. Expressions enclosed within parentheses are always evaluated first.

Comments

The Help Workshop interface does not provide a way to specify the BUILD option in your project file. Instead, it provides a Build Tags tab in the Options dialog box that enables you to specify what build tags to include and exclude. These build tags are placed in the [INCLUDE] and [EXCLUDE] sections respectively. If there is an [INCLUDE] or [EXCLUDE] section in your project file when you compile a file, Help Workshop ignores any BUILD option you may have specified.

To use a BUILD option instead of an [INCLUDE] or [EXCLUDE] section, you must load the project file into a text editor and add the option to the [OPTIONS] section. You must also add a [BUILDTAGS] section specifying all the build tags that you will be using.

Only one BUILD option can be specified per project file.

Help Workshop evaluates all build expressions from left to right, using the specified precedence rules.

Examples

The following examples assume that the [BUILDTAGS] section in the Help project defines the build tags DEMO, MASTER, and TEST_BUILD. Although the following examples show several BUILD options on consecutive lines using these build tags, only one BUILD option would be allowed in the project file.

	BUILD=DEMO      ;Compile all topics that have the DEMO tag

BUILD=DEMO & MASTER      ;Compile all topics with both the DEMO and MASTER tag

BUILD=DEMO | MASTER      ;Compile all topics with either the DEMO or MASTER tag

BUILD=(DEMO | MASTER) & TEST_BUILD      ;Compile all topics that have either the DEMO or MASTER tag and also the TEST_BUILD tag

BUILD=~ MASTER      ;Compile all topics that do not have the MASTER tag

{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;BUILDTAGS;BAS_INSERTING_BUILD_TAG')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;BUILDTAGS;BAS_INSERTING_BUILD_TAG')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 BUILD
\par \pard\keepn\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \fs16 BUILD=\plain\f1\fs16 expression
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 You specify the BUILD option in the [OPTIONS] section of the project (.hpj) file. You can use the BUILD option to include certain topics in and exclude them from the compiled Help file. The topics you include and exclude must contain the specified build tags. Use this option only if your topic (.rtf) files have build tags.
\par A topic contains a build tag if it includes a build tag footnote (*). Topics without build tags are always compiled, regardless of the expression in the BUILD option.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1625\ri125\fi-1495\brdrb\brdrs\tx1615 \b Parameter\tab Description\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1625\ri125\fi-1495\tx1615 expression\tab A logical statement that specifies which topics to include in or exclude from the build. This parameter consists of a combination of build tags (which must be specified in the [BUILDTAGS] section) and the following logical operators.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li2485\ri125\fi-855\brdrb\brdrs\tx2485 \b Operator\tab Description\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li2485\ri125\fi-855\tx2485 ~\tab Help Workshop compiles a topic only if the tag is not present. This operator has the highest precedence; Help Workshop applies it before any other operator.
\par &\tab Help Workshop compiles a topic only if it contains both build tags used in the expression. Help Workshop applies this operator only after the ~ operator has been applied.
\par |\tab Help Workshop compiles a topic if it has at least one of the build tags used in the expression. This operator has the lowest precedence; Help Workshop applies it only after all other operators have been applied.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx2485 Parentheses may be used to override operator precedence. Expressions enclosed within parentheses are always evaluated first.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx2485 \b Comments
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx2485 \plain\f1\fs16 The Help Workshop interface does not provide a way to specify the BUILD option in your project file. Instead, it provides a Build Tags tab in the Options dialog box that enables you to specify what build tags to include and exclude. These build tags are placed in the [INCLUDE] and [EXCLUDE] sections respectively. If there is an [INCLUDE] or [EXCLUDE] section in your project file when you compile a file, Help Workshop ignores any BUILD option you may have specified.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx2485 To use a BUILD option instead of an [INCLUDE] or [EXCLUDE] section, you must load the project file into a text editor and add the option to the [OPTIONS] section. You must also add a [BUILDTAGS] section specifying all the build tags that you will be using.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx2485 Only one BUILD option can be specified per project file.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx2485 Help Workshop evaluates all build expressions from left to right, using the specified precedence rules.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx2485 \b Examples
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx2485 \plain\f1\fs16 The following examples assume that the [BUILDTAGS] section in the Help project defines the build tags DEMO, MASTER, and TEST_BUILD. Although the following examples show several BUILD options on consecutive lines using these build tags, only one BUILD option would be allowed in the project file.
\par \pard\keep\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295\tx3775 \tab BUILD=DEMO      ;Compile all topics that have the DEMO tag\line
BUILD=DEMO & MASTER      ;Compile all topics with both the DEMO and MASTER tag\line
BUILD=DEMO | MASTER      ;Compile all topics with either the DEMO or MASTER tag\line
BUILD=(DEMO | MASTER) & TEST_BUILD      ;Compile all topics that have either the DEMO or MASTER tag and also the TEST_BUILD tag\line
BUILD=~ MASTER      ;Compile all topics that do not have the MASTER tag
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;BUILDTAGS;BAS_INSERTING_BUILD_TAG')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;BUILDTAGS;BAS_INSERTING_BUILD_TAG')}
\par
```

## 245. [BUILDTAGS] section

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:00f0`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:00f0
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `[BUILDTAGS] section`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    [BUILDTAGS] section
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `1K_K596`
  - Raw:

    ```rtf
    1K_K596
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `BUILDTAGS;HPJ_REF`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BUILDTAGS;HPJ_REF
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `BUILDTAGS section of project files;project files;project files, [BUILDTAGS] section`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BUILDTAGS section of project files;project files;project files, [BUILDTAGS] section
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
[BUILDTAGS] section

[BUILDTAGS]

tag

The [BUILDTAGS] section defines the valid build tags for a Help file. Help Workshop uses the build tags to determine which topics to include or exclude when building the Help file.

This section is used in conjunction with the build tag footnote (*) and the BUILD option. The build tag footnote associates a particular build tag with a given topic. If the build tag is included in the [BUILDTAGS] section and defined in the BUILD expression, Help Workshop compiles the topic; otherwise, it excludes the topic from the build.

Parameter	Description

tag	Specifies a build tag consisting of any combination of characters except spaces. Help Workshop strips any spaces it finds between the start of the build tag and the end of the tag. Build tags are case insensitive, so Help Workshop treats uppercase and lowercase characters as the same. Each build tag can have as many as 32 characters.

Comments

The Help Workshop interface does not provide a way to modify the [BUILDTAGS] section in your project file. To create or modify a [BUILDTAGS] section, load the project file into a text editor, and then add or edit the section.

Example

The following example defines four build tags in a sample project file:

	[BUILDTAGS]

DEMO	;topics to include in demo build

MASTER	;topics to include in master Help file

DEBUGBUILD	;topics to include in debugging build

TESTBUILD	;topics to include in a mini-build for testing

{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;BUILD;BAS_INSERTING_BUILD_TAG')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;BUILD;BAS_INSERTING_BUILD_TAG')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 [BUILDTAGS] section
\par \pard\keepn\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \fs16 [BUILDTAGS]\line
\plain\f1\fs16 tag
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 The [BUILDTAGS] section defines the valid build tags for a Help file. Help Workshop uses the build tags to determine which topics to include or exclude when building the Help file.
\par This section is used in conjunction with the build tag footnote (*) and the BUILD option. The build tag footnote associates a particular build tag with a given topic. If the build tag is included in the [BUILDTAGS] section and defined in the BUILD expression, Help Workshop compiles the topic; otherwise, it excludes the topic from the build.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1625\ri125\fi-1495\brdrb\brdrs\tx1615 \b Parameter\tab Description\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1625\ri125\fi-1495\tx1615 tag\tab Specifies a build tag consisting of any combination of characters except spaces. Help Workshop strips any spaces it finds between the start of the build tag and the end of the tag. Build tags are case insensitive, so Help Workshop treats uppercase and lowercase characters as the same. Each build tag can have as many as 32 characters.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1615 \b Comments
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1615 \plain\f1\fs16 The Help Workshop interface does not provide a way to modify the [BUILDTAGS] section in your project file. To create or modify a [BUILDTAGS] section, load the project file into a text editor, and then add or edit the section.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1615 \b Example
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1615 \plain\f1\fs16 The following example defines four build tags in a sample project file:
\par \pard\keep\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295\tx1795 \tab [BUILDTAGS]\line
DEMO\tab ;topics to include in demo build\line
MASTER\tab ;topics to include in master Help file\line
DEBUGBUILD\tab ;topics to include in debugging build\line
TESTBUILD\tab ;topics to include in a mini-build for testing
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;BUILD;BAS_INSERTING_BUILD_TAG')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;BUILD;BAS_INSERTING_BUILD_TAG')}
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
CHARSET

CHARSET=charset-value

You specify the CHARSET option in the [OPTIONS] section of the project (.hpj) file. CHARSET specifies the default character set to use for the fonts in the Help file.

If your RTF writer specifies a character set, it takes precedence over the CHARSET value. Microsoft Word for Windows versions 6.0 and later specify a character set. Earlier versions of Microsoft Word do not.

The CHARSET option is new.

{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;DEFFONT')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;DEFFONT')
```

### Raw RTF Body


### Decoded Body

```text
CITATION

CITATION=text

You specify the CITATION option in the [OPTIONS] section of the project (.hpj) file. CITATION appends a citation to the end of any information (except in a context-sensitive pop-up window) that is copied or printed from the Help file. This option is commonly used to attach a copyright notice to any information in your Help file that is copied or printed. You can specify up to 2,000 characters (1,000 for DBCS languages) for the citation text.

Note

·	The COPYRIGHT option also displays copyright text in WinHelp's Version dialog box.

{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;COPYRIGHT')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;COPYRIGHT')
```

CITATION appends a citation to the end of any information (except in a context-sensitive pop-up window) that is copied or printed from the Help file. This option is commonly used to attach a copyright notice to any information in your Help file that is copied or printed. You can specify up to 2,000 characters (1,000 for DBCS languages) for the citation text.
```rtf
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Note
```


### Decoded Body

```text
CNT

CNT=contents-file

You specify the CNT option in the [OPTIONS] section of the project (.hpj) file. CNT identifies the name of the contents (.cnt) file to associate with the Help file.

If the [OPTIONS] section does not include a CNT option, WinHelp looks for a contents file with the same filename as the Help file (and the .cnt filename extension). If neither of these files is found, WinHelp displays the topic specified by the topic ID in the CONTENTS option.

The CNT option is new.

{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;CONTENTS_hpj')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;CONTENTS_hpj')
```

```rtf
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 You specify the CNT option in the [OPTIONS] section of the project (.hpj) file. CNT identifies the name of the contents (.cnt) file to associate with the Help file.
\par If the [OPTIONS] section does not include a CNT option, WinHelp looks for a contents file with the same filename as the Help file (and the .cnt filename extension). If neither of these files is found, WinHelp displays the topic specified by the topic ID in the CONTENTS option.
\par The CNT option is new.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295
```

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `COMPRESS option in OPTIONS section;project files;project files, COMPRESS option`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    COMPRESS option in OPTIONS section;project files;project files, COMPRESS option
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
COMPRESS

COMPRESS=compression-level

You specify the COMPRESS option in the [OPTIONS] section of the project (.hpj) file. COMPRESS specifies the type of compression Help Workshop should use when compiling the Help file.

Parameter	Description

compression-level	Specifies the level of compression. The default value is 0.

Hex Value	Meaning

0x00	Help Workshop does not compress the Help file.

0x01	Help Workshop attempts to determine the best possible compression for the current Help file.

0x02	Help Workshop uses Phrase compression on text.

0x04	Help Workshop uses Hall compression on text.

0x08	Help Workshop uses Zeck compression on text.

0x10	Help Workshop uses RLE compression on bitmaps.

0x20	Help Workshop uses Zeck compression on bitmaps.

Comments

If you specify maximum compression using Help Workshop, it will write 0x12 as the value for the COMPRESS option in your project file.

If you specify phrase compression, Help Workshop creates a phrase-table file that has a .ph filename extension. If Help Workshop finds an existing phrase file, it deletes the file, and then creates a new one. If you select the Use Existing Phrase (.Ph) File check box on the Compression tab in the Options dialog box, Help Workshop uses your existing phrase file. Using an existing phrase file reduces compile time, but compression is less effective.

Hall compression uses the Ftsrch.dll file, which is included with Windows 95 and with Windows NT version 3.51.

If the COMPRESS option is set to 1, HIGH, or YES, Help Workshop chooses between Hall and Phrase compression based on the size of your topic (.rtf) files. If the total size of the topic files is greater than 1 MB, Help Workshop uses Hall compression.

{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;OLDKEYPHRASE;BAS_COMPRESSION')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;OLDKEYPHRASE;BAS_COMPRESSION')
```

```rtf
\b\fs24 COMPRESS
\par \pard\keepn\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \fs16 COMPRESS=\plain\f1\fs16 compression-level
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 You specify the COMPRESS option in the [OPTIONS] section of the project (.hpj) file. COMPRESS specifies the type of compression Help Workshop should use when compiling the Help file.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1985\ri125\fi-1855\brdrb\brdrs\tx1975 \b Parameter\tab Description\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1985\ri125\fi-1855\tx1975 compression-level\tab Specifies the level of compression. The default value is 0.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li3065\ri125\fi-1085\brdrb\brdrs\tx3055 \b Hex Value\tab Meaning\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li3065\ri125\fi-1085\tx3055 0x00\tab Help Workshop does not compress the Help file.
\par 0x01\tab Help Workshop attempts to determine the best possible compression for the current Help file.
\par 0x02\tab Help Workshop uses Phrase compression on text.
\par 0x04\tab Help Workshop uses Hall compression on text.
\par 0x08\tab Help Workshop uses Zeck compression on text.
\par 0x10\tab Help Workshop uses RLE compression on bitmaps.
\par 0x20\tab Help Workshop uses Zeck compression on bitmaps.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx3055 \b Comments
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx3055 \plain\f1\fs16 If you specify maximum compression using Help Workshop, it will write 0x12 as the value for the COMPRESS option in your project file.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx3055 If you specify phrase compression, Help Workshop creates a phrase-table file that has a .ph filename extension. If Help Workshop finds an existing phrase file, it deletes the file, and then creates a new one. If you select the Use Existing Phrase (.Ph) File check box on the Compression tab in the Options dialog box, Help Workshop uses your existing phrase file. Using an existing phrase file reduces compile time, but compression is less effective.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx3055 Hall compression uses the Ftsrch.dll file, which is included with Windows\'a095 and with Windows\'a0NT version 3.51.

\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295
```

- Marker `K`

    ```rtf
    CONFIG section of project files;project files;project files, [CONFIG] section
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
[CONFIG] section

[CONFIG]

macro

The [CONFIG] section contains one or more WinHelp macros that are run when WinHelp opens the Help file. This section can contain macros that carry out actions, such as creating buttons or menus, and macros that register routines in external DLLs as WinHelp macros. These routines can then be used the same as WinHelp macros.

Comments

When listing macros in the [CONFIG] section, include only one macro per line.

When a Help file opens, WinHelp runs the macros listed in the [CONFIG] section in the order in which they are specified.

Example

The following example registers two DLLs, creates a button, and sets the name of the How To Use Help file:

	[CONFIG]

RegisterRoutine(bmp, CopyBmp, S)

CreateButton(btn_copyart, Copy Art, CopyBmp(`myart.bmp'))

{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;MACRO_OVERVIEW;config_x')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;MACRO_OVERVIEW;config_x')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 [CONFIG] section
\par \pard\keepn\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \fs16 [CONFIG]\line
\plain\f1\fs16 macro
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 The [CONFIG] section contains one or more WinHelp macros that are run when WinHelp opens the Help file. This section can contain macros that carry out actions, such as creating buttons or menus, and macros that register routines in external DLLs as WinHelp macros. These routines can then be used the same as WinHelp macros.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Comments
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 When listing macros in the [CONFIG] section, include only one macro per line. 
\par When a Help file opens, WinHelp runs the macros listed in the [CONFIG] section in the order in which they are specified.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Example
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The following example registers two DLLs, creates a button, and sets the name of the How To Use Help file:
\par \pard\keep\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \tab [CONFIG]\line
RegisterRoutine(bmp, CopyBmp, S)\line
CreateButton(btn_copyart, Copy Art, CopyBmp(`myart.bmp'))
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;MACRO_OVERVIEW;config_x')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;MACRO_OVERVIEW;config_x')}
\par
```

```text
macro

The [CONFIG:name] section contains one or more macros that are run when WinHelp opens the corresponding window.

Comments

When listing macros in the [CONFIG:name] section, include only one macro per line.

When a Help file opens, WinHelp runs the macros listed in the [CONFIG:name] section in the order in which they are specified.

The [CONFIG:name] section is new.

Example

The following example closes all open secondary windows when the proc4 window is opened.

	[CONFIG:proc4]

CloseSecondarys()
```

```text
DBCS

DBCS=value

You specify the DBCS option in the [OPTIONS] section of the project (.hpj) file. DBCS specifies whether the files that will be compiled use a double-byte character set (DBCS).

Comments

Help Workshop recognizes certain language IDs (LCID option) and forces the DBCS option to a particular setting, no matter how you specify it in the project file. The following language IDs force DBCS=YES.

IdentifierLocaleIdentifierLocale
0x0411Japanese0x1004Singapore
0x0404Taiwan0x0C04Hong Kong
```

## 255. DEFFONT

    ```rtf
    default fonts;DEFFONT option in OPTIONS section;fonts,;fonts, default;project files;project files, DEFFONT option
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
DEFFONT

DEFFONT=fontname, font-size, charset

You specify the DEFFONT option in the [OPTIONS] section of the project (.hpj) file. DEFFONT specifies the default font for the lists on the Contents, Index and Find tabs and in the Topics Found dialog box.

Parameter	Description

fontname	Specifies the name of the font to use.

font-size	Specifies the point size of the font to use.

charset	Specifies the character set (CHARSET) to use.

Comment

The DEFFONT option is new.

{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;CHARSET')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;CHARSET')
```

## 256. ERRORLOG

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:00fb`



- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `ERRORLOG option in OPTIONS section;project files;project files, ERRORLOG option`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    ERRORLOG option in OPTIONS section;project files;project files, ERRORLOG option
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
ERRORLOG

ERRORLOG=error-filename

You specify the ERRORLOG option in the [OPTIONS] section of the project (.hpj) file. ERRORLOG directs Help Workshop to write all messages generated during the compilation of a Help file to a log file. As it compiles, Help Workshop also displays messages on the screen.

Note

·	Help Workshop can display only 64K of information when it runs with Windows 95, and up to 1 MB of information when it runs with Windows NT. However, there is no limit to the amount of information that Help Workshop can write to a log file.

{button ,AL(`BAS_COMPILE_MSG;HPJ_SECTIONS;REPORT;NOTES')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_COMPILE_MSG;HPJ_SECTIONS;REPORT;NOTES')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 ERRORLOG
\par \pard\keepn\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \fs16 ERRORLOG=\plain\f1\fs16 error-filename
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 You specify the ERRORLOG option in the [OPTIONS] section of the project (.hpj) file. ERRORLOG directs Help Workshop to write all messages generated during the compilation of a Help file to a log file. As it compiles, Help Workshop also displays messages on the screen.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Note
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\tab \f1 Help Workshop can display only 64K of information when it runs with Windows\'a095, and up to 1\'a0MB of information when it runs with Windows\'a0NT. However, there is no limit to the amount of information that Help Workshop can write to a log file.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`BAS_COMPILE_MSG;HPJ_SECTIONS;REPORT;NOTES')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BAS_COMPILE_MSG;HPJ_SECTIONS;REPORT;NOTES')}
\par
```

## 257. [FILES] section

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:00fc`

    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:00fc
    ```
- Marker `$`

    ```rtf
    [FILES] section
    ```

    ```rtf
    379Z0EN
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `FILES;HPJ_REF`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    FILES;HPJ_REF
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `FILES section of project files;project files;project files, [FILES] section`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    FILES section of project files;project files;project files, [FILES] section
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
[FILES] section

[FILES]

filename

The [FILES] section lists all topic (.rtf) files used to create the Help file. A Help project (.hpj) file must have a [FILES] section.

Comments

You can create an ASCII text file that contains a list of the topic files you want to include in a build, and then use the #include statement in the [FILES] section to specify the ASCII file.

The #include statement has the following syntax:

	#include filename

The filename must reside in the Help project folder, or must specify a complete path. Help Workshop does not use the INCLUDE environment variable to search for files.

Help Workshop searches for files in the project folder, in the path (if any) specified for the file, and in all folders specified by ROOT options in the [OPTIONS] section of the project file.

{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;ROOT')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;ROOT')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 [FILES] section
\par \pard\keepn\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \fs16 [FILES]\line
\plain\f1\fs16 filename
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 The [FILES] section lists all topic (.rtf) files used to create the Help file. A Help project (.hpj) file must have a [FILES] section.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Comments
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 You can create an ASCII text file that contains a list of the topic files you want to include in a build, and then use the #include statement in the [FILES] section to specify the ASCII file.
\par The #include statement has the following syntax:
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \b \tab #include \plain\f1\fs16 filename
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 The filename must reside in the Help project folder, or must specify a complete path. Help Workshop does not use the INCLUDE environment variable to search for files.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 Help Workshop searches for files in the project folder, in the path (if any) specified for the file, and in all folders specified by ROOT options in the [OPTIONS] section of the project file.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;ROOT')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;ROOT')}
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
FORCEFONT

FORCEFONT=fontname

You specify the FORCEFONT option in the [OPTIONS] section of the project (.hpj) file. FORCEFONT causes Help Workshop to substitute the specified font for any font used in your topic (.rtf) files.

Comments

The fontname can be any of the following fonts:

·	Arial

·	Athens

·	Bookman

·	Courier

·	Courier New

·	Geneva

·	Helv

·	Helvetica

·	London

·	Modern

·	Monaco

·	MS Sans Serif

·	MS Serif

·	Roman

·	Times

·	Times New Roman

·	Tms Rmn

{button ,AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;MAPFONTSIZE')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;HPJ_SECTIONS;MAPFONTSIZE')
```

```rtf
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 You specify the FORCEFONT option in the [OPTIONS] section of the project (.hpj) file. FORCEFONT causes Help Workshop to substitute the specified font for any font used in your topic (.rtf) files.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Comments
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The fontname can be any of the following fonts:

\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Courier

\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Geneva

\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Helvetica
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab London
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Modern
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Monaco
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab MS Sans Serif
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab MS Serif
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Roman
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Times
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Times New Roman
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Tms Rmn
```

## 259. FTS

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:00fe`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:00fe
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `FTS`
  - Raw:

    ```rtf
    FTS
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `RB72W`
  - Raw:
