## 354. Notifying DLLs of WinHelp events

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:00dd`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:00dd
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Notifying DLLs of WinHelp events`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Notifying DLLs of WinHelp events
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `DLL_WRITE_WINHELP_EVENTS`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DLL_WRITE_WINHELP_EVENTS
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `DLL_WRITE_WINHELP_EVENTS;HELPDLL`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DLL_WRITE_WINHELP_EVENTS;HELPDLL
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `DLLs;DLLs, notifying of WinHelp events`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DLLs;DLLs, notifying of WinHelp events
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Notifying DLLs of WinHelp Events

You might want your DLL to receive notification of WinHelp events (for example, when the user selects a jump or changes input focus to a program other than WinHelp). To do this, you add a LDLLHandler function to the DLL. The LDLLHandler function has the job of processing messages sent from WinHelp to the DLL.

When WinHelp loads a custom DLL, it looks for a function named LDLLHandler. If WinHelp finds LDLLHandler, it calls LDLLHandler.

The LDLLHandler function is illustrated in the following example:

PUBLIC     LONG PASCAL EXPORT LDLLHandler(

     WORD     wMsg,

     LONG     lParam1,

     LONG     lParam2)

     {-

          switch(wMsg) {-

               case DW_WHATMSG:

                    return DC_INITTERM | DC_JUMP;

               case DW_INIT:

                    return TRUE;

               case DW_TERM:

                    return TRUE;

               case DW_ACTIVATE:

                    return TRUE;

               case DW_CHGFILE:

                    return TRUE;

     }

     return FALSE;}

In this definition, wMsg identifies the message WinHelp has sent to the DLL. The two LONG parameters are passed with the message and depend on the message type.

When the user first performs an action that requires the custom DLL, WinHelp calls LDLLHandler with the wMsg parameter set to DW_WHATMSG. This message asks the DLL what types of WinHelp messages it wants to receive. LDLLHandler should return one or more of the flags in the following table with this information. WinHelp uses the flags returned by LDLLHandler to determine which messages the DLL wants to receive in the LDLLHandler function.

LDLLHandler

returns	WinHelp sends	Description

DC_MINMAX*DC_MINMAX	DW_MINMAX, DW_SIZE	Minimize, maximize, or resize WinHelp

DC_INITTERM*DC_INITTERM	DW_INIT, DW_TERM	Initialize or terminate DLL

DC_JUMP*DC_JUMP	DW_STARTJUMP, 	Jump or change .HLP file

	DW_ENDJUMP,

	DW_CHGFILE

DC_ACTIVATE*DC_ACTIVATE	DW_ACTIVATE	Give WinHelp or another program input focus

DC_CALLBACKS*DLL_WRITE_ACCESS_WH_FUNCTIONS	DW_CALLBACKS	Give DLL access to WinHelp entry points

These flags are combined using the standard C bitwise-OR (|) operator. For example, many DLLs request notification when WinHelp is about to initialize or terminate them. The sample LDLLHandler function above requests this notification and asks to be notified when the user has moved the input focus to or from WinHelp. It processes the DW_WHATMSG message as follows:

case DW_WHATMSG:

     return DC_INITTERM | DC_ACTIVATE;

{button ,AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW')} Related Topics%!AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Notifying DLLs of WinHelp Events
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 You might want your DLL to receive notification of WinHelp events (for example, when the user selects a jump or changes input focus to a program other than WinHelp). To do this, you add a \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  function to the DLL. The \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  function has the job of processing messages sent from WinHelp to the DLL.
\par When WinHelp loads a custom DLL, it looks for a function named \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16 . If WinHelp finds \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16 , it calls \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16 .
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 The \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  function is illustrated in the following example:
\par \pard\keep\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 PUBLIC     LONG PASCAL EXPORT LDLLHandler(\line
     WORD     wMsg,\line
     LONG     lParam1,\line
     LONG     lParam2)\line
     \{\-\line
          switch(wMsg) \{\-\line
               case DW_WHATMSG:\line
                    return DC_INITTERM | DC_JUMP;\line
               case DW_INIT:\line
                    return TRUE;\line
               case DW_TERM:\line
                    return TRUE;\line
               case DW_ACTIVATE:\line
                    return TRUE;\line
               case DW_CHGFILE:\line
                    return TRUE;\line
     \'7d\line
     return FALSE;\'7d
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 In this definition, wMsg identifies the message WinHelp has sent to the DLL. The two LONG parameters are passed with the message and depend on the message type.
\par When the user first performs an action that requires the custom DLL, WinHelp calls \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  with the wMsg parameter set to DW_WHATMSG. This message asks the DLL what types of WinHelp messages it wants to receive. \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  should return one or more of the flags in the following table with this information. WinHelp uses the flags returned by \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  to determine which messages the DLL wants to receive in the \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  function.
\par \pard\sl-235\li125\ri125\brdrb\brdrs\tx1705\tx3955 LDLLHandler\b \line
returns\tab WinHelp sends\tab Description
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li3955\ri125\fi-3825\tx1705\tx3955 \uldb \plain\f1\uldb\fs16 DC_MINMAX\plain\f1\ul\fs16 {\v *DC_MINMAX}\plain\f1\fs16 \tab DW_MINMAX, DW_SIZE\tab Minimize, maximize, or resize WinHelp
\par \uldb DC_INITTERM\plain\f1\ul\fs16 {\v *DC_INITTERM}\plain\f1\fs16 \tab DW_INIT, DW_TERM\tab Initialize or terminate DLL
\par \uldb DC_JUMP\plain\f1\ul\fs16 {\v *DC_JUMP}\plain\f1\fs16 \tab DW_STARTJUMP, \tab Jump or change .HLP file
\par \pard\sl-235\li3955\ri125\fi-3825\tx1705\tx3955 \tab DW_ENDJUMP, 
\par \tab DW_CHGFILE
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li3955\ri125\fi-3825\tx1705\tx3955 \uldb DC_ACTIVATE\plain\f1\ul\fs16 {\v *DC_ACTIVATE}\plain\f1\fs16 \tab DW_ACTIVATE\tab Give WinHelp or another program input focus
\par \uldb DC_CALLBACKS\plain\f1\ul\fs16 {\v *DLL_WRITE_ACCESS_WH_FUNCTIONS}\plain\f1\fs16 \tab DW_CALLBACKS\tab Give DLL access to WinHelp entry points
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1705\tx3955 These flags are combined using the standard C bitwise-OR (\b |\plain\f1\fs16 ) operator. For example, many DLLs request notification when WinHelp is about to initialize or terminate them. The sample \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  function above requests this notification and asks to be notified when the user has moved the input focus to or from WinHelp. It processes the DW_WHATMSG message as follows:
\par \pard\keep\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1705\tx3955 case DW_WHATMSG: \line
     return DC_INITTERM | DC_ACTIVATE;
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW')}
\par
```

## 355. DC_INITTERM flag

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `DC_INITTERM flag`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DC_INITTERM flag
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `DC_INITTERM`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DC_INITTERM
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `DC_INITTERM flag;DW_INIT;DW_TERM;flags;flags, DC_INITTERM`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DC_INITTERM flag;DW_INIT;DW_TERM;flags;flags, DC_INITTERM
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
DC_INITTERM Flag

The DC_INITTERM flag tells WinHelp that the DLL should receive initialization and termination messages.

{bmc bm1.bmp}	DW_INIT

After LDLLHandler returns a DC_INITTERM flag, WinHelp sends a DW_INIT message. This message tells LDLLHandler to perform any required initialization operations (such as initializing variables or loading strings). The lParam1 and lParam2 parameters are not used.

If LDLLHandler returns FALSE, WinHelp unloads the DLL and stops sending messages. If LDLLHandler returns TRUE, the DLL remains loaded.

{bmc bm1.bmp}	DW_TERM

When the user ends a WinHelp session, WinHelp sends a DW_TERM message. This message tells LDLLHandler to perform any required cleanup operations before the DLL is unloaded from memory. The lParam1 and lParam2 parameters are not used, and the return value has no meaning.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 
\b\fs24 DC_INITTERM Flag
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The DC_INITTERM flag tells WinHelp that the DLL should receive initialization and termination messages.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{bmc bm1.bmp\}\tab DW_INIT
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li295\ri125\tx295 After \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  returns a DC_INITTERM flag, WinHelp sends a DW_INIT message. This message tells \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  to perform any required initialization operations (such as initializing variables or loading strings). The lParam1 and lParam2 parameters are not used.
\par If \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  returns FALSE, WinHelp unloads the DLL and stops sending messages. If \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  returns TRUE, the DLL remains loaded.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{bmc bm1.bmp\}\tab DW_TERM
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li295\ri125\tx295 When the user ends a WinHelp session, WinHelp sends a DW_TERM message. This message tells \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  to perform any required cleanup operations before the DLL is unloaded from memory. The lParam1 and lParam2 parameters are not used, and the return value has no meaning.
\par
```

## 356. DC_MINMAX flag

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `DC_MINMAX flag`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DC_MINMAX flag
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `DC_MINMAX`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DC_MINMAX
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `DC_MINMAX flag;DW_MINMAX;DW_SIZE;flags;flags, DC_MINMAX`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DC_MINMAX flag;DW_MINMAX;DW_SIZE;flags;flags, DC_MINMAX
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
DC_MINMAX Flag

The DC_MINMAX flag tells WinHelp that the DLL should receive messages when the user minimizes, maximizes, or resizes the WinHelp window.

{bmc bm1.bmp}	DW_MINMAX

WinHelp sends the DLL a DW_MINMAX message if the user minimizes or maximizes the context window.

The lParam1 parameter to DW_MINMAX indicates which operation was performed: 1L for minimized or 2L for maximized.

The lParam2 parameter is not used.

If LDLLHandler returns TRUE for this message, WinHelp continues sending this message throughout the remainder of the session; if LDLLHandler returns FALSE, WinHelp stops sending it.

{bmc bm1.bmp}	DW_SIZE

WinHelp sends the DLL a DW_SIZE message if the user resizes the context window.

The lParam1 message specifies the horizontal size of the window in the low-order word and the vertical size in the high-order word.

The lParam2 parameter specifies the handle of the current window.

If LDLLHandler returns TRUE for this message, WinHelp continues sending this message throughout the remainder of the session; if LDLLHandler returns FALSE, WinHelp stops sending it.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 
\b\fs24 DC_MINMAX Flag
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The DC_MINMAX flag tells WinHelp that the DLL should receive messages when the user minimizes, maximizes, or resizes the WinHelp window.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{bmc bm1.bmp\}\tab DW_MINMAX
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li295\ri125\tx295 WinHelp sends the DLL a DW_MINMAX message if the user minimizes or maximizes the context window.
\par The lParam1 parameter to DW_MINMAX indicates which operation was performed: 1L for minimized or 2L for maximized.
\par The lParam2 parameter is not used.
\par If \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  returns TRUE for this message, WinHelp continues sending this message throughout the remainder of the session; if \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  returns FALSE, WinHelp stops sending it.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{bmc bm1.bmp\}\tab DW_SIZE
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li295\ri125\tx295 WinHelp sends the DLL a DW_SIZE message if the user resizes the context window.
\par The lParam1 message specifies the horizontal size of the window in the low-order word and the vertical size in the high-order word.
\par The lParam2 parameter specifies the handle of the current window.
\par If \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  returns TRUE for this message, WinHelp continues sending this message throughout the remainder of the session; if \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  returns FALSE, WinHelp stops sending it.
\par
```

## 357. DC_JUMP flag

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `DC_JUMP flag`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DC_JUMP flag
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `DC_JUMP`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DC_JUMP
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `DC_JUMP flag;DW_CHGFILE;DW_ENDJUMP;DW_STARTJUMP;flags;flags, DC_JUMP`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DC_JUMP flag;DW_CHGFILE;DW_ENDJUMP;DW_STARTJUMP;flags;flags, DC_JUMP
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
DC_JUMP Flag

The DC_JUMP flag tells WinHelp that the DLL should receive messages when the user runs a jump in the Help file.

{bmc bm1.bmp}	DW_STARTJUMP

WinHelp sends the DLL a DW_STARTJUMP message after the user runs a jump.

The lParam1 parameter to DW_STARTJUMP is not used. The lParam2 parameter specifies the handle of the current parent window.

If LDLLHandler returns TRUE for this message, WinHelp continues sending this message throughout the remainder of the session; if LDLLHandler returns FALSE, WinHelp stops sending it.

{bmc bm1.bmp}	DW_ENDJUMP

WinHelp sends the DLL a DW_ENDJUMP message after it displays the jump-destination topic.

The lParam1 parameter specifies the byte offset of that topic within the Help file's file system. This value can be passed to the LSeekHf function (defined in the DLL.H file) to perform a seek to that topic within the file.

The lParam2 parameter specifies the position of the scroll box in the topic. This value can be used in any of the standard Windows scrolling functions that accept scroll-position parameters.

If LDLLHandler returns TRUE for this message, WinHelp continues sending this message throughout the remainder of the session; if LDLLHandler returns FALSE, WinHelp stops sending it.

{bmc bm1.bmp}	DW_CHGFILE

WinHelp sends the DLL a DW_CHGFILE message if the user selects Open from the File menu to change .HLP files or jumps to a topic in a different .HLP file. If the message is the result of a jump to a new file, WinHelp sends the DW_CHGFILE message between the DW_STARTJUMP and DW_ENDJUMP messages.

The lParam1 parameter specifies a long pointer to the .HLP filename. The lParam2 parameter is not used.

If LDLLHandler returns TRUE for this message, WinHelp continues sending this message throughout the remainder of the session; if LDLLHandler returns FALSE, WinHelp stops sending it.
```

```rtf
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{bmc bm1.bmp\}\tab DW_STARTJUMP
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li295\ri125\tx295 WinHelp sends the DLL a DW_STARTJUMP message after the user runs a jump. 
\par The lParam1 parameter to DW_STARTJUMP is not used. The lParam2 parameter specifies the handle of the current parent window.
\par If \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  returns TRUE for this message, WinHelp continues sending this message throughout the remainder of the session; if \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  returns FALSE, WinHelp stops sending it.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{bmc bm1.bmp\}\tab DW_ENDJUMP
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li295\ri125\tx295 WinHelp sends the DLL a DW_ENDJUMP message after it displays the jump-destination topic.
\par The lParam1 parameter specifies the byte offset of that topic within the Help file's file system. This value can be passed to the \b LSeekHf\plain\f1\fs16  function (defined in the DLL.H file) to perform a seek to that topic within the file.
\par The lParam2 parameter specifies the position of the scroll box in the topic. This value can be used in any of the standard Windows scrolling functions that accept scroll-position parameters.
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li295\ri125\tx295 If \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  returns TRUE for this message, WinHelp continues sending this message throughout the remainder of the session; if \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  returns FALSE, WinHelp stops sending it.

If the message is the result of a jump to a new file, WinHelp sends the DW_CHGFILE message between the DW_STARTJUMP and DW_ENDJUMP messages.
\par The lParam1 parameter specifies a long pointer to the .HLP filename. The lParam2 parameter is not used.
```

### Decoded Body

```text
DC_ACTIVATE Flag

The DC_ACTIVATE flag tells WinHelp that the DLL should receive a DW_ACTIVATE message when the user gives either WinHelp or another program the input focus.

{bmc bm1.bmp}	DW_ACTIVATE

The lParam1 parameter to the DW_ACTIVATE message specifies whether WinHelp received (nonzero LONG value) or lost (0L) the input focus. The lParam2 parameter is not used.

If LDLLHandler returns TRUE for this message, WinHelp continues sending this message throughout the remainder of the session; if LDLLHandler returns FALSE, WinHelp stops sending it.
```

## 359. Calling WinHelp internal functions

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:00de`

    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:00de
    ```
- Marker `$`

    ```rtf
    Calling WinHelp internal functions
    ```
- Marker `#`

- Marker `>`

    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `DLL_WRITE_CALL_WH_INTERNAL;HELPDLL`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DLL_WRITE_CALL_WH_INTERNAL;HELPDLL
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `DLLs;DLLs, calling WinHelp internal functions;WinHelp 4.0;WinHelp 4.0, calling internal functions`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DLLs;DLLs, calling WinHelp internal functions;WinHelp 4.0;WinHelp 4.0, calling internal functions
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Calling WinHelp Internal Functions

WinHelp provides DLL authors with access to 16 of its internal functions. These functions perform operations in the internal .HLP file system, get global information about the currently open Help file, or display information in a standard WinHelp dialog box.

In this section, you'll learn how to access six of these internal functions. An overview of the mechanism is as follows:

{bmc bm1.bmp}	WinHelp sends a DW_WHATMSG message to the DLL.

{bmc bm1.bmp}	The LDLLHandler function in the DLL processes the DW_WHATMSG message by returning a DC_CALLBACKS flag.

{bmc bm1.bmp}	WinHelp sends a DW_CALLBACKS message specifying a pointer to an array of WinHelp internal functions.

{bmc bm1.bmp}	The LDLLHandler function uses the pointer passed in the DW_CALLBACKS message to obtain pointers to the WinHelp functions it will use.

{button ,AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW')} Related Topics%!AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Calling WinHelp Internal Functions
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 WinHelp provides DLL authors with access to 16 of its internal functions. These functions perform operations in the internal .HLP file system, get global information about the currently open Help file, or display information in a standard WinHelp dialog box.
\par In this section, you'll learn how to access six of these internal functions. An overview of the mechanism is as follows:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{bmc bm1.bmp\}\tab WinHelp sends a DW_WHATMSG message to the DLL.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{bmc bm1.bmp\}\tab The \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  function in the DLL processes the DW_WHATMSG message by returning a DC_CALLBACKS flag.
\par \{bmc bm1.bmp\}\tab WinHelp sends a DW_CALLBACKS message specifying a pointer to an array of WinHelp internal functions.
\par \{bmc bm1.bmp\}\tab The \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  function uses the pointer passed in the DW_CALLBACKS message to obtain pointers to the WinHelp functions it will use.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW')}
\par
```

## 360. Accessing WinHelp functions

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:00df`

    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:00df
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Accessing WinHelp functions`
  - Raw:

    ```rtf
    Accessing WinHelp functions
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `DLL_WRITE_ACCESS_WH_FUNCTIONS`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DLL_WRITE_ACCESS_WH_FUNCTIONS
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `DLL_WRITE_ACCESS_WH_FUNCTIONS;embed_dll;HELPDLL`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DLL_WRITE_ACCESS_WH_FUNCTIONS;embed_dll;HELPDLL
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `DLLs;DLLs, accessing WinHelp functions`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DLLs;DLLs, accessing WinHelp functions
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Accessing WinHelp Functions (DW_CALLBACKS)

If a DLL wants access to WinHelp internal functions, its LDLLHandler function handles the DW_WHATMSG message by returning a DC_CALLBACKS flag. When it gets this flag, WinHelp sends a DW_CALLBACKS message to the DLL. The lParam1 parameter is a long pointer to an array containing pointers to each of the 16 internal WinHelp functions. The DLL.H file defines symbolic names for indexing each function in the array.

In processing the DW_CALLBACKS message, the LDLLHandler function calls a function named GetCallBacks to specify which functions it wants to access. GetCallBacks is defined as follows in the example:

PUBLIC     BOOL PASCAL EXPORT GetCallBacks(

VPTR     VPtr,

LONG     lVersion)

{-

     // hfs level:

     lpfn_HfsOpenSz       = VPtr[HE_HfsOpenSz];

     lpfn_RcCloseHfs      = VPtr[HE_RcCloseHfs];

     lpfn_RcLLInfoFromHfs = VPtr[HE_RcLLInfoFromHfs];

     // bag level routines

     lpfn_FAccessHfs =  VPtr[HE_FAccessHfs];

     lpfn_HfOpenHfs  =  VPtr[HE_HfOpenHfs];

     lpfn_LcbReadHf  =  (LPFN_LCBREADHF) VPtr[HE_LcbReadHf];

     lpfn_RcCloseHf  =  VPtr[HE_RcCloseHf];

     return TRUE;

}

The VPtr parameter in GetCallBacks is the lParam1 pointer passed by the DW_CALLBACKS message from WinHelp. The GetCallBacks function gets pointers to the following WinHelp internal functions, which it uses later in the example.

Function	What it does

HfsOpenSz	Opens the .HLP file system (the compound file containing the files internal to the Help file).

RcCloseHfs	Closes the open .HLP file system.

RcLLInfoFromHfs	Maps a handle to the open .HLP file system (returned by HfsOpenSz) to low-level file information.

FAccessHfs	Determines whether a file within the .HLP file system is accessible.

HfOpenHfs	Opens a file within the .HLP file system.

LcbReadHf	Reads bytes from a file within the .HLP file system.

Note

This sample code does not take multiple instances of WinHelp into account. If more than one instance is started, the DLL receives different pointers to these callback functions—one pointer for each instance. You must keep track of which pointer is associated with each WinHelp instance. One way to do this is to create an array associating the task with the callback pointer.

{button ,AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW')} Related Topics%!AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Accessing WinHelp Functions (DW_CALLBACKS)
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 If a DLL wants access to WinHelp internal functions, its \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  function handles the DW_WHATMSG message by returning a DC_CALLBACKS flag. When it gets this flag, WinHelp sends a DW_CALLBACKS message to the DLL. The lParam1 parameter is a long pointer to an array containing pointers to each of the 16 internal WinHelp functions. The DLL.H file defines symbolic names for indexing each function in the array.
\par In processing the DW_CALLBACKS message, the \b LDLLHandler\plain\f1\fs16  function calls a function named \b GetCallBacks\plain\f1\fs16  to specify which functions it wants to access. \b GetCallBacks\plain\f1\fs16  is defined as follows in the example:
\par \pard\keep\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 PUBLIC     BOOL PASCAL EXPORT GetCallBacks(\line
VPTR     VPtr,\line
LONG     lVersion)\line
\{\-\line
\line
     // hfs level:\line
     lpfn_HfsOpenSz       = VPtr[HE_HfsOpenSz];\line
     lpfn_RcCloseHfs      = VPtr[HE_RcCloseHfs];\line
     lpfn_RcLLInfoFromHfs = VPtr[HE_RcLLInfoFromHfs];\line
\line
     // bag level routines\line
     lpfn_FAccessHfs =  VPtr[HE_FAccessHfs];\line
     lpfn_HfOpenHfs  =  VPtr[HE_HfOpenHfs];\line
     lpfn_LcbReadHf  =  (LPFN_LCBREADHF) VPtr[HE_LcbReadHf];\line
     lpfn_RcCloseHf  =  VPtr[HE_RcCloseHf];\line
     return TRUE;\line
\'7d
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 The VPtr parameter in \b GetCallBacks\plain\f1\fs16  is the lParam1 pointer passed by the DW_CALLBACKS message from WinHelp. The \b GetCallBacks\plain\f1\fs16  function gets pointers to the following WinHelp internal functions, which it uses later in the example.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1795\ri125\fi-1665\brdrb\brdrs\tx1795 \b Function\tab What it does
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1795\ri125\fi-1665\tx1795 \plain\f1\fs16 HfsOpenSz\tab Opens the .HLP file system (the compound file containing the files internal to the Help file).
\par RcCloseHfs\tab Closes the open .HLP file system.
\par RcLLInfoFromHfs\tab Maps a handle to the open .HLP file system (returned by HfsOpenSz) to low-level file information.
\par FAccessHfs\tab Determines whether a file within the .HLP file system is accessible.
\par HfOpenHfs\tab Opens a file within the .HLP file system.
\par LcbReadHf\tab Reads bytes from a file within the .HLP file system.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1795 \b Note
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1795 \plain\f1\fs16 This sample code does not take multiple instances of WinHelp into account. If more than one instance is started, the DLL receives different pointers to these callback functions\plain\f0\fs16 \'97\f1 one pointer for each instance. You must keep track of which pointer is associated with each WinHelp instance. One way to do this is to create an array associating the task with the callback pointer.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW')}
\par
```

## 361. Writing DLLs for embedded windows

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:00e0`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:00e0
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Writing DLLs for embedded windows`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Writing DLLs for embedded windows
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `DLL_WRITE_EMBEDDED_WINDOWS`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DLL_WRITE_EMBEDDED_WINDOWS
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `DLL_WRITE_EMBEDDED_WINDOWS;embed_dll;HELPDLL`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DLL_WRITE_EMBEDDED_WINDOWS;embed_dll;HELPDLL
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `DLLs;DLLs, writing for embedded windows;windows,;windows, embedded, writing DLLs for`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DLLs;DLLs, writing for embedded windows;windows,;windows, embedded, writing DLLs for
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Writing DLLs for Embedded Windows

In WinHelp version 4.0 you can create embedded windows to extend the functionality of WinHelp by placing objects in a fixed-size window under the control of a DLL. For example, you can create a DLL to display animation sequences or to play audio segments in an embedded window. However, in version 4.0 you cannot create an embedded window that functions as a hotspot. This section explains how to write custom DLLs for WinHelp.

To create an embedded window, authors insert an embedded window reference, {-ewx}, in the RTF source file.

WinHelp displays embedded windows within topic windows. To WinHelp, an embedded window is simply a child window of that topic window. Users cannot minimize, maximize, or resize an embedded window. Embedded windows cannot be used as hotspots. However, you can include hotspots in an embedded window if they are controlled by the DLL, but users will not be able to use the keyboard equivalents to access the hotspots. That is because embedded windows cannot receive the input focus, which means they cannot process keystrokes. So, you should not place anything in an embedded window that requires keyboard input from users.

WinHelp positions the embedded window using the justification character (left, right, or character) specified by the author in the embedded window reference. The embedded window DLL is expected to display the information in the window and resize the window appropriately. Help expects the window size to remain fixed as long as the topic is displayed. The window element and DLL determine the size and content of the embedded window, and Help arranges the other elements of the topic around the embedded window.

WinHelp displays embedded windows only when necessary — while the topic containing the embedded window is being displayed. However, an embedded window may exist while it is not being displayed (if the user scrolls the topic past the embedded window, for example). Because it is part of a specific topic, an embedded window goes away when the user displays a different topic.

{button ,AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW;ewx')} Related Topics%!AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW;ewx')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Writing DLLs for Embedded Windows
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 In WinHelp version 4.0 you can create embedded windows to extend the functionality of WinHelp by placing objects in a fixed-size window under the control of a DLL. For example, you can create a DLL to display animation sequences or to play audio segments in an embedded window. However, in version 4.0 you cannot create an embedded window that functions as a hotspot. This section explains how to write custom DLLs for WinHelp.
\par To create an embedded window, authors insert an embedded window reference, \b \{\-ew\plain\f1\fs16 x\b \'7d\plain\f1\fs16 , in the RTF source file.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 WinHelp displays embedded windows within topic windows. To WinHelp, an embedded window is simply a child window of that topic window. Users cannot minimize, maximize, or resize an embedded window. Embedded windows cannot be used as hotspots. However, you can include hotspots in an embedded window if they are controlled by the DLL, but users will not be able to use the keyboard equivalents to access the hotspots. That is because embedded windows cannot receive the input focus, which means they cannot process keystrokes. So, you should not place anything in an embedded window that requires keyboard input from users.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 WinHelp positions the embedded window using the justification character (left, right, or character) specified by the author in the embedded window reference. The embedded window DLL is expected to display the information in the window and resize the window appropriately. Help expects the window size to remain fixed as long as the topic is displayed. The window element and DLL determine the size and content of the embedded window, and Help arranges the other elements of the topic around the embedded window. 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 WinHelp displays embedded windows only when necessary \plain\f0\fs16 \'97\f1  while the topic containing the embedded window is being displayed. However, an embedded window may exist while it is not being displayed (if the user scrolls the topic past the embedded window, for example). Because it is part of a specific topic, an embedded window goes away when the user displays a different topic.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW;ewx')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW;ewx')}
```

## 362. Initialization of embedded windows

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:00e1`

    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:00e1
    ```
- Marker `$`

    ```rtf
    Initialization of embedded windows
    ```
- Marker `#`

  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    .5_VJN
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `embed_dll`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    embed_dll
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `DLLs;DLLs, initializing embedded windows;windows,;windows, initializing DLLs for`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DLLs;DLLs, initializing embedded windows;windows,;windows, initializing DLLs for
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Initialization of Embedded Windows

WinHelp creates embedded windows with window style WS_CHILD and a default size. It initializes the DLL for an embedded window before it displays the window.

When WinHelp creates the embedded window, it passes two strings in the WM_CREATE message. The lparam parameter points to a CREATESTRUCT structure, and the lpCreateParams field of the CREATESTRUCT structure points to a second structure defined as follows:

typedef  struct	tagCreateInfo

		{-

		short			idMajVersion;

 		short			idMinVersion;

 		LPSTR			lpstrFileName;

		LPSTR			lpstrAuthorData;

		HANDLE			hfs;

		DWORD			coForeground;

		DWORD			coBackground;

		}    CREATEINFO;

The fields in the structure are defined in the following table.

Field	Use

idMajVersion, idMinVersion	Identifies the version of WinHelp (and the version of the CREATESTRUCT structure used for embedded windows in that version of WinHelp). Currently, these fields are 0. If the idMinVersion field is a value other than 0, additional interfaces may be available to DLLs, but all the interfaces described in this chapter are still supported.

	The DLL should always verify that the value in the idMajVersion and idMinVersion fields of the CREATESTRUCT structure are 0. If they are not, the DLL might not work with that version of WinHelp.

lpstrFileName	Points to the fully qualified name of the .HLP file containing the embedded window. This field will be NULL if the data is not available. If the DLL uses the string contained in this field, it should make a copy of the string.

lpstrAuthorData	Points to the author-data parameter in the {-ewl}, {-ewc}, or {-ewr} reference from the source RTF file. This field will be NULL if the data is not available. If the DLL uses the string contained in this field, it should make a copy of the string.

hfs	Specifies a handle to the file system for the .HLP file.

coForeground,coBackground	Specify the foreground and background colors of the main Help window.O8.Q2 If the embedded window uses the same colors as the main Help window, the DLL can use the color values in these fields to set those colors in the embedded window.

{button ,AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW')} Related Topics%!AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Initialization of Embedded Windows
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 WinHelp creates embedded windows with window style WS_CHILD and a default size. It initializes the DLL for an embedded window before it displays the window.
\par When WinHelp creates the embedded window, it passes two strings in the WM_CREATE message. The lparam parameter points to a CREATESTRUCT structure, and the lpCreateParams field of the CREATESTRUCT structure points to a second structure defined as follows:
\par \pard\keep\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx355 typedef  struct\tab tagCreateInfo\line
\tab \tab \{\-\line
\tab \tab short\tab \tab \tab idMajVersion;\line
 \tab \tab short\tab \tab \tab idMinVersion;\line
 \tab \tab LPSTR\tab \tab \tab lpstrFileName;\line
\tab \tab LPSTR\tab \tab \tab lpstrAuthorData;\line
\tab \tab HANDLE\tab \tab \tab hfs;\line
\tab \tab DWORD\tab \tab \tab coForeground;\line
\tab \tab DWORD\tab \tab \tab coBackground;\line
\tab \tab \'7d    CREATEINFO;
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx355 The fields in the structure are defined in the following table.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li2705\ri125\fi-2575\brdrb\brdrs\tx2695 \b Field\f6 \tab \f1 Use\f6 
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li2705\ri125\fi-2575\tx2695 \plain\f1\fs16 idMajVersion, idMinVersion\tab Identifies the version of WinHelp (and the version of the CREATESTRUCT structure used for embedded windows in that version of WinHelp). Currently, these fields are 0. If the idMinVersion field is a value other than 0, additional interfaces may be available to DLLs, but all the interfaces described in this chapter are still supported.
\par \tab The DLL should always verify that the value in the idMajVersion and idMinVersion fields of the CREATESTRUCT structure are 0. If they are not, the DLL might not work with that version of WinHelp.
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li2705\ri125\fi-2575\tx2695 lpstrFileName\tab Points to the fully qualified name of the .HLP file containing the embedded window. This field will be NULL if the data is not available. If the DLL uses the string contained in this field, it should make a copy of the string.
\par lpstrAuthorData\tab Points to the author-data parameter in the \b \{\-ewl\'7d\plain\f1\fs16 , \b \{\-ewc\'7d\plain\f1\fs16 , or \b \{\-ewr\'7d\plain\f1\fs16  reference from the source RTF file. This field will be NULL if the data is not available. If the DLL uses the string contained in this field, it should make a copy of the string.
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li2705\ri125\fi-2575\tx2695 hfs\tab Specifies a handle to the file system for the .HLP file. 
\par coForeground,coBackground\tab Specify the foreground and background colors of the \ul main Help window.\plain\f1\fs16 {\v O8.Q2} If the embedded window uses the same colors as the main Help window, the DLL can use the color values in these fields to set those colors in the embedded window.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295
```

## 363. Embedded window behavior

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:00e2`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:00e2
    ```
- Marker `$`

  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Embedded window behavior
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `DLL_WRITE_EMBEDDED_WINDOW_BEHAVIOR`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DLL_WRITE_EMBEDDED_WINDOW_BEHAVIOR
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `DLL_WRITE_EMBEDDED_WINDOW_BEHAVIOR;embed_dll;HELPDLL`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DLL_WRITE_EMBEDDED_WINDOW_BEHAVIOR;embed_dll;HELPDLL
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `DLLs;DLLs, embedded window behavior;windows,;windows, embedded, behavior described`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DLLs;DLLs, embedded window behavior;windows,;windows, embedded, behavior described
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Embedded Window Behavior

While a topic is being displayed, WinHelp creates embedded windows when it needs to lay them out and destroys them when they are no longer needed. The window can be created and destroyed, then created again if necessary, while WinHelp tries to lay out the embedded window object. If you load information, you might want to do so during WM_SHOWWINDOW processing so that you don't have to load this information twice.

An embedded window may exist while it is not being displayed (for example, if the topic requires scrolling and the embedded window resides in the portion not currently displayed in the Help window). Do not set the window style to WS_VISIBLE or call the ShowWindow function during WM_CREATE processing. WinHelp displays the window when necessary.

As an embedded window DLL processes a WM_CREATE message, it is expected to set up any window styles the window uses and resize the window appropriately.

WinHelp positions the window using the justification character (left, right, or character) authored into the RTF file. WinHelp expects the window size to remain fixed during the window's lifetime.

Because it is a child window of the main Help window.O8.Q2 an embedded window does not need to destroy itself; it will be destroyed when the parent window is destroyed.

{button ,AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW')} Related Topics%!AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Embedded Window Behavior
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 While a topic is being displayed, WinHelp creates embedded windows when it needs to lay them out and destroys them when they are no longer needed. The window can be created and destroyed, then created again if necessary, while WinHelp tries to lay out the embedded window object. If you load information, you might want to do so during WM_SHOWWINDOW processing so that you don't have to load this information twice.
\par An embedded window may exist while it is not being displayed (for example, if the topic requires scrolling and the embedded window resides in the portion not currently displayed in the Help window). Do not set the window style to WS_VISIBLE or call the \b ShowWindow\plain\f1\fs16  function during WM_CREATE processing. WinHelp displays the window when necessary.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 As an embedded window DLL processes a WM_CREATE message, it is expected to set up any window styles the window uses and resize the window appropriately.
\par WinHelp positions the window using the justification character (left, right, or character) authored into the RTF file. WinHelp expects the window size to remain fixed during the window's lifetime.
\par Because it is a child window of the \ul main Help window.\plain\f1\fs16 {\v O8.Q2} an embedded window does not need to destroy itself; it will be destroyed when the parent window is destroyed.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW')}
\par
```

## 364. Message processing for embedded windows

- Marker `K`

  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    DLLs;DLLs, embedded window message processing;Windows messages, DLL processing
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Message Processing for Embedded Windows

Embedded windows receive most standard Windows messages, including mouse and WM_PAINT messages. Embedded windows should not take the input focus; therefore, they do not need to process keystrokes.

Embedded window DLLs must also process the following messages, which are specifically defined for use with embedded windows:

{bmc bm1.bmp}	EWM_RENDER (0x706A)*EWM_RENDER

{bmc bm1.bmp}	EWM_QUERYSIZE (0x706B)*EWM_QUERYSIZE

{bmc bm1.bmp}	EWM_ASKPALETTE (0x706C)*EWM_ASKPALETTE

{button ,AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW')} Related Topics%!AL(`DLL_WRITE_OVERVIEW')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Message Processing for Embedded Windows
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Embedded windows receive most standard Windows messages, including mouse and WM_PAINT messages. Embedded windows should not take the input focus; therefore, they do not need to process keystrokes.
\par Embedded window DLLs must also process the following messages, which are specifically defined for use with embedded windows:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{bmc bm1.bmp\}\tab \uldb EWM_RENDER (0x706A)\plain\f1\ul\fs16 {\v *EWM_RENDER}\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{bmc bm1.bmp\}\tab \uldb EWM_QUERYSIZE (0x706B)\plain\f1\ul\fs16 {\v *EWM_QUERYSIZE}\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \{bmc bm1.bmp\}\tab \uldb EWM_ASKPALETTE (0x706C)\plain\f1\ul\fs16 {\v *EWM_ASKPALETTE}\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295

\par
```

## 365. EWM_RENDER message

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `$`

- Marker `>`

- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `EWM_RENDER message;messages;messages, EWM_RENDER`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    EWM_RENDER message;messages;messages, EWM_RENDER
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
EWM_RENDER

WinHelp sends the EWM_RENDER message to an embedded window to get information about the image in the window. It uses this information when printing the image or placing it in the Clipboard. The wParam parameter to EWM_RENDER indicates the type of information returned by the message, and the LOWORD of the return value contains a handle to this information. Possible wParam values and return values are shown in the following table.

wParam	Return Value

CF_TEXT	Sharable global handle to a null-terminated ASCII string. WinHelp frees this handle.

CF_BITMAP	Handle to a bitmap. WinHelp removes this handle.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 
\b\fs24 EWM_RENDER
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 WinHelp sends the EWM_RENDER message to an embedded window to get information about the image in the window. It uses this information when printing the image or placing it in the Clipboard. The wParam parameter to EWM_RENDER indicates the type of information returned by the message, and the LOWORD of the return value contains a handle to this information. Possible wParam values and return values are shown in the following table.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1595\ri125\fi-1445\brdrb\brdrs\tx1595 \b wParam\tab Return Value
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1595\ri125\fi-1445\tx1595 \plain\f1\fs16 CF_TEXT\tab Sharable global handle to a null-terminated ASCII string. WinHelp frees this handle.
\par CF_BITMAP\tab Handle to a bitmap. WinHelp removes this handle.
\par
```

  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    EWM_QUERYSIZE message;messages;messages, EWM_QUERYSIZE
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
EWM_QUERYSIZE

WinHelp sends the EWM_QUERYSIZE message to an embedded window to obtain the size of the window. The wParam parameter is a handle to the device context for the window. The lParam parameter points to a POINT structure. The embedded window DLL should fill in the x field of the structure with the window's height and the y field of the structure with the window's width. WinHelp uses this information when laying out the topic in the topic window, when printing the topic, or when placing it in the Clipboard.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 
\b EWM_QUERYSIZE
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 WinHelp sends the EWM_QUERYSIZE message to an embedded window to obtain the size of the window. The wParam parameter is a handle to the device context for the window. The lParam parameter points to a POINT structure. The embedded window DLL should fill in the \b x\plain\f1\fs16  field of the structure with the window's height and the \b y\plain\f1\fs16  field of the structure with the window's width. WinHelp uses this information when laying out the topic in the topic window, when printing the topic, or when placing it in the Clipboard.
\par
```

## 367. EWM_ASKPALLETE message

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `$`

- Marker `#`

  - Raw:

- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `EWM_ASKPALETTE message;messages;messages, EWM_ASKPALETTE`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    EWM_ASKPALETTE message;messages;messages, EWM_ASKPALETTE
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
EWM_ASKPALETTE

WinHelp sends the EWM_ASKPALETTE message to all embedded windows displayed within a topic to get information about the palettes used in each window. The wParam and lParam parameters are not used.

When an embedded window receives an EWM_ASKPALETTE message, it should return a handle to the palette that it uses. WinHelp then selects the most appropriate palette for use in that topic. Usually, this palette is the one used for the first embedded window in the currently displayed part of the topic.

Before the DLL repaints an embedded window, it should send an EWM_ASKPALETTE message to the parent window to determine which palette to use. The following code fragment illustrates how to do this:

hpal = (HPALETTE)SendMessage(GetParent(hwnd), EWM_ASKPALETTE, 0, 0L)
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 
\b EWM_ASKPALETTE
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 WinHelp sends the EWM_ASKPALETTE message to all embedded windows displayed within a topic to get information about the palettes used in each window. The wParam and lParam parameters are not used.
\par When an embedded window receives an EWM_ASKPALETTE message, it should return a handle to the palette that it uses. WinHelp then selects the most appropriate palette for use in that topic. Usually, this palette is the one used for the first embedded window in the currently displayed part of the topic.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 Before the DLL repaints an embedded window, it should send an EWM_ASKPALETTE message to the parent window to determine which palette to use. The following code fragment illustrates how to do this:
\par hpal = (HPALETTE)SendMessage(GetParent(hwnd), EWM_ASKPALETTE, 0, 0L)
\par 
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Help RTF tokens

Help RTF tokens are an extended subset of tokens defined by the Microsoft Rich Text Format (RTF) standard. The RTF tokens specify character and paragraph properties, such as font, color, spacing, and alignment, for text and graphics in the Help file. The following topics describe the subset of RTF tokens recognized by Help Workshop. Use this information if you plan to edit RTF files directly or create an RTF reader/writer that converts files to RTF format.

{button ,AL("RTF1")} Related Topics%!AL("RTF1")
```

## 369. Rich-text format

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:0167`
  - Raw:

    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:0167
    ```
- Marker `$`

  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Rich-text format
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `1I0I9F`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    1I0I9F
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `RTF1`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    RTF1
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `files,;files, RTF;rich-text format;RTF files;RTF files, about rich-text format;RTF reader;topic files;topic files, about rich-text format`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    files,;files, RTF;rich-text format;RTF files;RTF files, about rich-text format;RTF reader;topic files;topic files, about rich-text format
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Rich-Text Format

The rich-text format (RTF) standard is a method of encoding formatted text and graphics for easy transfer between different programs and different operations. Generally, it is used by all Microsoft Word programs — Word for Windows, Word for the Macintosh, and Word for MS-DOS — for moving word-processing documents between different platforms without having to rely on special translation software or conversion utilities. Because the RTF standard provides a format for text and graphics interchange that can be used with different output devices and operating systems, Help Workshop also supports this standard. That means you can use almost any text editor that generates RTF output, including your own custom RTF editor, to create the source files that are built into Help files.

Software that takes a formatted file and turns it into an RTF file is referred to as an RTF "writer." Software that translates an RTF file into a formatted file is referred to as an RTF "reader." An RTF writer separates the program's control information from the actual text and writes a new file containing the text and RTF groups associated with that text. An RTF reader does the converse of this operation.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Rich-Text Format
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The rich-text format (RTF) standard is a method of encoding formatted text and graphics for easy transfer between different programs and different operations. Generally, it is used by all Microsoft Word programs \plain\f0\fs16 \'97\f1  Word for Windows, Word for the Macintosh, and Word for MS-DOS \plain\f0\fs16 \'97\f1  for moving word-processing documents between different platforms without having to rely on special translation software or conversion utilities. Because the RTF standard provides a format for text and graphics interchange that can be used with different output devices and operating systems, Help Workshop also supports this standard. That means you can use almost any text editor that generates RTF output, including your own custom RTF editor, to create the source files that are built into Help files.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 Software that takes a formatted file and turns it into an RTF file is referred to as an RTF "writer." Software that translates an RTF file into a formatted file is referred to as an RTF "reader." An RTF writer separates the program's control information from the actual text and writes a new file containing the text and RTF groups associated with that text. An RTF reader does the converse of this operation.
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Elements of RTF

Help RTF tokens are presented to Help Workshop in topic files, which are specified in the [FILES] section of a Help project (.HPJ) file. To Help Workshop a topic file consists of the following elements:

·	RTF tokens

·	Control symbols

·	Groups

·	Unformatted text

RTF tokens, control symbols, and braces constitute control information. Text grouping is used to define the format and placement of text and graphics in the Help file. All other characters in RTF text constitute plain text.

{button ,AL("element1;FILES")} Related Topics%!AL("element1;FILES")
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Elements of RTF
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Help RTF tokens are presented to Help Workshop in topic files, which are specified in the\b  \plain\f1\fs16 [FILES] section of a Help project (.HPJ) file. To Help Workshop a topic file consists of the following elements:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 RTF tokens
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 Control symbols
\par \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 Groups
\par \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 Unformatted text
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 RTF tokens, control symbols, and braces constitute control information. Text grouping is used to define the format and placement of text and graphics in the Help file. All other characters in RTF text constitute plain text.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL("element1;FILES")\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL("element1;FILES")}
\par
```

## 371. RTF token syntax

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:0169`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:0169
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `RTF token syntax`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    RTF token syntax
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `42X9YCM`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    42X9YCM
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `element1;RTF1`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    element1;RTF1
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `characters;characters, in RTF tokens;Help RTF tokens;RTF files;RTF files, about RTF tokens;RTF tokens;syntax of RTF tokens;tokens, RTF`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    characters;characters, in RTF tokens;Help RTF tokens;RTF files;RTF files, about RTF tokens;RTF tokens;syntax of RTF tokens;tokens, RTF
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
RTF token Syntax

An RTFtoken is a formatted tag that specifies a particular kind of information in the RTF file. An RTF token consists of a backslash (\) followed by an RTF token name and a delimiter:

\statement-name<delimiter>

Statments

RTF tokens always begin with a backslash (\). The statement-name identifies the RTF token and specifies what it does. No spaces or other characters may separate the backslash and the statement-name.

An RTF token may optionally have a number parameter immediately after the name. This number must be in the range of a signed integer (-32767 through 32767). No spaces or other characters may separate the name and the numeric parameter (if any).

Certain statements control properties (such as bold and italic) that have only two states. When the RTF token has no parameter or has a non-zero parameter, the statement is used to turn the property on. When the RTF token has a 0 (zero) parameter, the statement is used to turn the property off. For example, \b turns on bold, whereas \b0 turns off bold.

Delimiters

Delimiters are mandatory characters that separate the statement text of a parameter from subsequent text. A delimiter can be one of the following:

·	A space. The space is considered part of the statement.

·	A digit or a minus sign (-), which indicates that a numeric parameter follows. If a numeric parameter immediately follows the statement name, this parameter becomes part of the statement.

·	Any character other than a letter or digit. The delimiting character terminates the statement but is not considered part of the statement. A "letter" is an upper- or lowercase ASCII letter.

When a space is used as a delimiter, Help Workshop discards it. If any other character is used, Help Workshop processes it as text or as the start of another RTF token. For example, if a backslash is used as a delimiter, Help Workshop interprets it as the beginning of the next RTF token.

For example, the following line demonstrates usage of the \tab statement and a space delimiter:

{bmc bm4.bmp}	text in paragraph\tab more text
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 RTF token Syntax
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 An RTFtoken is a formatted tag that specifies a particular kind of information in the RTF file. An RTF token consists of a backslash (\'5c) followed by an RTF token name and a delimiter:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li715\ri125 \'5cstatement-name<delimiter>
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Statments
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 RTF tokens always begin with a backslash (\b \'5c\plain\f1\fs16 ). The statement-name identifies the RTF token and specifies what it does. No spaces or other characters may separate the backslash and the statement-name.
\par An RTF token may optionally have a number parameter immediately after the name. This number must be in the range of a signed integer (-32767 through 32767). No spaces or other characters may separate the name and the numeric parameter (if any).
\par Certain statements control properties (such as bold and italic) that have only two states. When the RTF token has no parameter or has a non-zero parameter, the statement is used to turn the property on. When the RTF token has a 0 (zero) parameter, the statement is used to turn the property off. For example, \b \'5cb\plain\f1\fs16  turns on bold, whereas \b \'5cb0\plain\f1\fs16  turns off bold.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Delimiters
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Delimiters are mandatory characters that separate the statement text of a parameter from subsequent text. A delimiter can be one of the following:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 A space. The space is considered part of the statement.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab A digit or a minus sign (-), which indicates that a numeric parameter follows. If a numeric parameter immediately follows the statement name, this parameter becomes part of the statement.
\par \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 Any character other than a letter or digit. The delimiting character terminates the statement but is not considered part of the statement. A "letter" is an upper- or lowercase ASCII letter.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 When a space is used as a delimiter, Help Workshop discards it. If any other character is used, Help Workshop processes it as text or as the start of another RTF token. For example, if a backslash is used as a delimiter, Help Workshop interprets it as the beginning of the next RTF token.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 For example, the following line demonstrates usage of the \b \'5ctab \plain\f1\fs16 statement and a space delimiter:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx355 \{bmc bm4.bmp\}\tab text in paragraph\'5ctab more text
\par
```

- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `backslash character;characters;characters, in control symbols;control symbols;RTF files;RTF files, about control symbols;symbols, control`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    backslash character;characters;characters, in control symbols;control symbols;RTF files;RTF files, about control symbols;symbols, control
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Control Symbols

A control symbol consists of a backslash (\) followed by a single non-letter. Control symbols require no further delimiting. The following control symbols are supported:

Control Symbol	Meaning

\|	Formula character

\~	Nonbreaking space

\-	Optional hyphen

\_	Nonbreaking hyphen

\:	Subentry in an index entry

\'hh	Hexadecimal value of a character in the current character set, where h is a hexadecimal digit in the range 0 through F.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Control Symbols
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 A control symbol consists of a backslash (\'5c) followed by a single non-letter. Control symbols require no further delimiting. The following control symbols are supported:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1795\ri125\fi-1665\brdrb\brdrs\tx1795 \b Control Symbol\tab Meaning
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1795\ri125\fi-1665\tx1795 \plain\f1\fs16 \'5c|\tab Formula character
\par \'5c~\tab Nonbreaking space
\par \'5c-\tab Optional hyphen
\par \'5c_\tab Nonbreaking hyphen
\par \'5c:\tab Subentry in an index entry
\par \'5c'hh\tab Hexadecimal value of a character in the current character set, where h is a hexadecimal digit in the range 0 through F.
\par
```

- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`

- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `element1`
  - Raw:

    ```rtf
    element1
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `braces characters;characters;characters, in groups;groups;groups, about groups;RTF files;RTF files, about groups`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    braces characters;characters;characters, in groups;groups;groups, about groups;RTF files;RTF files, about groups
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Groups

A group consists of Help RTF tokens and text enclosed in braces ({- }). The opening brace {- indicates the start of the group, and the closing brace } indicates the end of the group, as shown in this example:

{bmc bm4.bmp}	{-       group start, and

}       group end.

Different groups perform different functions in the RTF file. Some groups specify information such as the fonts, styles, colors, summary information, and document-format attributes used in the file. Other groups simply include text and the RTF tokens that define attributes for that text. Still other groups specify picture data, footnotes, bookmarks, annotations, and section- and paragraph-formatting attributes for the file.

Formatting specified within a group affects only the text within that group. Text within a group inherits any formatting of the text preceding the group.

Destinations

Some statements, referred to as destinations, mark the beginning of a collection of related text. A destination is marked by a control word that signals the beginning of the group. An example of a destination is the group where the Help-specific text follows the statement. Destination statements and their following text must be enclosed in braces, as in this example:

{bmc bm4.bmp}	{-\footnote main_contents}
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Groups
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 A group consists of Help RTF tokens and text enclosed in braces (\b \{\- \'7d\plain\f1\fs16 ). The opening brace \b \{\-\plain\f1\fs16  indicates the start of the group, and the closing brace \b \'7d\plain\f1\fs16  indicates the end of the group, as shown in this example:
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{bmc bm4.bmp\}\tab \{\-       group start, and\line
\'7d       group end.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 Different groups perform different functions in the RTF file. Some groups specify information such as the fonts, styles, colors, summary information, and document-format attributes used in the file. Other groups simply include text and the RTF tokens that define attributes for that text. Still other groups specify picture data, footnotes, bookmarks, annotations, and section- and paragraph-formatting attributes for the file.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 Formatting specified within a group affects only the text within that group. Text within a group inherits any formatting of the text preceding the group.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Destinations
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 Some statements, referred to as destinations, mark the beginning of a collection of related text. A destination is marked by a control word that signals the beginning of the group. An example of a destination is the group where the Help-specific text follows the statement. Destination statements and their following text must be enclosed in braces, as in this example:
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{bmc bm4.bmp\}\tab \{\-\'5cfootnote main_contents\'7d
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Unformatted Text

Unformatted text consists of any combination of 7-bit ASCII characters. Although characters whose values are greater than 127 are not permitted in topic files, the \' statement can be used to insert them in the final Help file. Help Workshop treats spaces as part of the text, but it discards carriage return and linefeed characters.

Because the backslash character (\) and braces ({- }) have specific meanings in RTF, they must be preceded with a backslash if you want to use them as plain text, as shown by the control symbols \\, \{-, and \}.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 
\b\fs24  
Unformatted Text
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Unformatted text consists of any combination of 7-bit ASCII characters. Although characters whose values are greater than 127 are not permitted in topic files, the \b \'5c'\plain\f1\fs16  statement can be used to insert them in the final Help file. Help Workshop treats spaces as part of the text, but it discards carriage return and linefeed characters.
\par Because the backslash character (\'5c) and braces (\{\- \'7d) have specific meanings in RTF, they must be preceded with a backslash if you want to use them as plain text, as shown by the control symbols \b \'5c\'5c\plain\f1\fs16 , \b \'5c\{\-\plain\f1\fs16 , and \b \'5c\'7d\plain\f1\fs16 .
\par
```

## 375. RTF semantics

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:016d`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:016d
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `RTF semantics`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    RTF semantics
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `1TKNZYJ`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    1TKNZYJ
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `rich-text format;RTF reader;RTF semantics`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    rich-text format;RTF reader;RTF semantics
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `RTF1`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    RTF1
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
RTF Semantics

When reading a stream of RTF text, an RTF reader must accomplish the following tasks:

1	Separate RTF control information from plain text.

2	Act on control information.

This is designed to be a relatively simple process, as described in the next section.

Some control information just contributes special characters to the plain text stream. Other information changes the program state, which includes document properties as a whole and a stack of group states that apply to parts of the document. The group state is saved by the opening {- brace and is restored by the closing } brace.

The current group state specifies:

·	The destination or part of the document that the plain text is building up.

·	The section formatting properties.

·	The paragraph formatting properties.

·	The character formatting properties.

3	Collect and properly dispose of any remaining plain text as directed by the current group state.

{button ,AL("semantics")} Related Topics%!AL("semantics")
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 
\b\fs24  
RTF Semantics
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 When reading a stream of RTF text, an RTF reader must accomplish the following tasks:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 1\tab Separate RTF control information from plain text.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 2\tab Act on control information.
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li295\ri125\tx295 This is designed to be a relatively simple process, as described in the next section.
\par Some control information just contributes special characters to the plain text stream. Other information changes the program state, which includes document properties as a whole and a stack of group states that apply to parts of the document. The group state is saved by the opening \b \{\-\plain\f1\fs16  brace and is restored by the closing \b \'7d\plain\f1\fs16  brace.
\par The current group state specifies:
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li455\ri125\fi-155\tx455 \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 The destination or part of the document that the plain text is building up.
\par \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 The section formatting properties.
\par \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 The paragraph formatting properties.
\par \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 The character formatting properties.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 3\tab Collect and properly dispose of any remaining plain text as directed by the current group state.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL("semantics")\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL("semantics")}
\par
```

## 376. Acting on control information (RTF)

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:016e`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:016e
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Acting on control information (RTF)`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Acting on control information (RTF)
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `13IMJK5`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    13IMJK5
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `semantics`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    semantics
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `characters;characters, actions taken by RTF reader;control information, acting on;RTF reader`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    characters;characters, actions taken by RTF reader;control information, acting on;RTF reader
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Acting on Control Information

When parsing the RTF stream, the RTF reader follows this process:

1	Reads the next character.

2	If the character is an opening brace ( char == '{-' ), stores the current state on the stack.

Or if the current state does not change, it then continues.

3	If the character is a closing brace ( char == '}' ), retrieves the current state from the stack.

Generally, this will change the state.

4	If the character is a backslash ( char == '\' ), collects the RTF token or control symbol and its parameter.

a.	Looks up the statement or symbol in the symbol table (a constant table) and acts according to the description found there. The parameter is left available for use by the action.

b.	Leaves a read pointer before or after the delimiter, as appropriate.

c.	After completing the action, continues.

5	If the character is anything other than {-, }, or \, writes the plain text character to the current destination using the current formatting properties.

6	Reads the next character.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Acting on Control Information
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 When parsing the RTF stream, the RTF reader follows this process:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 1\tab Reads the next character.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 2\tab If the character is an opening brace ( char == '\{\-' ), stores the current state on the stack. 
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li295\ri125\tx295 Or if the current state does not change, it then continues.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 3\tab If the character is a closing brace ( char == '\'7d' ), retrieves the current state from the stack.
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li295\ri125\tx295 Generally, this will change the state.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 4\tab If the character is a backslash ( char == '\'5c' ), collects the RTF token or control symbol and its parameter.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li455\ri125\fi-155\tx455 a.\tab Looks up the statement or symbol in the symbol table (a constant table) and acts according to the description found there. The parameter is left available for use by the action.
\par b.\tab Leaves a read pointer before or after the delimiter, as appropriate.
\par c.\tab After completing the action, continues.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 5\tab If the character is anything other than \b \{\-\plain\f1\fs16 , \b \'7d\plain\f1\fs16 , or \b \'5c\plain\f1\fs16 , writes the plain text character to the current destination using the current formatting properties.
\par 6\tab Reads the next character.
\par
```

## 377. Acting on symbol table entries (RTF)

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:016f`
  - Raw:

    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:016f
    ```

    ```rtf
    Acting on symbol table entries (RTF)
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `CTG.X3`
  - Raw:

    ```rtf
    CTG.X3
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `semantics`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf

    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `RTF reader;symbol table entries`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    RTF reader;symbol table entries
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Acting on Symbol Table Entries

When looking up RTF tokens and symbols in the symbol table, the RTF reader can take any of the following actions:

·	Change the destination to the destination described in the table entry.

Most destination changes are legal only immediately after an opening brace. Other restrictions may also apply (for example, footnotes may not be nested).

·	Change formatting property.

The symbol table entry will describe the property and whether the parameter is required.

·	Insert a special character.

The symbol table entry will describe the character code.

·	End of paragraph.

This can also be viewed as just a special character.

·	End of section.

This can also be viewed as just a special character.

·	Ignore the character.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Acting on Symbol Table Entries
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 When looking up RTF tokens and symbols in the symbol table, the RTF reader can take any of the following actions:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 Change the destination to the destination described in the table entry.
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li295\ri125\tx295 Most destination changes are legal only immediately after an opening brace. Other restrictions may also apply (for example, footnotes may not be nested).
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 Change formatting property.
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li295\ri125\tx295 The symbol table entry will describe the property and whether the parameter is required.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 Insert a special character.
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li295\ri125\tx295 The symbol table entry will describe the character code.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 End of paragraph.
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li295\ri125\tx295 This can also be viewed as just a special character.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 End of section.
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li295\ri125\tx295 This can also be viewed as just a special character.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 Ignore the character.
\par
```

- Marker `K`
