  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    files,;files, RTF;RTF files;RTF files, sample file;sample RTF file
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Sample RTF File

The following is an example of a simple but complete, single-topic RTF file that can be compiled by Help Workshop:

{-\rtf1\ansi \deff0\deflang1024

{-\fonttbl

{-\f0\froman Times New Roman;}

{-\f1\froman Symbol;}

{-\f2\fswiss Arial;}

{-\f3\froman MS Serif;}

{-\f4\fswiss MS Sans Serif;}

}

{-\colortbl;

\red0\green0\blue0;

\red0\green0\blue255;

\red0\green255\blue0;

\red255\green0\blue0;

\red255\green255\blue255;

#{-\footnote graphics_cont}

${-\footnote Graphics}

\pard\plain \li120\sb340\sa120\sl-320 \f3\fs28 Graphics

\par \pard\plain \li120 \f4\fs20 Use graphic images to illustrate concepts and present

information visually. Graphics include line art, icons, screen shots of the interface,

and graphics with hotspots ("hypergraphics").

\par

}

As noted earlier, the entire RTF file is one group, so the file begins and ends with braces ({-}). Other groups nested within the group containing the file define the header and text information.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Sample RTF File
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The following is an example of a simple but complete, single-topic RTF file that can be compiled by Help Workshop:
\par \pard\keep\sb75\sl-235\li715\ri125 \{\-\'5crtf1\'5cansi \'5cdeff0\'5cdeflang1024\b \line
\line
\plain\f1\fs16 \{\-\'5cfonttbl\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \{\-\'5cf0\'5cfroman Times New Roman;\'7d\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \{\-\'5cf1\'5cfroman Symbol;\'7d\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \{\-\'5cf2\'5cfswiss Arial;\'7d\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \{\-\'5cf3\'5cfroman MS Serif;\'7d\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \{\-\'5cf4\'5cfswiss MS Sans Serif;\'7d\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \'7d\line
\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \{\-\'5ccolortbl;\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \'5cred0\'5cgreen0\'5cblue0;\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \'5cred0\'5cgreen0\'5cblue255;\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \'5cred0\'5cgreen255\'5cblue0;\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \'5cred255\'5cgreen0\'5cblue0;\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \'5cred255\'5cgreen255\'5cblue255;\b \line
\line
\plain\f1\fs16 #\{\-\'5cfootnote graphics_cont\'7d\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 $\{\-\'5cfootnote Graphics\'7d\line
\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \'5cpard\'5cplain \'5cli120\'5csb340\'5csa120\'5csl-320 \'5cf3\'5cfs28 Graphics\b \line
\line
\plain\f1\fs16 \'5cpar \'5cpard\'5cplain \'5cli120 \'5cf4\'5cfs20 Use graphic images to illustrate concepts and present \b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 information visually. Graphics include line art, icons, screen shots of the interface, \line
and graphics with hotspots ("hypergraphics").\line
\'5cpar\line
\'7d
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 As noted earlier, the entire RTF file is one group, so the file begins and ends with braces (\b \{\-\'7d\plain\f1\fs16 ). Other groups nested within the group containing the file define the header and text information.
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Required Entries

The following entries must appear in the order shown immediately after the opening brace of the RTF file:

·	The RTF token \rtfn. This RTF token identifies the version (given by the number n) of the RTF standard used in the file. For Help Workshop, this RTF token must be \rtf1.

·	An RTF token identifying the character set used in the file. The RTF character set described in this appendix corresponds to the \ansi character set statement.

System default values, such as font number (\deffn statement), follow the character set statement. The \deffn statement specifies which font defined in the font table (see the next section) is the default font for the file.

The required entries appear as follows in the sample RTF file:

{-\rtf1\ansi\deff0
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf

\b\fs24 Required Entries
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The following entries must appear in the order shown immediately after the opening brace of the RTF file:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 The RTF token \b \'5crtf\plain\f1\fs16 n. This RTF token identifies the version (given by the number n) of the RTF standard used in the file. For Help Workshop, this RTF token must be \b \'5crtf1\plain\f1\fs16 .
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \f1\fs16 An RTF token identifying the character set used in the file. The RTF character set described in this appendix corresponds to the \b \'5cansi\plain\f1\fs16  character set statement.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 System default values, such as font number (\'5c\b deff\plain\f1\fs16 n statement), follow the character set statement. The \b \'5cdeff\plain\f1\fs16 n statement specifies which font defined in the font table (see the next section) is the default font for the file.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 The required entries appear as follows in the sample RTF file:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li715\ri125\tx295 \{\-\'5crtf1\'5cansi\'5cdeff0
\par
```

## 381. Font table (RTF)

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:0173`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:0173
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Font table (RTF)`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Font table (RTF)
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `KOO4SQ`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    KOO4SQ
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `groups1`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    groups1
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `font tables;groups;groups, font table;RTF files;RTF files, font table`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    font tables;groups;groups, font table;RTF files;RTF files, font table
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Font Table

The first group listed after the opening statements is the font table group. All the fonts that the RTF file uses must be declared in the font table. These fonts should correspond to the fonts that are present on the user's computer.

The font table group has the following form:

{-\fonttbl

{-\ffont-number\ffont-family font-name;}

{-\ffont-number\ffont-family font-name;}

.

.

.

}

The actual font table is the group beginning with the RTF token \fonttbl. Each font is defined as a separate group within the font-table group. Each font definition has three components: the font-number, the font-family, and the font-name.

The font-number is an integer that identifies the font.

The font-family is one of the following standard font families defined by the Windows operating system:

Control Word	Font Family	Examples

fnil	Default or unknown

froman	Roman	MS Serif and Palatino

fswiss	Swiss	Helvetica, MS Sans Serif

fmodern	Modern	Courier, Elite, Pica

fscript	Script	Cursive, Script, Zapf Chancery

fdecor	Decorative	Old English, Zapf Dingbats

ftech	Technical	Symbol

Each font definition must end with a semicolon (;) immediately before the closing brace.

If the RTF file uses a default font, the RTF token \defffont-number appears before the font-table group. The font-number is an integer indicating which font in the table is used for the default.

Example

The following group defines nine fonts:

{bmc bm4.bmp}	{-\fonttbl

{-\f0\froman Times New Roman;}

{-\f1\froman Symbol;}

{-\f2\fswiss Arial;}

{-\f3\froman MS Serif;}

{-\f4\fswiss MS Sans Serif;}

{-\f5\fmodern Courier;}

{-\f6\fdecor Zapf Dingbats;}

{-\f7\fswiss Helvetica Condensed;}

{-\f8\fswiss Helvetica;}

}

This example defines the following fonts:

Font #	Font Family	Font

0	Roman	Times New Roman

1	Technical	Symbol

2	Swiss	Arial

3	Roman	MS Serif

4	Swiss	MS Sans Serif

5	Modern	Courier

6	Decorative	Zapf Dingbats

7	Swiss	Helvetica Condensed

8	Swiss	Helvetica
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Font Table
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The first group listed after the opening statements is the font table group. All the fonts that the RTF file uses must be declared in the font table. These fonts should correspond to the fonts that are present on the user's computer.
\par The font table group has the following form:
\par \pard\keep\sb75\sl-235\li715\ri125 \b \{\-\'5cfonttbl\line
\{\-\'5cf\plain\f1\fs16 font-number\b \'5cf\plain\f1\fs16 font-family\b  \plain\f1\fs16 font-name\b ;\'7d\line
\{\-\'5cf\plain\f1\fs16 font-number\b \'5cf\plain\f1\fs16 font-family\b  \plain\f1\fs16 font-name\b ;\'7d\line
\plain\f1\fs16 .\line
.\line
.\line
\b \'7d
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The actual font table is the group beginning with the RTF token \b \'5cfonttbl\plain\f1\fs16 . Each font is defined as a separate group within the font-table group. Each font definition has three components: the font-number, the font-family, and the font-name.
\par The font-number is an integer that identifies the font.
\par The font-family is one of the following standard font families defined by the Windows operating system:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li3325\ri125\fi-3195\brdrb\brdrs\tx1525\tx3325 \b Control Word\tab Font Family\tab Examples
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li3325\ri125\fi-3195\tx1525\tx3325 fnil\plain\f1\fs16 \tab Default or unknown\tab 
\par \b froman\plain\f1\fs16 \tab Roman\tab MS Serif and Palatino
\par \b fswiss\plain\f1\fs16 \tab Swiss\tab Helvetica, MS Sans Serif
\par \b fmodern\plain\f1\fs16 \tab Modern\tab Courier, Elite, Pica
\par \b fscript\plain\f1\fs16 \tab Script\tab Cursive, Script, Zapf Chancery
\par \b fdecor\plain\f1\fs16 \tab Decorative\tab Old English, Zapf Dingbats
\par \b ftech\plain\f1\fs16 \tab Technical\tab Symbol
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1525\tx3325 Each font definition must end with a semicolon (;) immediately before the closing brace.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1525\tx3325 If the RTF file uses a default font, the RTF token \b \'5cdeff\plain\f1\fs16 font-number appears before the font-table group. The font-number is an integer indicating which font in the table is used for the default.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1525\tx3325 \b Example
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1525\tx3325 \plain\f1\fs16 The following group defines nine fonts:
\par \pard\keep\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{bmc bm4.bmp\}\tab \{\-\'5cfonttbl\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \{\-\'5cf0\'5cfroman Times New Roman;\'7d\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \{\-\'5cf1\'5cfroman Symbol;\'7d\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \{\-\'5cf2\'5cfswiss Arial;\'7d\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \{\-\'5cf3\'5cfroman MS Serif;\'7d\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \{\-\'5cf4\'5cfswiss MS Sans Serif;\'7d\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \{\-\'5cf5\'5cfmodern Courier;\'7d\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \{\-\'5cf6\'5cfdecor Zapf Dingbats;\'7d\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \{\-\'5cf7\'5cfswiss Helvetica Condensed;\'7d\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \{\-\'5cf8\'5cfswiss Helvetica;\'7d\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \'7d
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 This example defines the following fonts:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\brdrb\brdrs\tx985\tx2335 \b Font #\tab Font Family\tab Font
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx985\tx2335 0\plain\f1\fs16 \tab Roman\tab Times New Roman
\par \b 1\plain\f1\fs16 \tab Technical\tab Symbol
\par \b 2\plain\f1\fs16 \tab Swiss\tab Arial
\par \b 3\plain\f1\fs16 \tab Roman\tab MS Serif
\par \b 4\plain\f1\fs16 \tab Swiss\tab MS Sans Serif
\par \b 5\plain\f1\fs16 \tab Modern\tab Courier
\par \b 6\tab \plain\f1\fs16 Decorative\tab Zapf Dingbats
\par \b 7\tab \plain\f1\fs16 Swiss\tab Helvetica Condensed
\par \b 8\plain\f1\fs16 \tab Swiss\tab Helvetica
\par
```

## 382. Color table (RTF)

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`


- Marker `#`

- Marker `>`

- Marker `A`

- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `color tables;groups;groups, color-table;RTF files;RTF files, color table`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    color tables;groups;groups, color-table;RTF files;RTF files, color table
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Color Table

If the Help file uses color, the RTF file must also define a color table group to specify the individual colors. The color table group has the following form:

{-\colortbl;

\rednumber\greennumber\bluenumber;

\rednumber\greennumber\bluenumber;

.

.

.

}

Each definition specifies a value in the range 0 through 255 for the red, green, and blue components of the color. Unlike font entries in the font table, color entries in the color table do not specify a color number. Instead, Help Workshop assumes that the first color defined is color zero, the second color is color one, and so on.

Example

The following example defines the standard 16 colors in the Windows palette:

{bmc bm4.bmp}	{-\colortbl;

\red0\green0\blue0;

\red0\green0\blue255;

\red0\green255\blue255;

\red0\green255\blue0;

\red255\green0\blue255;

\red255\green0\blue0;

\red255\green255\blue0;

\red255\green255\blue255;

\red0\green0\blue127;

\red0\green127\blue127;

\red0\green127\blue0;

\red127\green0\blue127;

\red127\green0\blue0;

\red127\green127\blue0;

\red127\green127\blue127;

\red192\green192\blue192;

}
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Color Table
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 If the Help file uses color, the RTF file must also define a color table group to specify the individual colors. The color table group has the following form:
\par \pard\keep\sb75\sl-235\li715\ri125 \{\-\'5ccolortbl;\line
\'5crednumber\'5cgreennumber\'5cbluenumber;\line
\'5crednumber\'5cgreennumber\'5cbluenumber;\line
.\line
.\line
.\line
\'7d
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 Each definition specifies a value in the range 0 through 255 for the red, green, and blue components of the color. Unlike font entries in the font table, color entries in the color table do not specify a color number. Instead, Help Workshop assumes that the first color defined is color zero, the second color is color one, and so on.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Example
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The following example defines the standard 16 colors in the Windows palette:
\par \pard\keep\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{bmc bm4.bmp\}\tab \{\-\'5ccolortbl;\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \'5cred0\'5cgreen0\'5cblue0;\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \'5cred0\'5cgreen0\'5cblue255;\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \'5cred0\'5cgreen255\'5cblue255;\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \'5cred0\'5cgreen255\'5cblue0;\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \'5cred255\'5cgreen0\'5cblue255;\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \'5cred255\'5cgreen0\'5cblue0;\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \'5cred255\'5cgreen255\'5cblue0;\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \'5cred255\'5cgreen255\'5cblue255;\b \line
```

## 383. Style sheet (RTF)

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:0175`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:0175
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Style sheet (RTF)`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Style sheet (RTF)
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `23LQBV4`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    23LQBV4
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `main`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    main
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `groups1`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    groups1
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `groups;groups, style-sheet;RTF files;RTF files, style sheet;style sheets`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    groups;groups, style-sheet;RTF files;RTF files, style sheet;style sheets
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Style Sheet

In the standard RTF specification, the style sheet group defines the different styles defined for a document. In Word for Windows, a style is a named combination of formats (including those for characters, paragraphs, tabs, borders, and frames) that authors can apply to text using a defined sequence of keystrokes. Authors can add styles to a document template.

Help Workshop ignores the style sheet group in an RTF file. If you plan to use the RTF file only to create a Help file, you can omit the style sheet group from the file to save space. However, if you also plan to print documents from the RTF file, you can include a style sheet group to define styles for the document. Then, when you open the RTF file in Word for Windows, you can apply the styles that you have defined to text.

A style sheet group has the following format:

{-\stylesheet

{-\sstyle-num\style-specs style-name;}

{-\sstyle-num\style-specs style-name;}

.

.

.

}

Each style definition appears in a group within the style sheet group. The style definition is identified by a unique style number.

The exact style specifications follow the style number. The style specifications can include RTF tokens for any type of character, text, tab, border, or frame formatting.

The last part of the style definition is the style name. This is the name that appears in the Word for Windows drop-down list boxes that list the document's styles. A single space must separate the style specifications from the style name.

Each style definition must end with a semicolon (;) immediately before the closing brace.

In Word for Windows, styles may be based on previously defined styles. In this case, the style inherits the attributes of the previously defined style. A style may also define a style to be applied to the next paragraph in the document. The following RTF tokens may appear in the style sheet to activate these features:

Control Word	Meaning

\sbasedonstyle-num	The style defined in this group inherits the attributes of the style with the given style number. If this style does not inherit attributes from any style, style-num is 0.

\snextstyle-num	The style with the given style number is applied to the next paragraph in the document. If no style is to be applied to the next paragraph in the document, style-num is 0.

Example

Here is a portion of a sample style sheet:

{bmc bm4.bmp}	{-\stylesheet

{-\s229\li2160\ri720\tldot\tx8280\tqr\tx8640 \f5\fs20\lang1033 \sbasedon0\snext0 toc 4;}

.

.

.

{-\s17\qc\sb240\sl240\keepn \b\f5\fs28\lang1033 \sbasedon0\snext17 head;}

{-\s18\qj\sb240\sl240 \b\f5\fs20\lang1033 \sbasedon0\snext18 Company;}

{-\s19\qj\sb120\sl240\keepn \i\f5\fs20\lang1033 \sbasedon0\snext19 Product;}

}

This portion of the style sheet defines four styles numbered 229 (named "toc 4"), 17 (named "head"), 18 (named "Company"), and 19 (named "Product"). Style sheets 17 through 19 assume that the same style will be applied to the next paragraph as well as the paragraph to which the style is currently assigned. None of these styles inherits the attributes of a previously defined style.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Style Sheet
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 In the standard RTF specification, the style sheet group defines the different styles defined for a document. In Word for Windows, a style is a named combination of formats (including those for characters, paragraphs, tabs, borders, and frames) that authors can apply to text using a defined sequence of keystrokes. Authors can add styles to a document template.
\par Help Workshop ignores the style sheet group in an RTF file. If you plan to use the RTF file only to create a Help file, you can omit the style sheet group from the file to save space. However, if you also plan to print documents from the RTF file, you can include a style sheet group to define styles for the document. Then, when you open the RTF file in Word for Windows, you can apply the styles that you have defined to text.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 A style sheet group has the following format:
\par \pard\keep\sb75\sl-235\li715\ri125 \b \{\-\'5cstylesheet\line
\{\-\'5cs\plain\f1\fs16 style-num\b \'5c\plain\f1\fs16 style-specs style-name\b ;\'7d\line
\{\-\'5cs\plain\f1\fs16 style-num\b \'5c\plain\f1\fs16 style-specs style-name\b ;\'7d\line
\plain\f1\fs16 .\line
.\line
.\line
\b \'7d
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Each style definition appears in a group within the style sheet group. The style definition is identified by a unique style number.
\par The exact style specifications follow the style number. The style specifications can include RTF tokens for any type of character, text, tab, border, or frame formatting.
\par The last part of the style definition is the style name. This is the name that appears in the Word for Windows drop-down list boxes that list the document's styles. A single space must separate the style specifications from the style name.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 Each style definition must end with a semicolon (\b ;\plain\f1\fs16 ) immediately before the closing brace.
\par In Word for Windows, styles may be based on previously defined styles. In this case, the style inherits the attributes of the previously defined style. A style may also define a style to be applied to the next paragraph in the document. The following RTF tokens may appear in the style sheet to activate these features:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li2345\ri125\fi-2215\brdrb\brdrs\tx2335 \b Control Word\tab Meaning
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li2345\ri125\fi-2215\tx2335 \'5csbasedon\plain\f1\fs16 style-num\tab The style defined in this group inherits the attributes of the style with the given style number. If this style does not inherit attributes from any style, style-num is 0.
\par \b \'5csnext\plain\f1\fs16 style-num\tab The style with the given style number is applied to the next paragraph in the document. If no style is to be applied to the next paragraph in the document, style-num is 0.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx2335 \b Example
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx2335 \plain\f1\fs16 Here is a portion of a sample style sheet:
\par \pard\keep\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{bmc bm4.bmp\}\tab \{\-\'5cstylesheet\line
\{\-\'5cs229\'5cli2160\'5cri720\'5ctldot\'5ctx8280\'5ctqr\'5ctx8640 \'5cf5\'5cfs20\'5clang1033 \'5csbasedon0\'5csnext0 toc 4;\'7d\line
.\line
.\line
.\line
\{\-\'5cs17\'5cqc\'5csb240\'5csl240\'5ckeepn \'5cb\'5cf5\'5cfs28\'5clang1033 \'5csbasedon0\'5csnext17 head;\'7d\line
\{\-\'5cs18\'5cqj\'5csb240\'5csl240 \'5cb\'5cf5\'5cfs20\'5clang1033 \'5csbasedon0\'5csnext18 Company;\'7d\line
\{\-\'5cs19\'5cqj\'5csb120\'5csl240\'5ckeepn \'5ci\'5cf5\'5cfs20\'5clang1033 \'5csbasedon0\'5csnext19 Product;\'7d\line
\'7d
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 This portion of the style sheet defines four styles numbered 229 (named "toc 4"), 17 (named "head"), 18 (named "Company"), and 19 (named "Product"). Style sheets 17 through 19 assume that the same style will be applied to the next paragraph as well as the paragraph to which the style is currently assigned. None of these styles inherits the attributes of a previously defined style.
\par
```

## 384. Topic information (RTF)

- Marker `>`

    ```rtf
    main
    ```

  - Raw:

    ```rtf
    groups1;TopicInfo
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `groups;groups, footnote;RTF files;RTF files, topic information;topic files;topic files, about topic information in RTF files;topic information in RTF files;topics,;topics, about topic information in RTF files`
  - Raw:

    ```rtf
    groups;groups, footnote;RTF files;RTF files, topic information;topic files;topic files, about topic information in RTF files;topic information in RTF files;topics,;topics, about topic information in RTF files
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Topic Information

After setting up the required RTF entries for the Help file, you define the topic information. Topics consist of plain text and graphics that the user sees, Help Workshop-specific information that is defined in topic footnotes, character and paragraph formatting information, and page break delimiters.

Font and Formatting Information

Before any text is placed in the RTF file, the font name and font size must be specified. The \fn statement specifies the font name (n matches the font number defined in the font table). The \fsn statement specifies the font size (n is given in half-points). For example, using the sample font table just described, the following example defines the text as 10-point MS Sans Serif:

{bmc bm4.bmp}	\f4\fs20

If you want the text to have any special formatting characteristics, you must also define those before you write out the plain text. Help Workshop supports a number of character and paragraph formatting attributes that you can use to change the appearance and placement of text and graphics.

The following example defines the topic title text as 14-point MS Serif with 17 points of space before, 6 points of space after, and 16 points of leading. The topic title paragraph is also indented 6 points from the left margin:

{bmc bm4.bmp}	\pard\plain \li120\sb340\sa120\sl-320 \f3\fs28

Help Features in Footnote Groups

The following types of Help information are specified in \footnote groups:

·	Topic IDsAN2912>tables

·	Topic titles5WM68K>tables

·	Browse sequences142WH9O>tables

·	Keyword index entries1AV0ZGN>tables

·	Help macros run on topic entryBA77J.>tables

·	Build tagsJ6P1I>tables

·	CommentsBPRKVR>tables

·	Window typeVKJ5IQ>tables

Help Features Embedded in Text

Some Help features are implemented using common RTF commands or special codes embedded within text. The following types of Help features can be embedded in text:

·	Layout features like nonscrolling regions and nonwrapping text1MHELPH>tables

·	Hotspots for activating jumps, displaying pop-up windows, and running Help macrosXRUN4K>tables

·	Bitmaps2.7R04N>tables

·	Embedded panes for custom DLL objects8F428Y>tables

Topic End

When there is more than one topic in an RTF file, each topic must end with a \page statement.

Ending the last topic in a file with a \page statement is optional.

{button ,AL("RTF_SAMPLE_TOPIC;topic_info",0,"",`tables')} Related Topics%!AL("RTF_SAMPLE_TOPIC;topic_info",0,"",`tables')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Topic Information
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 After setting up the required RTF entries for the Help file, you define the topic information. Topics consist of plain text and graphics that the user sees, Help Workshop-specific information that is defined in topic footnotes, character and paragraph formatting information, and page break delimiters.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Font and Formatting Information
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Before any text is placed in the RTF file, the font name and font size must be specified. The \b \'5cf\plain\f1\fs16 n statement specifies the font name (n matches the font number defined in the font table). The \b \'5cfs\plain\f1\fs16 n statement specifies the font size (n is given in half-points). For example, using the sample font table just described, the following example defines the text as 10-point MS Sans Serif: 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{bmc bm4.bmp\}\tab \'5cf4\'5cfs20
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 If you want the text to have any special formatting characteristics, you must also define those before you write out the plain text. Help Workshop supports a number of character and paragraph formatting attributes that you can use to change the appearance and placement of text and graphics.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 The following example defines the topic title text as 14-point MS Serif with 17 points of space before, 6 points of space after, and 16 points of leading. The topic title paragraph is also indented 6 points from the left margin:
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{bmc bm4.bmp\}\tab \'5cpard\'5cplain \'5cli120\'5csb340\'5csa120\'5csl-320 \'5cf3\'5cfs28
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Help Features in Footnote Groups
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 The following types of Help information are specified in \b \'5cfootnote\plain\f1\fs16  groups:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \uldb \f1\fs16 Topic IDs\plain\f1\fs16 {\v AN2912>tables}
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \uldb \f1\fs16 Topic titles\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 5WM68K>tables}
\par \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \uldb \f1\fs16 Browse sequences\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 142WH9O>tables}
\par \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \uldb \f1\fs16 Keyword index entries\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 1AV0ZGN>tables}
\par \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \uldb \f1\fs16 Help macros run on topic entry\plain\f1\fs16 {\v BA77J.>tables}
\par \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \uldb \f1\fs16 Build tags\plain\f1\fs16 {\v J6P1I>tables}
\par \f2 \'b7\f3\fs12 \tab \uldb \f1\fs16 Comments\plain\f1\fs16 {\v BPRKVR>tables}
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab \uldb Window type\plain\f1\fs16 {\v VKJ5IQ>tables}
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Help Features Embedded in Text
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 Some Help features are implemented using common RTF commands or special codes embedded within text. The following types of Help features can be embedded in text:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab \uldb Layout features like nonscrolling regions and nonwrapping text\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 1MHELPH>tables}
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab \uldb Hotspots for activating jumps, displaying pop-up windows, and running Help macros\plain\f1\fs16 {\v XRUN4K>tables}
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab \uldb Bitmaps\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 2.7R04N>tables}
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab \uldb Embedded panes for custom DLL objects\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 8F428Y>tables}
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Topic End
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 When there is more than one topic in an RTF file, each topic must end with a \b \'5cpage\plain\f1\fs16  statement.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 Ending the last topic in a file with a \b \'5cpage\plain\f1\fs16  statement is optional.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL("RTF_SAMPLE_TOPIC;topic_info",0,"",`tables')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL("RTF_SAMPLE_TOPIC;topic_info",0,"",`tables')}
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Topic IDs

Topics within a Help file are usually identified by a unique topic ID. The following group specifies a topic ID for a topic:

#{-\footnote topic ID}

A topic ID is any string of up to 255 characters. You can use any character except #, =, >, @, !, or %. The first character of a topic ID cannot be a numeric character.

For example, this RTF entry defines the topic ID "main contents":

{bmc bm4.bmp}	#{-\footnote main contents}

{button ,AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')} Related Topics%!AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Topic IDs
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Topics within a Help file are usually identified by a unique topic ID. The following group specifies a topic ID for a topic:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li715\ri125 \b #\{\-\'5cfootnote\plain\f1\fs16  topic ID\b \'7d
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx8995 \plain\f1\fs16 A topic ID is any string of up to 255 characters. You can use any character except \b #\plain\f1\fs16 , \b =\plain\f1\fs16 , \b >\plain\f1\fs16 , \b @\plain\f1\fs16 , \b !\plain\f1\fs16 , or \b %\plain\f1\fs16 . The first character of a topic ID cannot be a numeric character.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx8995 For example, this RTF entry defines the topic ID "main contents":
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{bmc bm4.bmp\}\tab #\{\-\'5cfootnote main contents\'7d
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')}
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Topic Titles

Typically, a topic will also contain a topic title, which identifies the topic in Help dialog boxes. The following group specifies a topic title:

${-\footnote title-text}

A title string is any string of up to 50 characters. Any printable ASCII character may appear in a topic title, except the following reserved characters: # = + > @ * % !

Topic titles must not begin with a number. ASCII characters such as braces ({- }), brackets ([ ]), and backslashes (\) that are used as special characters in RTF must be prefixed by backslashes.

This example defines the title "Saving a Document":

{bmc bm4.bmp}	${-\footnote Saving a Document}

{button ,AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')} Related Topics%!AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Topic Titles
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Typically, a topic will also contain a topic title, which identifies the topic in Help dialog boxes. The following group specifies a topic title:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li715\ri125 \b $\{\-\'5cfootnote\plain\f1\fs16  title-text\b \'7d\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 A title string is any string of up to 50 characters. Any printable ASCII character may appear in a topic title, except the following reserved characters: # = + > @ * % ! 
\par Topic titles must not begin with a number. ASCII characters such as braces (\{\- \'7d), brackets ([ ]), and backslashes (\'5c) that are used as special characters in RTF must be prefixed by backslashes.
\par This example defines the title "Saving a Document":
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{bmc bm4.bmp\}\tab $\{\-\'5cfootnote Saving a Document\'7d
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')}
\par
```

## 387. Browse sequences (RTF)

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `A`

  - Raw:

- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `browse sequences;groups;groups, browse sequences;RTF files;RTF files, browse sequences`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    browse sequences;groups;groups, browse sequences;RTF files;RTF files, browse sequences
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Browse Sequences

A topic may belong to a single browse sequence, which is a group of topics the user can view in forward or backward sequence.

The following group assigns a topic to a browse sequence and to one or more topic groups:

+{-\footnote [sequence-name][:sequence-number]}

The sequence-name is the name of the browse sequence to which the topic is assigned. It may be omitted if the Help file has only one browse sequence.

The sequence-name may be followed by a sequence-number, which indicates where in the sequence the topic appears. In browse sequences, topics appear in order of sequence numbers. If no sequence number appears, topics in the sequence appear in their physical order within the file. If the [FILES] section of the Help project (.HPJ) file lists more than one RTF file, topics from the first RTF file appear first in the browse sequence, then topics from the second RTF file, and so on.

A colon must immediately precede the sequence number, if it appears. If a sequence name also appears, a colon must separate the sequence name and sequence number.

{button ,AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')} Related Topics%!AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Browse Sequences
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 A topic may belong to a single browse sequence, which is a group of topics the user can view in forward or backward sequence.
\par The following group assigns a topic to a browse sequence and to one or more topic groups:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li715\ri125 \b +\{\-\'5cfootnote\plain\f1\fs16  [sequence-name][\b :\plain\f1\fs16 sequence-number]\b \'7d\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 The sequence-name is the name of the browse sequence to which the topic is assigned. It may be omitted if the Help file has only one browse sequence.
\par The sequence-name may be followed by a sequence-number, which indicates where in the sequence the topic appears. In browse sequences, topics appear in order of sequence numbers. If no sequence number appears, topics in the sequence appear in their physical order within the file. If the [FILES] section of the Help project (.HPJ) file lists more than one RTF file, topics from the first RTF file appear first in the browse sequence, then topics from the second RTF file, and so on.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 A colon must immediately precede the sequence number, if it appears. If a sequence name also appears, a colon must separate the sequence name and sequence number.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')}
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Keyword Entries

There are three kinds of keyword entries: K-keywords, A-keywords, and multi-index keywords. Each type of entry has its own topic footnote.

K-keywords

K-keywords, denoted by the custom footnote mark 'K,' are used for searches with the Index button. The Index tab in the Help Topics dialog box displays a list of the keywords defined for topics in the Help file. From the Index, the user can jump to any topic in which a keyword has been defined. K-keywords are also used by the KLink macro to provide inter-topic jumps to the topics having the keywords specified as the macro's parameters.

Multi-index keywords

Multi-index keywords are denoted by any alpha character except 'A' and 'K.' They have identical index capabilities to K-keywords, but are intended for alternate indexes.

A-keywords

A-keywords, denoted by the custom footnote mark 'A,' are used for ALink macro jumps only. They do not appear in any index.

Examples

The following group assigns one or more k-keywords to a topic:

K{-\footnote keyword;[keyword;]...}

The following group assigns one or more a-keywords to a topic:

A{-\footnote keyword;[keyword;]...}

The following group assigns one or more multi-index keywords to a topic for an index referenced to R-keywords:

R{-\footnote keyword;[keyword;]...}

Keywords must be separated by semicolons (;).

{button ,AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')} Related Topics%!AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Keyword Entries
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 There are three kinds of keyword entries: K-keywords, A-keywords, and multi-index keywords. Each type of entry has its own topic footnote.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b K-keywords
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 K-keywords, denoted by the custom footnote mark 'K,' are used for searches with the Index button. The Index tab in the Help Topics dialog box displays a list of the keywords defined for topics in the Help file. From the Index, the user can jump to any topic in which a keyword has been defined. K-keywords are also used by the KLink macro to provide inter-topic jumps to the topics having the keywords specified as the macro's parameters.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Multi-index keywords
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Multi-index keywords are denoted by any alpha character except 'A' and 'K.' They have identical index capabilities to K-keywords, but are intended for alternate indexes.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b A-keywords
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 A-keywords, denoted by the custom footnote mark 'A,' are used for \b ALink\plain\f1\fs16  macro jumps only. They do not appear in any index.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Examples
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The following group assigns one or more \scaps k\plain\f1\fs16 -keywords to a topic:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li715\ri125 \b K\{\-\'5cfootnote\plain\f1\fs16  keyword\b ;\plain\f1\fs16 [keyword\b ;\plain\f1\fs16 ]...\b \'7d
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The following group assigns one or more \scaps a\plain\f1\fs16 -keywords to a topic:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li715\ri125 \b A\{\-\'5cfootnote\plain\f1\fs16  keyword\b ;\plain\f1\fs16 [keyword\b ;\plain\f1\fs16 ]...\b \'7d
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The following group assigns one or more multi-index keywords to a topic for an index referenced to R-keywords:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li715\ri125 \b R\{\-\'5cfootnote\plain\f1\fs16  keyword\b ;\plain\f1\fs16 [keyword\b ;\plain\f1\fs16 ]...\b \'7d
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Keywords must be separated by semicolons (\b ;\plain\f1\fs16 ).
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295
```

```text
Help can run commands automatically when the user jumps to a topic (from a hotspot, browse button, or search list).
```

## 390. Build tags (RTF)

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:0188`

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `J6P1I`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    J6P1I
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `build tags;groups;groups, build tags;RTF files;RTF files, build tags`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    build tags;groups;groups, build tags;RTF files;RTF files, build tags
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Build Tags

Build tags are labels used to exclude certain topics from a build. Topics without build tags are always included in the build. Topics with build tags are included or excluded using a build expression in the Help project (.HPJ) file.

The following group assigns one or more build tags to a topic:

*{-\footnote tag-name;[tag-name;]...}

Multiple build tags must be separated by semicolons (;).

Help Workshop supports two very different ways of processing build tags. If you use Help Workshop to specify the build tags in topics to include or exclude, then by default, any topic with a build tag will be excluded from the help file. If, however, you use another editor to add/edit the BUILD option and [BUILDTAGS] section, then all topics are included by default, whether or not they have a build tag.

{button ,AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')} Related Topics%!AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 
\b\fs24 Build Tags
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Build tags are labels used to exclude certain topics from a build. Topics without build tags are always included in the build. Topics with build tags are included or excluded using a build expression in the Help project (.HPJ) file.
\par The following group assigns one or more build tags to a topic:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li715\ri125 \b *\{\-\'5cfootnote\plain\f1\fs16  tag-name\b ;\plain\f1\fs16 [tag-name\b ;\plain\f1\fs16 ]...\b \'7d
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Multiple build tags must be separated by semicolons (\b ;\plain\f1\fs16 ).
\par Help Workshop supports two very different ways of processing build tags. If you use Help Workshop to specify the build tags in topics to include or exclude, then by default, any topic with a build tag will be excluded from the help file. If, however, you use another editor to add/edit the BUILD option and [BUILDTAGS] section, then all topics are included by default, whether or not they have a build tag.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')}
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Comments

Comments are any text that you want to include with a topic for your own purposes. Comments are ignored by Help Workshop.

The following group assigns a comment to a topic:

@{-\footnote comment-text}

Because comments are ignored, the comment-text can have as many standard characters as you want, including accented characters and spaces.

{button ,AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')} Related Topics%!AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')
```

```rtf
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li715\ri125 \b @\{\-\'5cfootnote \plain\f1\fs16 comment-text\b \'7d\plain\f1\fs16
```

```text
Layout Features

The following standard RTF commands are used to specify layout features in Help files:

Control Word	Meaning

\keepnkeepn_rtf>main	Makes affected text and pictures part of the nonscrolling region

\keepkeep_rtf>main	Makes affected text and pictures nonwrapping so they are truncated instead of wrapped if the window is resized
```

### WinHelp Footnotes

    ```rtf
    Hotspots (RTF)
    ```
- Marker `#`

  - Raw:

    ```rtf
    XRUN4K
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `tables`

- Marker `A`

  - Raw:

    ```rtf
    topic_info
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `groups;groups, hotspots;hotspots;hotspots, about hotspots;RTF files;RTF files, hotspots`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    groups;groups, hotspots;hotspots;hotspots, about hotspots;RTF files;RTF files, hotspots
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Hotspots

A hotspot is a region within a topic that performs an action when the user selects the region with the mouse. This action may be jumping to another topic, displaying a topic in a pop-up window, or running a Help macro.

The following group is used to create pop-up hotspots in a topic:

{-\ul hotspot text}{-\v [%|*]topic-ID}

The hotspot text is the topic text that performs the action when the user selects it. This text may be one of the standard \{-bmx\} RTF commands if the hotspot is a bitmap.

The percent sign (%) or asterisk (*) is used if you want to make the hotspot invisible (no green color, no underline) or just underlined (no green color).

The topic-ID identifies the topic to be displayed in the pop-up window.

The following groups are used to create jump or macro hotspots in a topic:

{-\uldb hotspot text}{-\v [%|*]hotspot action}

Or, you can use this format:

{-\strike hotspot text}{-\v [%|*]hotspot action}

The hotspot text is the topic text that performs the action when the user selects it. This text may be one of the standard \{-bmx\} RTF commands if the hotspot is a bitmap.

The percent sign (%) or asterisk (*) is used if you want to make the hotspot invisible (no green color, no underline) or just underlined (no green color).

The hotspot action may be one of the following:

·	A jump destination. This action has the following form:

topic-ID[@HLP-filename][>window-name]

The topic-ID identifies the topic the hotspot jumps to. When present, the second parameter (preceded by an at sign) identifies a different Help file in which the jump destination appears. If the jump destination is to appear in a secondary window, >window-type identifies the name of window in which the topic is to appear. The window-type must be defined in the [WINDOWS] section of the Help project (.HPJ) file.

·	A Help macro. This action has the following form:

!macro-string

The macro-string identifies the action Help performs when the user selects the hotspot. It can be any standard Help macro or any external DLL function registered in the [CONFIG] section of the project file.

If the hotspot text is invisible in the title, a percent sign (%) immediately precedes the hotspot action.

{button ,AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')} Related Topics%!AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Hotspots
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 A hotspot is a region within a topic that performs an action when the user selects the region with the mouse. This action may be jumping to another topic, displaying a topic in a pop-up window, or running a Help macro.
\par The following group is used to create pop-up hotspots in a topic:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li715\ri125 \b \{\-\'5cul\plain\f1\fs16  hotspot text\b \'7d\{\-\'5cv \plain\f1\fs16 [\b %|*\plain\f1\fs16 ]topic-ID\b \'7d
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The hotspot text is the topic text that performs the action when the user selects it. This text may be one of the standard \'5c\b \{\-bm\plain\f1\fs16 x\b \'5c\'7d\plain\f1\fs16  RTF commands if the hotspot is a bitmap.
\par The percent sign (%) or asterisk (*) is used if you want to make the hotspot invisible (no green color, no underline) or just underlined (no green color).
\par The topic-ID identifies the topic to be displayed in the pop-up window.
\par The following groups are used to create jump or macro hotspots in a topic:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li715\ri125 \b \{\-\'5culdb\plain\f1\fs16  hotspot text\b \'7d\{\-\'5cv \plain\f1\fs16 [\b %|*\plain\f1\fs16 ]hotspot action\b \'7d
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Or, you can use this format:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li715\ri125 \b \{\-\'5cstrike\plain\f1\fs16  hotspot text\b \'7d\{\-\'5cv \plain\f1\fs16 [\b %|*\plain\f1\fs16 ]hotspot action\b \'7d
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The hotspot text is the topic text that performs the action when the user selects it. This text may be one of the standard \'5c\b \{\-bm\plain\f1\fs16 x\b \'5c\'7d\plain\f1\fs16  RTF commands if the hotspot is a bitmap.
\par The percent sign (%) or asterisk (*) is used if you want to make the hotspot invisible (no green color, no underline) or just underlined (no green color).
\par The hotspot action may be one of the following:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab A jump destination. This action has the following form:
\par \pard\keep\sb55\sl-235\li295\ri125\tx295 topic-ID[\b @\plain\f1\fs16 HLP-filename][\b >\plain\f1\fs16 window-name]
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li295\ri125\tx295 The topic-ID identifies the topic the hotspot jumps to. When present, the second parameter (preceded by an at sign) identifies a different Help file in which the jump destination appears. If the jump destination is to appear in a secondary window, \b >\plain\f1\fs16 window-type identifies the name of window in which the topic is to appear. The window-type must be defined in the [WINDOWS] section of the Help project (.HPJ) file.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab A Help macro. This action has the following form:
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li295\ri125\tx295 \b !\plain\f1\fs16 macro-string
\par The macro-string identifies the action Help performs when the user selects the hotspot. It can be any standard Help macro or any external DLL function registered in the \b [CONFIG]\plain\f1\fs16  section of the project file.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 If the hotspot text is invisible in the title, a percent sign (\b %\plain\f1\fs16 ) immediately precedes the hotspot action.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')}
\par
```

- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `bitmaps,;bitmaps, adding to topics;graphics;graphics, adding to topics;RTF files;RTF files, bitmaps`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    bitmaps,;bitmaps, adding to topics;graphics;graphics, adding to topics;RTF files;RTF files, bitmaps
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Bitmaps

Bitmaps can be inserted into a topic with a command that tells Help the name of the bitmap file and how to position it. This command has the following form:

\{-bm[c | l | r] bitmapfile[; bitmapfile[;...]]]\}

The \{-bmx\} commands specify how the bitmap is aligned in the topic, as shown in the following table:

Control Word	Meaning

\{-bmc\}4AWD1BN>main	Positions the bitmap using character alignment. The bitmap is handled as if it were another character and it appears at the position in the line indicated by the reference. Text follows the bitmap, positioned at the base of the bitmap.

\{-bml\}11233NT>main	Positions the bitmap along the left margin, with text wrapping automatically along the right edge of the image.

\{-bmr\}11833NT>main	Positions the bitmap along the right margin, with text wrapping automatically along the left edge of the image.

The bitmap data is stored separately from the topic text, and a single copy of the bitmap is used for all the \{-bmc\}, \{-bml\}, and \{-bmr\} commands displaying that bitmap.

{button ,AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')} Related Topics%!AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Bitmaps
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Bitmaps can be inserted into a topic with a command that tells Help the name of the bitmap file and how to position it. This command has the following form:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li715\ri125 \b \'5c\{\-bm\plain\f1\fs16 [\b c |\plain\f1\fs16  \b l |\plain\f1\fs16  \b r\plain\f1\fs16 ] bitmapfile[\b ;\plain\f1\fs16  bitmapfile[\b ;\plain\f1\fs16 ...]]]\b \'5c\'7d\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 The \b \'5c\{\-bm\plain\f1\fs16 x\b \'5c\'7d\plain\f1\fs16  commands specify how the bitmap is aligned in the topic, as shown in the following table:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1705\ri125\fi-1575\brdrb\brdrs\tx1705 \b Control Word\tab Meaning
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1705\ri125\fi-1575\tx1705 \uldb \plain\f1\uldb\fs16 \'5c\{\-bmc\'5c\'7d\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 4AWD1BN>main}\tab Positions the bitmap using character alignment. The bitmap is handled as if it were another character and it appears at the position in the line indicated by the reference. Text follows the bitmap, positioned at the base of the bitmap.
\par \uldb \'5c\{\-bml\'5c\'7d\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 11233NT>main}\tab Positions the bitmap along the left margin, with text wrapping automatically along the right edge of the image.
\par \uldb \'5c\{\-bmr\'5c\'7d\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 11833NT>main}\tab Positions the bitmap along the right margin, with text wrapping automatically along the left edge of the image.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1705 The bitmap data is stored separately from the topic text, and a single copy of the bitmap is used for all the \'5c\{\-bmc\'5c\'7d, \'5c\{\-bml\'5c\'7d, and \'5c\{\-bmr\'5c\'7d commands displaying that bitmap. 
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')}
\par
```

## 396. Embedded windows (RTF)

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:018e`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:018e
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Embedded windows (RTF)`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Embedded windows (RTF)
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `8F428Y`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    8F428Y
    ```
- Marker `>`
  - Decoded: `tables`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    tables
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `topic_info`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    topic_info
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `embedded windows;RTF files;RTF files, embedded windows;windows,;windows, embedded`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    embedded windows;RTF files;RTF files, embedded windows;windows,;windows, embedded
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Embedded Windows

Embedded windows display text, pictures, or other objects in a window embedded within a Help topic. Authors can write their own custom DLLs to render elements in an embedded window.

An RTF entry for an embedded window has the following syntax:

\{-ew[c, l, r] DLL-name, window-class, author-data\}

The \{-ewx\} commands specify how the window is aligned in the topic, as shown in the following table:

Control Word	Meaning

\{-ewc\}ewc_rtf>main	Positions the window using character alignment. The window is handled as if it were another character and it appears at the position in the line indicated by the reference. Text follows the window, positioned at the base of the window.

\{-ewl\}7PA6CJ>main	Positions the window along the left margin, with text wrapping automatically along the right edge of the window.

\{-ewr\}7VA6CJ>main	Positions the window along the right margin, with text wrapping automatically along the left edge of the window.

The DLL-name is the filename of the DLL that renders the object in the window. This name should not have an extension, but it can have a relative path.

The window-class is the window-class name for the embedded pane as defined in the C source file for the DLL.

The author-data is a programmer-defined string passed to the DLL (in the WM_CREATE message) when Help creates the window.

{button ,AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')} Related Topics%!AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Embedded Windows
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Embedded windows display text, pictures, or other objects in a window embedded within a Help topic. Authors can write their own custom DLLs to render elements in an embedded window.
\par An RTF entry for an embedded window has the following syntax:
\par \b \'5c\{\-ew\plain\f1\fs16 [\b c\plain\f1\fs16 , \b l\plain\f1\fs16 , \b r\plain\f1\fs16 ] DLL-name, window-class, author-data\b \'5c\'7d\plain\f1\fs16 
\par The \b \'5c\{\-ew\plain\f1\fs16 x\b \'5c\'7d\plain\f1\fs16  commands specify how the window is aligned in the topic, as shown in the following table:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1705\ri125\fi-1575\brdrb\brdrs\tx1705 \b Control Word\tab Meaning
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1705\ri125\fi-1575\tx1705 \uldb \plain\f1\uldb\fs16 \'5c\{\-ewc\'5c\'7d\plain\f1\fs16 {\v ewc_rtf>main}\tab Positions the window using character alignment. The window is handled as if it were another character and it appears at the position in the line indicated by the reference. Text follows the window, positioned at the base of the window.
\par \uldb \'5c\{\-ewl\'5c\'7d\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7PA6CJ>main}\tab Positions the window along the left margin, with text wrapping automatically along the right edge of the window.
\par \uldb \'5c\{\-ewr\'5c\'7d\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7VA6CJ>main}\tab Positions the window along the right margin, with text wrapping automatically along the left edge of the window.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1705 The DLL-name is the filename of the DLL that renders the object in the window. This name should not have an extension, but it can have a relative path.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1705 The window-class is the window-class name for the embedded pane as defined in the C source file for the DLL. 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1705 The author-data is a programmer-defined string passed to the DLL (in the WM_CREATE message) when Help creates the window.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')}
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Sample Topic

In the sample RTF file, the following entries make up the single topic the file defines:

#{-\footnote graphics_cont}

${-\footnote Graphics}

\pard\plain \li120\sb340\sa120\sl-320 \f3\fs28 Graphics

\par \pard\plain \li120 \f4\fs20 Use graphic images to illustrate concepts and present

information visually. Graphics include line art, icons, screen shots of the interface,

and graphics with hotspots ("hypergraphics").

\par

{button ,AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')} Related Topics%!AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 


\b\fs24 Sample Topic
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 In the sample RTF file, the following entries make up the single topic the file defines:
\par \pard\keep\sb75\sl-235\li715\ri125 #\{\-\'5cfootnote graphics_cont\'7d\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 $\{\-\'5cfootnote Graphics\'7d\line
\b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 \'5cpard\'5cplain \'5cli120\'5csb340\'5csa120\'5csl-320 \'5cf3\'5cfs28 Graphics\b \line
\line
\plain\f1\fs16 \'5cpar \'5cpard\'5cplain \'5cli120 \'5cf4\'5cfs20 Use graphic images to illustrate concepts and present \b \line
\plain\f1\fs16 information visually. Graphics include line art, icons, screen shots of the interface, \line
and graphics with hotspots ("hypergraphics").\line
\'5cpar
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL("TopicInfo",0,"",`main')}
\par
```

- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `Help RTF tokens;RTF files;RTF files, about RTF tokens;RTF tokens;tokens, RTF`

    ```rtf
    Help RTF tokens;RTF files;RTF files, about RTF tokens;RTF tokens;tokens, RTF
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Overview of Help RTF token Support

Although Help Workshop supports many RTF tokens, it does not support them all. The following tables present the RTF tokens by group and briefly describe the statements that Help supports in this release of Help Workshop. The following tables are not intended to provide detailed information about the RTF tokens. For complete information, see the "Help RTF token Reference" topic.

Statement descriptions follow these conventions.

Convention	Meaning

Parentheses	Indicates the default value for the statement.

Overloaded	Indicates that the RTF token has a specific meaning in Help that is different from its print-based usage.

{button ,AL("overview")} Related Topics%!AL("overview")
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Overview of Help RTF token Support
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Although Help Workshop supports many RTF tokens, it does not support them all. The following tables present the RTF tokens by group and briefly describe the statements that Help supports in this release of Help Workshop. The following tables are not intended to provide detailed information about the RTF tokens. For complete information, see the "Help RTF token Reference" topic.
\par Statement descriptions follow these conventions.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1625\ri125\fi-1495\brdrb\brdrs\tx1615 \b Convention\tab Meaning
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1625\ri125\fi-1495\tx1615 \plain\f1\fs16 Parentheses\tab Indicates the default value for the statement.
\par Overloaded\tab Indicates that the RTF token has a specific meaning in Help that is different from its print-based usage.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL("overview")\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL("overview")}
\par
```

- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `Help RTF tokens;overloaded statements;RTF files;RTF files, overloaded statements;RTF tokens;tokens, RTF`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Help RTF tokens;overloaded statements;RTF files;RTF files, overloaded statements;RTF tokens;tokens, RTF
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Overloaded Statements

Help Workshop interprets some RTF tokens differently from their normal print-based usage. For example, standard RTF specifies that the \uldb statement indicates a double underline, but Help Workshop uses this statement to indicate a hotspot. The following table lists the RTF tokens that are overloaded for Help Workshop.

Statement	Print-based usage	Help Workshop usage

\footnote6M1HH_	Footnote	Special topic commands

\keepkeep_rtf	Keeps paragraph intact	Makes text nonwrapping

\keepnkeepn_rtf	Keeps paragraph with next	Creates a nonscrolling region at the top of the topic

\pagepage_rtf	Creates page break	Ends the current topic

\strike1NTK807	Creates strikethrough	Indicates a hotspot

\trqc1249FE.	Centers table row	Uses relative column widths

\ulul_rtf	Creates continuous underline	Indicates a link to a pop-up topic

\uldb12B7468	Creates double underline	Indicates a hotspot

\vv_rtf	Creates hidden text	Indicates the topic ID to jump to
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b\fs24 Overloaded Statements
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Help Workshop interprets some RTF tokens differently from their normal print-based usage. For example, standard RTF specifies that the \b \'5culdb\plain\f1\fs16  statement indicates a double underline, but Help Workshop uses this statement to indicate a hotspot. The following table lists the RTF tokens that are overloaded for Help Workshop.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li4315\ri125\fi-4185\brdrb\brdrs\tx1435\tx4315 \b Statement\tab Print-based usage\tab Help Workshop usage
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li4315\ri125\fi-4185\tx1435\tx4315 \uldb \plain\f1\uldb\fs16 \'5cfootnote\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 6M1HH_}\tab Footnote\tab Special topic commands
\par \uldb \'5ckeep\plain\f1\fs16 {\v keep_rtf}\tab Keeps paragraph intact\tab Makes text nonwrapping
\par \uldb \'5ckeepn\plain\f1\fs16 {\v keepn_rtf}\tab Keeps paragraph with next\tab Creates a nonscrolling region at the top of the topic
\par \uldb \'5cpage\plain\f1\fs16 {\v page_rtf}\tab Creates page break\tab Ends the current topic
\par \uldb \'5cstrike\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 1NTK807}\tab Creates strikethrough\tab Indicates a hotspot
\par \uldb \'5ctrqc\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 1249FE.}\tab Centers table row\tab Uses relative column widths
\par \uldb \'5cul\plain\f1\fs16 {\v ul_rtf}\tab Creates continuous underline\tab Indicates a link to a pop-up topic
\par \uldb \'5culdb\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 12B7468}\tab Creates double underline\tab Indicates a hotspot
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li4315\ri125\fi-4185\tx1435\tx4315 \uldb \'5cv\plain\f1\fs16 {\v v_rtf}\tab Creates hidden text\tab Indicates the topic ID to jump to
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Special Characters

If Help Workshop does not recognize a character, it ignores it.

For simplicity, ASCII 9 is treated the same as \tab and ASCII 10 is treated the same as \par. ASCII 13 is ignored. The control code \<10> is also ignored, even though it may be used to indicate a soft carriage return.

Statement	Meaning

\'hhH9XTYV	Hexadecimal value of specified character

\cellcell_rtf	End of table cell

\emdash1_S4HV.	Em dash character

\emspace1X9ISO4	Em space character

\endash99UDT3	En dash character

\enspaceGLD2DA	En space character

\ldblquoteXC5Y79	Left double quotation mark

\line1YHDZM6	Required line break; same as shift+enter

\lquoteD2X9GS	Left quotation mark

\pagepage_rtf	End of current topic. OVERLOADED

\parDL9__1	End of paragraph

\rdblquote12UI60T	Right double quotation mark

\row25GX_PL	End of table row

\rquote_.DCI4	Right quotation mark

\sectC83L2R	End of section and paragraph

\tabtab_rtf	Tab character
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\keepn\sb115\sa115\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\fs24 Special Characters
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 If Help Workshop does not recognize a character, it ignores it.
\par For simplicity, ASCII 9 is treated the same as \b \'5ctab\plain\f1\fs16  and ASCII 10 is treated the same as \b \'5cpar\plain\f1\fs16 . ASCII 13 is ignored. The control code \'5c<10> is also ignored, even though it may be used to indicate a soft carriage return.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1265\ri125\fi-1135\brdrb\brdrs\tx1255 \b Statement\tab Meaning
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1265\ri125\fi-1135\tx1255 \uldb \plain\f1\uldb\fs16 \'5c'hh\plain\f1\fs16 {\v H9XTYV}\tab Hexadecimal value of specified character
\par \uldb \'5ccell\plain\f1\fs16 {\v cell_rtf}\tab End of table cell
\par \uldb \'5cemdash\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 1_S4HV.}\tab Em dash character\uldb 
\par \'5cemspace\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 1X9ISO4}\tab Em space character\uldb 
\par \'5cendash\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 99UDT3}\tab En dash character\uldb 
\par \'5censpace\plain\f1\fs16 {\v GLD2DA}\tab En space character\uldb 
\par \'5cldblquote\plain\f1\fs16 {\v XC5Y79}\tab Left double quotation mark\uldb 
\par \'5cline\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 1YHDZM6}\tab Required line break; same as \scaps\fs17 shift\fs16 +\fs17 enter\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \uldb \'5clquote\plain\f1\fs16 {\v D2X9GS}\tab Left quotation mark\uldb 
\par \'5cpage\plain\f1\fs16 {\v page_rtf}\tab End of current topic. OVERLOADED
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1265\ri125\fi-1135\tx1255 \uldb \'5cpar\plain\f1\fs16 {\v DL9__1}\tab End of paragraph
\par \uldb \'5crdblquote\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 12UI60T}\tab Right double quotation mark\uldb 
\par \'5crow\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 25GX_PL}\tab End of table row
\par \uldb \'5crquote\plain\f1\fs16 {\v _.DCI4}\tab Right quotation mark\uldb 
\par \'5csect\plain\f1\fs16 {\v C83L2R}\tab End of section and paragraph
\par \uldb \'5ctab\plain\f1\fs16 {\v tab_rtf}\tab Tab character
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Destinations

A destination change resets all properties to their default values. Changes are legal only at the beginning of a group (statement and text enclosed in braces).

Statement	Meaning

\colortblIG1A_U	Color table
```

```text
Tables

The following statements specify table formatting properties:

Statement	Meaning

\cellxn8K00FX	Set absolute position of a table cell's right edge

\clmgfAABY8O	Mark first cell in a range of cells to be merged

\clmrgLBBY8O	Merge current cell with preceding cell

\trgaphnLLG5.9	Space between text in adjacent cells

\trleftn2N4IEVF	Set the position of left margin for the first cell in table row

\trowd3H85SN	Set table row defaults

\trqc1249FE.	Relative column widths. OVERLOADED

\trql12_9FE.	Left align text in each cell of table row (default)
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Help RTF token Reference

{bml bm14.shg}

The following is an alphabetical list of RTF tokens recognized by Help Workshop. To locate a particular statement, scroll down the topic or use the letter buttons above.

\ansi

The \ansi statement sets the American National Standards Institute (ANSI) character set. The Windows character set is essentially equivalent to the ANSI character set.

See Also

\windows1YTFWK

\b

The \b statement starts bold text. The statement applies to all subsequent text up to the next \plain or \b0 statement.

Comments

No \plain or \b0 statement is required if the \b statement and subsequent text are enclosed in braces. Braces limit the scope of a character property statement to the enclosed text only.

Example

The following example sets Note to bold:

{bmc bm4.bmp}	{-\b Note}   Setting the Auto option frees novice users from determining their system configurations.

See Also

\ii_rtf, \plain4KLX0ZP, \scapsAIIXHD

\bin

\binn

The \bin statement indicates the start of binary picture data. Help Workshop interprets subsequent bytes in the file as binary data. This statement is used in conjunction with the \pict statement.

Parameter	Description

n	Specifies the number of bytes of binary data following the statement.

Comments

A single space character must separate the \bin statement from subsequent bytes. Help Workshop assumes that all subsequent bytes, including linefeed and carriage return characters, are binary data. These bytes can have any value in the range 0 through 255. For this reason, the \bin statement is typically used in program-generated files only.

If the \bin statement is not given with a \pict statement, the default picture data format is hexadecimal.

See Also

\pict164LMJK

\bmc

\{-bmc filename\}

The bmc statement displays a specified bitmap or metafile in the current line of text. The statement positions the bitmap or metafile as if it were the next character in the line, aligning it on the base line and applying the current paragraph properties.

Parameter	Description

filename	Specifies the name of a file containing either a Windows bitmap, a placeable Windows metafile, a multi-resolution bitmap, or a segmented graphics bitmap.

Comments

Since the bmc statement is not a standard RTF token, Help Workshop relies on the opening and closing braces, including the backslashes (\), to distinguish the statement from regular text.

If a file containing a metafile is specified, the file must contain a placeable Windows metafile; Help Workshop will not accept standard Windows metafiles. Furthermore, Help Workshop sets the MM_ANISOTROPIC mode prior to displaying the metafile, so the placeable Windows metafile must either set the window origin and extents or set some other mapping mode.

Example

The following example inserts a bitmap representing a keyboard key in a paragraph:

{bmc bm4.bmp}	\par

Press the \{-bmc escape.bmp\} key to return to the main window.

\par

See Also

\bml11233NT, \bmr11833NT, \wbitmapwbitmap_rtf

\bml

\{-bml filename\}

The bml statement displays a specified bitmap or metafile at the left margin of the Help window. The first line of subsequent text aligns with the upper-right corner of the image and subsequent lines wrap along the right edge of the image.

Parameter	Description

filename	Specifies the name of a file containing either a Windows bitmap, a placeable Windows metafile, a multi-resolution bitmap, or a segmented graphics bitmap.

Comments

Since the bml statement is not a standard RTF token, Help Workshop relies on the opening and closing braces, including the backslashes (\), to distinguish the statement from regular text.

If a file containing a metafile is specified, the file must contain a placeable Windows metafile; Help Workshop will not accept standard Windows metafiles. Furthermore, Help Workshop sets the MM_ANISOTROPIC mode prior to displaying the metafile, so the placeable Windows metafile must either set the window origin and extents or set some other mapping mode.

Example

The following example places a bitmap at the left margin. The subsequent paragraph wraps around the bitmap:

{bmc bm4.bmp}	\par

\{-bml roadmap.bmp\}

The map at the left shows the easiest route to the school.

Although many people use Highway 125, there are fewer stops

and less traffic if you use Ames Road.

See Also

\bmc4AWD1BN, \bmr11833NT, \wbitmapwbitmap_rtf

\bmr

\{-bmr filename\}

The bmr statement displays a specified bitmap or metafile at the right margin of the Help window. The first line of subsequent text aligns with the upper-left corner of the image and subsequent lines wrap along the left edge of the image.

Parameter	Description

filename	Specifies the name of a file containing either a Windows bitmap, a placeable Windows metafile, a multi-resolution bitmap, or a segmented graphics bitmap.

Comments

Since the bmr statement is not a standard RTF token, Help Workshop relies on the opening and closing braces, including the backslashes (\), to distinguish the statement from regular text.

If a file containing a metafile is specified, the file must contain a placeable Windows metafile; Help Workshop will not accept standard Windows metafiles. Furthermore, Help Workshop sets the MM_ANISOTROPIC mode prior to displaying the metafile, so the placeable Windows metafile must either set the window origin and extents or set some other mapping mode.

Example

The following example places a bitmap at the right margin. The subsequent paragraph wraps around the bitmap:

{bmc bm4.bmp}	\par

\{-bmr roadmap.bmp\}

The map at the right shows the easiest route to the school.

Although many people use Highway 125, there are fewer stops

and less traffic if you use Ames Road.

See Also

\bmc4AWD1BN, \bml11233NT, \wbitmapwbitmap_rtf

\box

The \box statement draws a box around the current paragraph or picture. The statement applies to all subsequent paragraphs or pictures up to the next \pard statement.

Comments

For paragraphs, Help Workshop uses the height of the paragraph, excluding space before or after the paragraph, as the height of the box. For pictures (as defined by \pict statements), Help Workshop uses the specified height of the picture as the height of the box. For both paragraphs and pictures, the width of the box is equal to the space between the left and right indents.

WinHelp draws the box using the current border style.

Example

The following example draws a box around the paragraph:

{bmc bm4.bmp}	\par \box

{-\b Note}  Setting the Auto option frees novice users from

determining their system configurations.

\par \pard

See Also

\brdrb23WJX_P, \brdrl2EQYMK, \brdrr2KQYMK, \brdrt2MQYMK, \pardY4OHIO

\brdrb

The \brdrb statement draws a border below the current paragraph or picture. The statement applies to all subsequent paragraphs or pictures up to the next \pard statement.

Comments

WinHelp draws the border using the current border style.

See Also

\boxA4R47D, \brdrbarO4.C5_, \brdrl2EQYMK, \brdrr2KQYMK, \brdrt2MQYMK, \pardY4OHIO

\brdrbar

The \brdrbar statement draws a vertical bar to the left of the current paragraph or picture. The statement applies to all subsequent paragraphs or pictures up to the next \pard statement.

Comments

WinHelp draws the border using the current border style.

In a print-based document, the \brdrbar statement draws the bar on the right side of paragraphs on odd-numbered pages, but on the left side of paragraphs on even-numbered pages.

See Also

\boxA4R47D, \brdrb23WJX_P, \brdrl2EQYMK, \brdrr2KQYMK, \brdrt2MQYMK, \pardY4OHIO

\brdrdb

The \brdrdb statement selects a double line for drawing borders. The selection applies to all subsequent paragraphs or pictures up to the next \pard statement.

See Also

\brdrdot1MNOD1H, \brdrs2LQYMK, \brdrsh1_IWLUQ, \brdrt2MQYMKh, \pardY4OHIO

\brdrdot

The \brdrdot statement selects a dotted line for drawing borders. The selection applies to all subsequent paragraphs or pictures up to the next \pard statement.

See Also

\brdrdbYCWLUQ, \brdrs2LQYMK, \brdrsh1_IWLUQ, \brdrt2MQYMKh, \pardY4OHIO

\brdrl

The \brdrl statement draws a border to the left of the current paragraph or picture. The statement applies to all subsequent paragraphs or pictures up to the next \pard statement.

Comments

WinHelp draws the border using the current border style.

See Also

\boxA4R47D, \brdrb23WJX_P, \brdrbarO4.C5_, \brdrr2KQYMK, \brdrt2MQYMK, \pardY4OHIO

\brdrr

The \brdrr statement draws a border to the right of the current paragraph or picture. The statement applies to all subsequent paragraphs or pictures up to the next \pard statement.

Comments

WinHelp draws the border using the current border style.

See Also

\boxA4R47D, \brdrb23WJX_P, \brdrbarO4.C5_, \brdrl2EQYMK, \brdrt2MQYMK, \pardY4OHIO

\brdrs

The \brdrs statement selects a standard-width line for drawing borders. The selection applies to all subsequent paragraphs or pictures up to the next \pard statement.

See Also

\brdrdbYCWLUQ, \brdrdot1MNOD1H, \brdrsh1_IWLUQ, \brdrt2MQYMKh, \pardY4OHIO

n

\brdrsh

The \brdrsh statement selects a shadow outline for drawing borders. The selection applies to all subsequent paragraphs or pictures up to the next \pard statement.

See Also

\brdrdbYCWLUQ, \brdrdot1MNOD1H, \brdrs2LQYMK, \brdrt2MQYMKh, \pardY4OHIO

\brdrt

The \brdrt statement draws a border above the current paragraph or picture. The statement applies to all subsequent paragraphs or pictures up to the next \pard statement.

Comments

WinHelp draws the border using the current border style.

See Also
```
