## 857. D22V

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `D22V`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    D22V
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
{bmlt bm15.bmp}	Tip: Navigating within a Help file

To easily navigate within a Help file, click the File menu, and then make sure Help Author is checked. If a Help topic is open, you can move between topics by using key combinations. To move forward or backward one topic at a time, use ctrl+shift+right arrow or ctrl+shift+left arrow. To jump to the beginning or the end of your Help file, use ctrl+shift+home or ctrl+shift+end. To jump to a specific topic, use ctrl+shift+j. For more information, see Help in Help Workshop.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sl-235\ri125\tx535 \b \{bmlt bm15.bmp\}\tab Tip: Navigating within a Help file
\par \pard\sl-235\li535\ri125\tx535 \plain\f1\fs16 To easily navigate within a Help file, click the File menu, and then make sure Help Author is checked. If a Help topic is open, you can move between topics by using key combinations. To move forward or backward one topic at a time, use \scaps ctrl+shift+right arrow\plain\f1\fs16  or \scaps ctrl+shift+left arrow. \plain\f1\fs16 To jump to the beginning or the end of your Help file, use \scaps ctrl+shift+home \plain\f1\fs16 or \scaps ctrl+shift+end\plain\f1\fs16 . To jump to a specific topic, use \scaps ctrl+shift+j\plain\f1\fs16 . For more information, see Help in Help Workshop.
\par \pard\sl-235\li535\ri125\tx535 
\par
```

## 858. 11TC01R

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `11TC01R`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    11TC01R
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
{bmlt bm15.bmp}	Tip: Determining a topic's ID and source file

If the Help Author command on the File menu is checked, and you use your right mouse button to click any topic (including pop-ups), you can click Topic Information to view information about a topic. If the Include .Rtf Filename And Topic ID box in the Compile A Help File dialog box was checked when the Help file was compiled, information about the .rtf file a topic is in, in addition its topic ID also appears. For more information, see Help in Help Workshop.
```

If the Include .Rtf Filename And Topic ID box in the Compile A Help File dialog box was checked when the Help file was compiled, information about the .rtf file a topic is in, in addition its topic ID also appears. For more information, see Help in Help Workshop.
```rtf
\par \pard\sl-235\li535\ri125\tx535 
\par
```

## 860. JH.N6N

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `JH.N6N`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    JH.N6N
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
{bmlt bm15.bmp}	Tip: More flexibility in assigning topic IDs

You are no longer limited to using alphabetic and numeric characters for unmapped topic ID names. (Unmapped topic IDs identify topics that are not called from a program.) ```
```

## 861. 2AMEUNP

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `2AMEUNP`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    2AMEUNP
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
{bmlt bm15.bmp}	Tip: Viewing mapped Help topics

You can easily jump to every mapped topic ID in your project file by clicking the File menu, and then clicking Run WinHelp. After you specify Help (.hlp) and project (.hpj) filenames, and then click the Refresh button, all the mapped topic IDs appear in the Mapped Topic IDs list. You can view individual topics or view all topics in sequence. For more information, see context-sensitive Help in the View Help File dialog box.
```

```rtf

After you specify Help (.hlp) and project (.hpj) filenames, and then click the Refresh button, all the mapped topic IDs appear in the Mapped Topic IDs list. You can view individual topics or view all topics in sequence. For more information, see context-sensitive Help in the View Help File dialog box.
\par \pard\sl-235\li535\ri125\tx535 
\par
```

## 862. 54JNZXE

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `54JNZXE`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    54JNZXE
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
{bmlt bm15.bmp}	Tip: Adding the compile date to the Version dialog box

You can add the date on which your Help file was compiled to WinHelp's version dialog box. To do this, open your .hpj file, and then click the Options button. In the Display This Text In The Version Dialog Box area, add the following to the end of any text that appears there:

%date

The date will appear in English and cannot be translated.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sl-235\ri125\tx535 \b \{bmlt bm15.bmp\}\tab Tip: Adding the compile date to the Version dialog box
\par \pard\sl-235\li535\ri125\tx535 \plain\f1\fs16 You can add the date on which your Help file was compiled to WinHelp's version dialog box. To do this, open your .hpj file, and then click the Options button. In the Display This Text In The Version Dialog Box area, add the following to the end of any text that appears there:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li545\ri125\tx535 \b %date\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li545\ri125\tx535 The date will appear in English and cannot be translated. 
\par \pard\sl-235\li535\ri125\tx535 
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
{bmlt bm15.bmp}	Tip: Specifying a window type for individual topics

You can specify the window type that a topic should appear in when it is displayed from the Index or Find tab. To do this, use a greater-than sign (>) footnote in your .rtf file, and then specify the window type you want the topic to appear in. For example:

> main

For more information, see Help in Help Workshop.
```

```text
{bmlt bm15.bmp}	Tip: Making a bitmaps background transparent

You can make the background of a 16-color bitmap transparent by adding the letter "t" to the bitmap statement in your .rtf file, as in the following example:

{-bmlt 16color.bmp}

For more information, see Help in Help Workshop.
```

## 865. A4B09Z

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `A4B09Z`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    A4B09Z
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
{bmlt bm15.bmp}	Tip: Associating macros with keywords

You can easily specify a macro that runs whenever a user chooses a particular keyword in the Help Index. To do this, open your .hpj file, and then click Options. Click the Macros tab, and then click Add. You can then specify the keyword and title that will appear on the Help Index tab and in the Topics Found dialog box. When a user double-clicks the keyword or title, WinHelp launches the macro. For help on an item in the Keyword Macros dialog box, click {bmc bm0.bmp} in its title bar, and then click the item.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf

To do this, open your .hpj file, and then click Options. Click the Macros tab, and then click Add. You can then specify the keyword and title that will appear on the Help Index tab and in the Topics Found dialog box. When a user double-clicks the keyword or title, WinHelp launches the macro. For help on an item in the Keyword Macros dialog box, click \{bmc bm0.bmp\} in its title bar, and then click the item.
\par \pard\sl-235\li535\ri125\tx535 
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
{bmlt bm15.bmp}	Tip: Specifying the background color for pop-ups

You can choose a background color for pop-up topics by using the SetPopupColor macro. To change all pop-up – topic colors, open your project (.hpj) file, click Config, and then specify the SetPopupColor macro. To specify the color for an individual pop-up topic, specify the SetPopColor macro with the PopupId macro for the pop-up's hotspot. For more information, see Help in Help Workshop.
```

```text
{bmlt bm15.bmp}	Tip: Getting Help on error messages

If you receive an error message while you compile a file and aren't sure what it means, look up the error in Help. Help provides a description of all error messages and recommends solutions. For example, if you receive error HC1003, look up "HC1003" in the Help Index for Help Workshop.
```

## 871. 5.PB_MN

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `5.PB_MN`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    5.PB_MN
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
{bmlt bm15.bmp}	Tip: No need for quotation marks in macros

As a general rule, you do not need to use quotes in macros if they appear in either your topic file or project file. Help Workshop supplies all the quotes for you and determines which type of quote to use in nested macros.

Help Workshop automatically converts all macros to their shortest form. You can use the long name of a macro to add legibility to your .rtf files without affecting the size of your Help file.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sl-235\ri125\tx535 \b \{bmlt bm15.bmp\}\tab Tip: No need for quotation marks in macros
\par \pard\sl-235\li535\ri125\tx535 \plain\f1\fs16 As a general rule, you do not need to use quotes in macros if they appear in either your topic file or project file. Help Workshop supplies all the quotes for you and determines which type of quote to use in nested macros.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li545\ri125\tx535 Help Workshop automatically converts all macros to their shortest form. You can use the long name of a macro to add legibility to your .rtf files without affecting the size of your Help file.
\par \pard\sl-235\li535\ri125\tx535 
\par
```

## 873. 22WHJZF

  - Decoded: `22WHJZF`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    22WHJZF
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
{bmlt bm15.bmp}	Tip: Viewing messages that programs send to WinHelp

You can determine exactly what Topic IDs a program sends to WinHelp by clicking the View menu in Help Workshop, and then clicking WinHelp Messages. If the project file has never been opened or compiled by Help Workshop, only the mapped numeric values appear. However, if the project file has been opened or compiled at least once, the Topic ID is displayed along with its mapped numeric value.
```

However, if the project file has been opened or compiled at least once, the Topic ID is displayed along with its mapped numeric value.
```rtf
\par \pard\sl-235\li535\ri125\tx535 
\par
```

## 875. To create a jump to a topic or pop-up topic

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:00d2`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:00d2
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `To create a jump to a topic or pop-up topic`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    To create a jump to a topic or pop-up topic
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `PRO_INSERT_JUMP`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    PRO_INSERT_JUMP
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `PRO_INSERT_JUMP`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    PRO_INSERT_JUMP
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `% (percent sign) in jumps;@ (at sign) in jumps;> (greater-than sign);> (greater-than sign), in jumps;adding;adding, jumps to topics;at sign (@) in jumps;colors;colors, hotspots;creating;creating, hotspots;creating, jumps;double-underline;greater-than sign (>);greater-than sign (>), in jumps;hotspots;hotspots, creating;hotspots, for jumps;interfile jumps;jumps;jumps, color of;jumps, specifying window type for;jumps, to a pop-up topic;jumps, to another Help file;jumps, to another topic;linking multiple Help files;percent sign (%) in jumps;pop-up windows;pop-up windows, creating jumps to;single-underline;topics,;topics, adding hotspots to;underline character style for hotspots`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    % (percent sign) in jumps;@ (at sign) in jumps;> (greater-than sign);> (greater-than sign), in jumps;adding;adding, jumps to topics;at sign (@) in jumps;colors;colors, hotspots;creating;creating, hotspots;creating, jumps;double-underline;greater-than sign (>);greater-than sign (>), in jumps;hotspots;hotspots, creating;hotspots, for jumps;interfile jumps;jumps;jumps, color of;jumps, specifying window type for;jumps, to a pop-up topic;jumps, to another Help file;jumps, to another topic;linking multiple Help files;percent sign (%) in jumps;pop-up windows;pop-up windows, creating jumps to;single-underline;topics,;topics, adding hotspots to;underline character style for hotspots
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
To create a jump to a topic or pop-up topic

1	Place the insertion point directly after the text or bitmap that you want to be the hotspot,7BM6.K and then type the topic ID of the topic you want to jump to. Do not put a space between the hotspot and the topic ID.

2	Select the hotspot and then:

·	Apply the double-underline character style if you want another topic to be displayed.

·	Apply the single-underline character style if you want a pop-up topic,QAWJFN to be displayed.

3	Select the topic ID, and then apply the hidden character style.

Example

{bmct bm16.bmp}

Tips

·	If the hotspot text ends in a punctuation mark, include the mark when you apply the double-underline character style. Otherwise, a line break could cause the mark to appear at the beginning of a line.

·	To display the hotspot in the default text color (nongreen), insert an asterisk (*) at the beginning of the topic ID.

{bmct bm17.bmp}

·	To display the hotspot in the default text color (nongreen), without an underline, insert a percent sign (%) at the beginning of the topic ID.

{bmct bm18.bmp}

·	To create a jump to a topic in another Help file, insert an at sign (@) and the name of the other Help file at the end of the topic ID.

{bmct bm19.bmp}

·	To specify a window type for the topic you jump to, insert a greater than sign (>) and the window name at the end of the topic ID.

{bmct bm20.bmp}
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b To create a jump to a topic or pop-up topic
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 1\tab Place the insertion point directly after the text or bitmap that you want to be the \ul hotspot,\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7BM6.K} and then type the topic ID of the topic you want to jump to. Do not put a space between the hotspot and the topic ID.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 2\tab Select the hotspot and then:
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li455\ri125\fi-155\tx455 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Apply the double-underline character style if you want another topic to be displayed.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Apply the single-underline character style if you want a \ul pop-up topic,\plain\f1\fs16 {\v QAWJFN} to be displayed.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 3\tab Select the topic ID, and then apply the hidden character style.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Example
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li455\ri125\fi-155\tx455 \plain\f1\fs16 \{bmct bm16.bmp\}
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx455 \b Tips
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab If the hotspot text ends in a punctuation mark, include the mark when you apply the double-underline character style. Otherwise, a line break could cause the mark to appear at the beginning of a line.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab To display the hotspot in the default text color (nongreen), insert an asterisk (*) at the beginning of the topic ID.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li455\ri125\fi-155\tx455 \{bmct bm17.bmp\}
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab To display the hotspot in the default text color (nongreen), without an underline, insert a percent sign (%) at the beginning of the topic ID.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li455\ri125\fi-155\tx455 \{bmct bm18.bmp\}
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab To create a jump to a topic in another Help file, insert an at sign (@) and the name of the other Help file at the end of the topic ID.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li455\ri125\fi-155\tx455 \{bmct bm19.bmp\}
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab To specify a window type for the topic you jump to, insert a greater than sign (>) and the window name at the end of the topic ID.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li455\ri125\fi-155\tx455 \{bmct bm20.bmp\}
\par
```

## 876. To add a hotspot that runs a macro

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`


  - Decoded: `To add a hotspot that runs a macro`

    ```rtf
    To add a hotspot that runs a macro
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `PRO_INSERT_MACRO`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    PRO_INSERT_MACRO
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `PRO_INSERT_MACRO`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    PRO_INSERT_MACRO
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `! (exclamation point);! (exclamation point), in macro hotspots;adding;adding, macros in topics;creating;creating, hotspots;exclamation point (!);exclamation point (!), in macro hotspots;hotspots;hotspots, creating;hotspots, for macros;macros,;macros, adding hotspots for;macros, running from a hotspot;running macros;topics,;topics, adding hotspots for macros`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    ! (exclamation point);! (exclamation point), in macro hotspots;adding;adding, macros in topics;creating;creating, hotspots;exclamation point (!);exclamation point (!), in macro hotspots;hotspots;hotspots, creating;hotspots, for macros;macros,;macros, adding hotspots for;macros, running from a hotspot;running macros;topics,;topics, adding hotspots for macros
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
To add a hotspot that runs a macro

1	Place the insertion point directly after the text or bitmap reference that you want to be the hotspot,7BM6.K and then type an exclamation point (!). Do not leave a space between the exclamation point and the hotspot.

2	Type the macro command. Multiple macros can be specified by separating them with a colon (:) or semicolon(;).

3	Select the hotspot, and then apply the double-underline character style.

4	Select the exclamation point and the macro command, and then apply the hidden character style.

Examples

{bmct bm21.bmp}

{bmct bm22.bmp}

{button ,AL(`MACRO_REF_OVR;BAS_ASSOCIATIVE_JUMPS')} Related Topics%!AL(`MACRO_REF_OVR;BAS_ASSOCIATIVE_JUMPS')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b To add a hotspot that runs a macro
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 1\tab Place the insertion point directly after the text or bitmap reference that you want to be the \ul hotspot,\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7BM6.K} and then type an exclamation point (!). Do not leave a space between the exclamation point and the hotspot.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 2\tab Type the macro command. Multiple macros can be specified by separating them with a colon (:) or semicolon(;).
\par 3\tab Select the hotspot, and then apply the double-underline character style.
\par 4\tab Select the exclamation point and the macro command, and then apply the hidden character style.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Examples
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 \{bmct bm21.bmp\}
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{bmct bm22.bmp\}
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL(`MACRO_REF_OVR;BAS_ASSOCIATIVE_JUMPS')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`MACRO_REF_OVR;BAS_ASSOCIATIVE_JUMPS')}
\par
```

## 878. To add a bitmap to a topic

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:00d5`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:00d5
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `To add a bitmap to a topic`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    To add a bitmap to a topic
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `PRO_ADD_PICTURE`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    PRO_ADD_PICTURE
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `PRO_ADD_PICTURE`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    PRO_ADD_PICTURE
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `adding;adding, bitmaps to topics;aligning;aligning, bitmaps in topics;bitmaps;bitmaps, adding to topics;graphics;graphics, adding to topics;topics,;topics, adding bitmaps to`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    adding;adding, bitmaps to topics;aligning;aligning, bitmaps in topics;bitmaps;bitmaps, adding to topics;graphics;graphics, adding to topics;topics,;topics, adding bitmaps to
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
To add a bitmap to a topic

1	Place the insertion point where you want to display the bitmap.

2	To add a single instance of a bitmap, simply copy and paste it.

	If you will use multiple instancesE08QKF of the bitmap, create a link to its file by using the following syntax:

{-bmx filename.bmp}

For x, specify one or two of the values in the table below. For filename.bmp, specify the name of your bitmap file.

Value	Description

c	Aligns the bitmap as a text character.

l	Aligns the bitmap with the left margin.

r	Aligns the bitmap with the right margin.

t	Used with any of the above values, this indicates that the white pixels of the bitmap should be converted to the solid color closest to the background color of the window. This can be used only with 16-color bitmaps.

Note

·	You can specify multiple bitmaps by separating their names with a semicolon. In this case, WinHelp displays the bitmap that most closely matches the number of colors that can be displayed on a user's computer. For more information, click Related Topics.

{button ,AL(`PRO_MULT_COLOR_GRAPHICS;PRO_COMP_RESOLUTIONS;BAS_ADDING_GRAPHICS;BAS_MULT_RESOLUTION')} Related Topics%!AL(`PRO_MULT_COLOR_GRAPHICS;PRO_COMP_RESOLUTIONS;BAS_ADDING_GRAPHICS;BAS_MULT_RESOLUTION')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b To add a bitmap to a topic
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 1\tab Place the insertion point where you want to display the bitmap.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 2\tab To add a single instance of a bitmap, simply copy and paste it. 
\par \tab If you will use \ul multiple instances\plain\f1\fs16 {\v E08QKF} of the bitmap, create a link to its file by using the following syntax:
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li295\ri125\tx295 \b \{\-bm\plain\f1\fs16 x filename.bmp\b \'7d
\par \plain\f1\fs16 For x, specify one or two of the values in the table below. For filename.bmp, specify the name of your bitmap file.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li895\ri125\fi-625\brdrb\brdrs\tx895 \b Value\tab Description
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li895\ri125\fi-625\tx895 c\plain\f1\fs16 \tab Aligns the bitmap as a text character.
\par \b l\plain\f1\fs16 \tab Aligns the bitmap with the left margin.
\par \b r\plain\f1\fs16 \tab Aligns the bitmap with the right margin.
\par \b t\plain\f1\fs16 \tab Used with any of the above values, this indicates that the white pixels of the bitmap should be converted to the solid color closest to the background color of the window. This can be used only with 16-color bitmaps.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx895 \b Note
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab You can specify multiple bitmaps by separating their names with a semicolon. In this case, WinHelp displays the bitmap that most closely matches the number of colors that can be displayed on a user's computer. For more information, click Related Topics.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL(`PRO_MULT_COLOR_GRAPHICS;PRO_COMP_RESOLUTIONS;BAS_ADDING_GRAPHICS;BAS_MULT_RESOLUTION')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`PRO_MULT_COLOR_GRAPHICS;PRO_COMP_RESOLUTIONS;BAS_ADDING_GRAPHICS;BAS_MULT_RESOLUTION')}
\par
```

```text
{-mci filename.ext}
```

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`

- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `PRO_MULT_COLOR_GRAPHICS`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    PRO_MULT_COLOR_GRAPHICS
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `bitmaps;bitmaps, color depth;color depth;graphics;graphics, color depth;multiple color depth graphics`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    bitmaps;bitmaps, color depth;color depth;graphics;graphics, color depth;multiple color depth graphics
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
To configure a bitmap for multiple color depths

1	Create a bitmap file for each color depth1NQNUGQ that you want to make available.

2	Place the insertion point where you want the bitmap to be displayed, and then create a bitmap statement that includes each filename. Separate filenames with semicolons, as shown in the example.

	WinHelp automatically uses the bitmap file that is most appropriate for a user's computer.

Example

{-bml dog_16.bmp;dog_256.bmp;dog_mono.bmp}

{button ,AL(`PRO_ADD_PICTURE;BAS_MULT_COLOR_GRAPHICS;BAS_MULT_RESOLUTION;BAS_ADDING_GRAPHICS')} Related Topics%!AL(`PRO_ADD_PICTURE;BAS_MULT_COLOR_GRAPHICS;BAS_MULT_RESOLUTION;BAS_ADDING_GRAPHICS')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b To configure a bitmap for multiple color depths
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 1\tab Create a bitmap file for each \ul color depth\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 1NQNUGQ} that you want to make available.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 2\tab Place the insertion point where you want the bitmap to be displayed, and then create a bitmap statement that includes each filename. Separate filenames with semicolons, as shown in the example. 
\par \tab WinHelp automatically uses the bitmap file that is most appropriate for a user's computer.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Example
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 \{\-bml\'a0dog_16.bmp;dog_256.bmp;dog_mono.bmp\'7d
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL(`PRO_ADD_PICTURE;BAS_MULT_COLOR_GRAPHICS;BAS_MULT_RESOLUTION;BAS_ADDING_GRAPHICS')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`PRO_ADD_PICTURE;BAS_MULT_COLOR_GRAPHICS;BAS_MULT_RESOLUTION;BAS_ADDING_GRAPHICS')}
\par
```

## 881. E08QKF

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `E08QKF`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    E08QKF
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
If you use a bitmap in more than one place in your Help file, use a bitmap statement to create a link to it instead of copying and pasting the image. This reduces the size of your Help file because only one copy of the bitmap needs to be included in the file.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 If you use a bitmap in more than one place in your Help file, use a bitmap statement to create a link to it instead of copying and pasting the image. This reduces the size of your Help file because only one copy of the bitmap needs to be included in the file.
\par
```

## 882. 9UIBLJ

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `9UIBLJ`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    9UIBLJ
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
The absolute position is a setting that tells WinHelp that the coordinate values you specify for a window definition are to reflect the number of pixels, regardless of screen resolution. As a result, the size and position of the window will vary widely on different monitors, but the text in the windows will always wrap at the same places. If this setting is not specified, WinHelp adjusts the settings for the resolution of the monitor on which the Help file is displayed, and the window size and position will be roughly the same on all monitors, regardless of resolution.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 The absolute position is a setting that tells WinHelp that the coordinate values you specify for a window definition are to reflect the number of pixels, regardless of screen resolution. As a result, the size and position of the window will vary widely on different monitors, but the text in the windows will always wrap at the same places. If this setting is not specified, WinHelp adjusts the settings for the resolution of the monitor on which the Help file is displayed, and the window size and position will be roughly the same on all monitors, regardless of resolution.
\par
```

## 884. 7V8V.U

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `7V8V.U`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    7V8V.U
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
An ALink macro provides a link to topics by means of keywords listed in the A-footnotes of topic (.rtf) files. When a user clicks a hotspot that runs an ALink macro specifying a keyword, WinHelp jumps to the topic that contains the keyword, or, if more than one topic contains the same keyword, WinHelp displays the Topics Found dialog box listing the titles of the topics. Keywords used in A-footnotes are reserved for use by ALink macros only.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 An ALink macro provides a link to topics by means of keywords listed in the A-footnotes of topic (.rtf) files. When a user clicks a hotspot that runs an ALink macro specifying a keyword, WinHelp jumps to the topic that contains the keyword, or, if more than one topic contains the same keyword, WinHelp displays the Topics Found dialog box listing the titles of the topics. Keywords used in A-footnotes are reserved for use by ALink macros only.
\par
```

## 897. 32JI7C9

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `32JI7C9`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    32JI7C9
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
The default topic is the topic WinHelp displays whenever the user initiates a jump to a topic that is unavailable. For Help files that do not have an associated contents (.cnt) file, WinHelp displays the default topic as the first topic when the user first opens the Help file and when the Contents button is clicked. If you do not specify a default topic, WinHelp uses the first topic of the first file listed in the project (.hpj) file.
```

  - Decoded: `OYZM9`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    OYZM9
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
A hash number is a unique number generated from a topic ID. It is this number, and not the topic ID itself, that WinHelp uses. You can use the JumpHash macro to jump to a topic identified by a hash number, or you can use the Report command to list the hash numbers of all topics in a Help file.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 A hash number is a unique number generated from a topic ID. It is this number, and not the topic ID itself, that WinHelp uses. You can use the JumpHash macro to jump to a topic identified by a hash number, or you can use the Report command to list the hash numbers of all topics in a Help file.
\par
```

## 902. PZJ46U

### WinHelp Footnotes

  - Decoded: `PZJ46U`
  - Raw:

    ```rtf
    PZJ46U
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Help Author mode is a way of running a Help file for testing purposes, providing the author with special navigational ability and other information about the structure of topics, links, windows, and macros. The Help Author command is available on the Help Workshop File menu.
```

```rtf

\par
```

## 904. _B12QW

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `_B12QW`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    _B12QW
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
A nonscrolling region is a specified portion at the top of a topic that always remains visible, even if the user scrolls to the bottom of a long topic. The nonscrolling region is separated from the scrolling region by a thin, black line, and it can contain text, bitmaps, and hotspots. You specify a nonscrolling region by applying the Keep With Next paragraph style (or equivalent) to the paragraph(s) in the topic (.rtf) file.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 A nonscrolling region is a specified portion at the top of a topic that always remains visible, even if the user scrolls to the bottom of a long topic. The nonscrolling region is separated from the scrolling region by a thin, black line, and it can contain text, bitmaps, and hotspots. You specify a nonscrolling region by applying the Keep With Next paragraph style (or equivalent) to the paragraph(s) in the topic (.rtf) file.
\par
```

## 905. KMK38I

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `KMK38I`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    KMK38I
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
The Help Topics dialog box serves as the initial interface for the entire Help system. Typically, it contains Contents, Index, and Find tabs. The Help Topics dialog box appears when Help is run by using the HELP_FINDER command of the WinHelp API, or when a user double-clicks a Help file that is associated with a contents (.cnt) file that contains information for the Contents tab.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 The Help Topics dialog box serves as the initial interface for the entire Help system. Typically, it contains Contents, Index, and Find tabs. The Help Topics dialog box appears when Help is run by using the HELP_FINDER command of the WinHelp API, or when a user double-clicks a Help file that is associated with a contents (.cnt) file that contains information for the Contents tab.
\par
```

## 909. 9ML77Q

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `9ML77Q`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    9ML77Q
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
A keyword is a word or phrase associated with a topic, usually for indexing purposes. The same keyword can be associated with several topics. You assign keywords to topics in footnotes in the topic (.rtf) file.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 A keyword is a word or phrase associated with a topic, usually for indexing purposes. The same keyword can be associated with several topics. You assign keywords to topics in footnotes in the topic (.rtf) file.
\par
```

## 910. 1R9H33X

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `1R9H33X`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    1R9H33X
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
A KLink macro provides a link to topics by means of keywords listed in the K-footnotes of topic (.rtf) files. Keywords used in K-footnotes are also used in the index, so a KLink jump to a keyword has the same effect as clicking that keyword in the index. By contrast, ALink macros use keywords listed in A-footnotes, and these keywords are reserved for use by the ALink macro only. When a user clicks a hotspot that runs a KLink macro, WinHelp displays the Topics Found dialog box listing all the titles of topics associated with the K-keywords specified by the macro. The user can then jump to the desired topic by double-clicking the topic title.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 A KLink macro provides a link to topics by means of keywords listed in the K-footnotes of topic (.rtf) files. Keywords used in K-footnotes are also used in the index, so a KLink jump to a keyword has the same effect as clicking that keyword in the index. By contrast, ALink macros use keywords listed in A-footnotes, and these keywords are reserved for use by the ALink macro only. When a user clicks a hotspot that runs a KLink macro, WinHelp displays the Topics Found dialog box listing all the titles of topics associated with the K-keywords specified by the macro. The user can then jump to the desired topic by double-clicking the topic title.
\par
```

## 911. O8.Q2

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `O8.Q2`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    O8.Q2
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
The main Help window, unlike a secondary window, has a menu and a default button bar, and it cannot be autosized. WinHelp has default settings for the main window, but you can customize it by specifying it as "main" in the [WINDOWS] section of the project (.hpj) file. Only one main window can be displayed at a time.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 The main Help window, unlike a secondary window, has a menu and a default button bar, and it cannot be autosized. WinHelp has default settings for the main window, but you can customize it by specifying it as "main" in the [WINDOWS] section of the project (.hpj) file. Only one main window can be displayed at a time.
\par
```

## 918. QAWJFN

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `QAWJFN`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    QAWJFN
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
A pop-up topic is a topic that appears in a pop-up window. A pop-up topic can contain text, graphics, multimedia files, or hotspots. Typically, you use pop-up topics to convey additional information, such as the definition of a term or the description of a dialog box component.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 A pop-up topic is a topic that appears in a pop-up window. A pop-up topic can contain text, graphics, multimedia files, or hotspots. Typically, you use pop-up topics to convey additional information, such as the definition of a term or the description of a dialog box component. 
\par
```

## 919. 8XSV6X

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `8XSV6X`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    8XSV6X
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
A pop-up window is a window in which a pop-up topic is displayed. Pop-up windows have no menu or button bars and appear on top of the topic from which they are called. Pop-up windows disappear when a user clicks anywhere on the screen. A pop-up window can contain text, graphics, multimedia files, or hotspots. However, if you have a hotspot in a pop-up window, the first pop-up window closes when the user clicks the hotspot. WinHelp can display only one pop-up window at a time.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 A pop-up window is a window in which a pop-up topic is displayed. Pop-up windows have no menu or button bars and appear on top of the topic from which they are called. Pop-up windows disappear when a user clicks anywhere on the screen. A pop-up window can contain text, graphics, multimedia files, or hotspots. However, if you have a hotspot in a pop-up window, the first pop-up window closes when the user clicks the hotspot. WinHelp can display only one pop-up window at a time.
\par
```

## 920. About project files

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:007c`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:007c
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `About project files`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    About project files
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `idh_BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    idh_BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;BAS_HPJ`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;BAS_HPJ
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `files,;files, .hpj;files, about project files;hpj files, see project files;project files;project files, about project files`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    files,;files, .hpj;files, about project files;hpj files, see project files;project files;project files, about project files
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
About project files

The project (.hpj) file is an ASCII text file that is used to compile your Help file. The project file contains all the information Help Workshop needs to combine topic (.rtf) files and other elements into a Help file.

You use Help Workshop to create and modify project files. When you create a new project file, the minimum settings you need are already made for you. The remaining settings depend on the size and complexity of your Help file.

Each section and option in the project file has a different purpose, and each can be used to improve the effectiveness and usability of your Help file. For more information about the project files settings that are available in Help Workshop, open a project (.hpj) file or create a new one, and then do one of the following:

·	For Help on items in dialog boxes, click {bmc bm0.bmp} at the top of the dialog box, and then click the item.

·	For Help on items in a window, click the item or its label by using your right mouse button, and then click What's This.

{button ,AL(`BAS_HPJ;NEW_AUTHOR_HPJ;PRO_HPJ')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_HPJ;NEW_AUTHOR_HPJ;PRO_HPJ')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1525 

\b About project files
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1525 \plain\f1\fs16 The project (.hpj) file is an ASCII text file that is used to compile your Help file. The project file contains all the information Help Workshop needs to combine topic (.rtf) files and other elements into a Help file.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1525 You use Help Workshop to create and modify project files. When you create a new project file, the minimum settings you need are already made for you. The remaining settings depend on the size and complexity of your Help file.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx1525 Each section and option in the project file has a different purpose, and each can be used to improve the effectiveness and usability of your Help file. For more information about the project files settings that are available in Help Workshop, open a project (.hpj) file or create a new one, and then do one of the following:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab For Help on items in dialog boxes, click \{bmc bm0.bmp\} at the top of the dialog box, and then click the item.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab For Help on items in a window, click the item or its label by using your right mouse button, and then click What's This.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL(`BAS_HPJ;NEW_AUTHOR_HPJ;PRO_HPJ')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BAS_HPJ;NEW_AUTHOR_HPJ;PRO_HPJ')}
\par
```

## 921. About the contents file

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:007d`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:007d
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `About the contents file`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    About the contents file
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `3X_CONTENTS_DESIGN`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    3X_CONTENTS_DESIGN
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `3X_CONTENTS_DESIGN;BAS_CNT;BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;BAS_CONTENTS_CHECKLIST;DESIGN`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    3X_CONTENTS_DESIGN;BAS_CNT;BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;BAS_CONTENTS_CHECKLIST;DESIGN
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `books;books, described;contents files;contents files, about contents files;files,;files, .cnt;files, about contents files;headings;headings, described`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    books;books, described;contents files;contents files, about contents files;files,;files, .cnt;files, about contents files;headings;headings, described
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
About the contents file

The contents (.cnt) file is an ASCII text file that provides the instructions for the Contents tab in the Help Topics dialog box, and directs WinHelp to display the keywords of specified Help files on the Index and Find tabs.

You design your table of contents by specifying the following in your contents file.

{bmc bm23.bmp} Headings%S.O02O

{bmc bm23.bmp} Topics%2.PJRZS

{bmc bm23.bmp} Commands%ODRKC3

To create and edit contents files, use Help Workshop. For more information, click Related Topics.

Note

·	The Contents tab does not appear in the Help Topics dialog boxKMK38I unless the contents file contains at least one topic that jumps to a Help file on a user's computer. The valid jump must be present in the contents file when a user first opens the associated Help file.

{button ,AL(`BAS_CNT_31;3X_CONTENTS_DESIGN;NAVIGATION_DESIGN;CONTENTS_DESIGN;MULTIPLE_HELP_DESIGN;BAS_CNT;MORE_ABOUT_CNT_FILES;CONTENTS_DESIGN')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_CNT_31;3X_CONTENTS_DESIGN;NAVIGATION_DESIGN;CONTENTS_DESIGN;MULTIPLE_HELP_DESIGN;BAS_CNT;MORE_ABOUT_CNT_FILES;CONTENTS_DESIGN')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b About the contents file
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The contents (.cnt) file is an ASCII text file that provides the instructions for the Contents tab in the Help Topics dialog box, and directs WinHelp to display the keywords of specified Help files on the Index and Find tabs. 
\par You design your table of contents by specifying the following in your contents file. 
\par \uldb \{bmc bm23.bmp\} Headings\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %S.O02O}
\par \uldb \{bmc bm23.bmp\} Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %2.PJRZS}
\par \uldb \{bmc bm23.bmp\} Commands\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %ODRKC3}
\par To create and edit contents files, use Help Workshop. For more information, click Related Topics.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Note
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab The Contents tab does not appear in the \ul Help Topics dialog box\plain\f1\fs16 {\v KMK38I} unless the contents file contains at least one topic that jumps to a Help file on a user's computer. The valid jump must be present in the contents file when a user first opens the associated Help file.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL(`BAS_CNT_31;3X_CONTENTS_DESIGN;NAVIGATION_DESIGN;CONTENTS_DESIGN;MULTIPLE_HELP_DESIGN;BAS_CNT;MORE_ABOUT_CNT_FILES;CONTENTS_DESIGN')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BAS_CNT_31;3X_CONTENTS_DESIGN;NAVIGATION_DESIGN;CONTENTS_DESIGN;MULTIPLE_HELP_DESIGN;BAS_CNT;MORE_ABOUT_CNT_FILES;CONTENTS_DESIGN')}
\par
```

## 922. S.O02O

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `S.O02O`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    S.O02O
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Headings

Headings are represented by book icons and can contain a group of related topics and other headings. Users display the contents of a heading by double-clicking it.
```

## 923. 2.PJRZS

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `2.PJRZS`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    2.PJRZS
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Topics

Topics are represented by page icons and either display a topic or run a macro when a user double-clicks them. Along with the topic title and topic ID, you can specify which Help file a topic is in, and which window type you want the topic to appear in.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf

\par
```

## 924. ODRKC3

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `ODRKC3`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    ODRKC3
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Commands

Commands specify the scope and appearance of your table of contents and index, including:

·	The name of the default Help file (and window)

·	The title to display in the Help Topics dialog box

·	The names of Help files whose keywords you want to include on the Index tab and whose text you want to include on the Find tab of the Help Topics dialog box

·	The names of Help files WinHelp should search for keywords9ML77Q if the ALink7V8V.U and KLink1R9H33X macros are used in topics

·	The names and locations of other tabs to display in the Help Topics dialog box.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Commands
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Commands specify the scope and appearance of your table of contents and index, including:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab The name of the default Help file (and window)
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab The title to display in the Help Topics dialog box
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab The names of Help files whose keywords you want to include on the Index tab and whose text you want to include on the Find tab of the Help Topics dialog box
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab The names of Help files WinHelp should search for \ul keywords\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 9ML77Q} if the \ul ALink\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7V8V.U} and \ul KLink\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 1R9H33X} macros are used in topics
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab The names and locations of other tabs to display in the Help Topics dialog box.
\par
```

## 925. Overview: Compiling Help files

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `$`

- Marker `K`

  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    building, see compiling;compiling;compiling, overview;compiling, with compression;compression;Help files;Help files, compiling;Help Workshop;Help Workshop, compiling Help files;project files;project files, compiling
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Compiling Help files

You create Help files by compiling the project file. This brings together all the information in your project (.hpj) file, topic (.rtf) files, bitmap (.bmp) files, and DLL data files.

When you compile your Help file, you can choose from several compression options that make your Help file smaller. The greater the compression, the longer it takes to compile your file. You can temporarily turn off compression to reduce the amount of time it takes to compile.

{button ,AL("BAS_THE_HELP_COMPILER;BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;PRO_HPJ_COMPRESSION")} Related Topics%!AL("BAS_THE_HELP_COMPILER;BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;PRO_HPJ_COMPRESSION")
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 
\b 
\par Compiling Help files
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 You create Help files by compiling the project file. This brings together all the information in your project (.hpj) file, topic (.rtf) files, bitmap (.bmp) files, and DLL data files.
\par When you compile your Help file, you can choose from several compression options that make your Help file smaller. The greater the compression, the longer it takes to compile your file. You can temporarily turn off compression to reduce the amount of time it takes to compile.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL("BAS_THE_HELP_COMPILER;BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;PRO_HPJ_COMPRESSION")\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL("BAS_THE_HELP_COMPILER;BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;PRO_HPJ_COMPRESSION")}
\par
```

## 927. 16NZXVI

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `16NZXVI`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    16NZXVI
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Help Author mode

If a check mark appears beside the Help Author command on the File menu in Help Workshop, a line enabling Help Author features is added to your Win.ini file. Help Author mode provides additional information about the topics in a Help file, and enables you to more easily navigate from one topic to the next.

{button ,AL(`PRO_HELPAUTHOR;BAS_HELP_AUTHOR_MODE')} Related Topics%!AL(`PRO_HELPAUTHOR;BAS_HELP_AUTHOR_MODE')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Help Author mode
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 If a check mark appears beside the Help Author command on the File menu in Help Workshop, a line enabling Help Author features is added to your Win.ini file. Help Author mode provides additional information about the topics in a Help file, and enables you to more easily navigate from one topic to the next. 
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`PRO_HELPAUTHOR;BAS_HELP_AUTHOR_MODE')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`PRO_HELPAUTHOR;BAS_HELP_AUTHOR_MODE')}
\par
```

## 928. 34BRZY7

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `34BRZY7`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    34BRZY7
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Compare macro

The Compare macro enables you to display side-by-side instances of two Help files. This macro is especially useful for comparing original and translated versions of the same Help file. You can run the Compare macro while you are viewing your Help by pressing ctrl+shift+j, which displays the Jump dialog box.

{button ,AL(`Compare')} Related Topics%!AL(`Compare')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Compare macro
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The Compare macro enables you to display side-by-side instances of two Help files. This macro is especially useful for comparing original and translated versions of the same Help file. You can run the Compare macro while you are viewing your Help by pressing \scaps ctrl+shift+j\plain\f1\fs16 , which displays the Jump dialog box.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`Compare')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`Compare')}
\par
```

## 929. AMSXP0

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `AMSXP0`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    AMSXP0
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Test Contents File command

You can test your contents (.cnt) file in Help Workshop by clicking the Test menu, and then clicking Contents File. Help Workshop checks to make sure that the syntax in your contents file is correct. Help Workshop then jumps to every topic specified in the contents file and displays a message if one of the jumps does not work.

{button ,AL(`PRO_TEST_CNT')} Related Topics%!AL(`PRO_TEST_CNT')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Test Contents File command
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 You can test your contents (.cnt) file in Help Workshop by clicking the Test menu, and then clicking Contents File. Help Workshop checks to make sure that the syntax in your contents file is correct. Help Workshop then jumps to every topic specified in the contents file and displays a message if one of the jumps does not work.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`PRO_TEST_CNT')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`PRO_TEST_CNT')}
\par
```

## 930. 7ZNLR5

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `7ZNLR5`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    7ZNLR5
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Monitoring WinHelp messages

You can view WinHelp messages to determine whether actions in a Help file are working correctly. Using this feature, you can monitor WinHelp API calls, context-sensitive calls, which macros are being run, hotspot jumps, which topics and pop-ups are current, and which configuration (.gid) file is being read.

To monitor WinHelp messages in Help Workshop, click the View menu, and then click WinHelp Messages. Then open a Help file.

Tips

·	Messages are added to the log file at the insertion point, so if you move the insertion point during a session, make sure you return it to the end of the text.

·	Help Workshop tracks WinHelp messages for all instances of WinHelp. Make sure that only one instance of WinHelp is running.

{button ,AL(`PRO_TRACK_WINHELP_MSGS')} Related Topics%!AL(`PRO_TRACK_WINHELP_MSGS')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Monitoring WinHelp messages
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 You can view WinHelp messages to determine whether actions in a Help file are working correctly. Using this feature, you can monitor WinHelp API calls, context-sensitive calls, which macros are being run, hotspot jumps, which topics and pop-ups are current, and which configuration (.gid) file is being read.
\par To monitor WinHelp messages in Help Workshop, click the View menu, and then click WinHelp Messages. Then open a Help file.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Tips
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab Messages are added to the log file at the insertion point, so if you move the insertion point during a session, make sure you return it to the end of the text.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Help Workshop tracks WinHelp messages for all instances of WinHelp. Make sure that only one instance of WinHelp is running.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL(`PRO_TRACK_WINHELP_MSGS')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`PRO_TRACK_WINHELP_MSGS')}
\par
```

## 932. 76ZAYA

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `76ZAYA`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    76ZAYA
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Topic numbers

If Help Author is enabled, title bar text is replaced by unique topic numbers that identify each topic's position in the Help file. The first topic in the first topic (.rtf) file that is listed in your project (.hpj) file appears as topic number one. Subsequent topics are listed in the order specified in your project file. The number for a topic may change if the sequence of topics in your topic file changes or if you add or remove topics. To display a specific topic by its number, press ctrl+shift+j in a topic, and the type the topic number.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Topic numbers
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 If Help Author is enabled, title bar text is replaced by unique topic numbers that identify each topic's position in the Help file. The first topic in the first topic (.rtf) file that is listed in your project (.hpj) file appears as topic number one. Subsequent topics are listed in the order specified in your project file. The number for a topic may change if the sequence of topics in your topic file changes or if you add or remove topics. To display a specific topic by its number, press \scaps ctrl+shift+j\plain\f1\fs16  in a topic, and the type the topic number.
\par
```

## 933. CIVCB5

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `CIVCB5`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    CIVCB5
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Easier navigation

If Help Author is enabled, you can use key combinations to navigate within your Help file. If a Help topic is open, you can move to the following or preceding topic by pressing ctrl+shift+right arrow or ctrl+shift+left arrow. To move to the beginning or end of your Help file, press ctrl+shift+home or ctrl+shift+end.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Easier navigation
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 If Help Author is enabled, you can use key combinations to navigate within your Help file. If a Help topic is open, you can move to the following or preceding topic by pressing \scaps ctrl+shift+right arrow\plain\f1\fs16  or \scaps ctrl+shift+left arrow. \plain\f1\fs16 To move to the beginning or end of your Help file, press \scaps ctrl+shift+home \plain\f1\fs16 or \scaps ctrl+shift+end.\plain\f1\fs16 
\par
```

## 934. 12F4E2F

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `12F4E2F`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    12F4E2F
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Information about topics

If Help Author is enabled, a Topic Information command appears when you click a topic by using your right mouse button. Clicking this command displays the topic's title (as specified by its $ footnote), any macros specified by an entry macro (!) footnote, the current window type, and the Help filename and path. Additional information appears in this dialog box if you selected the Include .Rtf Filename And Topic ID In Help File check box in the Compile a Help File dialog box before you compiled. This adds the name of the .rtf file that contains the topic and the topic's ID to the Topic Information dialog box.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Information about topics
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 If Help Author is enabled, a Topic Information command appears when you click a topic by using your right mouse button. Clicking this command displays the topic's title (as specified by its $ footnote), any macros specified by an entry macro (!) footnote, the current window type, and the Help filename and path. Additional information appears in this dialog box if you selected the Include .Rtf Filename And Topic ID In Help File check box in the Compile a Help File dialog box before you compiled. This adds the name of the .rtf file that contains the topic and the topic's ID to the Topic Information dialog box.
\par
```

## 935. 1P_D_XO

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `1P_D_XO`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    1P_D_XO
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Information about hotspots

If Help Author is enabled, an Ask On Hotspots command appears when you click a topic by using your right mouse button. If a check mark appears beside this command, WinHelp does not run the hotspot jump or macro when you click it. Instead, it displays the hidden text associated with the hotspot jump or macro. You can then choose to run the jump or macro, or return to the topic.

Note

·	The Ask On Hotspots command might not work correctly if there are no keywords in your Help file.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Information about hotspots
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 If Help Author is enabled, an Ask On Hotspots command appears when you click a topic by using your right mouse button. If a check mark appears beside this command, WinHelp does not run the hotspot jump or macro when you click it. Instead, it displays the hidden text associated with the hotspot jump or macro. You can then choose to run the jump or macro, or return to the topic.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Note
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab The Ask On Hotspots command might not work correctly if there are no keywords in your Help file.
\par
```

## 936. Compiler messages

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:0080`

    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:0080
    ```


- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `compiler messages;compiler messages, about compiler messages;error handling;Help Workshop;Help Workshop, messages, described;messages;messages, about compiler messages;notes;warning messages`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    compiler messages;compiler messages, about compiler messages;error handling;Help Workshop;Help Workshop, messages, described;messages;messages, about compiler messages;notes;warning messages
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Compiler messages

Windows 95 can display up to 64K of compiler messages (Windows NT can display up to 1 MB). However, if you specify a log file in which to save messages, there is no size limit for the file. For more information about a message and how to resolve it, use the Index or Find tab in Help Workshop Help, and look up the corresponding message ID, for example "HC1003." While you compile your Help file, Help Workshop may display messages of the following types:

{bmc bm23.bmp} Notes%3ZJF2

{bmc bm23.bmp} Warnings%113Q8O6

{bmc bm23.bmp} Errors%10E5O5H

{button ,AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_THE_HELP_COMPILER;EXEC_HCW;EXEC_HCRTF;PRO_HPJ_ERRORLOG;PRO_HPJ_ERRORLOG')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_THE_HELP_COMPILER;EXEC_HCW;EXEC_HCRTF;PRO_HPJ_ERRORLOG;PRO_HPJ_ERRORLOG')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b Compiler messages
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Windows\'a095 can display up to 64K of compiler messages (Windows\'a0NT can display up to 1 MB). However, if you specify a log file in which to save messages, there is no size limit for the file. For more information about a message and how to resolve it, use the Index or Find tab in Help Workshop Help, and look up the corresponding message ID, for example "HC1003." While you compile your Help file, Help Workshop may display messages of the following types:
\par \ul \ul \{bmc bm23.bmp\}\ul  Notes\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %3ZJF2}
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \ul \ul \{bmc bm23.bmp\}\ul  Warnings\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %113Q8O6}
\par \ul \ul \{bmc bm23.bmp\}\ul  Errors\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %10E5O5H}
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_THE_HELP_COMPILER;EXEC_HCW;EXEC_HCRTF;PRO_HPJ_ERRORLOG;PRO_HPJ_ERRORLOG')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_THE_HELP_COMPILER;EXEC_HCW;EXEC_HCRTF;PRO_HPJ_ERRORLOG;PRO_HPJ_ERRORLOG')}
\par
```

## 937. 3ZJF2

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `3ZJF2`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    3ZJF2
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Notes are conditions that you should be aware of. They will probably not cause serious problems when you open your Help file. For example, "HC1003: Note: A paragraph mark is formatted as a hidden character."
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 Notes are conditions that you should be aware of. They will probably not cause serious problems when you open your Help file. For example, "HC1003: Note: A paragraph mark is formatted as a hidden character."
\par
```

## 938. 113Q8O6

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `113Q8O6`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    113Q8O6
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Warnings are conditions that will result in a defective Help file. For example, "HC3059: Warning: A hotspot is specified without a macro or topic ID."
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 Warnings are conditions that will result in a defective Help file. For example, "HC3059: Warning: A hotspot is specified without a macro or topic ID."
\par
```

## 939. 10E5O5H

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `10E5O5H`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    10E5O5H
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Errors are conditions that prevent the Help file from compiling. For example, "HC5005: Error: The file cannot be found."
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 Errors are conditions that prevent the Help file from compiling. For example, "HC5005: Error: The file cannot be found."
\par
```

## 940. Checklist for translating Help files

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:0081`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:0081
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Checklist for translating Help files`

    ```rtf
    Checklist for translating Help files
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `BAS_TRANSLATING`
  - Raw:

    ```rtf
    BAS_TRANSLATING
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `BAS_HPJ;BAS_TRANSLATING`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf

    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `contents files;contents files, translating;Help files;Help files, translating;Help system;Help system, localizing;localizing;localizing, Help files;project files;project files, localizing;testing;testing, translated Help files;translating;translating, Help files`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    contents files;contents files, translating;Help files;Help files, translating;Help system;Help system, localizing;localizing;localizing, Help files;project files;project files, localizing;testing;testing, translated Help files;translating;translating, Help files
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Checklist for translating Help files

WinHelp can display Help files compiled in any of 29 different languages. In addition, WinHelp provides tools for translating a Help file from one language to another while retaining the same structure.

You typically carry out the following tasks sequentially when you translate Help files:

1	Translate topic (.rtf) filesOGO6SZ

2	Change settings in project (.hpj) files42CA8G8

3	Translate contents (.cnt) files59DTMP

4	Compare original and translated versions1BQ.K66

{button ,AL(`test;BAS_SORT_LANGUAGE;BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;BAS_CONTENTS_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_TESTING_HELP_FILES;BAS_PROJECT_FILE_CHECKLIST;BAS_CONTENTS_CHECKLIST;BAS_THE_HELP_COMPILER;translate_hpj')} Related Topics%!AL(`test;BAS_SORT_LANGUAGE;BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;BAS_CONTENTS_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_TESTING_HELP_FILES;BAS_PROJECT_FILE_CHECKLIST;BAS_CONTENTS_CHECKLIST;BAS_THE_HELP_COMPILER;translate_hpj')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b Checklist for translating Help files
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 WinHelp can display Help files compiled in any of 29 different languages. In addition, WinHelp provides tools for translating a Help file from one language to another while retaining the same structure.
\par You typically carry out the following tasks sequentially when you translate Help files:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \uldb 1\tab Translate topic (.rtf) files\plain\f1\fs16 {\v OGO6SZ}\uldb 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 2\tab Change settings in project (.hpj) files\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 42CA8G8}\uldb 
\par 3\tab Translate contents (.cnt) files\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 59DTMP}\uldb 
\par 4\tab Compare original and translated versions\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 1BQ.K66}
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`test;BAS_SORT_LANGUAGE;BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;BAS_CONTENTS_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_TESTING_HELP_FILES;BAS_PROJECT_FILE_CHECKLIST;BAS_CONTENTS_CHECKLIST;BAS_THE_HELP_COMPILER;translate_hpj')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`test;BAS_SORT_LANGUAGE;BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;BAS_PROJ_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;BAS_CONTENTS_FILE_EDITOR;BAS_TESTING_HELP_FILES;BAS_PROJECT_FILE_CHECKLIST;BAS_CONTENTS_CHECKLIST;BAS_THE_HELP_COMPILER;translate_hpj')}
\par
```

## 941. OGO6SZ

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `OGO6SZ`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    OGO6SZ
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Translate topic (.rtf) files

Create copies of the original topic files before you translate the text. You will probably want to translate all the text in topics, including K-keywords,9ML77Q titles, and the text on authorable buttons.FO6MEW When translating K-keywords, retain one-to-one relationships for the sake of any KLink1R9H33X macros in the Help file. Be sure that keywords specified in KLink macros are updated to specify translated keywords. Be sure to retain the original topic IDs7.61TY in the # footnotes,.NSQ_T any hidden jump identifiers, browse tags, build tags,12MF62E and macros.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Translate topic (.rtf) files
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Create copies of the original topic files before you translate the text. You will probably want to translate all the text in topics, including \ul K-keywords,\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 9ML77Q} titles, and the text on \ul authorable buttons.\plain\f1\fs16 {\v FO6MEW} When translating K-keywords, retain one-to-one relationships for the sake of any \ul KLink\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 1R9H33X} macros in the Help file. Be sure that keywords specified in KLink macros are updated to specify translated keywords. Be sure to retain the original \ul topic IDs\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7.61TY} in the # \ul footnotes,\plain\f1\fs16 {\v .NSQ_T} any hidden jump identifiers, browse tags, \ul build tags,\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 12MF62E} and macros. 
\par
```

## 942. 42CA8G8

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `42CA8G8`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    42CA8G8
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Change settings in project (.hpj) files

Create a copy of the original project file. In Help Workshop, open the new project file, and then check the Translation command on the File menu. Most settings that should not be translated become unavailable. Make any necessary changes, including:

·	The sorting order for keywords

·	The default character set

·	The default fonts

·	Any path changes to point to the translated topic files

·	Whether the topic (.rtf) files use a double-byte character set (DBCS)
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Change settings in project (.hpj) files 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Create a copy of the original project file. In Help Workshop, open the new project file, and then check the Translation command on the File menu. Most settings that should not be translated become unavailable. Make any necessary changes, including:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab The sorting order for keywords
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab The default character set
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab The default fonts
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Any path changes to point to the translated topic files
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Whether the topic (.rtf) files use a double-byte character set (DBCS)
\par
```

```text
Translate contents (.cnt) files

```

```rtf
\par
```

## 944. 1BQ.K66

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `1BQ.K66`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    1BQ.K66
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Compare original and translated versions

The Compare macro enables you to view two Help files at once. This enables you to view the original topics and the corresponding translated topics side-by-side. To run the Compare macro, press ctrl+shift+j when a topic is open, and then type the following:

!compare("filename.hlp")

For filename.hlp, substitute the name of the Help file you want to compare with the file that is open.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Compare original and translated versions
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 The Compare macro enables you to view two Help files at once. This enables you to view the original topics and the corresponding translated topics side-by-side. To run the Compare macro, press \scaps ctrl+shift+j\plain\f1\fs16  when a topic is open, and then type the following:
\par \b !compare("filename.hlp")\plain\f1\fs16 
\par For filename.hlp, substitute the name of the Help file you want to compare with the file that is open.\b 
\par
```

- Marker `K`

  - Raw:

    ```rtf
    customizing;customizing, Help windows;Help system;Help system, customizing topic windows;Help windows;Help windows, customizing;secondary windows;secondary windows, customizing;topic windows, customizing;windows,;windows, customizing
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Customizing topic windows

WinHelp includes a predefined window called the main window, which has a menu bar and button bar. You can customize the main window or create new secondary windows.2ZK4PT

You can define as many as 255 window types (including the main window) in your project (.hpj) file. WinHelp can display up to 10 windows at a time (the main window and nine secondary windows). You can customize the following window properties:

{bmc bm23.bmp} Size and position%.T1IMB

{bmc bm23.bmp} Background color%IQNGV4

{bmc bm23.bmp} Title bar text%J7HORR

{bmc bm23.bmp} Button bar%6ORX.S.

{bmc bm23.bmp} Menus and menu commands%.SVEAR

{bmc bm23.bmp} Stay-on-top status%54MJKZG

{bmc bm23.bmp} Automatic window sizing%U0U8PP

{bmc bm23.bmp} Macros that run when a window opens%2KL9_I6

{button ,AL(`BAS_AUTH_MENU;BAS_CONFIG_MACRO;NAVIGATION_DESIGN;MACRO_REF_OVR;BAS_AUTH_BTN;BAS_AUTH_BTN;WINDOW_DESIGN')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_AUTH_MENU;BAS_CONFIG_MACRO;NAVIGATION_DESIGN;MACRO_REF_OVR;BAS_AUTH_BTN;BAS_AUTH_BTN;WINDOW_DESIGN')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

Customizing topic windows
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 WinHelp includes a predefined window called the main window, which has a menu bar and button bar. You can customize the main window or create new \ul secondary windows.\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 2ZK4PT}
\par You can define as many as 255 window types (including the main window) in your project (.hpj) file. WinHelp can display up to 10 windows at a time (the main window and nine secondary windows). You can customize the following window properties:
\par \uldb \{bmc bm23.bmp\} Size and position\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %.T1IMB}
\par \uldb \{bmc bm23.bmp\} Background color\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %IQNGV4}
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \uldb \{bmc bm23.bmp\} Title bar text\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %J7HORR}
\par \uldb \{bmc bm23.bmp\} Button bar\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %6ORX.S.}
\par \uldb \{bmc bm23.bmp\} Menus and menu commands\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %.SVEAR}
\par \uldb \{bmc bm23.bmp\} Stay-on-top status\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %54MJKZG}
\par \uldb \{bmc bm23.bmp\} Automatic window sizing\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %U0U8PP}
\par \uldb \{bmc bm23.bmp\} Macros that run when a window opens\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %2KL9_I6}
```

```text
Changing a window's size and position

You can specify the size and position of a window by opening your project (.hpj) file in Help Workshop, and then clicking the Windows button. Size and position settings are on the Position tab.

Tip
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Changing a window's button bar

You can add buttons to or remove them from a window's button bar by opening your project (.hpj) file in Help Workshop, clicking the Windows button, and then clicking the Buttons tab.

Notes

·	For help on an item in the Window Properties dialog box, click {bmc bm0.bmp} in its title bar, and then click the item.

·	In addition to the buttons provided by Help Workshop, you can also use Help macros to add up to 22 buttons to the button bar in a Help window. For more information, click Related Topics below.

{button ,AL("PRO_HPJ_MAIN_BUTTON_BAR;PRO_HPJ_ADD_BUTTON_BAR;MACRO_REF_BUTTON")} Related Topics%!AL("PRO_HPJ_MAIN_BUTTON_BAR;PRO_HPJ_ADD_BUTTON_BAR;MACRO_REF_BUTTON")
```

```text
Changing the main window's menu commands

You can add menus and menu commands to the menu bar in a main window by adding the necessary macros to the configuration section of your project (.hpj) file. To do this, open your project file in Help Workshop and click the Config button.

Tip

·	For help on an item in the Configuration Macros dialog box, click {bmc bm0.bmp} in its title bar, and then click the item.
```
