## 951. 54MJKZG

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `54MJKZG`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    54MJKZG
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Changing a window's on-top state

You can specify that a window remains in the foreground, even when a user jumps to another window or program. To do this, open your project (.hpj) file in Help Workshop, click the Windows button, and then make sure Keep Help Window On Top is checked.

Note

·	Users can override the Keep Help Window On Top setting by clicking a topic using their right mouse button, and then clicking Keep Help On Top.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Changing a window's on-top state
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 You can specify that a window remains in the foreground, even when a user jumps to another window or program. To do this, open your project (.hpj) file in Help Workshop, click the Windows button, and then make sure Keep Help Window On Top is checked.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Note
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab Users can override the Keep Help Window On Top setting by clicking a topic using their right mouse button, and then clicking Keep Help On Top.
\par
```

## 952. U0U8PP

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `U0U8PP`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    U0U8PP
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Specifying whether a window should auto-size

You can set up secondary windows so that their length automatically adjusts to accommodate the amount of text in the topic. To do this, open your project (.hpj) file in Help Workshop, click the Windows button, and then select the Auto-Size Height check box.

Notes

·	The Auto-Size Height setting overrides all other height settings and can be used only for secondary windows.

·	For help on an item in the Window Properties dialog box, click {bmc bm0.bmp} in its title bar, and then click the item.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Specifying whether a window should auto-size
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 You can set up secondary windows so that their length automatically adjusts to accommodate the amount of text in the topic. To do this, open your project (.hpj) file in Help Workshop, click the Windows button, and then select the Auto-Size Height check box.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Notes
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab The Auto-Size Height setting overrides all other height settings and can be used only for secondary windows.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab For help on an item in the Window Properties dialog box, click \{bmc bm0.bmp\} in its title bar, and then click the item.
\par
```

## 953. 2KL9_I6

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`

### Decoded Body

```text
Specifying macros that run when a window opens

You can run macros every time a particular window type opens by associating macros with that window type. For example, you may want to associate macros that add special menus, menu commands, and buttons to a window. To do this, open your project (.hpj) file in Help Workshop, click the Windows button, and then click the Macros tab.

Tips

·	By adding entry macros2BTJW15 to certain topics, you can make a particular command or button appear when a user opens some topics but not others.

·	For help on an item in the Window Properties dialog box, click {bmc bm0.bmp} in its title bar, and then click the item.

·	You can also assign macros to individual window types by opening your project (.hpj) file in Help Workshop, clicking the Windows button, and then clicking the Macros tab. Any macros you assign to the main window will override macros assigned to the main window in the Help Configuration dialog box. However, any macros you assign to a secondary window2ZK4PT apply only to that window type.

{button ,AL(`MACRO_OVERVIEW;MACRO_REF_MENU;PRO_HPJ_WINDOW_CONFIG_MACROS')} Related Topics%!AL(`MACRO_OVERVIEW;MACRO_REF_MENU;PRO_HPJ_WINDOW_CONFIG_MACROS')
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Specifying macros that apply to your entire Help file

You can specify macros that run whenever a user opens a Help file. For example, you can use macros to specify optional buttons, menus, or menu items for the main window and to access programs and capabilities outside the Help system. To do this, open your project (.hpj) file in Help Workshop, and then click the Config button.

Tips

·	For help on an item in the Configuration Macros dialog box, click {bmc bm0.bmp} in its title bar, and then click the item.

·	You can also assign macros to individual window types by opening your project (.hpj) file in Help Workshop, clicking the Windows button, and then clicking the Macros tab. Any macros you assign to the main window will override macros assigned to the main window in the Help Configuration dialog box. However, any macros you assign to a secondary window2ZK4PT apply only to that window type.

{button ,AL(`BAS_HPJ_CREATE_WINDOW;BAS_AUTH_BTN;MACRO_OVERVIEW;MACRO_REF_OVR')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_HPJ_CREATE_WINDOW;BAS_AUTH_BTN;MACRO_OVERRVIEW;MACRO_REF_OVR')
```

## 955. Overview of full-text search features

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`

    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `Find Setup wizard;Find tab;Find tab, about full-text search;full-text search;full-text search, about full-text search;Help system;Help system, full-text search availablility;index,;index, full-text search;search, full-text`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Find Setup wizard;Find tab;Find tab, about full-text search;full-text search;full-text search, about full-text search;Help system;Help system, full-text search availablility;index,;index, full-text search;search, full-text
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Overview of full-text search features

WinHelp version 4.0 includes a full-text search feature, which enables users to search through every word in Help files to find a match.

In most cases, when a user clicks the Find tab in the Help Topics dialog boxKMK38I for the first time, the Find Setup wizard appears. The wizard helps users set up a full-text search index. A full-text search index lists all the unique words in the Help file.

Instead of having users create their own full-text search index, you can create it for them and ship it with your Help files. The advantage of shipping a full-text search index is that full-text search features are immediately available to users. The disadvantage is that a full-text search index takes up space on your product's distribution disks.

To create a full-text search index for your users, open your project (.hpj) file, and then click the Options button. Click the FTS tab, and change settings as needed. This causes a full-text search (.fts) file to be created whenever you compile your Help file with maximum or Hall compression turned on.

{button ,AL(`NEW_WINHELP_INTERFACE;nofind_cnt;PRO_FULLTEXT_INDEX')} Related Topics%!AL(`NEW_WINHELP_INTERFACE;nofind_cnt;PRO_FULLTEXT_INDEX')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b 
\par Overview of full-text search features
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 WinHelp version 4.0 includes a full-text search feature, which enables users to search through every word in Help files to find a match. 
\par In most cases, when a user clicks the Find tab in the \ul Help Topics dialog box\plain\f1\fs16 {\v KMK38I} for the first time, the Find Setup wizard appears. The wizard helps users set up a full-text search index. A full-text search index lists all the unique words in the Help file. 
\par Instead of having users create their own full-text search index, you can create it for them and ship it with your Help files. The advantage of shipping a full-text search index is that full-text search features are immediately available to users. The disadvantage is that a full-text search index takes up space on your product's distribution disks.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 To create a full-text search index for your users, open your project (.hpj) file, and then click the Options button. Click the FTS tab, and change settings as needed. This causes a full-text search (.fts) file to be created whenever you compile your Help file with maximum or Hall compression turned on.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`NEW_WINHELP_INTERFACE;nofind_cnt;PRO_FULLTEXT_INDEX')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`NEW_WINHELP_INTERFACE;nofind_cnt;PRO_FULLTEXT_INDEX')}
\par
```

## 957. 4FEK_M6

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `4FEK_M6`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    4FEK_M6
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Title bar text for the Help Topics dialog box

You specify text for the title bar of the Help Topics dialog boxKMK38I by opening your contents (.cnt) file, and then typing the title in the Default Title box. If your Help system has more than one Help file, specifying a default title ensures that the Help Topics dialog box has the same title regardless of which Help file is open.

If no default title is specified in the contents file, WinHelp uses the Help title specified for the current Help file. You can specify the Help title by opening your project (.hpj) file, clicking the Options button, and then typing the title in the Help Title box.

If no default title is specified in the contents file and no Help title is specified in the project file, WinHelp displays the filename of the current Help file in the title bar of the Help Topics dialog box.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Title bar text for the Help Topics dialog box 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 You specify text for the title bar of the \ul Help Topics dialog box\plain\f1\fs16 {\v KMK38I} by opening your contents (.cnt) file, and then typing the title in the Default Title box. If your Help system has more than one Help file, specifying a default title ensures that the Help Topics dialog box has the same title regardless of which Help file is open.
\par If no default title is specified in the contents file, WinHelp uses the Help title specified for the current Help file. You can specify the Help title by opening your project (.hpj) file, clicking the Options button, and then typing the title in the Help Title box.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 If no default title is specified in the contents file and no Help title is specified in the project file, WinHelp displays the filename of the current Help file in the title bar of the Help Topics dialog box.
\par
```

Headings in the Topics Found dialog box

From the Help Index, the Topics Found dialog box appears when a user double-clicks a keyword9ML77Q that is associated with more than one topic. From within topics, the Topics Found dialog box appears when users click text that is associated with an ALink or KLink macro. The topic titles in this dialog box correspond to the titles you specify in the $ footnote in your topic (.rtf) file.
```

## 959. 1MIZBM.

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `1MIZBM.`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    1MIZBM.
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Title bar text for Help windows

You can specify text for the title bar of each window type by opening your project (.hpj) file, and then clicking the Windows button.

If no title bar text is specified for a window type, WinHelp uses the default title specified in your contents file. You can specify a default title, by opening your contents (.cnt) file, and then typing the title in the Default Title box.

If no default title is specified in the contents file, WinHelp uses the Help title specified for the current Help file for the main window, and leaves the title blank in secondary windows. You can specify the Help title by opening your project (.hpj) file, clicking the Options button, and then typing the title in the Help Title box.

If no default title is specified in the contents file and no Help title is specified in the project file, "Windows Help" appears in the title bar of the main Help window and secondary window titles will be blank.

Note

·	A Help window's title bar text also appears on a taskbar button when a Help window is open. The taskbar is the bar on your Windows 95 desktop that has the Start button on it. Buttons representing running programs appear on this bar.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf

\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 You can specify text for the title bar of each window type by opening your project (.hpj) file, and then clicking the Windows button. 
\par If no title bar text is specified for a window type, WinHelp uses the default title specified in your contents file. You can specify a default title, by opening your contents (.cnt) file, and then typing the title in the Default Title box. 
\par If no default title is specified in the contents file, WinHelp uses the Help title specified for the current Help file for the main window, and leaves the title blank in secondary windows. You can specify the Help title by opening your project (.hpj) file, clicking the Options button, and then typing the title in the Help Title box.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 If no default title is specified in the contents file and no Help title is specified in the project file, "Windows Help" appears in the title bar of the main Help window and secondary window titles will be blank. 
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Note
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab A Help window's title bar text also appears on a taskbar button when a Help window is open. The taskbar is the bar on your Windows\'a095 desktop that has the Start button on it. Buttons representing running programs appear on this bar.
\par
```

## 960. 4T1TZI

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `4T1TZI`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    4T1TZI
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
File titles in the Find Setup wizard

When users click the Find tab in the Help Topics dialog boxKMK38I for the first time, the Find Setup wizard typically appears. If a user chooses Custom in this wizard, a list of current Help files appears. From this list, users can choose which files they want to include in their full-text search index. The items in the list correspond to the Help titles specified for index files in your contents (.cnt) file. You can specify Help titles for index files by opening your contents file, and then clicking the Index Files button.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b File titles in the Find Setup wizard

If a user chooses Custom in this wizard, a list of current Help files appears. From this list, users can choose which files they want to include in their full-text search index. The items in the list correspond to the Help titles specified for index files in your contents (.cnt) file. You can specify Help titles for index files by opening your contents file, and then clicking the Index Files button.
\par
```

## 962. Designing context-sensitive Help

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:0086`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:0086
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `Designing context-sensitive Help`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Designing context-sensitive Help
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `GJ164W`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    GJ164W
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `BAS_HPJ;BAS_HPJ_ADD_MAP`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BAS_HPJ;BAS_HPJ_ADD_MAP
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `context-sensitive Help;context-sensitive Help, designing;context-sensitive Help, mapping topic IDs;dialog boxes;dialog boxes, designing context-sensitive Help for;Help system;Help system, designing context-sensitive Help;mapping topic IDs;project files;project files, mapping topic IDs;topic IDs;topic IDs, mapping`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    context-sensitive Help;context-sensitive Help, designing;context-sensitive Help, mapping topic IDs;dialog boxes;dialog boxes, designing context-sensitive Help for;Help system;Help system, designing context-sensitive Help;mapping topic IDs;project files;project files, mapping topic IDs;topic IDs;topic IDs, mapping
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Designing context-sensitive Help

In WinHelp version 4.0, you can provide context-sensitive Help for many of your program's interface elements. In dialog boxes, users typically display context-sensitive help by clicking {bmc bm0.bmp} at the top of a dialog box, and then clicking the item. In windows, users typically display context-sensitive help by clicking an item using their right mouse button, and then clicking What's This.

Help authors and program developers typically work together to implement context-sensitive Help. Help authors write the relevant Help topics, and program developers modify the program to call the correct Help topic. As part of this modification, developers assign numeric values to items that users are likely to want more information about. You can then map these numeric values to the appropriate topic IDs7.61TY in your project (.hpj) file.

Note

·	When assigning topic IDs to context-sensitive Help topics, make sure the first four characters in the ID are IDH_. Help Workshop recognizes that topic IDs beginning with these characters are ones that a program will call. While compiling, Help Workshop displays a list of topic IDs that are in your topic (.rtf) files, but have not been mapped to numeric values. Help Workshop also lists IDs that are mapped to numeric values but are not in your topic files. Using the IDH_ naming convention will help you find and resolve problems in your context-sensitive Help.

{button ,AL(`CONTEXT_SENSITIVE_DESIGN;MAP;NEW_CONTEXT;cs_help;PRO_HPJ_ADD_MAP')} Related Topics%!AL(`CONTEXT_SENSITIVE_DESIGN;MAP;NEW_CONTEXT;cs_help;PRO_HPJ_ADD_MAP')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b Designing context-sensitive Help
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 In WinHelp version 4.0, you can provide context-sensitive Help for many of your program's interface elements. In dialog boxes, users typically display context-sensitive help by clicking \{bmc bm0.bmp\} at the top of a dialog box, and then clicking the item. In windows, users typically display context-sensitive help by clicking an item using their right mouse button, and then clicking What's This.
\par Help authors and program developers typically work together to implement context-sensitive Help. Help authors write the relevant Help topics, and program developers modify the program to call the correct Help topic. As part of this modification, developers assign numeric values to items that users are likely to want more information about. You can then map these numeric values to the appropriate \ul topic IDs\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7.61TY} in your project (.hpj) file.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Note
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab When assigning topic IDs to context-sensitive Help topics, make sure the first four characters in the ID are IDH_. Help Workshop recognizes that topic IDs beginning with these characters are ones that a program will call. While compiling, Help Workshop displays a list of topic IDs that are in your topic (.rtf) files, but have not been mapped to numeric values. Help Workshop also lists IDs that are mapped to numeric values but are not in your topic files. Using the IDH_ naming convention will help you find and resolve problems in your context-sensitive Help.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`CONTEXT_SENSITIVE_DESIGN;MAP;NEW_CONTEXT;cs_help;PRO_HPJ_ADD_MAP')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`CONTEXT_SENSITIVE_DESIGN;MAP;NEW_CONTEXT;cs_help;PRO_HPJ_ADD_MAP')}
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Keyword jumps

Keyword jumps are new macros that provide indirect links to one or more Help topics. When users click a hotspot,7BM6.K button, or menu command that is associated with a keyword jump, WinHelp displays the Topics Found dialog box, which lists topics that contain the same keyword as the one specified by the hotspot. Keyword jumps are made possible by KLink and ALink macros. For more information about these macros, click one of the following buttons.

{bmc bm23.bmp} KLink keyword jumps%8WZJ5O

{bmc bm23.bmp} ALink keyword jumps%JULTCG

{button ,AL(`MACRO_REF_OVR;MULTIPLE_HELP_DESIGN;BAS_MACRO_JUMP;BAS_INSERTING_JUMP;BAS_INSERTING_POPUP;ALink;KLink;PRO_INSERT_MACRO;PRO_CNT_LINKS;BAS_TOPIC_FOOTNOTE;BAS_ADDING_KEYWORDS')} Related Topics%!AL(`MACRO_REF_OVR;MULTIPLE_HELP_DESIGN;BAS_MACRO_JUMP;BAS_INSERTING_JUMP;BAS_INSERTING_POPUP;ALink;KLink;PRO_INSERT_MACRO;PRO_CNT_LINKS;BAS_TOPIC_FOOTNOTE;BAS_ADDING_KEYWORDS')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b Keyword jumps
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Keyword jumps are new macros that provide indirect links to one or more Help topics. When users click a \ul hotspot,\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 7BM6.K} button, or menu command that is associated with a keyword jump, WinHelp displays the Topics Found dialog box, which lists topics that contain the same keyword as the one specified by the hotspot. Keyword jumps are made possible by KLink and ALink macros. For more information about these macros, click one of the following buttons.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li535\ri125\fi-425\tx535 \uldb \{bmc bm23.bmp\} KLink keyword jumps\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %8WZJ5O}\b 
\par \uldb \plain\f1\uldb\fs16 \{bmc bm23.bmp\} ALink keyword jumps\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %JULTCG}
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295

## 964. 8WZJ5O

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `8WZJ5O`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    8WZJ5O
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
KLink keyword jumps

KLink jumps are based on keywords specified by K-footnotes.NSQ_T in your topic (.rtf) files. For example, a KLink macro that specifies the keywords "macros" and "graphics" will present users with a Topics Found dialog box containing a list of all topics that have the word "macro" or "graphics" in their K footnotes. As you author additional topics that contain these keywords, the items in the Topics Found dialog box increase accordingly. However, if the topic from which a user runs the KLink macro contains the designated keyword in its K-footnote, that topic does not appear in the Topics Found dialog box.

The main benefit of KLink macros is that they are automatically updated as keywords are added to or deleted from topics. They also enable you to jump to topics in Help files that may be on a user's hard disk at some point in the future.

Note

·	Keywords specified in the KLink macro must exactly match K-keywords. For example, KLink(network) will not match the first-level index keyword "network," because the macro specification lacks a trailing comma.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b KLink keyword jumps
\par \plain\f1\fs16 KLink jumps are based on keywords specified by K-\ul footnotes\plain\f1\fs16 {\v .NSQ_T} in your topic (.rtf) files. For example, a KLink macro that specifies the keywords "macros" and "graphics" will present users with a Topics Found dialog box containing a list of all topics that have the word "macro" or "graphics" in their K footnotes. As you author additional topics that contain these keywords, the items in the Topics Found dialog box increase accordingly. However, if the topic from which a user runs the KLink macro contains the designated keyword in its K-footnote, that topic does not appear in the Topics Found dialog box.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 The main benefit of KLink macros is that they are automatically updated as keywords are added to or deleted from topics. They also enable you to jump to topics in Help files that may be on a user's hard disk at some point in the future. 
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Note
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab Keywords specified in the KLink macro must exactly match K-keywords. For example, KLink(network) will not match the first-level index keyword "network," because the macro specification lacks a trailing comma.
\par
```

## 965. JULTCG

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `JULTCG`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    JULTCG
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
ALink keyword jumps

ALink jumps are identical to KLink jumps except that the keywords they jump to are specified by A-footnotes in topics. A-keywords are used only for ALink macros and, unlike K-keywords, they never appear on the Index tab in the Help Topics dialog box. As with KLink macros, if the topic from which a user runs the ALink macro contains the designated keyword in its A-footnote, that topic does not appear in the Topics Found dialog box.

The main benefit of ALink macros is that they do not change, regardless of any changes to the K-keywords in topic (.rtf) files.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b ALink keyword jumps\plain\f1\fs16 
\par ALink jumps are identical to KLink jumps except that the keywords they jump to are specified by A-footnotes in topics. A-keywords are used only for ALink macros and, unlike K-keywords, they never appear on the Index tab in the Help Topics dialog box. As with KLink macros, if the topic from which a user runs the ALink macro contains the designated keyword in its A-footnote, that topic does not appear in the Topics Found dialog box.
\par The main benefit of ALink macros is that they do not change, regardless of any changes to the K-keywords in topic (.rtf) files.
\par
```

Using graphics in your Help files

In your topic files, you can include graphics of the following types: Windows bitmaps (.bmp or .dib), Windows metafiles (.wmf), Windows Help multiple-hotspot7BM6.K (SHED) bitmaps (.shg), and Windows Help multi-resolution bitmaps (.mrb). For more information about using graphics in your Help files, click one of the following buttons.

{bmc bm23.bmp} Graphics color support%WGKUZ3

{bmc bm23.bmp} Transparent bitmaps%E0QHFW
```

For more information about using graphics in your Help files, click one of the following buttons.
\par \uldb \{bmc bm23.bmp\} Graphics color support\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %WGKUZ3}
\par \uldb \{bmc bm23.bmp\} Transparent bitmaps\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %E0QHFW}
\par

## 967. WGKUZ3

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `WGKUZ3`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    WGKUZ3
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Graphics color support

WinHelp version 4.0 can display graphics with any number of colors. WinHelp automatically reduces the color depth1NQNUGQ as necessary to the maximum color depth allowed by a user's display driver. The result is sometimes unsatisfactory, especially in cases where more than one graphic appears on a user's screen. For greater control, you can use optimized 16-color images (which work well over a wide range of platforms) or the {-bmx} statement (which enables you to specify a series of graphics that have different color depths).
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Graphics color support
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 WinHelp version 4.0 can display graphics with any number of colors. WinHelp automatically reduces the \ul color depth\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 1NQNUGQ} as necessary to the maximum color depth allowed by a user's display driver. The result is sometimes unsatisfactory, especially in cases where more than one graphic appears on a user's screen. For greater control, you can use optimized 16-color images (which work well over a wide range of platforms) or the \{\-bmx\'7d statement (which enables you to specify a series of graphics that have different color depths).
\par
```

## 968. E0QHFW

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `E0QHFW`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    E0QHFW
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Transparent bitmaps

If you are using a colored background in your topics, you may want to use transparent bitmaps.L00JF5 To do this, add the letter t to the {-bmx} statement, for example, {-bmct}, {-bmlt}, or {-bmrt}. WinHelp replaces the white pixels in a transparent bitmap with the background color of the currently active window the first time the bitmap is displayed. If the background color is dithered, WinHelp uses a solid color closest to the dither for the bitmap's background color.

{button ,AL(`GRAPHICS_DESIGN;BAS_MULT_RESOLUTION;bmx;SHED')} Related Topics%!AL(`GRAPHICS_DESIGN;BAS_MULT_RESOLUTION;bmx;SHED')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Transparent bitmaps
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 If you are using a colored background in your topics, you may want to use \ul transparent bitmaps.\plain\f1\fs16 {\v L00JF5} To do this, add the letter t to the \{\-bmx\'7d statement, for example, \{\-bmct\'7d, \{\-bmlt\'7d, or \{\-bmrt\'7d. WinHelp replaces the white pixels in a transparent bitmap with the background color of the currently active window the first time the bitmap is displayed. If the background color is dithered, WinHelp uses a solid color closest to the dither for the bitmap's background color.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL(`GRAPHICS_DESIGN;BAS_MULT_RESOLUTION;bmx;SHED')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`GRAPHICS_DESIGN;BAS_MULT_RESOLUTION;bmx;SHED')}

- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `aspect ratio of bitmaps;bitmaps,;bitmaps, about bitmaps in Help files;bitmaps, aspect ratio;graphics;graphics, about graphics in Help files;graphics, multiple aspect ratio;Help files;Help files, graphics in;Help system;Help system, using graphics in;multiple aspect ratio graphics;multi-resolution graphics;multi-resolution graphics, about multiple aspect ratio graphics`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    aspect ratio of bitmaps;bitmaps,;bitmaps, about bitmaps in Help files;bitmaps, aspect ratio;graphics;graphics, about graphics in Help files;graphics, multiple aspect ratio;Help files;Help files, graphics in;Help system;Help system, using graphics in;multiple aspect ratio graphics;multi-resolution graphics;multi-resolution graphics, about multiple aspect ratio graphics
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Multiple aspect ratio graphics

If a specified bitmap's aspect ratio is different from the aspect ratio of a user's monitor, WinHelp attempts to compensate for the difference. The result is a slightly distorted bitmap. If a graphic includes text, the text may not be readable.

Help Workshop automatically removes the aspect ratio from bitmap (.bmp) files so that WinHelp will not stretch or shrink them. If you want WinHelp to distort your bitmaps, you must create them using the Multi-Resolution Bitmap Compiler. Doing so will preserve the aspect ratio of the bitmap, but the bitmap typically does not compress as well.

To include Multi-Resolution Bitmap Compiler (.mrb) files in a Help file, use the {-bmx} bitmap reference statement.

Note

·	If you want text in a graphic to change depending on the aspect ratio of a user’s monitor, use a metafile.

{button ,AL(`MRBC;bmx')} Related Topics%!AL(`BMRBC;bmx')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b Multiple aspect ratio graphics
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 If a specified bitmap's aspect ratio is different from the aspect ratio of a user's monitor, WinHelp attempts to compensate for the difference. The result is a slightly distorted bitmap. If a graphic includes text, the text may not be readable.
\par Help Workshop automatically removes the aspect ratio from bitmap (.bmp) files so that WinHelp will not stretch or shrink them. If you want WinHelp to distort your bitmaps, you must create them using the Multi-Resolution Bitmap Compiler. Doing so will preserve the aspect ratio of the bitmap, but the bitmap typically does not compress as well.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 To include Multi-Resolution Bitmap Compiler (.mrb) files in a Help file, use the \{\-bmx\'7d bitmap reference statement.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Note
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab If you want text in a graphic to change depending on the aspect ratio of a user\'92s monitor, use a metafile.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`MRBC;bmx')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BMRBC;bmx')}
\par
```

## 970. Multiple color depth graphics

### WinHelp Footnotes

    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `BAS_MULT_COLOR_GRAPHICS`

    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `bitmaps,;bitmaps, color depth;color depth;colors;colors, about multiple color depth graphics;graphics;graphics, color depth;Help files;Help files, graphics in;Help system;Help system, using graphics in;multiple color depth graphics`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    bitmaps,;bitmaps, color depth;color depth;colors;colors, about multiple color depth graphics;graphics;graphics, color depth;Help files;Help files, graphics in;Help system;Help system, using graphics in;multiple color depth graphics
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Multiple color depth graphics

If a specified bitmap's color depth1NQNUGQ is different from the color depth of a user's monitor, WinHelp attempts to compensate for the difference. This has no effect when a low color-depth graphic is displayed on a high color-depth monitor, but a high color-depth graphic may look bad when it is displayed on a low color-depth monitor.

To more precisely control the way a bitmap appears on computers that support different color depths, you can create a separate file for each bitmap — one file for each color depth users are likely to have. After you have created these files, you can list them in a single {-bmx} bitmap statement, separated by semicolons. WinHelp determines the color depth capability of a user's computer, and then uses the most appropriate bitmap file.

If you specify multiple bitmaps in a {-bmx} bitmap statement, you must specify the same number of bitmaps in the same order in all your topic (.rtf) files. If you do not do this, some of the bitmaps you specify may not appear. Or the bitmaps you specify may take twice as much room in your Help file. For example:

·	If you specify {-bmc Ralph_16.bmp; Petra_256.bmp} and {-bmc Ralph_16.bmp; Tony_256.bmp} in your topic files, only the first occurrence of the bitmap statement will be used in all cases. The Ralph_16.bmp and Petra_256.bmp bitmaps will appear in your Help file, but the Tony_256.bmp file will not.

·	If you specify {-bmc Nancy_16.bmp; Tami_256.bmp} and {-bmc Tami_256.bmp; Nancy_16.bmp}, both bitmaps will appear in your Help file, but will take twice as much space. Help Workshop considers each bitmap statement as unique because the bitmaps are specified in a different order.

·	If you specify {-bmc Ralph_16.bmp; Laura_256.bmp}, and later specify only {-bmc Ralph_16.bmp} in your topic files, Ralph_16.bmp and Laura_256.bmp will be used in both instances.

{button ,AL(`BAS_ADDING_GRAPHICS;BAS_MULT_RESOLUTION;bmx')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_ADDING_GRAPHICS;BAS_MULT_RESOLUTION;bmx')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b Multiple color depth graphics
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 If a specified bitmap's \ul color depth\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 1NQNUGQ} is different from the color depth of a user's monitor, WinHelp attempts to compensate for the difference. This has no effect when a low color-depth graphic is displayed on a high color-depth monitor, but a high color-depth graphic may look bad when it is displayed on a low color-depth monitor. 
\par To more precisely control the way a bitmap appears on computers that support different color depths, you can create a separate file for each bitmap \'97 one file for each color depth users are likely to have. After you have created these files, you can list them in a single \{\-bmx\'7d bitmap statement, separated by semicolons. WinHelp determines the color depth capability of a user's computer, and then uses the most appropriate bitmap file. 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 If you specify multiple bitmaps in a \{\-bmx\'7d bitmap statement, you must specify the same number of bitmaps in the same order in all your topic (.rtf) files. If you do not do this, some of the bitmaps you specify may not appear. Or the bitmaps you specify may take twice as much room in your Help file. For example:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab If you specify \{\-bmc Ralph_16.bmp; Petra_256.bmp\'7d and \{\-bmc Ralph_16.bmp; Tony_256.bmp\'7d in your topic files, only the first occurrence of the bitmap statement will be used in all cases. The Ralph_16.bmp and Petra_256.bmp bitmaps will appear in your Help file, but the Tony_256.bmp file will not.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab If you specify \{\-bmc Nancy_16.bmp; Tami_256.bmp\'7d and \{\-bmc Tami_256.bmp; Nancy_16.bmp\'7d, both bitmaps will appear in your Help file, but will take twice as much space. Help Workshop considers each bitmap statement as unique because the bitmaps are specified in a different order.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab If you specify \{\-bmc Ralph_16.bmp; Laura_256.bmp\'7d, and later specify only \{\-bmc Ralph_16.bmp\'7d in your topic files, Ralph_16.bmp and Laura_256.bmp will be used in both instances.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295

## 971. About training card Help

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:008b`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BROWSE0027:008b
    ```
- Marker `$`
  - Decoded: `About training card Help`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    About training card Help
    ```
- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `3DR7SH0`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    3DR7SH0
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `BAS_TRAIN_CARDS`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BAS_TRAIN_CARDS
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `Help system;Help system, designing training cards;TCard macro;training card Help`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    Help system;Help system, designing training cards;TCard macro;training card Help
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
About training card Help

Training cards are a set of Help topics in secondary windows that you can configure to send instructions to and receive instructions from another program. This is particularly useful in walking users through a procedure step by step. Whenever users correctly carry out a step, you can automatically display the next step. If users incorrectly carry out a step, you can display information that specifically addresses their mistakes.

Developers can use the WinHelp API in their programs to send information to WinHelp. Help authors can use the TCard macro in their Help files to send information to programs. Most Help authors will have to work closely with a program developer in designing an effective set of training cards.

{button ,AL(`TCARD_DESIGN;TCard;winhelp_api;WM_TCARD')} Related Topics%!AL(`TCARD_DESIGN;TCard;winhelp_api;WM_TCARD')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b About training card Help
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Training cards are a set of Help topics in secondary windows that you can configure to send instructions to and receive instructions from another program. This is particularly useful in walking users through a procedure step by step. Whenever users correctly carry out a step, you can automatically display the next step. If users incorrectly carry out a step, you can display information that specifically addresses their mistakes.
\par Developers can use the WinHelp API in their programs to send information to WinHelp. Help authors can use the TCard macro in their Help files to send information to programs. Most Help authors will have to work closely with a program developer in designing an effective set of training cards.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL(`TCARD_DESIGN;TCard;winhelp_api;WM_TCARD')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`TCARD_DESIGN;TCard;winhelp_api;WM_TCARD')}
\par
```

## 972. Customizing main window menus

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`

    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `customizing;customizing, menus;Help system;Help system, customizing main window menus;menus;menus, customizing`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    customizing;customizing, menus;Help system;Help system, customizing main window menus;menus;menus, customizing
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Customizing main window menus

Unlike a secondary window,2ZK4PT a Help main windowO8.Q2 features a menu bar. You can use standard menus and commands, or you can add new menus and menu items by using macros.

You can also use menus to display commands that can be organized into logical groups. For example, you can change the items on menus to reflect the type of information contained in a group of related topics. WinHelp can place a check mark next to a menu item to indicate that the item is off or on.

Menu items run macros when users click them, press a key combination, or press an accelerator key. You can make menu items available or unavailable during a Help session.

To use a menu macro, you must run it from the main window. If a topic contains a macro that adds an item to the Help menu, but that topic is displayed in a secondary window, the macro will have no effect.

{button ,AL(`MACRO_REF_MENU')} Related Topics%!AL(`MACRO_REF_MENU')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b Customizing main window menus
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Unlike a \ul secondary window,\plain\f1\fs16 {\v 2ZK4PT} a Help \ul main window\plain\f1\fs16 {\v O8.Q2} features a menu bar. You can use standard menus and commands, or you can add new menus and menu items by using macros.
\par You can also use menus to display commands that can be organized into logical groups. For example, you can change the items on menus to reflect the type of information contained in a group of related topics. WinHelp can place a check mark next to a menu item to indicate that the item is off or on.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 Menu items run macros when users click them, press a key combination, or press an accelerator key. You can make menu items available or unavailable during a Help session. 
\par To use a menu macro, you must run it from the main window. If a topic contains a macro that adds an item to the Help menu, but that topic is displayed in a secondary window, the macro will have no effect.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL(`MACRO_REF_MENU')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`MACRO_REF_MENU')}
\par
```

## 973. Customizing Help buttons

### WinHelp Footnotes

    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `button bar;button bar, customizing;buttons,;buttons, customizing;customizing;customizing, Help buttons;Help system;Help system, customizing Help buttons`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    button bar;button bar, customizing;buttons,;buttons, customizing;customizing;customizing, Help buttons;Help system;Help system, customizing Help buttons
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Customizing Help buttons

In WinHelp version 4.0, both main and secondary windows can have a button bar with as many as 22 buttons on it. You can use the standard button bar set or use a custom set of buttons. To define custom buttons, you use macros.

Buttons are best used to display a limited number of frequently used functions. They enable users to access features by a single mouse click or key combination.

Help buttons run macros when users click them, press a key combination, or press an accelerator key. You can make Help buttons available or unavailable during a Help session.

Notes

·	WinHelp automatically sizes buttons to fit the text displayed on the button.

·	Buttons are added to the button bar in the order in which you specify them.

·	Buttons can contain only text; they cannot contain graphics.

{button ,AL(`MACRO_REF_BUTTON;PRO_HPJ_ADD_BUTTON_BAR;PRO_ADD_BROWSE_BUTTONS;PRO_HPJ_MAIN_BUTTON_BAR')} Related Topics%!AL(`MACRO_REF_BUTTON;PRO_HPJ_ADD_BUTTON_BAR;PRO_ADD_BROWSE_BUTTONS;PRO_HPJ_MAIN_BUTTON_BAR')
```

\b Customizing Help buttons
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 In WinHelp version 4.0, both main and secondary windows can have a button bar with as many as 22 buttons on it. You can use the standard button bar set or use a custom set of buttons. To define custom buttons, you use macros.
\par Buttons are best used to display a limited number of frequently used functions. They enable users to access features by a single mouse click or key combination. 
\par Help buttons run macros when users click them, press a key combination, or press an accelerator key. You can make Help buttons available or unavailable during a Help session. 
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Notes
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab WinHelp automatically sizes buttons to fit the text displayed on the button.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Buttons are added to the button bar in the order in which you specify them.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Buttons can contain only text; they cannot contain graphics.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL(`MACRO_REF_BUTTON;PRO_HPJ_ADD_BUTTON_BAR;PRO_ADD_BROWSE_BUTTONS;PRO_HPJ_MAIN_BUTTON_BAR')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`MACRO_REF_BUTTON;PRO_HPJ_ADD_BUTTON_BAR;PRO_ADD_BROWSE_BUTTONS;PRO_HPJ_MAIN_BUTTON_BAR')}

Help system limits

The 32-bit technology of Windows 95 enables WinHelp version 4.0 to dramatically expand the capability of the Help system you author.

Item	Limit

Help file size	2 gigabytes

Topics per topic (.rtf) file	No practical limit

Topics per Help file	No practical limit

Topics per keyword9ML77Q	800

Topic footnote.NSQ_T length	16,383 characters

Keyword length	255 characters

Hotspot hidden text	4,095 characters

Help title string	127 characters

Topic title string	127 characters

Custom window title string	50 characters

Custom window name	8 characters

Copyright string	255 characters

Browse string	50 characters

Referenced bitmaps	65,535 bitmaps per Help file

Filename	259 characters

Font name	31 characters

Font ranges	20 ranges

Error log file	No limit (Help Workshop can display 64K of text in Windows 95 and 1 MB of text in Windows NT, but the log file itself has no size limit.)

CitationC02ROW string	2,000 characters

Window definitions	255 per Project file

Window caption	50 characters

contents file entries	No practical limit

Contents headings	9 levels (indented)

Contents topic strings	255 characters (127 in a DBCS language)

Contents heading text	No practical limit

{button ,AL(`DESIGN')} Related Topics%!AL(`DESIGN')
```

### WinHelp Footnotes

    ```
- Marker `>`

    tables
    ```
- Marker `A`
  - Decoded: `BAS_CNT;BAS_CNT_COMBINE_HLP_FILES;BAS_COMB_CONTENTS;DESIGN;MULTIPLE_HELP_DESIGN`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BAS_CNT;BAS_CNT_COMBINE_HLP_FILES;BAS_COMB_CONTENTS;DESIGN;MULTIPLE_HELP_DESIGN
    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `ALinks;ALinks, using with multiple Help files;combined index;combining multiple Help files;contents files;contents files, designing for multiple Help files;Contents tab;Contents tab, adding sections from multiple Help files;Help files;Help files, designing multiple;Help files, linking multiple;Help files, reinitializing;Help system;Help system, adding files after releasing;Help system, designing for multiple Help files;Help system, reinitializing;index,;index, combining keywords from multiple Help files;interfile jumps;jumps;jumps, to another Help file;keywords;keywords, combining from multiple Help files;KLinks;KLinks, using with multiple Help files;linking multiple Help files;merging multiple Help files;multiple Help files, designing;reinitializing Help files;Report command`
  - Raw:

    ALinks;ALinks, using with multiple Help files;combined index;combining multiple Help files;contents files;contents files, designing for multiple Help files;Contents tab;Contents tab, adding sections from multiple Help files;Help files;Help files, designing multiple;Help files, linking multiple;Help files, reinitializing;Help system;Help system, adding files after releasing;Help system, designing for multiple Help files;Help system, reinitializing;index,;index, combining keywords from multiple Help files;interfile jumps;jumps;jumps, to another Help file;keywords;keywords, combining from multiple Help files;KLinks;KLinks, using with multiple Help files;linking multiple Help files;merging multiple Help files;multiple Help files, designing;reinitializing Help files;Report command
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Designing for multiple Help files

You do not need to include all your Help topics within one Help file. For example, if the program you are documenting contains modular components, authoring separate Help files for each component is often a good idea. If a user decides not to install a component during setup or adds a new component later, the Help system still appears as a single unit. WinHelp version 4.0 can present a single index and a single table of contents for all the Help files you specify.

By planning ahead, you can make it possible to add information to your family of Help files after it has been released. By including the appropriate information in the contents file, you can automatically add new sections to the Contents tab and new keywords to the Index tab. You can also create jumps to topics that do not exist at the time you release your product but that might be added later.

When designing a Help system that uses multiple Help files or that may be augmented by additional Help files, consider the following:

·	In the contents file you can specify jumps to topics that are located in other Help files. If WinHelp does not find the referenced Help file on a user's computer, the topics located in that file do not appear on the Contents tab. This feature is useful if your program has several components that are optional for users to install. In this case, simply create separate Help files for each component, and if one is not installed, the topics for that component do not appear on the Contents tab. This minimizes distraction and confusion for users.

·	To prevent inconsistencies in the index, such as verb tense, capitalization, and the use of plurals, use the Report command in Help Workshop to generate a list of the keywords that are in the Help files you intend to combine.

·	You can use keyword jumps to jump to topics in your family of Help files or in another family of Help files. KLink macros provide jumps to multiple topics that contain specified indexed K-keywords. ALink macros provide jumps to topics that contain specified nonindexed A-keywords. These macros are especially useful if you have multiple Help files because the links are resolved at run-time, based on the Help files that are actually available when the macro is run.

Note

·	If you add files to your family of Help files after it has been released, the additions will not appear on the original Contents or Index tab until the Help file is reinitialized. To reinitialize the Help file, a setup program or your user must run the following command:

	winhelp -g filename.hlp

	For filename.hlp, substitute the name of the Help file you want to reinitialize.

{button ,AL(`BAS_OVERVIEW;BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;BAS_WRITE_INDEX;BAS_ASSOCIATIVE_JUMPS')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_OVERVIEW;BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;BAS_WRITE_INDEX;BAS_ASSOCIATIVE_JUMPS')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b Designing for multiple Help files
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 You do not need to include all your Help topics within one Help file. For example, if the program you are documenting contains modular components, authoring separate Help files for each component is often a good idea. If a user decides not to install a component during setup or adds a new component later, the Help system still appears as a single unit. WinHelp version 4.0 can present a single index and a single table of contents for all the Help files you specify.
\par By planning ahead, you can make it possible to add information to your family of Help files after it has been released. By including the appropriate information in the contents file, you can automatically add new sections to the Contents tab and new keywords to the Index tab. You can also create jumps to topics that do not exist at the time you release your product but that might be added later.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 When designing a Help system that uses multiple Help files or that may be augmented by additional Help files, consider the following:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab In the contents file you can specify jumps to topics that are located in other Help files. If WinHelp does not find the referenced Help file on a user's computer, the topics located in that file do not appear on the Contents tab. This feature is useful if your program has several components that are optional for users to install. In this case, simply create separate Help files for each component, and if one is not installed, the topics for that component do not appear on the Contents tab. This minimizes distraction and confusion for users.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab To prevent inconsistencies in the index, such as verb tense, capitalization, and the use of plurals, use the Report command in Help Workshop to generate a list of the keywords that are in the Help files you intend to combine.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab You can use keyword jumps to jump to topics in your family of Help files or in another family of Help files. KLink macros provide jumps to multiple topics that contain specified indexed K-keywords. ALink macros provide jumps to topics that contain specified nonindexed A-keywords. These macros are especially useful if you have multiple Help files because the links are resolved at run-time, based on the Help files that are actually available when the macro is run.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \b Note
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab If you add files to your family of Help files after it has been released, the additions will not appear on the original Contents or Index tab until the Help file is reinitialized. To reinitialize the Help file, a setup program or your user must run the following command:
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \b \tab winhelp\'a0-g filename.hlp
\par \plain\f1\fs16 \tab For filename.hlp, substitute the name of the Help file you want to reinitialize.\b 
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 \plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL(`BAS_OVERVIEW;BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;BAS_WRITE_INDEX;BAS_ASSOCIATIVE_JUMPS')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BAS_OVERVIEW;BAS_ABOUT_HPJ_FILES;BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;BAS_WRITE_INDEX;BAS_ASSOCIATIVE_JUMPS')}

## 976. Designing a table of contents

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:008e`

    ```
- Marker `$`

- Marker `#`

- Marker `A`

    ```
- Marker `K`

    ```rtf
    contents files;contents files, designing a table of contents;Contents tab;Contents tab, designing a table of contents;Help system;Help system, designing a table of contents;table of contents, designing
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Designing a table of contents

You design the information on the Contents tab by configuring a contents (.cnt) file. You may want to show all topics or show only those topics that beginning users are likely to need.

When designing a table of contents, keep the following in mind:

·	Book icons represent headings. Double-clicking a book icon enables users to see the subentries under that heading. Subentries can be other book icons or page icons.

·	Page icons represent topics. Double-clicking a page icon enables users to display a topic or run a macro. You can specify what window type a topic should appear in.

·	Page icons can jump to topics in other Help files.

·	Users can print the contents of a book icon by clicking the icon, and then clicking the Print button on the Contents tab.

·	You can include other contents files in your main contents file.

{button ,AL(`BAS_OVERVIEW;BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;PRO_CNT_ADD_BOOK;PRO_CNT_ADD_TOPIC;PRO_HPJ_SPECIFY_CNT_FILE;PRO_CNT_DYN;PRO_CNT_DEFAULT_HELP_FILE')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_OVERVIEW;BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;PRO_CNT_ADD_BOOK;PRO_CNT_ADD_TOPIC;PRO_HPJ_SPECIFY_CNT_FILE;PRO_CNT_DYN;PRO_CNT_DEFAULT_HELP_FILE')
```

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b Designing a table of contents
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 You design the information on the Contents tab by configuring a contents (.cnt) file. You may want to show all topics or show only those topics that beginning users are likely to need.
\par When designing a table of contents, keep the following in mind:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Book icons represent headings. Double-clicking a book icon enables users to see the subentries under that heading. Subentries can be other book icons or page icons.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Page icons represent topics. Double-clicking a page icon enables users to display a topic or run a macro. You can specify what window type a topic should appear in.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Page icons can jump to topics in other Help files.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Users can print the contents of a book icon by clicking the icon, and then clicking the Print button on the Contents tab.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab You can include other contents files in your main contents file.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 \{button ,AL(`BAS_OVERVIEW;BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;PRO_CNT_ADD_BOOK;PRO_CNT_ADD_TOPIC;PRO_HPJ_SPECIFY_CNT_FILE;PRO_CNT_DYN;PRO_CNT_DEFAULT_HELP_FILE')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BAS_OVERVIEW;BAS_CNT_OVERVIEW;PRO_CNT_ADD_BOOK;PRO_CNT_ADD_TOPIC;PRO_HPJ_SPECIFY_CNT_FILE;PRO_CNT_DYN;PRO_CNT_DEFAULT_HELP_FILE')}

### Decoded Body

```text
Designing pop-up topics

A pop-up is a window that "pops up" on top of a window or dialog box. WinHelp automatically sizes pop-up windows to fit the text or graphic in them. Pop-ups remain on screen until users click anywhere on their screen.

{bmc bm23.bmp} Typical uses for pop-ups%_I.CEK

{bmc bm23.bmp} Considerations for writing pop-up topics%16Z.AQ0

{button ,AL(`BAS_OVERVIEW;BAS_CREATE_TOPIC;BAS_INSERTING_POPUP')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_OVERVIEW;BAS_CREATE_TOPIC;BAS_INSERTING_POPUP')
```

\b Designing pop-up topics
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 A pop-up is a window that "pops up" on top of a window or dialog box. WinHelp automatically sizes pop-up windows to fit the text or graphic in them. Pop-ups remain on screen until users click anywhere on their screen.
\par \uldb \{bmc bm23.bmp\} Typical uses for pop-ups\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %_I.CEK}
\par \uldb \{bmc bm23.bmp\} Considerations for writing pop-up topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %16Z.AQ0}

Typical uses for pop-ups

## 979. 16Z.AQ0

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `16Z.AQ0`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    16Z.AQ0
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Considerations for writing pop-up topics

When you write pop-up topics, you may want to consider the following:

·	Unless you include an authorable Print button in pop-ups, users can print them only by clicking them with their right mouse button, and then clicking Print.

·	Topics that do not have titles specified by $ footnotes are typically excluded from the full-text searchATV8VK index. Because pop-ups are best used in context, it would be more confusing than helpful if users were able to find the text in them.

·	For context-sensitive Help topics that will be called from a program, make sure the first four characters in the topic ID are IDH_. This enables Help Workshop to detect whether topic IDs are mapped to numeric values in your project (.hpj) file.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Considerations for writing pop-up topics
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 When you write pop-up topics, you may want to consider the following:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Unless you include an authorable Print button in pop-ups, users can print them only by clicking them with their right mouse button, and then clicking Print.
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Topics that do not have titles specified by $ footnotes are typically excluded from the \ul full-text search\plain\f1\fs16 {\v ATV8VK} index. Because pop-ups are best used in context, it would be more confusing than helpful if users were able to find the text in them.
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab For context-sensitive Help topics that will be called from a program, make sure the first four characters in the topic ID are IDH_. This enables Help Workshop to detect whether topic IDs are mapped to numeric values in your project (.hpj) file.
\par
```

## 980. Guidelines for using video and animation

### WinHelp Footnotes

    ```
- Marker `K`
  - Decoded: `animation;animation, guidelines for using;avi files in Help topics;file size;Help system;Help system, multimedia guidelines;multimedia;multimedia, guidelines for using;video;video, guidelines for using`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    animation;animation, guidelines for using;avi files in Help topics;file size;Help system;Help system, multimedia guidelines;multimedia;multimedia, guidelines for using;video;video, guidelines for using
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Guidelines for using video and animation

Video and animation files can be included in any topic, including pop-up topics. WinHelp version 4.0 creates an embedded window for multimedia files and automatically sizes the window to accommodate the image or controller (if any). If the window is not large enough to display the controller, WinHelp crops the right and bottom edges. Users can resize the window to show any part of the controller that does not initially appear in the window.

Multimedia files tend to be large and can significantly increase a Help file's size. If your Help file is likely to be copied to a user's hard disk (rather than run from a CD-ROM), file size might be a an issue.

Note

·	You can ship multimedia files without including them in your Help file. This feature may be useful if you want to run sample files that are already included as part of the product, or that currently exist on a user's computer.

{button ,AL(`BAS_OVERVIEW;BAS_ADDING_VIDEO;BAS_ADDING_SOUND;TMPDIR;mci;PRO_ADD_VIDEO')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_OVERVIEW;BAS_ADDING_VIDEO;BAS_ADDING_SOUND;TMPDIR;mci;PRO_ADD_VIDEO')
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf
\pard\sb115\sa35\sl-235\li115\ri125 

\b Guidelines for using video and animation
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 Video and animation files can be included in any topic, including pop-up topics. WinHelp version 4.0 creates an embedded window for multimedia files and automatically sizes the window to accommodate the image or controller (if any). If the window is not large enough to display the controller, WinHelp crops the right and bottom edges. Users can resize the window to show any part of the controller that does not initially appear in the window.
\par Multimedia files tend to be large and can significantly increase a Help file's size. If your Help file is likely to be copied to a user's hard disk (rather than run from a CD-ROM), file size might be a an issue.
\par \pard\sb115\sl-235\li115\ri125 \b Note
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \plain\f2\fs16 \'b7\f1 \tab You can ship multimedia files without including them in your Help file. This feature may be useful if you want to run sample files that are already included as part of the product, or that currently exist on a user's computer.
\par \pard\sl-115\li265\ri125\fi-185\brdrb\brdrs\tx295 
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \{button ,AL(`BAS_OVERVIEW;BAS_ADDING_VIDEO;BAS_ADDING_SOUND;TMPDIR;mci;PRO_ADD_VIDEO')\} \uldb Related Topics\plain\f1\fs16 {\v %!AL(`BAS_OVERVIEW;BAS_ADDING_VIDEO;BAS_ADDING_SOUND;TMPDIR;mci;PRO_ADD_VIDEO')}
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Designing an index

A comprehensive keyword9ML77Q list is one of the most helpful navigating tools you can provide for users. In an index, you can provide synonyms that help users find information without requiring them to be familiar with your terminology. Even though WinHelp 4.0 supports full-text searchATV8VK features, you will probably want to prepare a keyword list. If users have only a full-text search index available to them, they can search for and find only words that are actually present in the text of the Help file.

{bmc bm23.bmp} Types of keywords%8DAFB1

{bmc bm23.bmp} Keyword guidelines%BDO.1Y

{button ,AL(`PRO_ADD_KEYWORD;PRO_ADD_LEVEL2_KEYWORDS;PRO_CNT_COMBINE_HELP_FILES')} Related Topics%!AL(`PRO_ADD_KEYWORD;PRO_ADD_LEVEL2_KEYWORDS;PRO_CNT_COMBINE_HELP_FILES')
```

In an index, you can provide synonyms that help users find information without requiring them to be familiar with your terminology. Even though WinHelp 4.0 supports \ul full-text search\plain\f1\fs16 {\v ATV8VK} features, you will probably want to prepare a keyword list. If users have only a full-text search index available to them, they can search for and find only words that are actually present in the text of the Help file.

## 982. 8DAFB1

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `8DAFB1`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    8DAFB1
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Types of keywords

It is typically best to include several types of keywords in each topic. When you specify keywords, you may want to include:

·	Nontechnical terms that are likely to occur to beginning users

·	Technical terms that are likely to occur to advanced users

·	Common synonyms for technical terms

·	Words that describe the topic generally

·	Words that describe specific subjects within the topic

·	Inverted forms of keyword phrases. For example, "combining Help files" and "Help files, combining"
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf

\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 It is typically best to include several types of keywords in each topic. When you specify keywords, you may want to include:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Nontechnical terms that are likely to occur to beginning users
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Technical terms that are likely to occur to advanced users
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Common synonyms for technical terms
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Words that describe the topic generally
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Words that describe specific subjects within the topic
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Inverted forms of keyword phrases. For example, "combining Help files" and "Help files, combining"
\par
```

## 983. BDO.1Y

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `#`
  - Decoded: `BDO.1Y`
  - Raw:
    ```rtf
    BDO.1Y
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
Keyword guidelines

If you want your index to look similar to those in Windows 95 Help, you may want to consider the following:

·	Always use the gerund form of verbs for keywords that begin with a verb

·	Always use the plural form of nouns

·	Use synonyms consistently throughout a family of Help files

·	Make sure that keyword phrases beginning with the same word are consistently general or consistently specific

You can create first and second-level index entries.DOQEEK Users can either double-click second-level index entries to view particular topics or double-click a first-level entry to display the titles of all subentries in the Topics Found dialog box.
```

### Raw RTF Body

```rtf

\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125 \plain\f1\fs16 If you want your index to look similar to those in Windows\'a095 Help, you may want to consider the following:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Always use the gerund form of verbs for keywords that begin with a verb
\par \pard\sb45\sl-235\li295\ri125\fi-185\tx295 \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Always use the plural form of nouns
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Use synonyms consistently throughout a family of Help files
\par \f2 \'b7\f1 \tab Make sure that keyword phrases beginning with the same word are consistently general or consistently specific
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx295 You can create first and \ul second-level index entries.\plain\f1\fs16 {\v DOQEEK} Users can either double-click second-level index entries to view particular topics or double-click a first-level entry to display the titles of all subentries in the Topics Found dialog box.
\par
```

### Decoded Body

```text
Designing Help windows

When considering what type of information should go in mainO8.Q2 and secondary windows,2ZK4PT consider the following features and limitations of each window type:

·	Secondary windows do not have a menu bar.

·	Secondary windows do not provide access to a history window or to bookmarks.

·	You cannot automatically size the main window to accommodate the amount of text in a topic.

If you are keeping your Help window on top, consider its size and placement. Ideally, users should be able to simultaneously read and carry out a procedure.

{button ,AL(`BAS_OVERVIEW;BAS_HPJ_CREATE_WINDOW')} Related Topics%!AL(`BAS_OVERVIEW;BAS_HPJ_CREATE_WINDOW')va
```

- Marker `K`

  - Raw:

    context-sensitive Help;context-sensitive Help, structure containing information about requested item;HELPINFO structure;HELPINFO_MENUITEM parameter;HELPINFO_WINDOW parameter;reference;reference, WinHelp APIs
    ```

### Decoded Body

```text
HELPINFO

typedef  struct  tagHELPINFO {-  	// hi

    UINT   cbSize;

    int    iContextType

    int    iCtrlId;

    HANDLE hItemHandle;

    DWORD  dwContextId;

    POINT  MousePos;

} HELPINFO, FAR *LPHELPINFO;

The HELPINFO structure contains information about an item for which context-sensitive Help has been requested.

Parameter	Description

cbSize	Size of this structure, in bytes.

iContextType	Type of context for which Help is requested. This member can be one of these values:

Value	Meaning

HELPINFO_MENUITEM	Help requested for a menu item.

HELPINFO_WINDOW	Help requested for a control or window.

iCtrlld	Identifier of the window or control if the iContextType parameter is HELPINFO_WINDOW, or identifier of the menu item if the iContextType parameter is HELPINFO_MENUITEM.

hItemHandle	Identifier of the child window or control if iContextType is HELPINFO_WINDOW, or identifier of the associated menu if iContextType is HELPINFO_MENUITEM.

dwContextId	Help context identifier of the window or control.

MousePos	A POINT structure that contains the screen coordinates of the mouse cursor. This parameter is useful for providing Help based on the position of the mouse cursor.

{button ,KL("WM_HELP message;POINT")} Related Topics%!KL("WM_HELP message;POINT")
```

### Raw RTF Body

\b\fs24 HELPINFO
\par \pard\keepn\sb75\sl-195\li115\ri125\tx355 \plain\f1\fs16 typedef  struct  tagHELPINFO \{\-  \tab // hi\line
    UINT   cbSize;\line
    int    iContextType\line
    int    iCtrlId;\line
    HANDLE hItemHandle;\line
    DWORD  dwContextId;\line
    POINT  MousePos;\line
\'7d HELPINFO, FAR *LPHELPINFO;
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li115\ri125\tx355 The \b HELPINFO\plain\f1\fs16  structure contains information about an item for which context-sensitive Help has been requested.
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li1435\ri125\fi-1305\brdrb\brdrs\tx1435 \b Parameter\tab Description\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1435\ri125\fi-1305\tx1435 cbSize\tab Size of this structure, in bytes.
\par iContextType\tab Type of context for which Help is requested. This member can be one of these values:
\par \pard\sb75\sl-235\li3425\ri125\fi-1985\brdrb\brdrs\tx3415 \b Value\tab Meaning\plain\f1\fs16 
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li3425\ri125\fi-1985\tx3415 HELPINFO_MENUITEM\tab Help requested for a menu item.
\par HELPINFO_WINDOW\tab Help requested for a control or window.
\par \pard\sb55\sl-235\li1435\ri125\fi-1305\tx1435 iCtrlld\tab Identifier of the window or control if the iContextType parameter is HELPINFO_WINDOW, or identifier of the menu item if the iContextType parameter is HELPINFO_MENUITEM.
\par hItemHandle\tab Identifier of the child window or control if iContextType is HELPINFO_WINDOW, or identifier of the associated menu if iContextType is HELPINFO_MENUITEM.
\par dwContextId\tab Help context identifier of the window or control.
\par MousePos\tab A \b POINT\plain\f1\fs16  structure that contains the screen coordinates of the mouse cursor. This parameter is useful for providing Help based on the position of the mouse cursor.

## 986. HELPWININFO structure

### WinHelp Footnotes

- Marker `+`
  - Decoded: `BROWSE0027:00e5`

    BROWSE0027:00e5
    ```

    HELPWININFO structure
    ```
- Marker `#`
